- [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] CITY OF TAMPA, FLORIDA - RFQ - Complete work needed to migrate monitoring and control capabilities to the VTScada software. - Document SCADA network diagrams and control system programming. - Assist the City with selecting and implementing a new software system that can generate plant operating reports needed for regulatory requirements and for monitoring plant performance. - Develop and evaluate alternatives for replacing the fiber optic cable network, prepare cost estimates and construction sequencing for the alternatives, prepare contract documents for the selected alternative, including signed and sealed construction plans with specifications, final cost estimate, and preparation of contract documents for bidding. Provide services during the construction of the fiber optic cable replacement including shop drawings review, responding to request for information(RFI’s), and associated activities. Estimated fee is $2,000,000 - $4,000,000. Reference Drawings and any additional material may be found at: www.tampagov.net/contractadministration/programs/architectural-engineering-construction-and-related-rfqs Questions may be directed to Jim Greiner, P.E., Contract Administration, City of Tampa, (813) 274-8598, or E-Mail jim.greiner@tampagov.net. A pre-submission conference will be conducted at 2 PM March 2,2026, in the City Council Chambers, third floor Old City Hall, 315 E. Kennedy Blvd. Tampa, FL 33602. Attendance is not required. In accordance with the Americans with Disabilities Act(“ADA”) and Section 286.26, Florida Statutes, persons with disabilities needing a reasonable accommodation to participate in this public hearing or meeting should contact the City of Tampa’s ADA Coordinator at least 48 hours prior to the proceeding. The ADA Coordinator may be contacted via phone at 813-274-3964, email at TampaADA@tampagov.net, or by submitting an ADA - Accommodations Request form available online at tampagov.net/ADARequest. An individual or entity(“Firm”) responding to this RFQ must provide evidence of any required licenses, certificates, or registrations with its submission or within 10 days thereof in order to be considered. Ashley Drive, Suite 1000 • Tampa, FL 33602 • 813.630.4498 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 1. Introduction 1.1 Background The HFC AWTP is permitted to treat 96 mgd of raw sewage with a Type I two-stage, high rate (pure oxygen and conventional fine bubble aeration) activated sludge biological domestic wastewater treatment plant. This plant is operated to reduce the Total Nitrogen(TN) in the effluent to advanced standards, using biological treatment for nitrification and filtration for denitrification, and to discharge high-level disinfected and dechlorinated effluent to Tampa Bay. Residuals generated by this facility are anaerobically digested and dewatered for land application as a Class B residual. Currently, annual average daily flows are approximately 60 mgd and peak flows are approximately 200 mgd. The rehabilitation to the HFC AWTP will consist of the implementation of improvements identified in the Facility Master Plan Capital Improvement Project List for Fiscal Years 0-5 to maintain system reliability, increase operating efficiency, and meet current and future regulatory requirements. This Technical Memorandum details the SCADA Design Criteria. These criteria will be utilized for all instrumentation and control design performed as part of these improvements. 1.2 Workshops Three(3) workshops were held with the City in developing the SCADA Design Criteria. The findings of these workshops are documented herein and establish the minimum standards to use for developing P&IDs, specifying software and hardware, and guide the overall approach and sequencing for the replacement of the facility wide fiber communications network. The workshops addressed the following topics:  Workshop 1 - P&ID standards, facility fiber network improvements, approach to PLC/HMI software development, and internet/site security protection  Workshop 2 - Hardware standards  Workshop 3 - Design approach Meeting notes are provided in Appendix E for reference. HFC AWTP Master Plan June 10,2022 Improvements: SCADA Design Criteria Technical Memorandum Page 1 of 15 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] CITY OF TAMPA, FLORIDA - RFQ objective of the project is to migrate the remaining facilities operating on HSQ software to the VTScada software so that the entire plant is monitored and controlled through the VTScada software system. The City has recently completed new SCADA and Process Control System(PCS) programming, configuration and design standards. The project will ensure that the plant’s monitoring and control systems meet these standards. The objectives of the project will include, but not limited to: - Migrate the SCADA system from the existing HSQ software to VTScada software while ensuring continuous operation of the treatment plant. - Ensure that the new monitoring and control system aligns with the City's standards, including the use of approved widgets and graphic symbols for operator screens. - Implement a new software system that can generate plant operating reports needed for regulatory requirements and for monitoring plant performance. The new software system shall retain historical data from HSQ and shall be capable of generating reports using both historical and current data. - Ensure the system is scalable for future expansions and upgrades. - Provide the design and implementation plan for replacing the treatment plant’s fiber optic cable network. Services will include, but will not be limited to the following: - Complete an assessment of the existing SCADA system and related infrastructure to determine the requirements, scopes of work, priorities, and cost estimates to complete the migration to the VTScada software. Page 2 of 5 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 1. Objectives With the changes being implemented at the HFC AWTP, the City aims to select a new report creation software to ensure efficient identification of plant performance and regulatory compliance. Given the critical nature of these reports, it is vital that the chosen software integrates seamlessly with the existing database systems and offers comprehensive functionality for data analysis and reporting. To achieve this goal, the City has tasked Hazen and Sawyer(Hazen) with reviewing and evaluating various software platforms capable of meeting the plant's rigorous requirements. As part of this mandate, Hazen conducted a thorough assessment of multiple report creation software options to determine the most suitable solution for the HFC AWTP's needs. The evaluation process involved comparing the features, capabilities, and budgetary costs of each software platform. Hazen's objective was to identify a software solution that not only provides robust reporting capabilities but also enhances the overall operational efficiency of the plant. This includes the ability to generate accurate, real-time reports that offer insight into key metrics such as water flow rates, chemical usage, and equipment status. Moreover, the ideal software should facilitate user-friendly report creation and customization, enabling plant operators and management to easily interpret data and make informed decisions. Ensuring consistency and reliability in report generation is paramount, as these reports play a crucial role in maintaining the plant’s operational excellence and regulatory compliance. The findings of Hazen's comprehensive evaluation are summarized in the Report Generator Software Evaluation document, which outlines the strengths and weaknesses of each software platform considered. Based on this evaluation, Hazen has provided a recommendation for the standard software platform to be adopted for report creation moving forward. This recommendation is aimed at optimizing the plant's reporting processes and supporting the City's ongoing commitment to operational improvement and regulatory adherence. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 6 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3. Recommend Network Sequencing 3.1 Maintaining Monitoring and Reporting Once the P&IDs are developed and preliminary control descriptions are available, discussion on where new PLC panels will be located and how I/O will be connected to the new PLC modules will be made at milestones for each project. A detailed sequence on how to maintain monitoring and reporting will be developed as part of these discussions. These sequencing plans will be reviewed with City staff prior to completing the 90% Design. 3.2 Replacing Fiber Cables All fiber cables for each process, which includes fibers for the camera feeds, will be replaced as part of any improvement to that process. During development of the Preliminary P&IDs a thorough site investigations shall be performed to verify the existing fiber runs from the associated PLCs back to the main network fiber patch panel. This includes removing electrical manhole covers and draining the manholes. The purpose of these investigations is to determine what existing conduit runs will be used to best show on the design site plans for the fiber installation. At any point thereafter, investigations shall be performed to confirm new cable can be run through the existing conduits without removing the existing fiber cables. Final network diagram will include fiber cable labeling. Labeling for the cables and for the ports at patch panels will be installed in the field to match the network diagram. Multiple cables run through the same conduits and manholes will be depicted on the network site plan. 3.3 Conversion to VTScada VTScada HMI programming will be developed and placed in service parallel to the existing HSQ HMI system on a separate network rack and made available for interface on new workstations throughout the plant, as well as on wireless devices. As a process is made available on the VTScada HMI and is installed, tested, and passed an approved reliability demonstration period, the associated HSQ HMI display screens could be removed or made view only. The goal will be to get each process on the VTScada HMI to use for setpoint adjustment, monitoring, and/or manual override. However, there will be some parameters that may impact separate processes still controlled only through HSQ. A plan will need to be developed for such situations to provide a protocol to operations until all control parameters have been switched over to VTScada. HFC AWTP Master Plan June 10,2022 Improvements: SCADA Design Criteria Technical Memorandum Page 15 of 15 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] During the installation of new fiber cables, the existing cables shall be left in place. It is recommended to perform field investigations during the design phases to verify new fiber cable can to installed utilizing the existing infrastruce. 2.3 HMI Software Conversion 2.3.1 VTScada The City is in the process of creating VTScada screens to replace the existing HSQ system. Currently, the City has implement monitoring only screens for some of the facilities through VTScada, this application is running in parallel the existing HSQ application. The following facilities have VTScada screens for monitoring only:  Junction Chamber #1  Screen and Grit Buildings  Primary Sedimentation Tanks  Digestion  STAR The following City preferences have been identified for implementing the HMI software conversion:  Migrate all monitoring and control capabilities to VTScada. All new HMI software applications and screens will be based on using VTScada.  Screens developed in VTScada are to be similar to existing screens, matching the layout of the P&IDs.  An Overview of each facility is to be provided that is similar to existing overview displays.  Control and monitoring of particular process areas within the facility shall be accessible by clicking on that area on the overview display to bring up the “P&ID” type display for the area.  Individual equipment control shall be accessible by clicking on that equipment to bring up dashboard style pop up control screens similar to existing.  During development of displays, City staff will be provided examples of high-performance graphic displays for their review to decide if such displays offer a better grasp of overview for each process area. At this time, the plant processes, collection system pump stations, and stormwater stations have separate VTScada realms within one application on the same server. The City is considering implementing separate VTScada applications for each of these areas. HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvement projects will include some high-performance screens following ISA101 Design Standard. Workshops for operators will be held as part of each project. Control descriptions will be developed prior to those workshops. High performance displays will be discussed at these workshops to decide design of such displays and if they are to be provided. HFC AWTP Master Plan June 10,2022 Improvements: SCADA Design Criteria Technical Memorandum Page 10 of 15 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] The historical data from HSQ is to be saved and made available in the VTScada system. This shall be addressed in the Main Pump Station and HPO Reactor Improvements project currently under design. At a minimum, data starting from the beginning of 2000 shall be transferred. 2.3.2 PLC / HMI Software Integration The design team will coordinate with the process integrator and the City to develop the software. City staff will participate in actual development of graphics, tag creation, and programming to become more familiar with VTScada for future development and system troubleshooting. At the beginning of each construction phase, a pre-construction instrumentation coordination meeting will be held to review software development scope of work and to determine the role and responsibilities. The City staff will provide oversight of the HMI screen and PLC controls development. For the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvements Dewatering Facility Replacement project, RTP will be developing the HMI screens with oversight from the City. These displays will be used to set standards for displays to provide for all improvements. 2.3.3 HMI Security The following extent of exposure to internet connectivity will be incorporated into the SCADA design for each process improvement:  VPN connection for remote setup for vendor access to HMI when allowed by the City.  Similar to existing, access to HMI by City staff through handheld devices and laptops to connect through internet VPN over wireless cellular means. Previous efforts to create wireless hotspots proved to be inadequate at this site.  New controls at the PLC level on the fiber network shall be accessible through similar wireless internet VPN connectivity. 2.4 Site Security & Monitoring The existing plant camera system only includes ten(10) cameras. These are listed in Appendix G. With each improvement, design will include new cameras to eventually be able to monitor all areas within the plant. Location, number, and type of cameras will be discussed with City staff as part of the detail design phase of each HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvement. The existing SONY system is to be replaced with a GENETEC system, including a new server. The initial upgrade will occur during the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvements Dewatering Facility Replacement, which is concurrently under construction. All existing cameras will be transitioned to the new GENETEC system once it is operational. Entry gates will utilize GENETEC card reader system with interface to the new base system to allow monitoring of entry points, two-way voice communication for visitors, and remote operation of gates. Additionally, the City may replace the site perimeter fence with an eight-foot(8’) tall fence at some point in the future. HFC AWTP Master Plan June 10,2022 Improvements: SCADA Design Criteria Technical Memorandum Page 11 of 15 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Job no 620500-001, Change 03, Project 20-500 - HFCAWTF Master Plan SCADA DESIGN CRITERIA - WORK SHOP 2 Meeting Notes Date: October 21,2021 Location: 2545 Guy N. Verger Blvd., Tampa, FL 33605 & Teams Virtual Meeting Time: 01:30pm EST Attendees: See Sign-in Sheet DISCUSSION ITEMS: A. Hardware & Commissioning- Review of sample from Dewatering & Grit projects 1. Hardware a. PLCs - In general, provide independent vendor PLC hardware for individual equipment systems (like presses, centrifuges, pump VFDs, etc) that interface with overall system PLCs provided by integrator. The City did not have preferences for most components. If parts go bad, City just replaces with the standard at that time. Items that are City preferences include (Hazen to verify full model numbers for specific features noted): 1) PLC CPU - GE Rx3i IC695CPE305 for single CPUs and IC695CPE410 for hot swappable CPUs. 2) AI module - IC694ALG223,16-channel single, 8 differential, isolated 3) AO module - IC694ALG392,8 channel 4) DI module - IC694MDL241,16 input, 24Vdc 5) DO module - IC694MDL940,16 relay outputs 6) PLC power supply - IC695PSA140 Hazen and Sawyer • 10002 Princess Palm Avenue, Suite 200 • Tampa, FL 33619 • 813.630.4498 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Appendix B Introduction This is Appendix B of the City of Tampa’s standards for Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) and Process Control System(PCS) programming, configuration and design standards for The City of Tampa referred to as “The City” or abbreviated as “COT” throughout this document Appendix B contains the referenced forms and documents required to perform deviations of standards and changes of the City of Tampa’s process control system. The City SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual is intended to be applied by The City’s contractors, consultants, and in-house personnel when developing or modifying any portion of The City’s control system. The control system is a utility-wide system built upon a common human machine interface(HMI) system and is comprised of the following: Process Control System(PCS): The industrial control system for processes within the boundaries of the plant(Howard F. Curren AWTP). The primary function of the system is control and monitoring of plant operations. The PCS is comprised of the local HMI subsystem and various programmable logic controllers(PLCs) and operator interface terminals(OITs). Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) System: The industrial control system for processes outside the boundaries of the plants. The primary function of this system is data acquisition; remote sites are designed to run autonomously without supervisory control function. The SCADA system serves both Water SCADA and Sewer SCADA in an integrated environment and is comprised of telemetry equipment with a supervisory HMI. This manual is divided into three main volumes plus two appendices: Volume 1 contains the control system policies and procedures that apply to the management, maintenance and modification of the control system. Volume 1 includes policies for: - Programming laptop use - Management of change(MOC) - Software and application revision control - Cybersecurity - Other related topics. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards Appendix B ­ Forms and Documents January 2025 3 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software Introduction The Howard F. Curren Advanced Water Treatment Plant(HFC AWTP) owns and operates a comprehensive Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) system. This system is primarily composed of General Electric(GE) PAC System Rx3i and 90-30 series programmable logic controllers(PLCs) at the field level, and VTScada by Trihedral at the SCADA/HMI level. SCADA systems are critical for monitoring and controlling industrial processes, providing real-time data, and enabling remote control and automation of plant operations. As part of ongoing improvement efforts, the City is currently in the process of upgrading the controllers throughout all facilities to more advanced Allen Bradley controllers, which are manufactured by Rockwell Automation. These new controllers are expected to enhance the reliability and efficiency of the plant’s operations, offering improved performance and extended capabilities. The data generated by the SCADA system is stored in a robust database, which serves as the backbone for gathering insights into the plant's performance. This data includes various metrics such as water flow rates, chemical usage, and equipment status, among others. It is essential for ensuring that the plant meets stringent regulatory requirements for water quality, which are set by environmental authorities to protect public health and the environment. To maintain these high standards, the data needs to be analyzed periodically. This analysis involves examining trends, identifying any anomalies or deviations, and generating reports that summarize the plant's performance. These reports are crucial for the plant's operators and management, as they provide a clear overview of the plant’s operations and highlight any areas that may require attention or improvement. Therefore, selecting the right report creation software is of paramount importance. The software must integrate seamlessly with the existing database, ensuring that the data is accurately captured and processed. It should also offer user-friendly interfaces for creating and customizing reports, allowing users to easily interpret the data and make informed decisions. Consistency and reliability in report generation are critical, as these reports play a vital role in maintaining the plant’s operational excellence and regulatory compliance. - [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 2 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 HMI Software The SCADA software currently utilized by the City is VTScada 12.1.58, development environment from Trihedral Engineering, contact the City for version being used. This software suite forms the basis of the system, as well as providing the majority of the data acquisition drivers. All development of HMI screens and system wide visualization will utilize this software. All new HMI screens and graphical elements are required to be developed using the latest available City provided ChangeSet file. A ChangeSet file contains the instructions required to re-create an application. This is not an exact backup but a compendium of changes and the base information to reproduce a functional application. In general, ChangeSets are used to distribute new applications, distribute updates to an existing application, or add extra features to OEM layers. This ChangeSet file will contain all of the latest container tags, display definitions, and library widgets needed to support development activities. Each project shall request, via email to the City’s project manager (or assigned City Operational Technology(OT) contact), a copy of the current ChangeSet application immediately prior to initiating programming activities. Although updates to the standard may occasionally occur, the files provided on request date shall be utilized throughout the project. Only those updates which are determined mission critical (have severe consequences to operations, life or equipment safety) will be handled on a case by case basis through direct notice to programmers by the City’s project manager or assigned OT contact. The distribution ChangeSet file will be re-created periodically as functional elements are added and/or enhanced. Additionally, other exports will be created as required in order to maintain an appropriate level of application consistency between the City’s templates and any standalone in development. The City’s SCADA system is a managed VTScada application which has been developed using custom City developed library widgets. Refer to the City Standard Object Library Programming Guideline for details on the currently available object template library or submit an email request to the City’s project manager. - [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Replacement of the Existing Fiber Cables: The existing fiber cables included in the network are past their useful life having been installed several decades ago. If the cables were to be replaced, it would have to be done with parallel new fiber cables that allow the existing network to function without interruption. However, the 11 fiber cables entering through a conduit beneath the main patch panel in the Main Pump Station Building network room leave the conduit with very little room to pass through additional fiber. Although it’s possible to utilize MaxCell sleeves to snake new fiber cables through the one existing conduit, it would be easier to install a new conduit run if the network rack were to remain where it is currently located. 2.2.3 Recommended Fiber Network Upgrades Given the available documentation on the fiber network and the site investigation, the following is recommended for upgrading the network:  Run new fiber cable in existing fiber cable conduit from new control systems at each building installed under each individual amendment package (to the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvement project) to connect to a new patch panel near the existing network rack. New fiber runs from this new patch panel are to the be connected to the existing network equipment. The existing network rack is located within the existing Main Pump Station network equipment room.  Install new fiber cables from the upgraded PLCs associated with process improvement, utilizing existing conduit duct bank and manholes, through either electrical manhole E-6 or E-7, then through the new conduits to the new network rack.  Gradually remove existing fiber runs as associated processes are upgraded and successfully running off the new network.  Keep both networks operational until the last replacement fiber run is complete. The existing plant fiber patch panel located near the existing network rack is to be kept in service until all processes have been updated and connected with new fiber runs upon which the existing patch panel can be removed.  Provide redundant fiber loops at the outer edges. As an example, a new fiber run between PLC 17 and PLC 7 would allow data transmission for PLCs in either quadrant back to the main network rack regardless of where conduit runs might be lost. PLC 18 (located in Filter Building No. 1) is the most critical to have fiber redundancy as loss of this PLC would result in loss of the plant’s permit analyzers and flow-proportional chemical feeds and samplers, which could shut down STAR. Fiber loops would require some reconfiguration of the programming to designate the “normal” communication path and a backup path if normal route is lost. During the design phase of each improvement to HFC AWTP, the existing network diagram and the new network diagram will be updated to maintain accurate records of the both systems until the existing is fully removed from service. Camera feeds are on the same fiber cable as SCADA, utilizing separate fibers. This approach shall be continued for new fiber cables. HFC AWTP Master Plan June 10,2022 Improvements: SCADA Design Criteria Technical Memorandum Page 9 of 15 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software Introduction The Howard F. Curren Advanced Water Treatment Plant(HFC AWTP) owns and operates a comprehensive Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) system. This system is primarily composed of General Electric(GE) PAC System Rx3i and 90-30 series programmable logic controllers(PLCs) at the field level, and VTScada by Trihedral at the SCADA/HMI level. SCADA systems are critical for monitoring and controlling industrial processes, providing real-time data, and enabling remote control and automation of plant operations. As part of ongoing improvement efforts, the City is currently in the process of upgrading the controllers throughout all facilities to more advanced Allen Bradley controllers, which are manufactured by Rockwell Automation. These new controllers are expected to enhance the reliability and efficiency of the plant’s operations, offering improved performance and extended capabilities. The data generated by the SCADA system is stored in a robust database, which serves as the backbone for gathering insights into the plant's performance. This data includes various metrics such as water flow rates, chemical usage, and equipment status, among others. It is essential for ensuring that the plant meets stringent regulatory requirements for water quality, which are set by environmental authorities to protect public health and the environment. To maintain these high standards, the data needs to be analyzed periodically. This analysis involves examining trends, identifying any anomalies or deviations, and generating reports that summarize the plant's performance. These reports are crucial for the plant's operators and management, as they provide a clear overview of the plant’s operations and highlight any areas that may require attention or improvement. Therefore, selecting the right report creation software is of paramount importance. The software must integrate seamlessly with the existing database, ensuring that the data is accurately captured and processed. It should also offer user-friendly interfaces for creating and customizing reports, allowing users to easily interpret the data and make informed decisions. Consistency and reliability in report generation are critical, as these reports play a vital role in maintaining the plant’s operational excellence and regulatory compliance. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 5 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 4.2 Ignition Cost Ignition non cloud edition has an upfront cost of $24,337.50 with a yearly support cost of $4867.5. The city is expected to spend a total of $29,205.00 on the first year, $53,542.50 for five years, $77,880 for ten years and $102,217.50 at fifteen years. Because of this cost the software platform lands a ranking of 3 on the pricing as although it has a higher initial cost than DreamReport over the course of a few years the cost is lower than DreamReport. Overview Ignition in the non-cloud version of the software lacks the functionality of being able to be set up so that multiple users can access different kinds of data, view and create reports. This would mean that to gain this feature the software would need to be run in a hybrid method that requires a cloud version of the software to be run in parallel. Because of this the software scored a ranking of 2 for the user dashboard configuration, and 1 for custom report creation. The platform is able to integrate with VTScada depending on how the database is set up. This is comparable to other offerings; Ignition scored a rank of 1 for the VTScada integration category after taking into consideration the need to have a link between the cloud edition of the software to VTScada as well. Ignition can also integrate with LIMS as it has many generic communication systems, and it should be able to pull the data from any system if it uses a generic system. Due to the required cloud link to the plant network, it also scored a 1 in the database integration category. Ignition can schedule reports that can be emailed out to users, but because it lacks other significant features in this category to make it stand out it scored a ranking of 1 in the category of automated reports. NIST cybersecurity standards are self-attested to be followed by Ignition however it is not certified to be following these standards which could leave room for uncertainty. Due to this level of uncertainty the software scores a rank of 2 for the Security category. The software platform can be set up to meet 21 CFR part 11 compliance requirements like the other platforms. The software platform ranks 1 for 21CFR part 11 after taking into consideration other categories’ rankings. Browser usability is not available without having the hybrid model due to this lack of capability from the platform in the non cloud version of the software it scores a rank of 1 for the browser usability category. Ignition has an exceptional capability to expand without limit with an unlimited number of tags and users being able to be used this unlimited ability to expand allows the software platform to score a rank of 4 for the scalability category due to the lack of any restrictions on the size of the platform. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 8 SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 Cybersecurity Policy This section contains cybersecurity requirements for all network components recommended by Hazen at a minimum, however the City shall employ the best practices to promote SCADA & PCS security. This is in addition to specific configuration requirements for integration into PCS/SCADA system networks covered in other sections. All persons are required to review and acknowledge the City’s policies before accessing the PCS/SCADA system. 8.1General 1) All servers, workstations laptops and other devices shall be managed by City policy. 2) The use of unencrypted communication(e.g. email, etc.) for PCS/SCADA system related correspondence which includes information related to cybersecurity and networking is prohibited. All electronically transmitted documents shall be password protected; the password shall be shared with the intended participants via alternate out of band communications channels(e.g. voice, text, etc.). 8.2Asset management 1) Work Management - PCS/SCADA system assets shall be entered into and maintained using the City’s asset management system. Additions, changes, and deletions shall be performed in the system within one week. 2) Device Inventory - Maintain an inventory of all physical devices throughout the PCS/SCADA system networks. Include all network nodes and communication- capable devices (e.g., servers, workstations, laptops, OIT, PLCs, PACs, switches, routers, firewalls, etc.). This inventory shall include all devices, whether connected to the PCS/SCADA system networks or not. Maintain the inventory in a spreadsheet. Include the supplier, manufacturer, model number, serial number, subnet, network address, hardware address, machine name, and installation location. 3) Software Inventory - Maintain an inventory of all software platforms and applications (e.g., operating systems, HMI applications, historians, OPC servers, programming software, IDE software, drivers, office productivity software, utilities, etc.). Maintain the inventory in a spreadsheet. Include supplier, product vendor, product name, product version, patching level, firmware version, licensing methods, and licensing keys. Maintain additional information as required to allow reinstallation of software after final acceptance. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 1. Introduction 1.1 Background The HFC AWTP is permitted to treat 96 mgd of raw sewage with a Type I two-stage, high rate (pure oxygen and conventional fine bubble aeration) activated sludge biological domestic wastewater treatment plant. This plant is operated to reduce the Total Nitrogen(TN) in the effluent to advanced standards, using biological treatment for nitrification and filtration for denitrification, and to discharge high-level disinfected and dechlorinated effluent to Tampa Bay. Residuals generated by this facility are anaerobically digested and dewatered for land application as a Class B residual. Currently, annual average daily flows are approximately 60 mgd and peak flows are approximately 200 mgd. The rehabilitation to the HFC AWTP will consist of the implementation of improvements identified in the Facility Master Plan Capital Improvement Project List for Fiscal Years 0-5 to maintain system reliability, increase operating efficiency, and meet current and future regulatory requirements. This Technical Memorandum details the SCADA Design Criteria. These criteria will be utilized for all instrumentation and control design performed as part of these improvements. 1.2 Workshops Three(3) workshops were held with the City in developing the SCADA Design Criteria. The findings of these workshops are documented herein and establish the minimum standards to use for developing P&IDs, specifying software and hardware, and guide the overall approach and sequencing for the replacement of the facility wide fiber communications network. The workshops addressed the following topics:  Workshop 1 - P&ID standards, facility fiber network improvements, approach to PLC/HMI software development, and internet/site security protection  Workshop 2 - Hardware standards  Workshop 3 - Design approach Meeting notes are provided in Appendix E for reference. HFC AWTP Master Plan June 10,2022 Improvements: SCADA Design Criteria Technical Memorandum Page 1 of 15 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] CITY OF TAMPA, FLORIDA - RFQ objective of the project is to migrate the remaining facilities operating on HSQ software to the VTScada software so that the entire plant is monitored and controlled through the VTScada software system. The City has recently completed new SCADA and Process Control System(PCS) programming, configuration and design standards. The project will ensure that the plant’s monitoring and control systems meet these standards. The objectives of the project will include, but not limited to: - Migrate the SCADA system from the existing HSQ software to VTScada software while ensuring continuous operation of the treatment plant. - Ensure that the new monitoring and control system aligns with the City's standards, including the use of approved widgets and graphic symbols for operator screens. - Implement a new software system that can generate plant operating reports needed for regulatory requirements and for monitoring plant performance. The new software system shall retain historical data from HSQ and shall be capable of generating reports using both historical and current data. - Ensure the system is scalable for future expansions and upgrades. - Provide the design and implementation plan for replacing the treatment plant’s fiber optic cable network. Services will include, but will not be limited to the following: - Complete an assessment of the existing SCADA system and related infrastructure to determine the requirements, scopes of work, priorities, and cost estimates to complete the migration to the VTScada software. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3. Recommend Network Sequencing 3.1 Maintaining Monitoring and Reporting Once the P&IDs are developed and preliminary control descriptions are available, discussion on where new PLC panels will be located and how I/O will be connected to the new PLC modules will be made at milestones for each project. A detailed sequence on how to maintain monitoring and reporting will be developed as part of these discussions. These sequencing plans will be reviewed with City staff prior to completing the 90% Design. 3.2 Replacing Fiber Cables All fiber cables for each process, which includes fibers for the camera feeds, will be replaced as part of any improvement to that process. During development of the Preliminary P&IDs a thorough site investigations shall be performed to verify the existing fiber runs from the associated PLCs back to the main network fiber patch panel. This includes removing electrical manhole covers and draining the manholes. The purpose of these investigations is to determine what existing conduit runs will be used to best show on the design site plans for the fiber installation. At any point thereafter, investigations shall be performed to confirm new cable can be run through the existing conduits without removing the existing fiber cables. Final network diagram will include fiber cable labeling. Labeling for the cables and for the ports at patch panels will be installed in the field to match the network diagram. Multiple cables run through the same conduits and manholes will be depicted on the network site plan. 3.3 Conversion to VTScada VTScada HMI programming will be developed and placed in service parallel to the existing HSQ HMI system on a separate network rack and made available for interface on new workstations throughout the plant, as well as on wireless devices. As a process is made available on the VTScada HMI and is installed, tested, and passed an approved reliability demonstration period, the associated HSQ HMI display screens could be removed or made view only. The goal will be to get each process on the VTScada HMI to use for setpoint adjustment, monitoring, and/or manual override. However, there will be some parameters that may impact separate processes still controlled only through HSQ. A plan will need to be developed for such situations to provide a protocol to operations until all control parameters have been switched over to VTScada. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software not indicate whether the system cost more for additional tags as the quote was created with 100,000 tags and 5 concurrent users. However, due to the uncertainty of whether an additional cost would be incurred to add more tags the platform scores a ranking of 2 in the scalability category. The PI System is able to have custom calculations and tracking for KPIs with an expansive built in library, there is also an available bi-directional SQL database toolkit that can read and write data back to the SQL database. Due to the ability for the platform to do these tasks with add on toolkits it scores a ranking of 3 on the specialized functions category. Integration with the HMI is able to be achieved through the PI Interface for OPC DA or RDBMS tools. The platform scores a ranking of 3 for the HMI integration category. AVEVA has a commitment to software support and development as their lifecycle practices align with ISO 27034, they also provide a security bulletin every few months to ensure that customers are up to date with alerts on cyber security and compliance. Patches are available whenever bugs or security concerns are found. The Platform ranks 4 in the Software support and maintenance category. Redundancy can be set up on the PI System with different kinds of disaster recovery at all levels of the system. Sales did not specify the additional cost, but it was requested to have redundancy quoted, on the quote it is not specified how much of the cost can be attributed to redundancy. Due to the uncertainty of the pricing the platform ranks 1 in the redundancy category. AVEVA is the largest company of the set of companies that were evaluated. AVEVA is not a domestic company, and neither is its parent Schneider Electric. However, due to the large international presence of both companies with over 170 million matches on google for AVEVA alone, it ranks 4 in the Company Prominence category. Customer service was poor, getting in contact with sales took a considerable amount of effort, several calls and emails had to be made before being able to be connected to a salesperson who was unable to proceed until I was able to disclose information about the client. The sales representative was not very well versed in the capabilities of the PIVision software and only referred to online videos about the platform. There was a large delay as well in receiving a quote and they did not have any wastewater specific sample reports that were easily referenced. Due to the difficulty in acquiring information about the platform it is ranked 1 in the Customer Service category. Advantages Some of the unique advantages of PI Platform is the ability to integrate with Microsoft active directory. This will enable users to sign in and access their own reports and data visualization tools in the PIVision system. This integration with Microsoft active directory will allow the operator’s own sign in to be used with the software platform, this is incredibly useful as the use of active directory streamlines the IT operations and cybersecurity implementation. This cuts down on the effort required to integrate any new plant operators as IT can use the very sign on that is used by Active Directory onto the PIVision platform HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software The platform can integrate with the HMI however that depends on how it was set up, if the HMI was not set up in a way that it can integrate with Ignition then it might not adequately integrate, because of this it scored a rank of 1 for the HMI integration category. Ignition’s offering of a permanent license for the on-site server has regular security updates. The cloud version of the server will be updated with the latest features and security updates. Support can be provided by Ignition for the on-site server which would help ensure that the platform runs smoothly. This is comparable to other platforms however the necessity of having to manage a cloud server in addition to the on-site server gives the platform a rank of 1 for the Software support and maintenance category. Ignition offers a redundancy license that is provided at 50% the cost of the main license which is in line with other offerings and scores it a rank of 2 on the redundancy category. Inductive Automation is a small company based out of Folsom California. With between 11 to 50 employees and 9 million matches on Google. After considering the size of the other companies reviewed it scores a ranking of 2 in the company prominence category. Customer service was quick and knowledgeable, with pricing clearly visible on the website. Sales representatives were quick to answer messages, schedule meetings, answer questions and return quotes in a timely manner. Ignition scores a ranking of 3 on the customer service category. Advantages Ignition is an all-in-one solution where licensing is based upon the server license and the amount of time that the cloud server is running. The Ignition platform has an unlimited number of tags can be tracked, and an unlimited number of people can edit and create reports at the same time. This allows for great flexibility as once the software is purchased no additional costs will concur for additional licensing for tags or users, so The City is able to expand their processes as much as they desire. Some other advantages for the Ignition platform include self-attested NIST cybersecurity compliance. Although Ignition has not been directly verified by NIST they still self-report to follow all applicable practices to ensure high cybersecurity and regulatory compliance for their software. This is incredibly important, especially in today’s world where cybercriminals pose a constant threat to automated infrastructure systems. Additionally, ignition can be set up to meet 21 CFR part 11 compliance requirements, another bonus that makes the data and the reports from this platform trustworthy, reliable, and equivalent to paper records and handwritten signatures. Disadvantages For multiple users to be able to have access to a web portal to be able to create their own reports the cloud version of this software must be used which requires a different licensing agreement where the cost is related to the amount of time that the service is being used which The City indicated that they did not HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 22 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 4.3 PI System Cost The PI System and its required add on platforms have a yearly licensing cost of $174,126.62, with a 5 year estimated cost of $870,633.10, a 10 year estimated cost of $1,741,266.20, and a 15 year estimated cost of $ $2,611,899.30 this platform is the highest cost option out of all of the other platforms reviewed, because of this it scored a ranking of 1 on the cost category. Overview With the PI system, users can create layouts and save them on their own dashboards with a clean and intuitive user interface. This capability leads the PI system to score a 4 on the user dashboard configuration category. The PI System can create reports quickly and effectively from the User’s dashboard and is able to export them as PDFs and other file formats that are supported by Excel. This ability to create the reports and view them quickly let the PI system platform score a 4 on the Custom Report creation category. VTScada integration can be provided by using the correct adapter for the PI system as it has adapters for a multitude of different platforms and databases that give it great flexibility. Objectives With the changes being implemented at the HFC AWTP, the City aims to select a new report creation software to ensure efficient identification of plant performance and regulatory compliance. Given the critical nature of these reports, it is vital that the chosen software integrates seamlessly with the existing database systems and offers comprehensive functionality for data analysis and reporting. To achieve this goal, the City has tasked Hazen and Sawyer(Hazen) with reviewing and evaluating various software platforms capable of meeting the plant's rigorous requirements. As part of this mandate, Hazen conducted a thorough assessment of multiple report creation software options to determine the most suitable solution for the HFC AWTP's needs. The evaluation process involved comparing the features, capabilities, and budgetary costs of each software platform. Hazen's objective was to identify a software solution that not only provides robust reporting capabilities but also enhances the overall operational efficiency of the plant. This includes the ability to generate accurate, real-time reports that offer insight into key metrics such as water flow rates, chemical usage, and equipment status. Moreover, the ideal software should facilitate user-friendly report creation and customization, enabling plant operators and management to easily interpret data and make informed decisions. Ensuring consistency and reliability in report generation is paramount, as these reports play a crucial role in maintaining the plant’s operational excellence and regulatory compliance. The findings of Hazen's comprehensive evaluation are summarized in the Report Generator Software Evaluation document, which outlines the strengths and weaknesses of each software platform considered. Based on this evaluation, Hazen has provided a recommendation for the standard software platform to be adopted for report creation moving forward. This recommendation is aimed at optimizing the plant's reporting processes and supporting the City's ongoing commitment to operational improvement and regulatory adherence. - [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 1. Introduction The Howard F. Curren Advanced Water Treatment Plant(HFC AWTP) owns and operates a Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) system currently composed mainly of General Electric(GE) PACSystems Rx3i and 90-30 series programmable logic controllers(PLCs) at the field level. General Electric was acquired by Emerson in February, 2019. This document refers to GE equipment as GE to maintain familiarity with City’s existing Standards. As manufacturers continue to make advancements in product development, older versions of hardware advance towards “legacy” status. Products in legacy status are taken out of production and typically are no longercommercially available from the manufacturer. Replacement parts must then be sourced from third party sellers, increasing the risk for price inflation and long lead times. In that case, a set of spares must be stringently maintained to prevent unnecessary down time in the event of a failure. Additionally, manufacturers eventually stop developing and releasing software updates for legacy controllers, which may pose a security risk on network connected systems. A majority of HFC AWTP’s PLC hardware has entered the legacy phase. While PLC upgrades may be costly up front, updating the PLCs reduces the risk of unplanned downtime and enhances network security. 2. Objectives A majority of the existing PLCs reached legacy status in 2018 (GE 90-30 and GE 90-70 series). PLC hardware for these products are no longer supported by the manufacturer. With the impending replacement of all PLC hardware as part of the on-going design-build project, the City has chosen to evaluate alternatives to develop a new standard for PLCs at the plant. Existing PLCs at the plant were evaluated by the City. As a result, Hazen and Sawyer(Hazen) was asked to review the existing PLC hardware to determine equivalent alternatives. Four major PLC vendors were evaluated for consideration whenever existing PLC line ups are to be replaced as part of on-going process improvements at HFC AWTP. The different PLC models were compared including features and budgetary costs for full system replacement. The findings are summarized in this PLC Hardware Evaluation along with a recommendation for the standard PLC hardware platform going forward. 3. Assessment of Existing PLCs & Industry Review 3.1 Existing PLCs An audit was performed by McKim & Creed & Hazen in June 2018 with assistance from HFC AWTP staff to quantify the number of existing PLCs installed at the facility. HFC AWTP staff provided information about the existing PLCs at these sites including existing network diagrams, model of PLC at each location, quantity, for review and use for this evaluation. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System PLC Hardware Evaluation 6 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 16 4 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Naming Conventions Consistent naming across systems and controllers facilitates the City’s technicians’ efforts during troubleshooting. As such, a series of standards has been developed to address these needs. 4.1Tag Naming Conventions As data is generated by the various elements of the process, the easiest way to track things is through human decipherable tag names. The tags stand in the place of the somewhat arcane addressing and naming conventions that manufacturers use to address their points. This also results in a language construct that is regular and nonrepetitive. It is extremely important that the naming system yield unique names with a consistent format throughout the entire system. As such, the following naming convention is to be utilized throughout the entire City system. These mnemonics are to be applied to any and all elements that might be transferred from a controller to another system, i.e., SCADA, HMI, OIT, gateway, or controller. This system shall be utilized, but in a paired down manner, for internal registers and flags within a controller. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. - [Proposal / Procurement Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 3.1.16 Customer service The company must have representatives that are easy to reach out to and get in touch with, the representatives must also be well versed with the software and its capabilities. The company must have representatives that are easy to reach out to and get in touch with, and the representatives must also be well-versed with the software and its capabilities. This is crucial for a wastewater operator because having accessible and knowledgeable support ensures that any issues or questions can be promptly addressed, minimizing downtime and maintaining smooth operations. Quick and efficient customer service can make a significant difference in resolving technical problems, optimizing the use of the software, and ensuring that the wastewater treatment plant operates at peak efficiency. This level of support is essential for handling the complex and critical nature of wastewater management, where delays or errors can have significant consequences. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. 3.2 Software comparison matrix The software platforms DreamReport, Ignition, PI System, and XLReport are all reliable reporting software platforms and within each of the evaluation factors no one supplier was always top ranked as each software has differences in making a recommendation difficult. A matrix decision analysis will be used with weighted values to identify the most optimal software solution. Each Software was ranked relative to each other on a scale of 1-4 with 4 being considered the most favorable and 1 being the least favorable for that category. Then a requirement importance factor will be applied to each parameter, a 5 being considered the highest importance and 1 being considered the of least importance. Rankings were assigned based on the review of the software representative responses as well as further research when needed. Weight factors were assigned based on Hazen’s perception of what would be most important to HFC AWTP staff. A summation of each of the ranking score times the respective weight factor was made. The Software supplier with the highest overall number was determined to be the best choice based on Hazen’s evaluation. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 15 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 1 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 Introduction This is Volume 3 of the City of Tampa’s standards for Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) and Process Control System(PCS) programming, configuration and design standards for the City of Tampa referred to as “The City” or abbreviated as “COT” throughout this document Volume 3 is a compilation of procedures for development and programming of the HMI and OIT. This volume provides both guidance and specific examples to set up these interfaces The City SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual is intended to be applied by the City’s contractors, consultants, and in-house personnel when developing or modifying any portion of the City’s control system. The control system is a utility-wide system built upon a common human machine interface(HMI) system and is comprised of the following: Process Control System(PCS): The industrial control system for processes within the boundaries of the plant(Howard F. Curren AWTP). The primary function of the system is control and monitoring of plant operations. The PCS is comprised of the local HMI subsystem and various programmable logic controllers(PLCs) and operator interface terminals(OITs). Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) System: The industrial control system for processes outside the boundaries of the process areas, ability to monitor and/or control the process from the control room from outside the plant. The primary function of this system is data acquisition; processes or remote sites are designed to run autonomously without supervisory control function. The SCADA system serves both remote sites such as lift stations and wastewater treatment plant facility in an integrated environment and is comprised of telemetry equipment with a supervisory HMI. This manual is divided into three main volumes plus two appendices: Volume 1 contains the control system policies and procedures that apply to the management, maintenance and modification of the control system. Volume 1 includes policies for: - Programming laptop use HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 8 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation Glossary Ethernet: A computer networking standard that defines a family of protocols, communication media, and device interfaces. Human Machine Interface(HMI): A computer that runs graphical interface software for monitoring and supervisory control of a control system. Local Area Network(LAN): Made up of the connection media, networking components, and nearby devices that communicate with each other. Remote Telemetry Unit: A controller for local equipment that communicates with the master telemetry unit. Uninterruptible Power Supply(UPS): A battery-backed source fed by the utility service during normal operation. Variable Frequency Drive(VFD): A motor control that allows for continuous variations in running speed. Virtual Private Network(VPN): A secure means of connecting a physically remote host to a local area network, typically via the Internet. VTScada: The software application used at the HFC AWTP to read and write real-time data to and from controllers. Win 411: A component of Win 911 that enables simple audio reporting of analog values. Win 911: A software application used to annunciate and acknowledge alarms to/from remote users. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] **3.3 Industry Reviews** The GE product line used at the HFC AWTP is one of many PLC manufacturers in the United States and this product line has a significant share of the municipal wastewater industry. Two other prominent PLC manufacturers that have significant market share in the municipal wastewater include: - **Rockwell(Allen Bradley Logix series)** - **Schneider(Modicon M540/580)** The products of these manufacturers, along with those of **GE(RX3i series)**, were selected as viable options to be considered for further evaluation in the PLC upgrades at the HFC AWTP as all three are widely known, utilized, and recognized in the municipal wastewater treatment market. The PLC evaluation performed here focused on PLC using a centralized architecture with a single processing unit and not Distributed Control Systems(DCS) such as Ovation, Foxboro and PlantPAX and thereby no reference to DCS is covered in this evaluation. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 4.1.7 Longevity Established and reputable PLC manufacturers have a proven track record of delivering quality products and reliable support. Based on customer reviews, industry feedback, and the company's history to gauge its product line implementation and commitment to customer satisfaction were used for ranking. 4.1.8 Compatibility with HMI(VT SCADA) software HFC AWTP uses VT SCADA as HMI software. Evaluated PLCs were ranked based on the compatibility with VT SCADA. This is to ensure that the PLC can be easily incorporated into the existing control system and that the operator interface is simple. 4.1.9 Library Components Common industrial protocol(CIP) represents a well-defined data representation, connection management, and messaging protocol that operates over an independent transport and physical layer. A PLC using a CIP is considered flexible for integrating to any HMI software like VT SCADA, it provides simplicity in Automatic Tag Creation, a huge benefit. PLCs are compared and ranking is based on availability and extent of library components in VT SCADA. 4.1.10 Cost Another important issue for evaluation is cost of PLC, which includes the cost of hardware, software, overall value and also considering factors beyond the initial purchase cost. Additionally, factored in with the evaluation are the cost of communication modules, and accessories required for HFC AWTP applications. 4.1.11 Asset and Program management PLC brands with a centralized tool capable of securing, managing, versioning, tracking and reporting automation-related asset information are evaluated within this category. 4.1.12 Functionality and Diagnostics feasibility A wide varity of software and hardware are necessary to enhance the functionality and diagnostics of PLCs. Best choice is given to the PLC that has diagnostics and troubleshooting tools for swiftly identifying defects and difficulties. This can help with troubleshooting the various faults so as to reduce downtime. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. The PLCs have the capacity to operate in ladder logic, structured text, and functional block diagram. These tags will be constructed from context type tags designed to mimic the structure of the controller data. A one to one relationship shall be maintained. Tag construction will be approached such that as needs change elements inside of a context tag can be added or deprecated without having to edit all graphical elements utilizing the tag. 6.3Controller Tag Naming Standard Reference the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual, Volume 2, PLC, for tag naming requirements. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Figure 10 Recommended Alarm Rater per Operator Guidelines It is critical that alarms be generated at a rate at which the operator can recognize the alarm and address the cause. Control system acceptance requires the average alarm rate per operator to fall within the predictive classification, number of operators for a particular system will be confirmed at pre-programming workshop. 5.2.5 Cascaded Alarms Cascaded alarms are those in which one precipitating alarm also results in multiple others. The subsequent alarms are considered to be false alarms and are to be eliminated from the system. The generation of alarms should be considered, as much as possible, in a first-in-first-out manner. Additionally, an event should only generate one unique alarm. An example of this is a motor stopping while running. The precipitating event is the Uncommanded Stop. Subsequent, related alarms such as Fail to Start are not to be generated. Alarms, such as a process generating a Low and subsequently a Low-Low alarm are not considered to be cascaded events since each condition has a separate setpoint and the duration between events provides additional troubleshooting information to the operator. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 29 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3. Recommend Network Sequencing 3.1 Maintaining Monitoring and Reporting Once the P&IDs are developed and preliminary control descriptions are available, discussion on where new PLC panels will be located and how I/O will be connected to the new PLC modules will be made at milestones for each project. A detailed sequence on how to maintain monitoring and reporting will be developed as part of these discussions. These sequencing plans will be reviewed with City staff prior to completing the 90% Design. 3.2 Replacing Fiber Cables All fiber cables for each process, which includes fibers for the camera feeds, will be replaced as part of any improvement to that process. During development of the Preliminary P&IDs a thorough site investigations shall be performed to verify the existing fiber runs from the associated PLCs back to the main network fiber patch panel. This includes removing electrical manhole covers and draining the manholes. The purpose of these investigations is to determine what existing conduit runs will be used to best show on the design site plans for the fiber installation. At any point thereafter, investigations shall be performed to confirm new cable can be run through the existing conduits without removing the existing fiber cables. Final network diagram will include fiber cable labeling. Labeling for the cables and for the ports at patch panels will be installed in the field to match the network diagram. Multiple cables run through the same conduits and manholes will be depicted on the network site plan. 3.3 Conversion to VTScada VTScada HMI programming will be developed and placed in service parallel to the existing HSQ HMI system on a separate network rack and made available for interface on new workstations throughout the plant, as well as on wireless devices. As a process is made available on the VTScada HMI and is installed, tested, and passed an approved reliability demonstration period, the associated HSQ HMI display screens could be removed or made view only. The goal will be to get each process on the VTScada HMI to use for setpoint adjustment, monitoring, and/or manual override. However, there will be some parameters that may impact separate processes still controlled only through HSQ. A plan will need to be developed for such situations to provide a protocol to operations until all control parameters have been switched over to VTScada. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 4.1.13 Maintenance PLC requires routine maintenance and upkeeping, software upgrades and version controls are necessary to mitigate PLC downtime. PLCs with tools to manage software version, notification feature and license upgrades are ranked based on the available options. 4.1.14 Disaster Recovery PLCs hard drives occasionally are inoperable for a variety of reasons, such as power quality event impacting the central processing unit(CPU). A PLC with ability to hold program within a USB or SD card or a monitoring tool to provide PLC health condition is an important factor used for evaluation. 4.1.15 PLC change management PLCs program modifications are necessary when operational changes occur. The ability of the PLC to monitor and store the user actions is helpful in change management. PLC evaluation is based on the best tools available within the PLC to monitor user actions. 4.1.16 SCADA Software version control A PLC monitoring the HMI and ease of auditing for program management is a useful feature. PLCs with a cyber security plan are ranked top in this category. 4.1.17 PLC Size Choosing a PLC supplier who offers a diverse choice of PLCs will benefit as each process within the HFC AWTP requires various number of monitoring and control I/O. Availability of wide range of size selection within each PLC supplier is considered for evaluation. 4.1.18 PLC Security and Recovery HFC AWTP safety is a critical consideration. PLC suppliers that offer integrated safety features or compatibility with safety modules are recommended. These features include safety inputs, relay outputs, and the ability to implement safety functions according to relevant safety standards. Compliance with safety requirements adds a layer of protection to the HFC AWTP and is an important factor for evaluation. 4.1.19 Ease of Integration with equipment PLCs with predeveloped custom function blocks between instruments and PLC CPUs, VFDs and MCCs are crucial for ease of integration. Rankings are based on ease of integration with major equipment used at HFC AWTP. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 6.4Tag Naming Best Practices The following are a series of best practices and limitations for tags. The differences in tagging are in part based on the differences between PLC programming and the limitations of VTScada. For example, PLC programming is restricted to using underscores as dashes are not able to be used. In comparison dashes are able to be used in HMI programming. These practices do not supersede the tag naming convention specified in Volume 2. Reference the tag naming conventions in Volume 2 for details. This is provided for consideration of locally created tags. These rules particularly apply to context tags used in the construction of complex object tags. - Tag names must be no longer than 64 characters in length (This rule refers only to the tag's immediate name, not its full name, which may include a lengthy list of parent tags.) - Full tag names must be no longer than 253 characters in length (This rule refers to the tag's full name, which may include many parent tags.) - Tag names must not duplicate other named Items. (tags cannot be named "TagBrowser" or "Test", since those names are used elsewhere in VTScada.) - A tag may not be named "Value" or "Area" since those are the names of tag properties. Tags, pages, and widgets may not share a name. Tag names may not duplicate type names - a tag cannot be named "Calculation" or "Analog Status". - Avoid using common English words unless appended with a prefix or suffix. - Valid tag name characters include: o Any combination of alphanumeric characters(A through Z and 0 through 9) o Spaces, except at the beginning or end of the name. o Period(.) o Symbols other than those listed below (e.g., use of the number hatch(#), and underscore(_) characters in tag names is acceptable) - Invalid tag name characters consist of: o Backslash (\ used to separate parent names) o Forward slash(/) o Colon(:) o Asterisk(*) HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 29 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 1. Introduction The Howard F. Curren Advanced Water Treatment Plant(HFC AWTP) owns and operates a Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) system currently composed mainly of General Electric(GE) PACSystems Rx3i and 90-30 series programmable logic controllers(PLCs) at the field level. General Electric was acquired by Emerson in February, 2019. This document refers to GE equipment as GE to maintain familiarity with City’s existing Standards. As manufacturers continue to make advancements in product development, older versions of hardware advance towards “legacy” status. Products in legacy status are taken out of production and typically are no longercommercially available from the manufacturer. Replacement parts must then be sourced from third party sellers, increasing the risk for price inflation and long lead times. In that case, a set of spares must be stringently maintained to prevent unnecessary down time in the event of a failure. Additionally, manufacturers eventually stop developing and releasing software updates for legacy controllers, which may pose a security risk on network connected systems. A majority of HFC AWTP’s PLC hardware has entered the legacy phase. While PLC upgrades may be costly up front, updating the PLCs reduces the risk of unplanned downtime and enhances network security. 2. Objectives A majority of the existing PLCs reached legacy status in 2018 (GE 90-30 and GE 90-70 series). PLC hardware for these products are no longer supported by the manufacturer. With the impending replacement of all PLC hardware as part of the on-going design-build project, the City has chosen to evaluate alternatives to develop a new standard for PLCs at the plant. Existing PLCs at the plant were evaluated by the City. As a result, Hazen and Sawyer(Hazen) was asked to review the existing PLC hardware to determine equivalent alternatives. Four major PLC vendors were evaluated for consideration whenever existing PLC line ups are to be replaced as part of on-going process improvements at HFC AWTP. The different PLC models were compared including features and budgetary costs for full system replacement. The findings are summarized in this PLC Hardware Evaluation along with a recommendation for the standard PLC hardware platform going forward. 3. Assessment of Existing PLCs & Industry Review 3.1 Existing PLCs An audit was performed by McKim & Creed & Hazen in June 2018 with assistance from HFC AWTP staff to quantify the number of existing PLCs installed at the facility. HFC AWTP staff provided information about the existing PLCs at these sites including existing network diagrams, model of PLC at each location, quantity, for review and use for this evaluation. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System PLC Hardware Evaluation 6 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

Software Comparison 3.1 Software Requirements 3.1.1 Cost The software should be priced competitively and not require unnecessary expenses. This is important for a wastewater operator because managing a wastewater treatment plant involves many ongoing costs, and budget constraints are often a significant concern. By ensuring that the software is price competitive, operators can allocate resources more effectively across various critical areas, including maintenance, staffing, and system upgrades. Competitive pricing also allows for the possibility of adopting other necessary technologies or services without stretching the budget too thin. Moreover, avoiding unnecessary expenses helps in achieving long-term financial sustainability, ensuring that the plant can continue to operate efficiently and respond to any emergent needs without financial strain. Due to the financial importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.2 User Dashboard customization On the user’s own dashboard, the software should have the ability to create data trends and graphs saved to the user’s account for private viewing or public access by other users. The dashboard will include customization for the user to create their own pages with tag linking for simple key performance indicators and allow the display to be saved for future viewing or sharing. This functionality is particularly important for plant operators as it enables efficient monitoring of critical plant metrics, such as water quality indicators and equipment performance. By having the ability to customize and save a dashboard, operators can quickly access vital information, make informed decisions, and ensure compliance with regulatory standards. Due to the operational impact of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.3 Custom Report Creation On the user’s own dashboard, the software should have the ability to create data trends and graphs saved to the user’s account for private viewing or public access by other users. The dashboard should include customization for the user to create their own pages with tag linking for simple key performance indicators and allow the display to be saved for future viewing or sharing. This functionality is particularly important for plant operators as it enables the efficient monitoring of critical plant metrics, such as water quality indicators and equipment performance. By having the ability to customize and save specific data points, operators can quickly access vital information, make informed decisions, and ensure compliance with regulatory standards. Due to the operational impact and importance of this requirement it is given an importance factor of 5. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F.
