- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3 SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 SCADA & PCS Standards Deviation Request 3.1General The City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Convention Manual is intended to provide consistent, reliable controls within all plants. However, it is recognized there may be specific instances in which a contractor or supplier may not be able to fully comply with the standards for software, equipment, cabling and other control system components supplied or developed under a contract or other procurement method or may not be able to program or configure the system in conformance with these standards. When these cases arise, the contractor or supplier shall submit a request for deviation from the City’s standards using the Standards Deviation Request Form included in Appendix B. The City will review the issues documented on the form and any supplemental documentation that is supplied with the request, and if compelling reasons are presented, the City may grant the deviation. The standards deviation request shall meet the following criteria: 1) It shall clearly indicate those sections of the standards relevant to the proposed deviation(s). 2) It shall verify that compliance with the standards form which the deviation(s) is requested is, under the circumstances, not feasible. 3) It shall include a specific description of the proposed alternative to the requirements in the City’s standards and include supporting documentation. Documentation may include references demonstrating successful use by other public utilities. 3.2Procedure The process to request a deviation from the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions is as follows: 1) Fill out and submit the Standards Deviation Request Form. The form shall be filled out electronically and submitted via e-mail to the City’s designated project manager or other designated contact. The city will supply the blank electronic form on request. A copy of the form is included in Appendix B. 2) Transmit supporting documentation securely through City approved transmittal methods if file contents are sensitive such as VTS changesets, PLC programs, etc. Drawings, mark-ups, photographs, sketches, or vendor-supplied product information with the deviation request e-mail. If attachments exceed mailbox HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - Policies Volume 1 of 3 February 2025 9 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

The user interface looks to be extremely refined and responsive; the level of customization and quality of product is a significant advantage of this platform as none of the other platforms seem to have this level of quality to their products. A responsive and intuitive interface that is quickly able to be learned can aid in the reduction of errors and man hours spent in training and acquiring staff proficiency on the platform can reduce the long term operating costs of a plant. Disadvantages One of the most prominent disadvantages of the PIVision software is the cost, the high cost of the software makes this the most expensive option of all the software platforms that are available by a significant number. This high cost is something that The City must take into consideration as it is significantly higher over the long term than the other platforms even with some of the unique advantages that the platform may provide. Such high costs over the long term should be considered heavily as the costs could be used for other plant operations or improvements. Another disadvantage was the lackluster customer service, the difficulty in getting connected to a sales representative and the lack of knowledge about the software platform might a reflection of how the company might operate. If their sales representatives are difficult to contact and are not very knowledgeable about their product it could be an indication of a company culture of substandard care of their customers and products which could make operating the software difficult in the long term which could have unforeseen expenses in the number of man hours, it might take to remedy an issue with the software or integration of features. In addition to this the sales representative was not able to provide me with pricing as pricing is handled by their partner company. This made it difficult to gauge the potential scalability of the software platform past 100,000 tags or 5 concurrent users. Although the plant may not end up using over 100,000 tags in the near future, advances with new advanced sensors, tag numbers may significantly increase over the long term which might necessitate a greater number of tags. This could incur an additional recurring expense to the software platform that is currently not being projected. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 26 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 3.1.4 VTScada integration The software must be able to pull data from the VTScada database without the user having access to the VTScada system or database. This capability is crucial for a plant operator because it ensures seamless integration and data flow without compromising the security and integrity of the VTScada system. By allowing data to be accessed and utilized without direct interaction with the VTScada environment, plant operators can streamline operations, improve data handling efficiency, and minimize the risk of unauthorized access or potential system disruptions. This feature also facilitates the creation of comprehensive reports and dashboards, enabling integrators to monitor and optimize plant performance while maintaining stringent security protocols. Due to the level of importance of ensuring that the software platform is well integrated with the report creating platform this requirement is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.5 Other Databases The software must be able to pull data from sources other than VTScada such as LIMS for lab works, HSQ for data not yet available in VTScada. This capability is crucial for a wastewater plant operator because it ensures comprehensive data integration and accessibility. By allowing the software to gather information from multiple databases, operators can obtain a holistic view of the plant's operations. This integrated approach facilitates better monitoring and management of plant metrics, enhances decisionmaking processes, and ensures compliance with regulatory standards. Moreover, it minimizes the risk of data silos, improves operational efficiency, and supports the creation of detailed and accurate reports, which are essential for optimizing plant performance and maintaining environmental safety. Due to the importance of being able to link to other databases this requirement is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.6 Automated Report Generation The software must have the ability to automatically generate reports and email these reports to users on a schedule. This feature is particularly vital for operators as it ensures timely access to essential data without the need for manual report generation. Automated reports can include critical metrics such as water quality indicators, equipment performance data, and regulatory compliance status. By receiving these reports on a predefined schedule, operators can swiftly identify and address potential issues, streamline operational workflows, and maintain adherence to environmental regulations. Additionally, automated report generation reduces administrative burden, allowing operators to focus on more strategic and high-priority tasks. Due to the level critical nature of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.7 Security The software must be compliant with NIST standards for cybersecurity to ensure safety for use in the plant. This is crucial for a wastewater operator because it guarantees that all data and operations are protected from cyber threats. Given the sensitive nature of wastewater treatment processes, any breach could lead to significant environmental, and risk to human health and lives. Robust cybersecurity ensures the integrity and reliability of the system, allowing operators to focus on maintaining plant performance HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 2.4 XLReport SmartSights, founded in 1993 and headquartered in Austin, Texas, is known for delivering advanced industrial reporting and data analysis solutions, including products like XLReport, WIN911, and SyTech. The company, with a team of 50-200 employees according to LinkedIn, has a modest but growing industry presence, with around 79,000 search results on Google. Launched in 2000, XLReport is SmartSights' reporting software, featuring a licensing system based on the number of users needed for editing and custom report creation, with unlimited tag tracking available. On-site installation allows XLReport users to access reporting software through a web browser, making reporting quick and flexible on any device that is able to access the plant network. With support for various report formats and automated email reporting, XLReport’s capabilities are powered by Microsoft Excel, though it operates independently of the Excel application itself. SmartSights offers XLReport under both perpetual and subscription-based licenses, with the latter providing software upgrades and support. For enhanced reliability, a redundancy server can be added at 50% of the main server cost. The software can also be bundled with WIN911, SmartSights’ alarming platform, to enable fast, reliable plant alerts via SMS or email. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. - [Deliverables / Submittals / Documentation] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3 SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 SCADA & PCS Standards Deviation Request 3.1General The City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Convention Manual is intended to provide consistent, reliable controls within all plants. However, it is recognized there may be specific instances in which a contractor or supplier may not be able to fully comply with the standards for software, equipment, cabling and other control system components supplied or developed under a contract or other procurement method or may not be able to program or configure the system in conformance with these standards. When these cases arise, the contractor or supplier shall submit a request for deviation from the City’s standards using the Standards Deviation Request Form included in Appendix B. The City will review the issues documented on the form and any supplemental documentation that is supplied with the request, and if compelling reasons are presented, the City may grant the deviation. The standards deviation request shall meet the following criteria: 1) It shall clearly indicate those sections of the standards relevant to the proposed deviation(s). 2) It shall verify that compliance with the standards form which the deviation(s) is requested is, under the circumstances, not feasible. 3) It shall include a specific description of the proposed alternative to the requirements in the City’s standards and include supporting documentation. Documentation may include references demonstrating successful use by other public utilities. 3.2Procedure The process to request a deviation from the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions is as follows: 1) Fill out and submit the Standards Deviation Request Form. The form shall be filled out electronically and submitted via e-mail to the City’s designated project manager or other designated contact. The city will supply the blank electronic form on request. A copy of the form is included in Appendix B. 2) Transmit supporting documentation securely through City approved transmittal methods if file contents are sensitive such as VTS changesets, PLC programs, etc. Drawings, mark-ups, photographs, sketches, or vendor-supplied product information with the deviation request e-mail. If attachments exceed mailbox HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - Policies Volume 1 of 3 February 2025 9 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Testing / Startup / Commissioning] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 2.5 Hardware 2.5.1 General Requirements All improvements shall adhere the following requirements:  All programmers, whether vendor or integrator, are to follow the same standards for hardware and software.  Provide computer workstations in key buildings as directed by the City to allow full control and monitoring of entire plant from each location. Existing computer workstations will not be replaced.  Provide independent vendor PLC hardware for individual equipment systems that interface with overall system PLCs provided by integrator. Signals to vendor supplied controls shall be hardwired for all control I/O. Supplemental information such as power usage or other diagnostics can be over Ethernet link from vendor supplied PLCs.  Provide a local control station at each piece of equipment(HOR, L/R, FOR, Start/Stop, etc) for local control with a means to lockout the equipment(lockout stop pushbutton) when in the off position. All such controls to be NEMA4X industrial grade and Square D 9001SK series.  Provide spare parts for CPUs, I/O Cards, Network Switches, fuses, and other components that are critical for operation or prone to wearing out.  Instrument specifications will be in data sheet format for specific make and model numbers rather than lengthy descriptions intended for “or equal” interpretation.  Electrical gear shall be provide with the manufacturer’s standard power monitoring devices.  Witnessed factory testing will not be required. Specifications will require onsite checkout with City staff followed by a 48-hour demonstration test.  Project specifications will require training for both operations and maintenance staff. Operation and maintenance manuals shall be required to be on hand for associated training. 2.5.2 Control Panels Control Panels shall be specified to be provided with the components outlined in Table 2-4, for panel components not listed in this table the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvements Dewatering Facility Replacement project will be used as a standard. Improvements shall include the following requirements:  Dual OITs will be provided on all panels with PLCs, this includes main process panels and individual equipment panels. Both OITs shall have full redundancy, generally one will be used for setpoints and the other for monitoring.  Within panels, CAT6 cable shall be shielded industrial cable and rated for 600V if exposed to 480V power.  Wiring within panels shall separate for different voltages and purposes. The City’s Test Environment has a limited number of concurrent seats available for use. Person(s) responsible for the update shall submit an emailed schedule request to the City’s OT contact, or assigned contact, a minimum of two weeks in advance of the required testing date to confirm availability. The City will confirm the schedule via email or coordinate alternate dates with the requesting person(s) if required. Confirmation will be provided at least one week prior to the request. Person(s) responsible for modifications are required to document the testing and facilitate the City’s witness and approval. A test plan shall be submitted to the City and approved prior to scheduling testing. All application programs shall be loaded into the City’s SCADA &PCS Change Management Server at the time of deployment into the city’s test environment. 6.5PLC Logic Modifications All PLC programs are saved in the City’s SCADA & PCS Change Management Server. Modifications shall be made utilizing the City provided programming laptop (Refer to Section 4 of this document). The SCADA & PCS Change Management Server shall be utilized to maintain versions via a check-in/check-out process. The SCADA & PCS Change Management Server transaction comments are required to be completed and shall contain initials of the person making the change, company name, and approved change request form file name. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] EBO Guidelines for Evaluation Points on RFP and CCNA Proposals Equal Business Opportunity Evaluation Weighted Points: CCNA Proposal Guidelines Under CCNA solicitations, proposers must submit to preconstruction Good Faith Efforts(GFE) requirements covering the inclusion of City of Tampa-certified SLBE firms. Such inclusion shall be clearly addressed and documented utilizing DMI 10,20, and 50 Forms. Proof of certification shall include copies of current certification certificates. This applies to ALL Phase 1 preconstruction design services. Points awarded during the shortlist selection process will be more heavily weighed predominantly on the design side (this does not preclude identification of phase 2 projections of construction participation that follow in the future, i.e., GMPs). In order to ensure the maximum points, a proposer must clearly identify and quantify its planned participation without ambiguity. Simply marking “To Be Determined” (TBD) will not satisfy this requirement and may result in significantly lower ratings. Finally, additional favorable consideration will be granted to the firm(s) that, beyond all others, provide the highest relevant and most binding participation. Additional Evaluation Information: The evaluation includes, but is not limited to, the following criteria: - SLBE subcontractors listed to be utilized(DMI Form 20) - Percentage of proposal/scope committed to SLBE subcontracting. - The collective factors in determining the total points awarded will be based on the overall weight of evidence in the proposal that specified the participation. - Subcontractors are utilized for meaningful tasks, which are viewed as related to the core scope of work. In all cases, the Proposer and/or subcontractor(s) must be SLBE certified prior to the opening date and time of the RFP to be eligible to earn SLBE rating points. The City of Tampa's Office of Equal Business Opportunity will evaluate the SLBE participation evaluation process. The Successful Proposer will be required to execute the DMI 40 Form(Letter of Intent-LOI) with their subcontractors/sub-consultants prior to award. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software DreamReport has the advantage of being NIST certified for cybersecurity. Wastewater treatment is a critical part of everyday life that the environment and many people’s lives depend on. Being certified for adequate cybersecurity standards ensures adequate safety and security is being followed. DreamReport also can meet 21 CFR part 11 compliance requirements, this makes the reports and findings trustworthy, reliable, and equivalent to paper records and handwritten signatures. DreamReport is extensively used in the life sciences industry due to this ability. Additionally, DreamReport can be integrated into HMI and PLCs, readily and easily with the large number of adapters that are available on the software platform. This can make it easy to integrate with the Plant’s current system and any potential changes that may happen in the long term. Having this flexibility is a significant advantage Disadvantages DreamReport has some disadvantages related to the size of Ocean Data Systems, the company that created the software platform. Although it is not the smallest company out of the set of companies that were reviewed it is a non-domestic company. Although this may not be a significant issue for the usability of the software it should be considered in the case that there are any restrictions on whether or not the City is allowed to operate software from non-domestic companies. DreamReport’s licensing structure for concurrent users is similar to that of the tag numbers but with a small amount of added flexibility with the licensing sizes. DreamReport has licenses that can be combined for the number of concurrent users. Meaning that if the plant requires for example 15 users a 10 user license and a 5 user license can be combined to meet the number of required licenses. This makes increasing the number of users incur an additional recurring cost that should be taken into account. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Software Comparison 3.1 Software Requirements 3.1.1 Cost The software should be priced competitively and not require unnecessary expenses. This is important for a wastewater operator because managing a wastewater treatment plant involves many ongoing costs, and budget constraints are often a significant concern. By ensuring that the software is price competitive, operators can allocate resources more effectively across various critical areas, including maintenance, staffing, and system upgrades. Competitive pricing also allows for the possibility of adopting other necessary technologies or services without stretching the budget too thin. Moreover, avoiding unnecessary expenses helps in achieving long-term financial sustainability, ensuring that the plant can continue to operate efficiently and respond to any emergent needs without financial strain. Due to the financial importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.2 User Dashboard customization On the user’s own dashboard, the software should have the ability to create data trends and graphs saved to the user’s account for private viewing or public access by other users. The dashboard will include customization for the user to create their own pages with tag linking for simple key performance indicators and allow the display to be saved for future viewing or sharing. This functionality is particularly important for plant operators as it enables efficient monitoring of critical plant metrics, such as water quality indicators and equipment performance. By having the ability to customize and save a dashboard, operators can quickly access vital information, make informed decisions, and ensure compliance with regulatory standards. Due to the operational impact of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.3 Custom Report Creation On the user’s own dashboard, the software should have the ability to create data trends and graphs saved to the user’s account for private viewing or public access by other users. The dashboard should include customization for the user to create their own pages with tag linking for simple key performance indicators and allow the display to be saved for future viewing or sharing. This functionality is particularly important for plant operators as it enables the efficient monitoring of critical plant metrics, such as water quality indicators and equipment performance. By having the ability to customize and save specific data points, operators can quickly access vital information, make informed decisions, and ensure compliance with regulatory standards. Due to the operational impact and importance of this requirement it is given an importance factor of 5. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software Specialized functions can be used, with custom scripting and SQL capabilities being enabled to search and edit the database if there is a need to do so. This gives the XLReport software platform a ranking of 2 for the specialized functions category. Software security and bug updates are provided for the software platform whenever there are any vulnerabilities identified. maintenance is provided through the subscription model, this is comparable to other offerings, because of this the XLReport platform ranks 2 for the software support and maintenance A redundant server can be installed at a cost of 50% of the main license which is standard with the other offerings. The software platform ranks 3 on the redundancy category for this reason. SmartSights is a small company with 50-200 employees that is based out of Austin Texas, it is a medium sized company that brings up about 79,000 matches on google with more employees than the other domestic option. Because of this it ranks 1 on the company prominence category Customer service was relatively quick with the sales representatives being able to provide technical information on the 2nd call and being able to answer questions relatively quickly. Although the response was quick the quality of the reports that were provided were substandard as they were split over several pages and incomplete. Because of this the company ranks 2 on the customer service category. Advantages XLReport has the advantage of having an unlimited number of tags that are available to be tracked, this gives the software great flexibility and a great ability to scale without worry of having to handle an additional expense if more tags are required to be added. Another advantage for XLReport is that the technology they are based around is Microsoft Excel which makes the software intuitive to use and make it easier for plant operators to be proficient in than other software platforms. Disadvantages One of the biggest disadvantages of XLReport is the fact that the software platform is not NIST compliant. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 3.1.4 VTScada integration The software must be able to pull data from the VTScada database without the user having access to the VTScada system or database. This capability is crucial for a plant operator because it ensures seamless integration and data flow without compromising the security and integrity of the VTScada system. By allowing data to be accessed and utilized without direct interaction with the VTScada environment, plant operators can streamline operations, improve data handling efficiency, and minimize the risk of unauthorized access or potential system disruptions. This feature also facilitates the creation of comprehensive reports and dashboards, enabling integrators to monitor and optimize plant performance while maintaining stringent security protocols. Due to the level of importance of ensuring that the software platform is well integrated with the report creating platform this requirement is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.5 Other Databases The software must be able to pull data from sources other than VTScada such as LIMS for lab works, HSQ for data not yet available in VTScada. This capability is crucial for a wastewater plant operator because it ensures comprehensive data integration and accessibility. By allowing the software to gather information from multiple databases, operators can obtain a holistic view of the plant's operations. This integrated approach facilitates better monitoring and management of plant metrics, enhances decisionmaking processes, and ensures compliance with regulatory standards. Moreover, it minimizes the risk of data silos, improves operational efficiency, and supports the creation of detailed and accurate reports, which are essential for optimizing plant performance and maintaining environmental safety. Due to the importance of being able to link to other databases this requirement is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.6 Automated Report Generation The software must have the ability to automatically generate reports and email these reports to users on a schedule. This feature is particularly vital for operators as it ensures timely access to essential data without the need for manual report generation. Automated reports can include critical metrics such as water quality indicators, equipment performance data, and regulatory compliance status. By receiving these reports on a predefined schedule, operators can swiftly identify and address potential issues, streamline operational workflows, and maintain adherence to environmental regulations. Additionally, automated report generation reduces administrative burden, allowing operators to focus on more strategic and high-priority tasks. Due to the level critical nature of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.7 Security The software must be compliant with NIST standards for cybersecurity to ensure safety for use in the plant. This is crucial for a wastewater operator because it guarantees that all data and operations are protected from cyber threats. Given the sensitive nature of wastewater treatment processes, any breach could lead to significant environmental, and risk to human health and lives. Robust cybersecurity ensures the integrity and reliability of the system, allowing operators to focus on maintaining plant performance HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 12 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

Report Generation Software Overview This section compares the relative advantages and disadvantages of each software platform considered. 4.1 DreamReport Cost DreamReport has a yearly license cost of $20,266 per year. Extrapolating into the future with an assumed fixed licensing cost DreamReport will cost $101,330 for 5 years, $202,660 for 10 years, and $303,990 for 15 years. Comparing this cost to other options over the long term nets a ranking of 2 on the pricing category. Overview DreamReport has good dashboard usability, with specific data metrics being able to be displayed for a range of time on a user’s dashboard. Additional dashboards can be created that can aid in the viewing of additional performance metrics. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Replacement of the Existing Fiber Cables: The existing fiber cables included in the network are past their useful life having been installed several decades ago. If the cables were to be replaced, it would have to be done with parallel new fiber cables that allow the existing network to function without interruption. However, the 11 fiber cables entering through a conduit beneath the main patch panel in the Main Pump Station Building network room leave the conduit with very little room to pass through additional fiber. Although it’s possible to utilize MaxCell sleeves to snake new fiber cables through the one existing conduit, it would be easier to install a new conduit run if the network rack were to remain where it is currently located. 2.2.3 Recommended Fiber Network Upgrades Given the available documentation on the fiber network and the site investigation, the following is recommended for upgrading the network:  Run new fiber cable in existing fiber cable conduit from new control systems at each building installed under each individual amendment package (to the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvement project) to connect to a new patch panel near the existing network rack. New fiber runs from this new patch panel are to the be connected to the existing network equipment. The existing network rack is located within the existing Main Pump Station network equipment room.  Install new fiber cables from the upgraded PLCs associated with process improvement, utilizing existing conduit duct bank and manholes, through either electrical manhole E-6 or E-7, then through the new conduits to the new network rack.  Gradually remove existing fiber runs as associated processes are upgraded and successfully running off the new network.  Keep both networks operational until the last replacement fiber run is complete. The existing plant fiber patch panel located near the existing network rack is to be kept in service until all processes have been updated and connected with new fiber runs upon which the existing patch panel can be removed.  Provide redundant fiber loops at the outer edges. As an example, a new fiber run between PLC 17 and PLC 7 would allow data transmission for PLCs in either quadrant back to the main network rack regardless of where conduit runs might be lost. PLC 18 (located in Filter Building No. 1) is the most critical to have fiber redundancy as loss of this PLC would result in loss of the plant’s permit analyzers and flow-proportional chemical feeds and samplers, which could shut down STAR. Fiber loops would require some reconfiguration of the programming to designate the “normal” communication path and a backup path if normal route is lost. During the design phase of each improvement to HFC AWTP, the existing network diagram and the new network diagram will be updated to maintain accurate records of the both systems until the existing is fully removed from service. Camera feeds are on the same fiber cable as SCADA, utilizing separate fibers. This approach shall be continued for new fiber cables. HFC AWTP Master Plan June 10,2022 Improvements: SCADA Design Criteria Technical Memorandum Page 9 of 15 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Scope of Work] 5.2.3 Alarm Rationalization Alarm rationalization is the process of reviewing, validating, and justifying the alarms that meet the criteria of a critical alarm. In other words, the rationalization specifies only those points in the process system that require alarming. The ultimate goal of alarm rationalization is to determine the most efficient number of alarms to ensure that the process system is safe and remains within normal operating range. Alarm rationalization must be accomplished in conjunction with the City at the preprogramming workshop. The basic process can be outlined as: - Check Alarm Validity - Determine the consequence of inaction - Document Cause, confirmation, and corrective action - Document operator response time - Assign alarm priority - Alarm classification - Determine alarm attributes - Address need for special handling All unused or deprecated alarms are to be removed from the system. Alarms that are no longer needed are not to be abandoned in place (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 FBD sheets should be limited in size to that which can be printed on an 11” x 17” sheet of paper. 6.3Task and Program Files Tasks provide the broadest level of organization. They also provide a level of execution control, that being continuous, periodic, event amongst others. Only two types of tasks are to be employed at the City: continuous and periodic. All other types of task types will not be employed. 6.3.1 Periodic tasks Periodic tasks are designed to execute on a fixed period of time, typically in milliseconds. These tasks are conducive when working with a process or system that is slow, or requires the processing on a regular basis. Creation of periodic tasks should be kept to a minimum(six or less) as required by the controlled process or area. The periodic task execution rate should be set to allow ample time for program execution without burdening the processor. Programmers shall frequently monitor and adjust the execution rate based on the task time. All programs shall be commissioned with task settings tuned such that no overlaps exist in the program. During commissioning, the main program overlap counter shall be confirmed at zero and FAULTLOG object shall not have any minor faults for overlap. Each periodic task must be set with a priority from 1 to 15, with 1 representing the highest priority. It is recommended that each task be set with a unique priority. Fast Scan Task Parameter Setting Default rate 10ms Priority 1 Normal Scan Task Parameter Setting Default rate 250ms Priority 3 HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. - [Scope of Work] 6.4Main or "0" Each program folder must contain a routine to be named "Main" or "0"; the language type specified for this routine should be LD. The purpose of the main routine is to govern the flow of the program. The jump subroutine(JSR) instructions shall be used to reference all other logical routines in the PLC program. No other logic is permitted in the main routine. The first JSR of the main routine shall be to the initialization subroutine conditioned on first scan flag from PLC. This will allow initialization logic to run each time the PLC returns to run after power cycle or other interruption. (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] CITY OF TAMPA, FLORIDA - RFQ objective of the project is to migrate the remaining facilities operating on HSQ software to the VTScada software so that the entire plant is monitored and controlled through the VTScada software system. The City has recently completed new SCADA and Process Control System(PCS) programming, configuration and design standards. The project will ensure that the plant’s monitoring and control systems meet these standards. The objectives of the project will include, but not limited to: - Migrate the SCADA system from the existing HSQ software to VTScada software while ensuring continuous operation of the treatment plant. - Ensure that the new monitoring and control system aligns with the City's standards, including the use of approved widgets and graphic symbols for operator screens. - Implement a new software system that can generate plant operating reports needed for regulatory requirements and for monitoring plant performance. The new software system shall retain historical data from HSQ and shall be capable of generating reports using both historical and current data. - Ensure the system is scalable for future expansions and upgrades. - Provide the design and implementation plan for replacing the treatment plant’s fiber optic cable network. Services will include, but will not be limited to the following: - Complete an assessment of the existing SCADA system and related infrastructure to determine the requirements, scopes of work, priorities, and cost estimates to complete the migration to the VTScada software. However, it is recognized there may be specific instances in which a contractor or supplier may not be able to fully comply with the standards for software, equipment, cabling and other control system components supplied or developed under a contract or other procurement method or may not be able to program or configure the system in conformance with these standards. When these cases arise, the contractor or supplier shall submit a request for deviation from the City’s standards using the Standards Deviation Request Form included in Appendix B. The City will review the issues documented on the form and any supplemental documentation that is supplied with the request, and if compelling reasons are presented, the City may grant the deviation. The standards deviation request shall meet the following criteria: 1) It shall clearly indicate those sections of the standards relevant to the proposed deviation(s). 2) It shall verify that compliance with the standards form which the deviation(s) is requested is, under the circumstances, not feasible. 3) It shall include a specific description of the proposed alternative to the requirements in the City’s standards and include supporting documentation. Documentation may include references demonstrating successful use by other public utilities. 3.2Procedure The process to request a deviation from the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions is as follows: 1) Fill out and submit the Standards Deviation Request Form. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 The AOI interface contains a duality in its nature. From the programmer’s perspective, this allows them to interact with the code-block in a regular manner. From the graphical developer’s perspective, this presents a reliable interface to contained data that simplifies their data management requirements. This interface is a strictly enforced contract with all parties into what the contained data is and to which the inner workings of the blocks code can respond. The parameters are constructed of the controller’s basic data types. Examples are Booleans, integers, and real numbers. The parameters are also capable of handling complex data elements like arrays and user defined types(UDTs). This allows the developer the opportunity to source whatever data is required for the contained logic process. There are more subtle advantages to the use of AOIs from the controller’s perspective. Typically, the controller only maintains one copy of the executable code without regard to the total number of actual used instances. The only requirement on the controller is to have enough memory to contain the interface and internal variable data. The end result is a much smaller controller footprint. AOIs shall be used for any function that will be repetitive. An example of this is a valve. There is a high probability that a system will contain multiple valves. As such the generation of one block of code that addresses the needs for all similar acting valves can be a significant time saver. AOIs should not be used for one time code or code that has the potential to be modified(such as during start-up). AOIs are typically compiled and subsequently downloaded to the controller for optimization purposes. Modifying them typically requires the entire controller to be taken out of active state while a completely new download takes place. This can have a significant impact on the process. A secondary function of AOIs is the development and encapsulation of calculations or custom control requirements. Calculations are generally fixed in nature; an example being the solution to the quadratic equation. There is little value or clarity to be gained by exposing the details of the equation to the general codebase. The only thing that realistically matters are the results. There is also the case of supplementing or extending the functionality of native programming elements. There will often be cases where special requirements need to be applied to a native function that is not supplied or supported. In these cases, an AOI provides the opportunity for a simplified solution. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 39 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

After commissioning activities have been completed, system shall run for 30 days in hands-off mode to document alarm rates for modified alarms. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 o AC = Reference to the type of card, AC indicates voltage type. o DI = Reference to type of input, DI indicates discrete input. o MOD-04 = Indicates the module number. 4.5Tasks This section describes the various objects available for the organization of program logic within the controller and their associated parameters. The section provides guidelines for selection and organization of logic into these objects and configuration of their properties. Three tasks are used, fast, normal, and slow. The tasks are typically reserved for message communications with other devices. The fast scan task has the highest priority of 1, the normal scan task has a priority of 3, being used for all regular monitoring and control operations. The slow scan task has a priority of 5, being reserved for processor monitoring. Each task shall contain a single program within itself and program name shall follow a similar naming as the task. 4.5.1 Task Structure Tasks are the highest organizational unit in the controller and dictate the order of execution. The nomenclature applied to tasks must represent the broadest definition of the contents. 4.5.2 Program Structure Programs represent the middle tier of the organizational structure. At this layer there is more additional granularity added to the overall structure and the naming conventions must reflect that. Naming at this layer must not overlap with the layer above. For example: - P01_Fast Program - P02_Normal Program - P03_Slow Program Individual names at this tier may be duplicated in other tasks but must be unique per task. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 24 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 4.5.3 Routine Structure This is the lowest tier of programming structure. As such the name must provide clarity into the exact programming construct. Each routine is linked to a process area for fast identification and each process area has 3 subroutines associated, except for IO subroutines that are 4. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 1. Objectives With the changes being implemented at the HFC AWTP, the City aims to select a new report creation software to ensure efficient identification of plant performance and regulatory compliance. Given the critical nature of these reports, it is vital that the chosen software integrates seamlessly with the existing database systems and offers comprehensive functionality for data analysis and reporting. To achieve this goal, the City has tasked Hazen and Sawyer(Hazen) with reviewing and evaluating various software platforms capable of meeting the plant's rigorous requirements. As part of this mandate, Hazen conducted a thorough assessment of multiple report creation software options to determine the most suitable solution for the HFC AWTP's needs. The evaluation process involved comparing the features, capabilities, and budgetary costs of each software platform. Hazen's objective was to identify a software solution that not only provides robust reporting capabilities but also enhances the overall operational efficiency of the plant. This includes the ability to generate accurate, real-time reports that offer insight into key metrics such as water flow rates, chemical usage, and equipment status. Moreover, the ideal software should facilitate user-friendly report creation and customization, enabling plant operators and management to easily interpret data and make informed decisions. Ensuring consistency and reliability in report generation is paramount, as these reports play a crucial role in maintaining the plant’s operational excellence and regulatory compliance. The findings of Hazen's comprehensive evaluation are summarized in the Report Generator Software Evaluation document, which outlines the strengths and weaknesses of each software platform considered. Based on this evaluation, Hazen has provided a recommendation for the standard software platform to be adopted for report creation moving forward. This recommendation is aimed at optimizing the plant's reporting processes and supporting the City's ongoing commitment to operational improvement and regulatory adherence. All development of HMI screens and system wide visualization will utilize this software. All new HMI screens and graphical elements are required to be developed using the latest available City provided ChangeSet file. A ChangeSet file contains the instructions required to re-create an application. This is not an exact backup but a compendium of changes and the base information to reproduce a functional application. In general, ChangeSets are used to distribute new applications, distribute updates to an existing application, or add extra features to OEM layers. This ChangeSet file will contain all of the latest container tags, display definitions, and library widgets needed to support development activities. Each project shall request, via email to the City’s project manager (or assigned City Operational Technology(OT) contact), a copy of the current ChangeSet application immediately prior to initiating programming activities. Although updates to the standard may occasionally occur, the files provided on request date shall be utilized throughout the project. Only those updates which are determined mission critical (have severe consequences to operations, life or equipment safety) will be handled on a case by case basis through direct notice to programmers by the City’s project manager or assigned OT contact. The distribution ChangeSet file will be re-created periodically as functional elements are added and/or enhanced. Additionally, other exports will be created as required in order to maintain an appropriate level of application consistency between the City’s templates and any standalone in development. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 New software requirements must be submitted to the City with suitable lead time, a minimum of four weeks, for a security evaluation and loading. It is the developer’s responsibility to ensure all of the required tools are in place prior to beginning a start-up. General Programming Practices 3.1Languages The IEC 61131-3 standard includes for five languages: Ladder Logic(LD), Function Block Diagram(FBD), Instruction List, and Sequential Function Flowchart. The only allowable languages for all City projects and implementations are LD, and FBD. 3.1.1 Ladder Logic LD is a graphical, symbol-based language with a foundation in circuit diagrams and relay logic hardware. The logic is organized in a series of rungs composed of Boolean elements whose logical result is then applied to outputs or action elements. This can simplistically be considered to be a series of IF/THEN statements. The ladder rungs are scanned in a top-down, left to right manner enforcing strict order of execution. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 7 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 HMI Graphics General Requirements Graphic screens will be composed of many individual types of data elements, a significant portion of which are alpha-numeric. To maintain consistency across the application, the following requirements are to be adhered to. 7.1Color The use of color throughout the application is limited. The nature of high performance graphics restricts colors to those elements that require attention. In order to provide a consistent color pallet throughout the application colors have been defined for use. Refer to Appendix A, Definitions and General Reference, for a listing of those colors approved for use. The colors have been defined using the standard RGB color system. The color name is as defined by Microsoft along with the color values in red, green, and blue and hex code. Throughout this document, where specific colors are required, these color names will be used. The only times the full color spectrum is allowed is on map based screens and those places where photographs are utilized. 7.2Process Values Displaying live process values will be handled differently than static text. Values depicted from the controller will be in bold, blue. This is in contrast to the units of measure that will be indicated in non- bold, black either near the process value or not at all in the case of bar graphs or other animated elements. Leading zero’s(0) will not be utilized except to indicate a fraction(e.g., 0.12). When dynamic values are being used in a column generally align the values on the decimal point. In mixed cases, floating point values and integers, the values are to be aligned to the implied decimal point. In integer only cases the values are to be right aligned. Where applicable and feasible, the process value will indicate its quality. This will be accomplished by including a small indication adjacent to the value indicating “BAD”. This shall be highlighted with a white background to improve legibility. In the event that the value is currently under simulation, that is a manual value being driven by the operator, the indication “SIM” will be used in place of “BAD”. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 1. Introduction 1.1 Background The HFC AWTP is permitted to treat 96 mgd of raw sewage with a Type I two-stage, high rate (pure oxygen and conventional fine bubble aeration) activated sludge biological domestic wastewater treatment plant. This plant is operated to reduce the Total Nitrogen(TN) in the effluent to advanced standards, using biological treatment for nitrification and filtration for denitrification, and to discharge high-level disinfected and dechlorinated effluent to Tampa Bay. Residuals generated by this facility are anaerobically digested and dewatered for land application as a Class B residual. Currently, annual average daily flows are approximately 60 mgd and peak flows are approximately 200 mgd. The rehabilitation to the HFC AWTP will consist of the implementation of improvements identified in the Facility Master Plan Capital Improvement Project List for Fiscal Years 0-5 to maintain system reliability, increase operating efficiency, and meet current and future regulatory requirements. This Technical Memorandum details the SCADA Design Criteria. These criteria will be utilized for all instrumentation and control design performed as part of these improvements. 1.2 Workshops Three(3) workshops were held with the City in developing the SCADA Design Criteria. The findings of these workshops are documented herein and establish the minimum standards to use for developing P&IDs, specifying software and hardware, and guide the overall approach and sequencing for the replacement of the facility wide fiber communications network. The workshops addressed the following topics:  Workshop 1 - P&ID standards, facility fiber network improvements, approach to PLC/HMI software development, and internet/site security protection  Workshop 2 - Hardware standards  Workshop 3 - Design approach Meeting notes are provided in Appendix E for reference. HFC AWTP Master Plan June 10,2022 Improvements: SCADA Design Criteria Technical Memorandum Page 1 of 15 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 6 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Program Structure The City’s programs will be written utilizing LD, and FBD languages only. Any exceptions must be submitted for approval following the procedures contained in the City’s Standards Deviation Request Policy (Reference Volume 1, SCADA & PCS Standards Deviation Request). 6.1Ladder Logic LD is the preferred language when performing complex Boolean logic. This would include actions such as top-down Boolean logic, interlock development, sequencing equipment operations, or control of I/O elements. Function blocks(AOIs, FBs, FCs) may be utilized as part of the rung logic; however, this approach should be minimized. Block programming requirements often require support branches whose sole purpose is to port data into and out of the block. This approach results in rungs that are large and less comprehensible. The preferred approach in this case is to only use blocks that all of the parameters are supplied and visible; typical of internal instructions. Rungs shall be organized in a one concept per rung manner. Stringing logic across rungs through the use of intermediary bits is to be avoided as much as possible. Logic that becomes excessively large will require being broken into multiple rungs. In cases where logic is split, it is incumbent on the developer to provide clarity and explain logic flow. LD shall be limited in size to that which can be printed on an 11” x 17” sheet of paper, either portrait or landscape. LD is a suitable language for the construction of custom function blocks. The same programming constraints are to be applied in this case as well. 6.2Function Block Diagram FBD is preferred when working with blocks and process values and control. Operations such as analog/digital input mapping, PID control elements, stand-alone alarm handling, and totalization should be handled in this manner. It is possible to perform Boolean logic in FBD; however, this should be kept to a minimum. The density of information contained on the sheet should be as concise and legible as possible. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Slow Scan Task Parameter Setting Default rate 500ms Priority 5 6.4Main or “0” Each program folder must contain a routine to be named “Main” or “0”; the language type specified for this routine should be LD. The purpose of the main routine is to govern the flow of the program. The jump subroutine(JSR) instructions shall be used to reference all other logical routines in the PLC program. No other logic is permitted in the main routine. The first JSR of the main routine shall be to the initialization subroutine conditioned on first scan flag from PLC. This will allow initialization logic to run each time the PLC returns to run after power cycle or other interruption. 6.5Subroutines Routines are the lowest level of program structure; routines are subsets of programs. Subroutines are routines that are not designated as the main routine. Subroutines should contain the code for control modules, equipment modules, or other compartmentalized pieces of code or equipment. Subroutines are beneficial for encapsulating a specific function that is a subset of the larger program scope. For traceability, all routines are called from the program main routine. This allows for a single location where the program flow can be quickly identified. The following are exceptions: - Routines that capture event data such as hourly data or report data Routine names shall be named after the process areas the routine interacts with. The naming shall be _XXX_AREA_NAME, where XXX indicates the loop number and AREA_NAME is the description of the loop number area 6.6Functions and Function Blocks There are a set of predefined blocks supplied by the City to be used for specific functions. These blocks provide a common, known functionality in the PLC whose interface connects to OITs, HMIs and SCADA system. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software The scalability of this software is more restrictive than other software platforms as the number of tags and users are licensed, this means that for smaller installations there will be an increase in cost for more tags being added but once a certain threshold is met then an unlimited tag licensing option is available, this is the same case with the number of concurrent users that can create reports. Even though the City’s tag licensing would fall under the unlimited category the more restrictive nature of the licensing of the software caused the platform to score a ranking of 1 when comparing against the other platforms on the scalability evaluation criteria. Specialized functionality allows plant operators to create custom SQL queries and create customized scripts to generate reports or extract data in ways that would not otherwise be available DreamReport is able to run specialized functions and calculations because of this it scores a ranking of 4 on the specialized functions category. DreamReport is able to be integrated with the HMI through their large number of drivers and connectors, which enables the triggered report functionality. Due to this DreamReport scores a ranking of 4 on the HMI integration category. DreamReport security updates and general updates are provided as part of the subscription-based model, the software scores a ranking of 3 for the software support and maintenance category. A redundancy license is available for 50% of the cost of the main license, this is comparable to other offerings, the software scores a ranking of 4 on the redundancy category. Ocean Data Systems, the company that makes DreamReport is a small company of 11-50 employees based out of Europe, with 75 million matches on Google, it scores a ranking of 3 on the company prominence category. Ocean Data Systems had by far the quickest responding and most knowledgeable sales representatives who were able to be reached quickly and provided exceptional knowledge of the uses of the software platform and had the required documentation quickly available the platform scored a ranking of 4 on the customer service category. Advantages DreamReport has several different advantages that make it a unique and useful tool for The City. The software has some useful advantages such as the ability to automatically generate reports upon specific triggers. For example, a water sample parameter going above a certain threshold could trigger an automatic report generation that could display the historical trendline of the parameter alongside other important data to figure out the reason for parameter deviation. This gives the ability to streamline meeting regulatory compliance. Another advantage for DreamReport is the multitude of drivers that are available to The City. DreamReport’s large number of drivers make it easy to integrate with other software and other databases that make integration much easier than other reporting platforms including manual data entry. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 1. Introduction The Howard F. Curren Advanced Water Treatment Plant(HFC AWTP) owns and operates a Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) system currently composed mainly of General Electric(GE) PACSystems Rx3i and 90-30 series programmable logic controllers(PLCs) at the field level. General Electric was acquired by Emerson in February, 2019. This document refers to GE equipment as GE to maintain familiarity with City’s existing Standards. As manufacturers continue to make advancements in product development, older versions of hardware advance towards “legacy” status. Products in legacy status are taken out of production and typically are no longercommercially available from the manufacturer. Replacement parts must then be sourced from third party sellers, increasing the risk for price inflation and long lead times. In that case, a set of spares must be stringently maintained to prevent unnecessary down time in the event of a failure. Additionally, manufacturers eventually stop developing and releasing software updates for legacy controllers, which may pose a security risk on network connected systems. A majority of HFC AWTP’s PLC hardware has entered the legacy phase. While PLC upgrades may be costly up front, updating the PLCs reduces the risk of unplanned downtime and enhances network security. 2. Objectives A majority of the existing PLCs reached legacy status in 2018 (GE 90-30 and GE 90-70 series). PLC hardware for these products are no longer supported by the manufacturer. With the impending replacement of all PLC hardware as part of the on-going design-build project, the City has chosen to evaluate alternatives to develop a new standard for PLCs at the plant. Existing PLCs at the plant were evaluated by the City. As a result, Hazen and Sawyer(Hazen) was asked to review the existing PLC hardware to determine equivalent alternatives. Four major PLC vendors were evaluated for consideration whenever existing PLC line ups are to be replaced as part of on-going process improvements at HFC AWTP. The different PLC models were compared including features and budgetary costs for full system replacement. The findings are summarized in this PLC Hardware Evaluation along with a recommendation for the standard PLC hardware platform going forward. 3. Assessment of Existing PLCs & Industry Review 3.1 Existing PLCs An audit was performed by McKim & Creed & Hazen in June 2018 with assistance from HFC AWTP staff to quantify the number of existing PLCs installed at the facility. HFC AWTP staff provided information about the existing PLCs at these sites including existing network diagrams, model of PLC at each location, quantity, for review and use for this evaluation. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 6.9.2 User Defined Data Types Data is the foundation of what the controllers are utilizing. All controllers support and supply atomic data types; examples are Booleans, integers, and real numbers. These are the fundamental elements upon which control programs are built. Controllers also natively support arrays of these basic types, providing a primitive complex type. The user definted data type(UDT) is the logical extension of the primitive complex type into a structure that is constructed of multiple data types. In this case a UDT is a container for Boolean elements, integers, reals, arrays, and even other UDTs. UDTs are not to be used for programming HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Replacement of the Existing Fiber Cables: The existing fiber cables included in the network are past their useful life having been installed several decades ago. If the cables were to be replaced, it would have to be done with parallel new fiber cables that allow the existing network to function without interruption. However, the 11 fiber cables entering through a conduit beneath the main patch panel in the Main Pump Station Building network room leave the conduit with very little room to pass through additional fiber. Although it’s possible to utilize MaxCell sleeves to snake new fiber cables through the one existing conduit, it would be easier to install a new conduit run if the network rack were to remain where it is currently located. 2.2.3 Recommended Fiber Network Upgrades Given the available documentation on the fiber network and the site investigation, the following is recommended for upgrading the network:  Run new fiber cable in existing fiber cable conduit from new control systems at each building installed under each individual amendment package (to the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvement project) to connect to a new patch panel near the existing network rack. New fiber runs from this new patch panel are to the be connected to the existing network equipment. The existing network rack is located within the existing Main Pump Station network equipment room.  Install new fiber cables from the upgraded PLCs associated with process improvement, utilizing existing conduit duct bank and manholes, through either electrical manhole E-6 or E-7, then through the new conduits to the new network rack.  Gradually remove existing fiber runs as associated processes are upgraded and successfully running off the new network.  Keep both networks operational until the last replacement fiber run is complete. The existing plant fiber patch panel located near the existing network rack is to be kept in service until all processes have been updated and connected with new fiber runs upon which the existing patch panel can be removed.  Provide redundant fiber loops at the outer edges. As an example, a new fiber run between PLC 17 and PLC 7 would allow data transmission for PLCs in either quadrant back to the main network rack regardless of where conduit runs might be lost. PLC 18 (located in Filter Building No. 1) is the most critical to have fiber redundancy as loss of this PLC would result in loss of the plant’s permit analyzers and flow-proportional chemical feeds and samplers, which could shut down STAR. Fiber loops would require some reconfiguration of the programming to designate the “normal” communication path and a backup path if normal route is lost. During the design phase of each improvement to HFC AWTP, the existing network diagram and the new network diagram will be updated to maintain accurate records of the both systems until the existing is fully removed from service. Camera feeds are on the same fiber cable as SCADA, utilizing separate fibers. This approach shall be continued for new fiber cables. HFC AWTP Master Plan June 10,2022 Improvements: SCADA Design Criteria Technical Memorandum Page 9 of 15 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 1 SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 Introduction This is Volume 1 of the City of Tampa’s standards for Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) and Process Control System(PCS) programming, configuration and design standards for The City of Tampa referred to as “The City” or abbreviated as “COT” throughout this document Volume 1 is a compilation of policies and procedures and provides both guidance and specific requirements for control system work. The City SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual is intended to be applied by the City’s contractors, consultants, and in-house personnel when developing or modifying any portion of the City’s control system. The control system is a utility-wide system built upon a common human machine interface(HMI) system and is comprised of the following: Process Control System(PCS): The industrial control system for processes within the boundaries of the plant(Howard F. Curren AWTP). The primary function of the system is control and monitoring of plant operations. The PCS is comprised of the local HMI subsystem and various programmable logic controllers(PLCs) and operator interface terminals(OITs). Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) System: The industrial control system for processes outside the boundaries of the process areas, ability to monitor and/or control the process from the control room from outside the plant. The primary function of this system is data acquisition; processes or remote sites are designed to run autonomously without supervisory control function. The SCADA system serves both remote sites such as lift stations and wastewater treatment plant facility in an integrated environment and is comprised of telemetry equipment with a supervisory HMI. This manual is divided into three main volumes plus two appendices: Volume 1 contains the control system policies and procedures that apply to the management, maintenance and modification of the control system. Volume 1 includes policies for: - Programming laptop use - Management of change(MOC) - Software and application revision control - Cybersecurity - Other related topics. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - Policies Volume 1 of 3 February 2025 6 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

This policy applies to all modifications of City SCADA & PCS assets including but not limited to HMIs, OITs, historians, network equipment, PLC, and radios. 6.2Responsibilities To ensure the MOC policy is implemented and maintained in accordance with the procedures listed in this policy, the identified City Employees and contractors have the responsibilities identified in Section 2. 6.3Management of Change Approval Requirements All modifications to the control system shall be reviewed and approved prior to deployment on the live production system. A change request shall be submitted to the City for approval utilizing the SCADA & PCS Change Request Form provided in Appendix B. Change requests shall include the following: 1) Description of change 2) Location of change (e.g., PLC name, subroutine and rungs, HMI screen name, etc.) 3) Name, company name, and contact phone number for person implementing change 4) Copy of completed test plan from the City’s Test Environment 5) Copy of proposed field test verification plan 6) Back-out plan including details on how to revert to a previous known good installation. No change shall be implemented without written approval from the City. The City’s OT contact is responsible for assigning appropriate approval authority and coordinating with City operations. - [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software and compliance without worrying about potential cyber-attacks. Due to the impact of plant safety this requirement it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.8 21 CFR Part 11 Compliance The software should be able to meet 21 CFR Part 11 compliance. This ensures that electronic records and signatures are trustworthy, reliable, and equivalent to paper records. Compliance with 21 CFR Part 11 means that the software adheres to strict standards for data integrity, security, and traceability, which are essential for regulatory audits and inspections. This level of compliance helps operators maintain accurate records of plant operations, ensure the validity of data used for decision-making, and demonstrate adherence to legal and environmental regulations. Ultimately, 21 CFR Part 11 compliance provides a framework that supports the integrity and reliability of the plant’s data management system. Due to the non-critical but important nature of this requirement it is given an importance factor of 3. 3.1.9 Browser Usability The software should have the ability to operate out of a browser setting to generate reports and to allow for the quick ability to view trends, and other plant information. This is crucial for a wastewater operator as it provides flexibility to access real-time data and reports from any device with internet connectivity, without being confined to a specific workstation. This ensures that operators can monitor plant performance, track water quality indicators, and respond promptly to any issues, no matter where they are. As this requirement directly impacts plant performance it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.10 Scalability The software should be flexible enough to expand if The City requires an expansion without incurring a significant increase in cost or complexity. The software should allow the system to grow and adapt in response to the changing needs and demands of the plant. As the plant expands, either through increased capacity or the addition of new processes and technologies, the software must be able to accommodate these changes seamlessly. Scalable software ensures that operators can continue to manage and monitor the plant effectively without facing major disruptions. Due to the non-critical aspect of this requirement and lower level of importance it is given an importance factor of 2. 3.1.11 Specialized functions Running custom SQL queries enables operators to extract specific datasets from the database, facilitating in-depth analysis and troubleshooting. This flexibility ensures that the software can be adapted to evolving operational requirements and provides the operator with powerful tools to maintain optimum plant performance, identify trends, and make data-driven decisions. Overall, these features contribute to a more responsive, efficient, and innovative wastewater management system. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 no alarming information will be generated. Scaling, beyond the conversion of integers to reals, is not to be employed. All failure states will be discussed with the City at pre-programming workshop to determine the proper course of action. Messages array bits are each mapped to a variable where variables 0XXX (where XXX is the unique variable number) are reserved for digital outputs, 1XXX (where XXX is the unique variable number) are reserved for digital inputs, and 4XXX (where XXX is the unique variable name) are reserved for analog inputs/outputs which are read/write blocks. 6.8.4 SCADA IO This is data and information that is being specifically marshalled for use by the SCADA system. These SCADA IO points shall be kept minimal, the basic ones that are provided and used are the simulation and disable IO points, some may be added if needed and should be documented accordingly. 6.9Data Structures Internal to most modern controllers is the concept of structured data. This is typically represented employing two methods, add-on instructions/function blocks and user data types. Each of these concepts offers opportunities to add clarity and reusability to the overall control program. User data types shall not be used for the development of new code. Only predefined add on instructions shall be used. 6.9.1 Function Blocks/Add-on Instructions For the purpose of this discussion these will be referred to as add-on instructions(AOIs) AOIs represent a unique collection of data and logic. The logic is customarily developed using the controller’s standard programming languages. The AOI also presents a usable interface to the logic through a series of parameters, both inputs and outputs. The AOI also can contain internal data registers that maintain the unique state of the instance of the AOI. The AOI in short is a complete, unique encapsulation of control data and logic. The primary benefit of the AOI is in its reusability. The advantage to the developer is that logic only has to be developed and tested once. Once proven, this logic can be reused countless times with the assurance that it will always function the same across all instances. This aids in the rapid development of entire systems. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 5 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Alarms The basic definition of an alarm(per ISA-18.2, Alarm Management) is an audible and/or visual indication to the operator that an equipment malfunction, process deviation or other abnormal condition requires a response. This standard contains definitions that help to define the various elements that must be addressed for IO derived and Setpoint derived alarms and what functionality must be applied. 5.1General Requirements The following section contains requirements and best practices that are to be applied to City alarms. 5.1.1 Generation Location All alarms will be generated and managed as close to the source as possible. This implies that all alarms will be generated in the controller, if at all possible. Utilizing alarming at the HMI/SCADA layer is the least desirable and is only to be utilized if controller based is not possible. Alarm generation will be consistent throughout the system. The alarms must mean the same thing in all cases and must be generated utilizing consistent logic. All capabilities must be implemented consistently. An example of this would be: Current value > Alarm setpoint vs Current Value ≥ Alarm setpoint ( the former case of ‘>’ is preferred) for setpoint derived alarms. Alarm generation capabilities that might be contained in the analog I/O modules themselves are to be used. These embedded alarming capabilities are not available in all modules and thus represent a mixed system. Alarming will be generated by the first or primary utilizing element. This would mean that analog values would be alarmed at the block that was performing the conversion from raw input value to scaled engineering units. In the case of discrete inputs this would be in the block that is performing the primary use or monitoring. 5.1.2 Basic configuration The following outlines the various basic elements for alarms. 5.1.2.1 Setpoints Setpoints for each alarm condition will be provided. This will be appropriate to HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 26 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 LD is the preferred language for Boolean equations. This is also an acceptable language for the development of functions and functional blocks. 3.1.2 Function Block Diagram FBD is a graphical, symbolic language based on the assembly of interconnected functional elements. The logic is similar to ladder logic, with left to right, top to bottom order. The individual functional elements(blocks) can be, and often are, complex with numerous input and output points, as well as configuration points. Program constructs are organized into “sheets”, akin to a page, on which the blocks are rendered. FBD offers the opportunity to implement consistent, repetitive programming in a compact, regular manner. As such, this approach allows for the simplified representation of complex operations. Figure 2 Function Block Diagram FBD is the preferred language for process values as well as utilization of complex functional elements. 3.2Object Library Usage The library of function blocks, and functions remove large blocks of repetitive code as well as aggregate data into one cohesive unit. Any communication between the controller and the HMI or OIT(s) must utilize City approved objects. Refer to Section 6.6 for a listing of supplied function blocks. During development the programmer shall review and, where possible, populate all relevant parameter fields for each object instance. For example, this may include setting engineering unit scaling for analog instructions, programming limit switch and position HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F.
