Source protection of code is prohibited. This includes all code created or used by systems integrators, programmers, vendors and OEM suppliers. Unless contractually stipulated to the contrary, all source code, images, databases, and supporting documentation are the property of the City. As such, all of this information is to be supplied to the City no later than initiation of site acceptance testing. 3.4Documentation It is the programmer’s responsibility to provide an annotated program compliant with the minimum documentation requirements defined in this section for all program files. As a general guideline, the level of documentation provided should allow for a technician with no familiarity of the functions of the specific unit process to read the comments and fully understand the inputs, outputs, and logic implemented in the program. 3.4.1 Object Parameters, UDT and Tag Comments It is required that all tags including object parameters and UDT elements be provided with completed description fields. Refer to Section 5 for more information. For every PLC data type created, there is an associated faceplate in the system as shown in Figure 40. The objects have been named using the following structure: FP_DATATYPE. Example: A "Knife Valve" object corresponds to the "Open-Close Actuator" data type. When a user interacts with an object of this data type, the associated faceplate is displayed on the screen. Figure 40 Standard Faceplates A special case is the Security Settings faceplate. Unlike other faceplates, it is not associated with any data type in the PLC or with any macro. Instead, it functions independently to allow users to manage security settings within the system such as: adding new users, editing existing users or removing existing users. 14.4Faceplate Display and Macros To ensure that the correct information is displayed when a faceplate is activated, macros have been created for each faceplate as shown in Figure 41. When a user interacts with an object, the corresponding macro is executed, and the related faceplate pops up. Example: If the user clicks on "Knife Valve No. 1,epsilon which is of type "Open-Close Actuator,epsilon the "Open-Close Actuator Macro" is triggered. This displays the "Open-Close Actuator Faceplate" with information specific to "Knife Valve No. 1." HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 2 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 HMI Software The SCADA software currently utilized by the City is VTScada 12.1.58, development environment from Trihedral Engineering, contact the City for version being used. This software suite forms the basis of the system, as well as providing the majority of the data acquisition drivers. All development of HMI screens and system wide visualization will utilize this software. All new HMI screens and graphical elements are required to be developed using the latest available City provided ChangeSet file. A ChangeSet file contains the instructions required to re-create an application. This is not an exact backup but a compendium of changes and the base information to reproduce a functional application. In general, ChangeSets are used to distribute new applications, distribute updates to an existing application, or add extra features to OEM layers. This ChangeSet file will contain all of the latest container tags, display definitions, and library widgets needed to support development activities. Each project shall request, via email to the City’s project manager (or assigned City Operational Technology(OT) contact), a copy of the current ChangeSet application immediately prior to initiating programming activities. Although updates to the standard may occasionally occur, the files provided on request date shall be utilized throughout the project. Only those updates which are determined mission critical (have severe consequences to operations, life or equipment safety) will be handled on a case by case basis through direct notice to programmers by the City’s project manager or assigned OT contact. The distribution ChangeSet file will be re-created periodically as functional elements are added and/or enhanced. Additionally, other exports will be created as required in order to maintain an appropriate level of application consistency between the City’s templates and any standalone in development. The City’s SCADA system is a managed VTScada application which has been developed using custom City developed library widgets. Refer to the City Standard Object Library Programming Guideline for details on the currently available object template library or submit an email request to the City’s project manager. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 4.10Troubleshooting Troubleshooting displays provide overview and detailed status into the health of the system. These displays are organized in a hierarchical manner utilizing the drill-down approach. At their maximum depth the displays will provide detailed information into the selected component. This methodology applies to field equipment as well as system hardware and components such as PLCs, and communications drivers. All of the displays, by nature, are to be customized to address the specifics of the equipment. At a minimum the controller and fault status along with its diagnostic information is to be provided. As much information as possible is to be provided. 4.11Flow Totals Flow totalization displays provide a quick overview of the complete facility flow values. These displays are considered to be in the process unit overview family; however, often reach into more areas than a process unit. Flow total displays should span no more than the plant to which they are attached. Totals associated with wet wells or towers will be clustered in zones/regions similar to the Remote Site summaries. These displays will represent a basic overview of the facility with the flows and totals through the critical sections. The individual totalizer faceplates will provide additional depth of information into current day totals, previous day totals, monthly totals, and annual totals. 4.12Runtime and Starts Totals Runtime and starts totals displays provide a quick overview of the complete facility motor and pump usage. These displays are considered to be in the process unit overview family. These displays will provide detailed information into the operation of electromechanical devices. The individual motor and pump faceplates will provide additional depth of information into the current day usage, previous day, monthly, and annual totals. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 6 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Program Structure The City’s programs will be written utilizing LD, and FBD languages only. Any exceptions must be submitted for approval following the procedures contained in the City’s Standards Deviation Request Policy (Reference Volume 1, SCADA & PCS Standards Deviation Request). 6.1Ladder Logic LD is the preferred language when performing complex Boolean logic. This would include actions such as top-down Boolean logic, interlock development, sequencing equipment operations, or control of I/O elements. Function blocks(AOIs, FBs, FCs) may be utilized as part of the rung logic; however, this approach should be minimized. Block programming requirements often require support branches whose sole purpose is to port data into and out of the block. This approach results in rungs that are large and less comprehensible. The preferred approach in this case is to only use blocks that all of the parameters are supplied and visible; typical of internal instructions. Rungs shall be organized in a one concept per rung manner. Stringing logic across rungs through the use of intermediary bits is to be avoided as much as possible. Logic that becomes excessively large will require being broken into multiple rungs. In cases where logic is split, it is incumbent on the developer to provide clarity and explain logic flow. LD shall be limited in size to that which can be printed on an 11” x 17” sheet of paper, either portrait or landscape. LD is a suitable language for the construction of custom function blocks. The same programming constraints are to be applied in this case as well. 6.2Function Block Diagram FBD is preferred when working with blocks and process values and control. Operations such as analog/digital input mapping, PID control elements, stand-alone alarm handling, and totalization should be handled in this manner. It is possible to perform Boolean logic in FBD; however, this should be kept to a minimum. The density of information contained on the sheet should be as concise and legible as possible. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 30 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 The AOI interface contains a duality in its nature. From the programmer’s perspective, this allows them to interact with the code-block in a regular manner. From the graphical developer’s perspective, this presents a reliable interface to contained data that simplifies their data management requirements. This interface is a strictly enforced contract with all parties into what the contained data is and to which the inner workings of the blocks code can respond. The parameters are constructed of the controller’s basic data types. Examples are Booleans, integers, and real numbers. The parameters are also capable of handling complex data elements like arrays and user defined types(UDTs). This allows the developer the opportunity to source whatever data is required for the contained logic process. There are more subtle advantages to the use of AOIs from the controller’s perspective. Typically, the controller only maintains one copy of the executable code without regard to the total number of actual used instances. The only requirement on the controller is to have enough memory to contain the interface and internal variable data. The end result is a much smaller controller footprint. AOIs shall be used for any function that will be repetitive. An example of this is a valve. There is a high probability that a system will contain multiple valves. As such the generation of one block of code that addresses the needs for all similar acting valves can be a significant time saver. AOIs should not be used for one time code or code that has the potential to be modified(such as during start-up). AOIs are typically compiled and subsequently downloaded to the controller for optimization purposes. Modifying them typically requires the entire controller to be taken out of active state while a completely new download takes place. This can have a significant impact on the process. A secondary function of AOIs is the development and encapsulation of calculations or custom control requirements. Calculations are generally fixed in nature; an example being the solution to the quadratic equation. There is little value or clarity to be gained by exposing the details of the equation to the general codebase. The only thing that realistically matters are the results. There is also the case of supplementing or extending the functionality of native programming elements. There will often be cases where special requirements need to be applied to a native function that is not supplied or supported. In these cases, an AOI provides the opportunity for a simplified solution. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 39 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 4.1.5 Equipment Number Distinct equipment numbers shall be incremented sequentially based on multiple equipment working as part of a system in parallel or series operation. Wherever possible, the equipment number shall match the equipment descriptor or start with 01 and increment based on the number of matching loop number and type items in that system. For example: - Sludge feed pump 1 shall have an equipment number of 01, pump 2 shall have an equipment number of 02, and so on. - Three discharge pumps from one tank, pumping to a common header, would share the same loop number and iterate the equipment number to make the tag unique. - Three tanks, each with one discharge pump will have the same equipment number, but the loop numbers will be different to make the tag unique. 4.1.6 Redundancy letter If two or more instruments serve the same purpose at an identical location in a loop, they shall be labeled with an identical loop and equipment number with a letter appended, starting at A, to identify the instrument as redundant. For example if there are two PIT instruments that share a header one should be labeled H-065-PIT-110-01-A and the other labeled H-065-PIT-110-01-B 4.1.7 ISA Naming Convention The ISA naming are used to standardize the identification of instruments and equipment in process control systems. Each instrument or equipment is assigned a unique tag consisting of the loop number followed by up to three uppercase letters that describe the function. For example - “FIT” stands for Flow Indicating Transmitter - “YA” stands for State Alarm These conventions ensure consistency and clarity in documentation and communication across different disciplines. Please refer to Appendix A for the ISA naming conventions. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 21 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] 5.2.3 Alarm Rationalization Alarm rationalization is the process of reviewing, validating, and justifying the alarms that meet the criteria of a critical alarm. In other words, the rationalization specifies only those points in the process system that require alarming. The ultimate goal of alarm rationalization is to determine the most efficient number of alarms to ensure that the process system is safe and remains within normal operating range. Alarm rationalization must be accomplished in conjunction with the City at the preprogramming workshop. The basic process can be outlined as: - Check Alarm Validity - Determine the consequence of inaction - Document Cause, confirmation, and corrective action - Document operator response time - Assign alarm priority - Alarm classification - Determine alarm attributes - Address need for special handling All unused or deprecated alarms are to be removed from the system. Alarms that are no longer needed are not to be abandoned in place (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Slow Scan Task Parameter Setting Default rate 500ms Priority 5 6.4Main or “0” Each program folder must contain a routine to be named “Main” or “0”; the language type specified for this routine should be LD. The purpose of the main routine is to govern the flow of the program. The jump subroutine(JSR) instructions shall be used to reference all other logical routines in the PLC program. No other logic is permitted in the main routine. The first JSR of the main routine shall be to the initialization subroutine conditioned on first scan flag from PLC. This will allow initialization logic to run each time the PLC returns to run after power cycle or other interruption. 6.5Subroutines Routines are the lowest level of program structure; routines are subsets of programs. Subroutines are routines that are not designated as the main routine. Subroutines should contain the code for control modules, equipment modules, or other compartmentalized pieces of code or equipment. Subroutines are beneficial for encapsulating a specific function that is a subset of the larger program scope. For traceability, all routines are called from the program main routine. This allows for a single location where the program flow can be quickly identified. The following are exceptions: - Routines that capture event data such as hourly data or report data Routine names shall be named after the process areas the routine interacts with. The naming shall be _XXX_AREA_NAME, where XXX indicates the loop number and AREA_NAME is the description of the loop number area 6.6Functions and Function Blocks There are a set of predefined blocks supplied by the City to be used for specific functions. These blocks provide a common, known functionality in the PLC whose interface connects to OITs, HMIs and SCADA system. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 o AC = Reference to the type of card, AC indicates voltage type. o DI = Reference to type of input, DI indicates discrete input. o MOD-04 = Indicates the module number. 4.5Tasks This section describes the various objects available for the organization of program logic within the controller and their associated parameters. The section provides guidelines for selection and organization of logic into these objects and configuration of their properties. Three tasks are used, fast, normal, and slow. The tasks are typically reserved for message communications with other devices. The fast scan task has the highest priority of 1, the normal scan task has a priority of 3, being used for all regular monitoring and control operations. The slow scan task has a priority of 5, being reserved for processor monitoring. Each task shall contain a single program within itself and program name shall follow a similar naming as the task. 4.5.1 Task Structure Tasks are the highest organizational unit in the controller and dictate the order of execution. The nomenclature applied to tasks must represent the broadest definition of the contents. 4.5.2 Program Structure Programs represent the middle tier of the organizational structure. At this layer there is more additional granularity added to the overall structure and the naming conventions must reflect that. Naming at this layer must not overlap with the layer above. For example: - P01_Fast Program - P02_Normal Program - P03_Slow Program Individual names at this tier may be duplicated in other tasks but must be unique per task. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 24 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 4.5.3 Routine Structure This is the lowest tier of programming structure. As such the name must provide clarity into the exact programming construct. Each routine is linked to a process area for fast identification and each process area has 3 subroutines associated, except for IO subroutines that are 4. For Example, - _000_ is associated with IO Mapping. o _000_ANALOG_INPUT_MAPPING o _000_ANALOG_OUTPUT_MAPPING o _000_DIGITAL_IO_MAPPING o _000_DISCRETE_IO_MAPPING - _010_Main_PUMP_STATION_ALM - _010_Main_PUMP_STATION_ASSETS - _010 Main_PUMP_STATION_CONTROLS HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 25 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 the signal being monitored, analog or digital. All setpoints will be supplied in engineering units. 5.1.2.2 Toggling Alarms Alarms cannot be individually enabled/disabled. Equipment must be disabled in order to disable the alarms associated to that piece of equipment. Otherwise, all the equipment’s associated alarms will remain enabled. 5.1.2.3 Debounce Times Debounce times are the period(in mSec) for which the signal must be in excess of the indicated setpoint limit before the alarm is generated. Setting a value to zero eliminates the debounce function completely. Alarms are currently all set to have debounce times at zero 5.1.2.4 Dead-Band Amount The dead-band amount is used to eliminate the chattering of alarms around the setpoint by requiring the process variable to retreat from the setpoint by a set amount before the alarm resets. A dead-band setting is applied to all limit alarms monitoring a process value. The configuration parameter is applicable to analog alarms only. The amount is supplied in engineering units with a value of zero deactivating the feature. Alarms are currently all set to have debounce times at zero. 5.1.2.5 Shelving or Bypassing The ability to bypass, or shelve, an alarm for a specified period will not be provided. Alarms cannot be bypassed or shelved; alarms can only be disabled. Disabling alarms is done through disabling the associated equipment. 5.1.3 Alarm conditions All alarm conditions are expected to apply positive logic. When the alarm value is a Boolean 1/true/on then the alarm is to be considered present/active. When the alarm value is a Boolean 0/false/off then the alarm is considered to be not present/inactive. 5.2Definition and Rationalization 5.2.1 Analog Alarms The following are available alarm conditions for analog values. These will support the basic typical requirements. Condition Description Low-Low(LL) The current scaled value is below the configured Low-Low setpoint(value < Low-Low SP) Low(L) The current scaled value is below the configured HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 27 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Low setpoint(value High SP) High High(HH) The current scaled value is Above the configured High-High setpoint(value > High-High SP) 5.2.2 Discrete Alarms The following are the available alarm conditions for digital values Condition Description Alarm(Alm) The current value is in the configured alarm state. 5.2.3 Alarm Rationalization Alarm rationalization is the process of reviewing, validating, and justifying the alarms that meet the criteria of a critical alarm. In other words, the rationalization specifies only those points in the process system that require alarming. The ultimate goal of alarm rationalization is to determine the most efficient number of alarms to ensure that the process system is safe and remains within normal operating range. Alarm rationalization must be accomplished in conjunction with the City at the preprogramming workshop. The basic process can be outlined as: - Check Alarm Validity - Determine the consequence of inaction - Document Cause, confirmation, and corrective action - Document operator response time - Assign alarm priority - Alarm classification - Determine alarm attributes - Address need for special handling All unused or deprecated alarms are to be removed from the system. Alarms that are no longer needed are not to be abandoned in place 5.2.4 Alarm Generation Guidelines The generation of alarms is to be consistent with the ISA-18.2 alarm management guidelines. The overgeneration of alarms is indicative of ill-considered alarm policies. As such the alarming frequency should be considered against the following matrix. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 4.1.3 Equipment and Instrument Tags The City has been using an alphanumeric designation for process equipment identification. This was to be used for equipment tags. The alphanumeric designations to use for equipment are outlined in Appendix A. Additional designations are to follow the ISA standards as presented on instrument legends followed by “000” to be the same alphanumeric format as equipment tags. 4.1.4 Loop number This refers to the specific loop number as illustrated on the P&IDs. This is required to maintain a direct relationship between the P&IDs and generated information. Loop numbers will be assigned on the P&IDs following the process flow for each line. Loop numbers will increment starting at the top left most process line and follow that process line until it ends or joins with another, sheet to sheet, for all process equipment within a particular building. Loops are defined by each separate function in a process (manual valve, pump with related integral instruments, control valves, etc.) as assigned by the instrument engineer. Process equipment that works as a system, such as centrifuge sludge feed pump 1,2, and 3, shall share the same loop number and increment the equipment number. For example, if pump 1 has [loop]-[equipment] 101-01 pump two would be 101-02, and pump 3 would be 101-03. This also applies to dedicated equipment for that pump; an upstream and downstream pump isolation valve could be 100-01 and 102-01 respectively. Pumps 2 and 3 upstream would be 100-02,102-02, and 100-03,102-03. Instrumentation associated with a pump (pressure switch, temperature transmitter, etc.) installed upstream or downstream of the pup, without a process control equipment separating the pump from the instrumentation shall, shall use the same loop number as the pump. There will be more than 100 loops within a building so three digits will be used. The City will also use loop numbers separately numbered for equipment that normally is not shown on P&IDs (switchgear, network equipment, etc.) as well as all assets that don’t have control signals associated with them (manual valves, check valves, air release valves, etc.). Each P&ID will indicate the first and last tag used on the drawing along with 10 loop numbers for future use. The sequential loop numbers are outlined in Appendix A HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 20 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Module configurations are to be kept as close to manufacturer default(out of the box) as possible. This is to facilitate the replacement of modules on failure at some point in the future. In all cases ‘Hot Swappable’ modules are preferred. Modules that support advanced features, such as the HART protocol, should adhere to the spirit of the specification as much as possible. The ability to employ these additional features, such as multiple data points on a signal channel, offer cost savings and convenience. However, beyond returning the value and any indication of the quality of that data, extended features should not be utilized. Diagnostic points such as broken wire detect, under range, and over range are to be enabled, if selectable, and the signals utilized as faults to the analog input blocks. Module fault information is to be aggregated into a common signal and utilized as fault inputs to the analog blocks. In the case of inputs and outputs, either fault status information should be supplied to the primary controlling element. This allows the blockware to be cognizant that the element(s) that are being controlled will be non-responsive. This is applicable to equipment position control/feedback and speed control/feedback. 6.8.2 Digital IO Each point is assigned a variable. Typically, there are few configuration elements; however, in the case that such configuration elements are available, those elements are to be used sparingly or not at all. All signal diagnostic capabilities are to be employed. Filters on the input signals are not to be utilized and in all cases turned off. In the case of outputs, fault status information should be supplied to the primary controlling element. This allows the blockware to be cognizant that the element(s) that are being controlled will be non-responsive. 6.8.3 Message IO All peer-to-peer tag values passed through message arrays shall be read through the fast scan. Message routines shall be named by applying the prefix “MSG_” to the paired controller’s name, followed by “_IO”. An example would be “MSG_ H-010-PLC000-800­ 01_IO” (Howard F. Curren AWTP, MPS Electrical room, PLC number 1). Messaged data will be handled in a similar manner as module supplied data, through analog blocks. In the case of messaged data, the success or failure of communications will be used as the data quality. As the receiving controller is not the owner of the data HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 37 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

Design Criteria 2.1 P&ID Format and Tag Conventions During the workshops, the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvements Dewatering Facility Replacement project was used to create a template of the format the P&ID format. The instrumentation legend and example P&IDs from that project are provided in Appendix C. The Tagging protocol will be different for process equipment versus instrumentation tags. This software will aid in creating more accurate I/O lists, developing instrument lists using stored mega data, and ease in entering and editing tags. In order to develop tags for each design, building numbers and acronyms will be required. Refer to Table 2-2 for all known building identification. Prior to developing P&IDs for each improvement, coordination with the City will occurred to confirm all building designations. For separate buildings with the same designation, revised designations may be warranted. 2.1.1 Equipment Tags / Location Description Equipment will be identified on the drawings and tagged on the equipment in accordance with the City’s CMMS. This format is as follows: XXXYYY-ZZZ-BB, where: XXXYYY CMMS Equipment ID - Refer to Table 2-1 for common designations ZZZ Building Number - Refer to Table 2-2 for a list of HFC AWTP building numbers BB Equipment Number - Items with the same equipment ID and Building Number will be numbered sequential from 01 to 99 to establish a unique equipment tag for all equipment. Tag numbers will be affixed to non-movable surface(not motor or pump) to associate the tag number to a location. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 7.3DNP3 Exception Configuration The communication protocol between remote controllers and the supervisory(SCADA) system will be based on the DNP3 protocol as specified by DNP.ORG. This protocol shall be only used on remote sites. The transport layer for communications is to be appropriate for the application, e.g., LAN, serial modems, etc. No proprietary protocols are to be implemented. The master will be configured to perform its polling utilizing events(exceptions). Only periodically will static values be requested to insure validity and quality. The definition of what constitutes an event are dependent on the nature of the data point and acceptable excursion limits. All events will include a timestamp to facilitate sequence of event generation. The data is to be formatted and marshalled to facilitate optimal polling. Data being read from LAN based sources will by nature support a larger number of points being read than those from serial based devices. As such the data being sent must have the event thresholds and organization considered prior to implementation. Due to the potential communications loss events that can occur to some remote stations the concept of store & forward will be fully implemented. Therefore, all controllers must support this method of operation. This functionality may be supplied either natively or by the use of in-chassis interface modules. Each device is to be configured to maintain the data as long as possible, this is heavily dependent on the device design. Standard polling of remote locations by the SCADA system is to be configured such that the entire store & forward buffer may be read. This is much more critical when communicating to devices utilizing serial or cellular modems where outstations are typically read in a round-robin approach. In these cases, acquiring the data and storing the data into the historian in chronological order is critical. Stations that have a higher probability of continuous connection may be configured for unsolicited messaging. This feature is to be used sparingly and only for critical events. This feature will not be supportable in systems employing round-robin polling. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 43 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 6.9.2 User Defined Data Types Data is the foundation of what the controllers are utilizing. All controllers support and supply atomic data types; examples are Booleans, integers, and real numbers. These are the fundamental elements upon which control programs are built. Controllers also natively support arrays of these basic types, providing a primitive complex type. The user definted data type(UDT) is the logical extension of the primitive complex type into a structure that is constructed of multiple data types. In this case a UDT is a container for Boolean elements, integers, reals, arrays, and even other UDTs. UDTs are not to be used for programming HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 40 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 appended to the controller name utilizing the structure _r## where the hash marks reflect the specific version number. The specifics of the version as well as the reasoning for incrementing will be contained in the internal program documentation. An example of this is: H_075_PLC000_800_01_r04.ext The file name above indicates this is version 4 of the controller H_075_PLC000_800_01 The ‘ext’ indicates the manufacturer’s assigned extension for the file. When periodic backups are taken, the files will have a date code added to the file name. This will be in the form of yymmdd. This reflects the year, month, and day, in two(2) digit format upon which the file was saved. If further granularity is required then hhmm, representing hour in 24 hour time and the minute, may be added to the date code. A full example of the backup file naming convention is: H-075_PLC000_800_01_r04_2410041603.ext This indicates that the backup was captured on October 4,2024 at 4:03 pm. In this manner, multiple versions and backups may exist in the same subdirectory without interfering with each other. The data coding also leverages the native lexicographic sort order of Microsoft file systems. 4.4Module Naming Each module must be assigned a unique description in the controller to aid in identifying the hardware. The naming convention for modules is defined as: R#S#_[Module Type] Where: R# = Physical Rack number S# = Physical Slot number [Module Type] = the common acronym taken from the manufacturers part number Examples: - 1756_IA16I_R2S4_ACDI_MOD_04 o 1756_IA16I = This indicates the model ref. o R2S4 = Reference to location, rack 2 slot 4. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 23 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 FBD sheets should be limited in size to that which can be printed on an 11” x 17” sheet of paper. 6.3Task and Program Files Tasks provide the broadest level of organization. They also provide a level of execution control, that being continuous, periodic, event amongst others. Only two types of tasks are to be employed at the City: continuous and periodic. All other types of task types will not be employed. 6.3.1 Periodic tasks Periodic tasks are designed to execute on a fixed period of time, typically in milliseconds. These tasks are conducive when working with a process or system that is slow, or requires the processing on a regular basis. Creation of periodic tasks should be kept to a minimum(six or less) as required by the controlled process or area. The periodic task execution rate should be set to allow ample time for program execution without burdening the processor. Programmers shall frequently monitor and adjust the execution rate based on the task time. All programs shall be commissioned with task settings tuned such that no overlaps exist in the program. During commissioning, the main program overlap counter shall be confirmed at zero and FAULTLOG object shall not have any minor faults for overlap. Each periodic task must be set with a priority from 1 to 15, with 1 representing the highest priority. It is recommended that each task be set with a unique priority. Fast Scan Task Parameter Setting Default rate 10ms Priority 1 Normal Scan Task Parameter Setting Default rate 250ms Priority 3 HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 31 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 3.1.4 VTScada integration The software must be able to pull data from the VTScada database without the user having access to the VTScada system or database. This capability is crucial for a plant operator because it ensures seamless integration and data flow without compromising the security and integrity of the VTScada system. By allowing data to be accessed and utilized without direct interaction with the VTScada environment, plant operators can streamline operations, improve data handling efficiency, and minimize the risk of unauthorized access or potential system disruptions. This feature also facilitates the creation of comprehensive reports and dashboards, enabling integrators to monitor and optimize plant performance while maintaining stringent security protocols. Due to the level of importance of ensuring that the software platform is well integrated with the report creating platform this requirement is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.5 Other Databases The software must be able to pull data from sources other than VTScada such as LIMS for lab works, HSQ for data not yet available in VTScada. This capability is crucial for a wastewater plant operator because it ensures comprehensive data integration and accessibility. By allowing the software to gather information from multiple databases, operators can obtain a holistic view of the plant's operations. This integrated approach facilitates better monitoring and management of plant metrics, enhances decisionmaking processes, and ensures compliance with regulatory standards. Moreover, it minimizes the risk of data silos, improves operational efficiency, and supports the creation of detailed and accurate reports, which are essential for optimizing plant performance and maintaining environmental safety. Due to the importance of being able to link to other databases this requirement is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.6 Automated Report Generation The software must have the ability to automatically generate reports and email these reports to users on a schedule. This feature is particularly vital for operators as it ensures timely access to essential data without the need for manual report generation. Automated reports can include critical metrics such as water quality indicators, equipment performance data, and regulatory compliance status. By receiving these reports on a predefined schedule, operators can swiftly identify and address potential issues, streamline operational workflows, and maintain adherence to environmental regulations. Additionally, automated report generation reduces administrative burden, allowing operators to focus on more strategic and high-priority tasks. Due to the level critical nature of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.7 Security The software must be compliant with NIST standards for cybersecurity to ensure safety for use in the plant. This is crucial for a wastewater operator because it guarantees that all data and operations are protected from cyber threats. Given the sensitive nature of wastewater treatment processes, any breach could lead to significant environmental, and risk to human health and lives. Robust cybersecurity ensures the integrity and reliability of the system, allowing operators to focus on maintaining plant performance HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 12 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 5. Software recommendation Based on the outcome of the decision matrix the best choice for report creation software would be Ocean Data System’s DreamReport platform. Although other platforms had advantages over the DreamReport platform the overall scores for the DreamReport platform were 205 for the weighted score and 52 for the unweighted score. DreamReport offers a range of advantages that make it a valuable software platform for various applications. Its dashboard usability is commendable, allowing users to display specific data metrics over a range of time and create additional dashboards for viewing performance metrics. This flexibility in dashboard configuration enhances the user experience by providing tailored views of critical data. The platform supports custom report creation, although it is limited by the number of available licenses. However, additional licenses can be added to the system, which allows for greater customization and scalability in reporting capabilities. Integration with VTScada is available, with over 100 drivers available to connect different databases, making it compatible with many different systems. This extensive integration capability ensures that DreamReport can work seamlessly with various data sources, enhancing its utility in diverse operational environments. The software also excels in database integration due to the large number of available drivers, which facilitates smooth data flow and management across different platforms. Automatic triggered reports can be set up based on specific conditions or schedules, providing flexibility in automation. For instance, reports can be generated when a specific water parameter or equipment function reaches a certain threshold, or they can be scheduled to run at regular intervals. These reports can be sent out to specific individuals through email in various formats, such as PDF, ensuring timely and efficient communication of critical information. DreamReport is compliant with NIST cybersecurity standards and can be set up to be 21 CFR part 11 compliant, enhancing its security credentials and making it suitable for use in regulated industries. While the browser usability is good for viewing, report editing must be done through the report creator software. This limitation is offset by the ease of use and look of the browser user interface, which scored well in evaluations. Additionally, DreamReport provides regular security and general updates as part of its subscription model, ensuring that the software remains up-to-date and secure. The availability of a redundancy license at a competitive cost further enhances its appeal, providing a cost-effective solution for ensuring system reliability. The company behind DreamReport, Ocean Data Systems, is noted for its responsive and knowledgeable customer service. Their sales representatives are quick to respond and provide exceptional knowledge of the software platform, which adds to the overall positive user experience. In summary, DreamReport stands out for its robust integration capabilities, flexible automation options, and strong security compliance. While there are some limitations in terms of scalability and browserbased report creation, the platform's specialized functions, regular updates, and excellent customer service make it a compelling choice for organizations looking to enhance their data reporting and management capabilities. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 30 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

At this time, the plant processes, collection system pump stations, and stormwater stations have separate VTScada realms within one application on the same server. The City is considering implementing separate VTScada applications for each of these areas. HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvement projects will include some high-performance screens following ISA101 Design Standard. Workshops for operators will be held as part of each project. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 Key Concepts 3.1VTScada Development platform The VTScada development platform contains key concepts that need to be understood in order to plan, design, and implement an application. Key concepts for tag based configuration, tag widgets data acquisition, historian, configuration nodes, HMI clients, and development nodes are described below. 3.1.1 Tag Based Configuration Tags are software elements representing physical objects or concepts. Tags represent communications ports, device drivers, I/O elements for a device, historical logging of data, and alarms. The combination of various functional tags into larger units will create orderly, consistent structures that may then be directly utilized or combined together further with other functional elements. The underlying concept to the system is that everything is a tag. Whether a single atomic element or a collection of complex elements the end result is simply considered to be a tag. This in turn allows the tags to be referenced as a single item with all of the inheritted properties immediately available. 3.1.2 Tag Widgets Tag information is visualized to the operators utilizing widgets. These widgets may be indicators, push buttons, numeric values, numeric entries, gauges, variable fill, and more. The crucial point to this is that the visualization elements are very tightly coupled with the data elements. 3.1.3 Data Acquisition Data acquisition(DA) is to be configured as appropriate to the controller(s) being monitored. The preferred communications pathways are as follows: - For PCS or process: Ethernet/IP or Modbus TCP - For remote telemetry: DNP3 protocol over Ethernet HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Recommend Network Sequencing 3.1 Maintaining Monitoring and Reporting Once the P&IDs are developed and preliminary control descriptions are available, discussion on where new PLC panels will be located and how I/O will be connected to the new PLC modules will be made at milestones for each project. A detailed sequence on how to maintain monitoring and reporting will be developed as part of these discussions. Final network diagram will include fiber cable labeling. Labeling for the cables and for the ports at patch panels will be installed in the field to match the network diagram. Multiple cables run through the same conduits and manholes will be depicted on the network site plan. 3.3 Conversion to VTScada VTScada HMI programming will be developed and placed in service parallel to the existing HSQ HMI system on a separate network rack and made available for interface on new workstations throughout the plant, as well as on wireless devices. As a process is made available on the VTScada HMI and is installed, tested, and passed an approved reliability demonstration period, the associated HSQ HMI display screens could be removed or made view only. The goal will be to get each process on the VTScada HMI to use for setpoint adjustment, monitoring, and/or manual override. However, there will be some parameters that may impact separate processes still controlled only through HSQ. After commissioning activities have been completed, system shall run for 30 days in hands-off mode to document alarm rates for modified alarms. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 6 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Program Structure The City’s programs will be written utilizing LD, and FBD languages only. Any exceptions must be submitted for approval following the procedures contained in the City’s Standards Deviation Request Policy (Reference Volume 1, SCADA & PCS Standards Deviation Request). 6.1Ladder Logic LD is the preferred language when performing complex Boolean logic. This would include actions such as top-down Boolean logic, interlock development, sequencing equipment operations, or control of I/O elements. Function blocks(AOIs, FBs, FCs) may be utilized as part of the rung logic; however, this approach should be minimized. Block programming requirements often require support branches whose sole purpose is to port data into and out of the block. This approach results in rungs that are large and less comprehensible. The preferred approach in this case is to only use blocks that all of the parameters are supplied and visible; typical of internal instructions. Rungs shall be organized in a one concept per rung manner. Stringing logic across rungs through the use of intermediary bits is to be avoided as much as possible. Logic that becomes excessively large will require being broken into multiple rungs. In cases where logic is split, it is incumbent on the developer to provide clarity and explain logic flow. LD shall be limited in size to that which can be printed on an 11” x 17” sheet of paper, either portrait or landscape. LD is a suitable language for the construction of custom function blocks. The same programming constraints are to be applied in this case as well. 6.2Function Block Diagram FBD is preferred when working with blocks and process values and control. Operations such as analog/digital input mapping, PID control elements, stand-alone alarm handling, and totalization should be handled in this manner. It is possible to perform Boolean logic in FBD; however, this should be kept to a minimum. The density of information contained on the sheet should be as concise and legible as possible. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 30 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

Volume 2 addresses practices for development and programming of control processors such as: - Programmable logic controllers(PLCs) of various types Including requirements for programming software - Alarm processing - Control loops - Control logic structure - Input/output(I/O) layout and signal processing - Programming comments - Tagging and naming conventions. Volume 3 contains practices for development and programming of human-machine interface(HMI) and operator interface terminals(OITs). Standards for programming and display development, historian data exchange, and security are included in this volume. Appendix A Contains definitions and general references that provide clarity and detail to volumes 1 to 3 of the City of Tampa’s SCADA standards manuals. Appendix B Contains forms and documents needed for deviations and documentation of changes to the City’s PCS or SCADA systems. Compliance with the standards and conventions outlined in the manual is required for all projects that add to or modify the control system. If a contractor or staff member identifies a need for an addition or modification to the standards for a specific project, or if in the course of the work it is found that some part of the standards cannot be adhered to, a standards deviation request may be submitted in accordance with the requirements provided in Section 3 of Volume 1 and in Appendix B. This proactive approach not only secures the plant's operations but also aligns with regulatory standards and best practices for infrastructure management. This resilience is essential in safeguarding the plant's functionality and ensuring that operators can respond promptly to any issues that arise. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] 5.2.3 Alarm Rationalization Alarm rationalization is the process of reviewing, validating, and justifying the alarms that meet the criteria of a critical alarm. In other words, the rationalization specifies only those points in the process system that require alarming. The ultimate goal of alarm rationalization is to determine the most efficient number of alarms to ensure that the process system is safe and remains within normal operating range. Alarm rationalization must be accomplished in conjunction with the City at the preprogramming workshop. The basic process can be outlined as: - Check Alarm Validity - Determine the consequence of inaction - Document Cause, confirmation, and corrective action - Document operator response time - Assign alarm priority - Alarm classification - Determine alarm attributes - Address need for special handling All unused or deprecated alarms are to be removed from the system. Alarms that are no longer needed are not to be abandoned in place (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 Key Concepts 3.1VTScada Development platform The VTScada development platform contains key concepts that need to be understood in order to plan, design, and implement an application. Key concepts for tag based configuration, tag widgets data acquisition, historian, configuration nodes, HMI clients, and development nodes are described below. 3.1.1 Tag Based Configuration Tags are software elements representing physical objects or concepts. Tags represent communications ports, device drivers, I/O elements for a device, historical logging of data, and alarms. The combination of various functional tags into larger units will create orderly, consistent structures that may then be directly utilized or combined together further with other functional elements. The underlying concept to the system is that everything is a tag. Whether a single atomic element or a collection of complex elements the end result is simply considered to be a tag. This in turn allows the tags to be referenced as a single item with all of the inheritted properties immediately available. 3.1.2 Tag Widgets Tag information is visualized to the operators utilizing widgets. These widgets may be indicators, push buttons, numeric values, numeric entries, gauges, variable fill, and more. The crucial point to this is that the visualization elements are very tightly coupled with the data elements. 3.1.3 Data Acquisition Data acquisition(DA) is to be configured as appropriate to the controller(s) being monitored. The preferred communications pathways are as follows: - For PCS or process: Ethernet/IP or Modbus TCP - For remote telemetry: DNP3 protocol over Ethernet HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 February 2025 12 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 no alarming information will be generated. Scaling, beyond the conversion of integers to reals, is not to be employed. All failure states will be discussed with the City at pre-programming workshop to determine the proper course of action. Messages array bits are each mapped to a variable where variables 0XXX (where XXX is the unique variable number) are reserved for digital outputs, 1XXX (where XXX is the unique variable number) are reserved for digital inputs, and 4XXX (where XXX is the unique variable name) are reserved for analog inputs/outputs which are read/write blocks. 6.8.4 SCADA IO This is data and information that is being specifically marshalled for use by the SCADA system. These SCADA IO points shall be kept minimal, the basic ones that are provided and used are the simulation and disable IO points, some may be added if needed and should be documented accordingly. 6.9Data Structures Internal to most modern controllers is the concept of structured data. This is typically represented employing two methods, add-on instructions/function blocks and user data types. Each of these concepts offers opportunities to add clarity and reusability to the overall control program. User data types shall not be used for the development of new code. Only predefined add on instructions shall be used. 6.9.1 Function Blocks/Add-on Instructions For the purpose of this discussion these will be referred to as add-on instructions(AOIs) AOIs represent a unique collection of data and logic. The logic is customarily developed using the controller’s standard programming languages. The AOI also presents a usable interface to the logic through a series of parameters, both inputs and outputs. The AOI also can contain internal data registers that maintain the unique state of the instance of the AOI. The AOI in short is a complete, unique encapsulation of control data and logic. The primary benefit of the AOI is in its reusability. The advantage to the developer is that logic only has to be developed and tested once. Once proven, this logic can be reused countless times with the assurance that it will always function the same across all instances. This aids in the rapid development of entire systems. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 38 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 1. Introduction The Howard F. Curren Advanced Water Treatment Plant(HFC AWTP) owns and operates a Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) system currently composed mainly of General Electric(GE) PACSystems Rx3i and 90-30 series programmable logic controllers(PLCs) at the field level. General Electric was acquired by Emerson in February, 2019. This document refers to GE equipment as GE to maintain familiarity with City’s existing Standards. As manufacturers continue to make advancements in product development, older versions of hardware advance towards “legacy” status. Products in legacy status are taken out of production and typically are no longercommercially available from the manufacturer. Replacement parts must then be sourced from third party sellers, increasing the risk for price inflation and long lead times. In that case, a set of spares must be stringently maintained to prevent unnecessary down time in the event of a failure. Additionally, manufacturers eventually stop developing and releasing software updates for legacy controllers, which may pose a security risk on network connected systems. A majority of HFC AWTP’s PLC hardware has entered the legacy phase. While PLC upgrades may be costly up front, updating the PLCs reduces the risk of unplanned downtime and enhances network security. 2. Objectives A majority of the existing PLCs reached legacy status in 2018 (GE 90-30 and GE 90-70 series). PLC hardware for these products are no longer supported by the manufacturer. With the impending replacement of all PLC hardware as part of the on-going design-build project, the City has chosen to evaluate alternatives to develop a new standard for PLCs at the plant. Existing PLCs at the plant were evaluated by the City. As a result, Hazen and Sawyer(Hazen) was asked to review the existing PLC hardware to determine equivalent alternatives. Four major PLC vendors were evaluated for consideration whenever existing PLC line ups are to be replaced as part of on-going process improvements at HFC AWTP. The different PLC models were compared including features and budgetary costs for full system replacement. The findings are summarized in this PLC Hardware Evaluation along with a recommendation for the standard PLC hardware platform going forward. 3. Assessment of Existing PLCs & Industry Review 3.1 Existing PLCs An audit was performed by McKim & Creed & Hazen in June 2018 with assistance from HFC AWTP staff to quantify the number of existing PLCs installed at the facility. HFC AWTP staff provided information about the existing PLCs at these sites including existing network diagrams, model of PLC at each location, quantity, for review and use for this evaluation. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System PLC Hardware Evaluation 6 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 FBD sheets should be limited in size to that which can be printed on an 11” x 17” sheet of paper. 6.3Task and Program Files Tasks provide the broadest level of organization. They also provide a level of execution control, that being continuous, periodic, event amongst others. Only two types of tasks are to be employed at the City: continuous and periodic. All other types of task types will not be employed. 6.3.1 Periodic tasks Periodic tasks are designed to execute on a fixed period of time, typically in milliseconds. These tasks are conducive when working with a process or system that is slow, or requires the processing on a regular basis. Creation of periodic tasks should be kept to a minimum(six or less) as required by the controlled process or area. The periodic task execution rate should be set to allow ample time for program execution without burdening the processor. Programmers shall frequently monitor and adjust the execution rate based on the task time. All programs shall be commissioned with task settings tuned such that no overlaps exist in the program. During commissioning, the main program overlap counter shall be confirmed at zero and FAULTLOG object shall not have any minor faults for overlap. Each periodic task must be set with a priority from 1 to 15, with 1 representing the highest priority. It is recommended that each task be set with a unique priority. Fast Scan Task Parameter Setting Default rate 10ms Priority 1 Normal Scan Task Parameter Setting Default rate 250ms Priority 3 HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. In those cases, suitable suggested additions should be submitted to the City prior to use for consideration and approval. All tags will begin with this designation for the HFC AWTP. 4.1.2 Building Number This refers to the building the equipment is located in. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] 6.4Main or "0" Each program folder must contain a routine to be named "Main" or "0"; the language type specified for this routine should be LD. The purpose of the main routine is to govern the flow of the program. The jump subroutine(JSR) instructions shall be used to reference all other logical routines in the PLC program. No other logic is permitted in the main routine. The first JSR of the main routine shall be to the initialization subroutine conditioned on first scan flag from PLC. This will allow initialization logic to run each time the PLC returns to run after power cycle or other interruption. (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 2 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 HMI Software The SCADA software currently utilized by the City is VTScada 12.1.58, development environment from Trihedral Engineering, contact the City for version being used. This software suite forms the basis of the system, as well as providing the majority of the data acquisition drivers. All development of HMI screens and system wide visualization will utilize this software. All new HMI screens and graphical elements are required to be developed using the latest available City provided ChangeSet file. A ChangeSet file contains the instructions required to re-create an application. This is not an exact backup but a compendium of changes and the base information to reproduce a functional application. In general, ChangeSets are used to distribute new applications, distribute updates to an existing application, or add extra features to OEM layers. This ChangeSet file will contain all of the latest container tags, display definitions, and library widgets needed to support development activities. Each project shall request, via email to the City’s project manager (or assigned City Operational Technology(OT) contact), a copy of the current ChangeSet application immediately prior to initiating programming activities. Although updates to the standard may occasionally occur, the files provided on request date shall be utilized throughout the project. Only those updates which are determined mission critical (have severe consequences to operations, life or equipment safety) will be handled on a case by case basis through direct notice to programmers by the City’s project manager or assigned OT contact. The distribution ChangeSet file will be re-created periodically as functional elements are added and/or enhanced. Additionally, other exports will be created as required in order to maintain an appropriate level of application consistency between the City’s templates and any standalone in development. The City’s SCADA system is a managed VTScada application which has been developed using custom City developed library widgets. Refer to the City Standard Object Library Programming Guideline for details on the currently available object template library or submit an email request to the City’s project manager. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 February 2025 10 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] **2.2.2 Field Investigation** [ TABLE ] In order to verify the exact routing of the fiber network, site investigations were performed. The following observations were made during these investigations: - PLC 19 in the Main Pump Station is connected to the facility network rack directly over a CAT6 cable, fiber is not used. - There are eleven main trunk fiber cables landed at a fiber patch panel for process connections located at the Main Pump Station that then are connected to the facility network rack. - There are additional fiber cables landed at a separate fiber patch panel in the network rack room, however these fiber lines were installed by the City for various staff workstations and not part of the facility process fiber network. - The Red Zone was found to consist of the following fiber runs: - o One fiber cable that connects the network to PLC 11, PLC 12, PLC 13, and PLC10 as follows: - Patch panel near PLC 12 has a trunk cable back to the network rack patch panel - PLC 12 connects by fiber to the patch panel near PLC 12 - PLC 11 connects by fiber to the patch panel near PLC 12 - PLC 13 connects by fiber to a patch panel near PLC 13 which then connects to the patch panel near PLC 12 - PLC 10 connects by fiber to the patch panel near PLC 13 - o One fiber cable that connects the network to PLC 5 and PLC 39 as follows: - PLC 39 connects by fiber to a patch panel at PLC 5 - PLC 5 connects by fiber to the patch panel in PLC 5 panel HFC AWTP Master Plan **June 10,2022** Improvements: SCADA Design Criteria ![](_page_22_Picture_0.jpeg) ![](_page_22_Picture_1.jpeg) - New PLCs can connect directly to plant fiber network, they do not have to connect through a remote telemetry unit. - Uninterruptible power supplies shall be an APC UPS without ethernet connections and with a bypass switch for easy change out. Relays shall be used on UPS outputs to provide alarm when panel is on UPS power. - Panels shall be provided with twenty-precent(20%) spare capacity for I/O with ten-precent(10%) installed spare I/O. **Table 2-4: Standard Control Panel Components** (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 15)Physical access to control panels, wiring closets, server rooms, data centers, and control rooms shall be controlled, at a minimum, through a keyed lock. 16)Unused router and switch ports shall be normally turned off and turned on for configuration and testing purposes. 17)Remote access using the remote desktop protocol(RDP) without domain authentication, authorization, accounting, and encryption is prohibited. The use of remote control software (e.g., Dameware, VNC, RealVNC, TeamViewer, etc.) which does not support domain authentication, authorization, accounting, and strong encryption is prohibited. 18)Remote access shall be performed using laptops owned and configured by the City. Remote access shall utilize a VPN to first connect and authenticate the user to the business network. A second VPN shall be used to connect and authenticate the user to the process information network(PIN). Once the VPN connections are established, RDP shall be used to connect to a jump host(workstation) in the PIN. Then, a second RDP connection shall be made to an engineering workstation on the PCS. All RDP connections shall use strong authentication against an authentication, authorization, accounting(AAA) server and strong encryption. 19)Session controls shall monitor and automatically logout after 10 minutes of inactivity. 20)All software components that are not required for the operation and/or maintenance of the PCS/SCADA system shall be removed. If removal is not technically feasible, software not required for operation and/or maintenance shall be disabled. This removal shall not impede the function of the PCS/SCADA system. If software that is not required cannot be removed or disabled, document a specific explanation. 21)Disable all network ports, protocols, and services not required by the PCS/SCADA system. 22)The PCS/SCADA system networks shall be protected through network segmentation as follows: a. The PCS/SCADA system networks shall be isolated from external connections including the internet. This includes remote SCADA site communications. b. The business network and PCS/SCADA system networks shall be separate and direct communications between the two networks shall be denied. All traffic shall terminate in the PIN described below. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - Policies Volume 1 of 3 February 2025 27 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] The historical data from HSQ is to be saved and made available in the VTScada system. This shall be addressed in the Main Pump Station and HPO Reactor Improvements project currently under design. At a minimum, data starting from the beginning of 2000 shall be transferred. 2.3.2 PLC / HMI Software Integration The design team will coordinate with the process integrator and the City to develop the software. City staff will participate in actual development of graphics, tag creation, and programming to become more familiar with VTScada for future development and system troubleshooting. At the beginning of each construction phase, a pre-construction instrumentation coordination meeting will be held to review software development scope of work and to determine the role and responsibilities. The City staff will provide oversight of the HMI screen and PLC controls development. For the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvements Dewatering Facility Replacement project, RTP will be developing the HMI screens with oversight from the City. These displays will be used to set standards for displays to provide for all improvements. 2.3.3 HMI Security The following extent of exposure to internet connectivity will be incorporated into the SCADA design for each process improvement:  VPN connection for remote setup for vendor access to HMI when allowed by the City.  Similar to existing, access to HMI by City staff through handheld devices and laptops to connect through internet VPN over wireless cellular means. Previous efforts to create wireless hotspots proved to be inadequate at this site.  New controls at the PLC level on the fiber network shall be accessible through similar wireless internet VPN connectivity. 2.4 Site Security & Monitoring The existing plant camera system only includes ten(10) cameras. These are listed in Appendix G. With each improvement, design will include new cameras to eventually be able to monitor all areas within the plant. Location, number, and type of cameras will be discussed with City staff as part of the detail design phase of each HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvement. The existing SONY system is to be replaced with a GENETEC system, including a new server. The initial upgrade will occur during the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvements Dewatering Facility Replacement, which is concurrently under construction. All existing cameras will be transitioned to the new GENETEC system once it is operational. Entry gates will utilize GENETEC card reader system with interface to the new base system to allow monitoring of entry points, two-way voice communication for visitors, and remote operation of gates. Additionally, the City may replace the site perimeter fence with an eight-foot(8’) tall fence at some point in the future. - [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 2.5 Hardware 2.5.1 General Requirements All improvements shall adhere the following requirements:  All programmers, whether vendor or integrator, are to follow the same standards for hardware and software.  Provide computer workstations in key buildings as directed by the City to allow full control and monitoring of entire plant from each location. Existing computer workstations will not be replaced.  Provide independent vendor PLC hardware for individual equipment systems that interface with overall system PLCs provided by integrator. Signals to vendor supplied controls shall be hardwired for all control I/O. Supplemental information such as power usage or other diagnostics can be over Ethernet link from vendor supplied PLCs.  Provide a local control station at each piece of equipment(HOR, L/R, FOR, Start/Stop, etc) for local control with a means to lockout the equipment(lockout stop pushbutton) when in the off position. All such controls to be NEMA4X industrial grade and Square D 9001SK series.  Provide spare parts for CPUs, I/O Cards, Network Switches, fuses, and other components that are critical for operation or prone to wearing out.  Instrument specifications will be in data sheet format for specific make and model numbers rather than lengthy descriptions intended for “or equal” interpretation.  Electrical gear shall be provide with the manufacturer’s standard power monitoring devices.  Witnessed factory testing will not be required. Specifications will require onsite checkout with City staff followed by a 48-hour demonstration test.  Project specifications will require training for both operations and maintenance staff. Operation and maintenance manuals shall be required to be on hand for associated training. 2.5.2 Control Panels Control Panels shall be specified to be provided with the components outlined in Table 2-4, for panel components not listed in this table the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvements Dewatering Facility Replacement project will be used as a standard. Improvements shall include the following requirements:  Dual OITs will be provided on all panels with PLCs, this includes main process panels and individual equipment panels. Both OITs shall have full redundancy, generally one will be used for setpoints and the other for monitoring.  Within panels, CAT6 cable shall be shielded industrial cable and rated for 600V if exposed to 480V power.  Wiring within panels shall separate for different voltages and purposes. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 8 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 HMI Graphics Alarming 8.1Overview Alarm configuration requirements are contained in the City’s Alarm Management Strategy (Reference the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual, Volume 2). Any alarm modification, addition or deletion must follow alarm definition and rationalization procedures discussed in the alarm management strategy prior to implementation. It is important to note that although OIT and HMI system functions can be used to monitor and generate alarms, in the City’s PCS process alarms shall be generated in the PLCs which will be logged and displayed on the HMIs and OITs. HMI application shall not be configured to perform alarm calculations. HMI generated alarms will be utilized only for alarms and events initiated from within the HMI software such as software status alarms, loss of communications with a PLC and other system level alarming. Alarm limits must be adjustable through the HMI graphics screens; however, those alarm limits will be used by the PLC in determining if there is an alarm condition. The nature of the distributed PCS is such that the PLC-based process control logic can and will continue to operate, even if one or more HMIs become unavailable. As a result, the PLC must be able to determine alarm conditions and act accordingly without the need for continuous communication with the HMI system. The City’s Standard Object Library and add-on instruction(AOI) standard library objects shall be utilized for configuration of PLC and HMI. 8.2Nuisance Alarm Suppression Techniques All nuisance alarm suppression shall be by approved methods documented in the City’s Alarm Management Strategy (Reference the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual, Volume 2 for more details on alarm suppression techniques). Disabling equipment will disable the alarms associated with the equipment. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 February 2025 44 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

GE 90-30 series, as well as the GE 90-70 series, reached legacy status as of 2018. The Rx3i I/O modules are still in production but may reach legacy status 7 years after an End of Life will be announced. 3.3 Industry Reviews The GE product line used at the HFC AWTP is one of many PLC manufacturers in the United States and this product line has a significant share of the municipal wastewater industry. Two other prominent PLC manufacturers that have significant market share in the municipal wastewater include: - Rockwell(Allen Bradley - Logix series) - Schneider(Modicon - M540/580) The products of these manufacturers, along with those of GE(RX3i series), were selected as viable options to be considered for further evaluation in the PLC upgrades at the HFC AWTP as all three are widely known, utilized, and recognized in the municipal wastewater treatment market. The PLC evaluation performed here focused on PLC using a centralized architecture with a single processing unit and not Distributed Control Systems(DCS) such as Ovation, Foxboro and PlantPAX and thereby no reference to DCS is covered in this evaluation. According to the CONTROL 2023 Readers’ Choice Awards, as shown in the link here, (https://www.controlglobal.com/home/article/21546009/2023-readers-choice-awards) the survey rankings are presented below for these three product lines under the Water & Wastewater Processing category. GE and Rockwell products were consistently in the top 3 with Schneider being consistently listed in the top 10. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F.
