Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 16 4 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Naming Conventions Consistent naming across systems and controllers facilitates the City’s technicians’ efforts during troubleshooting. As such, a series of standards has been developed to address these needs. 4.1Tag Naming Conventions As data is generated by the various elements of the process, the easiest way to track things is through human decipherable tag names. The tags stand in the place of the somewhat arcane addressing and naming conventions that manufacturers use to address their points. This also results in a language construct that is regular and nonrepetitive. It is extremely important that the naming system yield unique names with a consistent format throughout the entire system. As such, the following naming convention is to be utilized throughout the entire City system. These mnemonics are to be applied to any and all elements that might be transferred from a controller to another system, i.e., SCADA, HMI, OIT, gateway, or controller. This system shall be utilized, but in a paired down manner, for internal registers and flags within a controller. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 17 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Figure 8 Tag Naming Convention PLC programming does not accept hyphens, so PLC tags must use underscores to be compatible with the program. The tagging standards shall remain the same in the PLC with the only difference being the use of underscores instead of hyphens. The figure below shows the tagging as it will be used inside the PLC program. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 18 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

These stations may be based on workstations, laptops, or mobile devices. As such additional consideration must be given to the functionality of the screens as the platform changes. All workstation-based HMI clients will be added to the configuration of the system. This allows for remote administration of the machines as well as the ability to push updates to the systems. Mobile clients, either portable or vehicle mounted, will be based on Windows 10 with cellular communications for data access. As with all systems these stations will be added to the system configuration for centralized management and application synchronization. 3.1.6 Deployment Nodes Deployment nodes are workstations on which new applications will be methodically deployed. These stations will have the system property “Automatically Deploy Local Changes” disabled. These stations accept newly developed/tested and approved ChangeSet files and provide a platform to methodically deploy the edits. Once loaded these changes will be “Accepted for Distribution” and the native application synchronization service will distribute the application to the configured nodes. 3.1.7 Historian The process value historian function will be conversely aligned with the primary and backup function of the data acquisition servers. In this manner the primary data acquisition server system will function as the backup historian server. All process value data storage files will be specified to reside in a subdirectory on a non-operating system drive partition under the name “.\Historian”. The alarm databases will also be located on the data acquisition servers in separate, native alarm database files. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 The physical data is required to be aggregated with the quality information into one cohesive unit with a new unique name. This point or tag name is what will be used in lieu of the physical I/O address and any/all of its quality Programmers shall organize all I/O, (local, networked and messaged) into four subroutines, _000_ANALOG_INPUT_MAPPING, _000_ANALOG_OUTPUT_MAPPING, _000_DIGITAL_IO_MAPPING, _000_DISCRETE_IO_MAPPING To clarify the above: - All Inputs must be mapped into variable objects with unique, symbolic tag names - In all cases input points must be used with quality information supplied. - All outputs will be directly controlled by unique, symbolic tag names. I/O mapping programs shall be organized within the normal scan periodic task. Program nomenclature shall follow the guideline provided below. The number and types shall be limited to those required for the project but will likely include at least some of these: - Analog_IO - Digital_IO - Message_IO - Network_IO - Modbus_IO Some controllers support the use of tag aliasing, which allows for the abstraction of complex input or output addressing into a single symbolic name. This feature is not to be employed in place of the mapping requirements outlined above. This methodology does not include the quality of the data in question. 6.8.1 Analog IO The configuration of the individual point type is to be performed in the module. Modules that support multiple types of input, such as 4-20mA and 0-10VDC must be set to the appropriate selection at the module. Any input module specific configurations, such as scaling and alarming are not to be utilized at the module level. All signal diagnostic capabilities are to be employed. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 8.3Commissioning Requirements Commissioning activities create abnormal alarm situations; this section contains requirements for alarm and event system during commissioning activities. 8.3.1 Operational Readiness Review Meetings Alarms and events risk review shall be reviewed at operational readiness meetings to determine alarm owner(contractor or operations) based on risk to process and processing equipment. 8.3.2 Alarms in Active Commissioning State Alarms and events which are not turned over to operations at operational readiness review are required to be suppressed by means of PLC logic suppression outside of testing hours to avoid presenting operators with nuisance and/or unknown alarms. 8.3.3 Alarm and Event Acceptance Matrices The City utilizes the defined average alarm rate of less than six alarms per hour period as defined in alarm management strategy in Volume 2 as maximum allowable alarm rate for acceptance of modifications to alarm system. After commissioning activities have been completed, system shall run for 30 days in hands-off mode to document alarm rates for modified alarms. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 7 SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 Recovery and Restore Policy 7.1General The City’s SCADA & PCS Recovery and Restore Policy is intended to establish requirements to promote efficient and accurate system restoration after a failure has occurred. The City’s SCADA & PCS consist of numerous highly integrated software packages, which undergo continual change for plant maintenance, operational improvements, and projects. This policy and procedure addresses requirements to promote known good system backups; and procedures to identify and utilize the backups and asset management systems during recovery. This policy applies to all City SCADA & PCS software assets. 7.2Backup Storage Requirements The City has deployed a SCADA & PCS Change Management server for version control and file storage for SCADA & PCS Software applications including PLC, and OIT network application files as well as HMI standard object templates. If the programmer does not have access to the City’s SCADA & PCS Change Management Server, then the programmer shall request a data transfer for the following: - HMI backup - HMI standard object templates - OIT standard object templates The following applications shall be stored in the SCADA & PCS change management server at initial installation and after any modification has been deployed in the production environment: - PLC program files including network configuration files - OIT application files - Network switch configuration files - Radio configuration files All contractors completing on-site startup/commissioning modifications to operating equipment are required to complete change documentation and upload files to SCADA & PCS Change Management Server at the end of each shift. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] During the installation of new fiber cables, the existing cables shall be left in place. It is recommended to perform field investigations during the design phases to verify new fiber cable can to installed utilizing the existing infrastruce. 2.3 HMI Software Conversion 2.3.1 VTScada The City is in the process of creating VTScada screens to replace the existing HSQ system. Currently, the City has implement monitoring only screens for some of the facilities through VTScada, this application is running in parallel the existing HSQ application. The following facilities have VTScada screens for monitoring only:  Junction Chamber #1  Screen and Grit Buildings  Primary Sedimentation Tanks  Digestion  STAR The following City preferences have been identified for implementing the HMI software conversion:  Migrate all monitoring and control capabilities to VTScada. All new HMI software applications and screens will be based on using VTScada.  Screens developed in VTScada are to be similar to existing screens, matching the layout of the P&IDs.  An Overview of each facility is to be provided that is similar to existing overview displays.  Control and monitoring of particular process areas within the facility shall be accessible by clicking on that area on the overview display to bring up the “P&ID” type display for the area.  Individual equipment control shall be accessible by clicking on that equipment to bring up dashboard style pop up control screens similar to existing.  During development of displays, City staff will be provided examples of high-performance graphic displays for their review to decide if such displays offer a better grasp of overview for each process area. At this time, the plant processes, collection system pump stations, and stormwater stations have separate VTScada realms within one application on the same server. The City is considering implementing separate VTScada applications for each of these areas. HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvement projects will include some high-performance screens following ISA101 Design Standard. Workshops for operators will be held as part of each project. Control descriptions will be developed prior to those workshops. High performance displays will be discussed at these workshops to decide design of such displays and if they are to be provided. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 5 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 Graphic Symbols and Object Library The ISA 101 HMI standard is an industry standard developed by the ISA 101 HMI committee which establishes standards, recommended best practices and guidelines pertaining to HMI interfaces in process automation applications. The ISA 101 HMI standard serves as a guideline to help organizations design, build and operate effective HMI applications. The City’s Standard Object Library incorporates recommendations from ISA 101. All new objects and displays shall follow the current ISA 101 recommendations at time of development to maintain system with latest industry standards. The City’s Standard Object Library provides a set of ready to use HMI objects for use in the development of HMI graphics. The object library makes it easy to create an interface that is more consistent in both appearance and function. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 4.1 PLC Comparison Factors: 4.1.1 Functionality w/ Ethernet An important function to consider for PLC evaluation is to review the impact on functionality of the PLC when more process equipment is added to the network to communicate with the PLC via any communication protocol. Evaluation is performed with Ethernet(widely used) considered as communication protocol between the various equipment such as VFDs, MCC, and vendor supplied control panels and if the functionality of the PLC would be stable. 4.1.2 Market Share This shows the size of a company, a useful metric in illustrating a company’s dominance and competitiveness in a given field. This factor is a key indicator of a company’s competitiveness, it can also be an indicator of growth, success, and stability. It is considered for the evaluation as key indicator in view of reliability of the PLC and dependability. 4.1.3 PLC Features This is a critical factor when looking at PLC selection criteria and its suitability for the intended application. Adequate number and type of I/O, High-speed processor, large memory capacity, multiple communication options, redundancy and safety features are key factors used for selection. 4.1.4 Support Technical support and service from the manufacturer or vendor is an important evaluation criterion. Reliable customer support, training resources, and a warranty or maintenance program along with local distribution network is key to ensure product availability and quick response in keeping systems in service. 4.1.5 Programmers Availability Local assistance and access to knowledge and resources are invaluable in ensuring PLC system's smooth operation, maintenance and troubleshooting. In any new construction effort, having multiple programmers employed by local integrators will help keep new construction on schedule and affordable. 4.1.6 Local Availability PLC components that are readily available, including spare parts in the event of equipment failure to minimize downtime and ensure that the control system is rapidly operational again, supply chain and lead times are key factors in selecting a PLC lineup. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System PLC Hardware Evaluation 10 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 3.1.12 HMI Integration The software should be easy to integrate into The City’s HMI - VTScada. This integration is crucial for a wastewater operator because VTScada is a widely used HMI system that provides realtime monitoring and control of plant operations. By ensuring compatibility with VTScada, the software allows operators to seamlessly access and manage all relevant data through a single interface, enhancing operational efficiency and reducing the risk of errors. This not only supports proactive maintenance and troubleshooting but also helps in ensuring regulatory compliance and optimizing plant performance. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. 3.1.13 Software support and maintenance The software should have support security updates and technical support. This is important for a wastewater operator because security is paramount in protecting sensitive data and ensuring the integrity of the system. Regular security updates help safeguard the system against potential cyber threats, vulnerabilities, and unauthorized access, which can compromise the operation of the plant. By having these updates included without extra charges, operators can maintain a high level of security consistently, ensuring continuous protection without worrying about additional expenses. This proactive approach not only secures the plant's operations but also aligns with regulatory standards and best practices for infrastructure management. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. 3.1.14 Redundancy The software should be able to be set up with a redundant system to allow for the use of the report creator even if the main server is taken offline. This is crucial for a wastewater operator because redundancy ensures continuous access to critical reporting tools, minimizing downtime and maintaining smooth operations. In the event of a system failure or maintenance, having a redundant setup means that data analysis can proceed without interruption, thus supporting timely decision-making and regulatory compliance. This resilience is essential in safeguarding the plant's functionality and ensuring that operators can respond promptly to any issues that arise. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. 3.1.15 Company prominence The software company should be a reputable company with good industry presence and expertise. This is important for a wastewater operator because a reputable company ensures that the software is reliable, secure, and compliant with industry standards. A company with a strong industry presence is more likely to be aware of the latest advancements and regulatory changes, ensuring that the software remains up-todate and effective in managing the complex requirements of wastewater treatment. Expertise in the field means that the company can provide high-quality support and tailored solutions, addressing specific operational challenges and improving overall plant performance. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 PCS/SCADA system networks (e.g., PIN, business network, remote access, dial-up modems, alarm notification systems, vendors, other municipalities, etc.). 12)Authentication System Inventory - Maintain an inventory of all authentication systems in use by the PCS/SCADA system (e.g., Active Directory, LDAP, RADIUS, TACACS+, etc.). Indicate where local authentication is used exclusively. 8.3Access control 1) Identities and credentials shall be centrally managed through domain level services for authorized devices and users. The authentication solution shall be dedicated to the PCS/SCADA system using a different set of identities and credentials than the business network. Authentication shall not be based on trusts or any other link with the business network. 2) A backup domain controller shall be maintained in a separate building from the primary domain controller. Both domain controllers shall be fed from separate power sources and back by separate UPSs. 3) All network devices shall use IEEE 802.1x for authentication to the network. 4) A role-based access control system shall be used throughout the PCS/SCADA System and shall be configured to operate using the principle of least privilege. This includes operating system permissions, file access, user accounts, service accounts, application-to-application communications, and component system services. Access permissions shall be centrally managed and limited to only the functionality needed to perform job functions associated with the account. 5) Default host accounts (e.g., guest, administrator, etc.) shall be removed. If the accounts are required for normal or emergency operation, they shall be recreated under a different name than the default account. Coordinate with the PCS/SCADA system software vendors. 6) Unique user IDs shall be used for authentication. The use of shared accounts is prohibited. 7) The PCS/SCADA system shall require identification and authentication before allowing any actions by a user. 8) Wherever it is possible to do so, PCS/SCADA system equipment (e.g., servers, workstations, laptops, etc.) shall, as part of the boot process, display to users a system use notification message or banner before granting access to the system that provides privacy and security notices which states that: a. Users are accessing a critical infrastructure information system HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - Policies Volume 1 of 3 February 2025 25 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

An industrial DMZ shall act as a buffer network(i.e., DMZ) between the business network and the PCS/SCADA system networks. i. The industrial DMZ shall be the only means of communications beyond the PCS/SCADA system domain. Other architectures (including the use of dual or multi-homed hosts) are prohibited. The use of dial-up modems is prohibited. The use of cellular routers is prohibited. ii. The PCS/SCADA system shall operate autonomously (industrial DMZ, other plant PCS, etc.) except for remote SCADA sites associated with the SCADA system. Remote SCADA sites shall operate autonomously with no dependency on SCADA communications; the only exception would be a site that must receive a monitoring signal from another site for control purposes (e.g., an elevated tank sends a level signal to pump station to control the level in the elevated tank). d. The industrial DMZ may, based on project scope, contain physical or virtual workstations and servers needed to facilitate data exchange with business network applications. Backup repository e. The industrial DMZ shall be configured to prevent host-to-host communications unless specifically required to meet operational requirements. In such cases, ports, protocols, and initiating direction shall be configured to minimize connectivity in accordance with least functionality and least privilege principles. f. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software DreamReport has the advantage of being NIST certified for cybersecurity. Wastewater treatment is a critical part of everyday life that the environment and many people’s lives depend on. Being certified for adequate cybersecurity standards ensures adequate safety and security is being followed. DreamReport also can meet 21 CFR part 11 compliance requirements, this makes the reports and findings trustworthy, reliable, and equivalent to paper records and handwritten signatures. DreamReport is extensively used in the life sciences industry due to this ability. Additionally, DreamReport can be integrated into HMI and PLCs, readily and easily with the large number of adapters that are available on the software platform. This can make it easy to integrate with the Plant’s current system and any potential changes that may happen in the long term. Having this flexibility is a significant advantage Disadvantages DreamReport has some disadvantages related to the size of Ocean Data Systems, the company that created the software platform. Although it is not the smallest company out of the set of companies that were reviewed it is a non-domestic company. Although this may not be a significant issue for the usability of the software it should be considered in the case that there are any restrictions on whether or not the City is allowed to operate software from non-domestic companies. DreamReport’s licensing structure for concurrent users is similar to that of the tag numbers but with a small amount of added flexibility with the licensing sizes. DreamReport has licenses that can be combined for the number of concurrent users. Meaning that if the plant requires for example 15 users a 10 user license and a 5 user license can be combined to meet the number of required licenses. This makes increasing the number of users incur an additional recurring cost that should be taken into account. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 20 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 7 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Standard Logic Descriptions 7.1Initialization and Non-Volatile Memory The controller maintains its program, data, and configuration through non-volatile memory(NVM). This is memory that can exist in several forms, battery backed up RAM, EEPROM, or compact flash device(SD card). This technology allows the processor to return to its previous state after being powered up. The controller must provide some type of non-volatile memory. At no time is it acceptable to rely on continuous power. The NVM must be capable of withstanding the same environmental conditions as the controller. The particular form-factor of the NVM is dependent on the manufacturer. The preferred types of NVM are those that do not rely on batteries, or other mechanical devices(such as hard disk drives). This memory shall also not be read-only but support re-writing using the native development tools. The process of loading the program after power loss must be inherent in the controller natively. At a minimum the non-volatile memory must return the control program to active RAM. The ability of returning retained variable data to its previous state is a function that is desirable. However, when the ability to return retained data values is not supported, the control code itself must account for this and suitable initialization and start-up logic. 7.2Peer-to-peer Communications Within the plant all messaging shall be performed over Ethernet. If Ethernet is not available to the device, alternatives may be employed with City approval. For remote locations the preferred platform is Cellular modems however, if cellular modems are not appropriate, with City approval, acceptable alternative platforms are serial radio modems, and satellite for extremely remote locations. In all cases, alternative peer-to-peer communications are to be submitted to the City for review and approval prior to implementation. All inter-processor communications will be performed utilizing native communication control elements. An example of this is to utilize the “MSG” instruction in a ControlLogix controller. All communications will be performed in a read-only manner. At no time is a data write, or push, allowed. This leaves the receiving end in a non-deterministic condition in which the receiving controller is unsure of all of the factors affecting communications. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 41 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 New software requirements must be submitted to the City with suitable lead time, a minimum of four weeks, for a security evaluation and loading. It is the developer’s responsibility to ensure all of the required tools are in place prior to beginning a start-up. General Programming Practices 3.1Languages The IEC 61131-3 standard includes for five languages: Ladder Logic(LD), Function Block Diagram(FBD), Instruction List, and Sequential Function Flowchart. The only allowable languages for all City projects and implementations are LD, and FBD. 3.1.1 Ladder Logic LD is a graphical, symbol-based language with a foundation in circuit diagrams and relay logic hardware. The logic is organized in a series of rungs composed of Boolean elements whose logical result is then applied to outputs or action elements. This can simplistically be considered to be a series of IF/THEN statements. The ladder rungs are scanned in a top-down, left to right manner enforcing strict order of execution. Figure 1 Ladder Logic HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 10 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Figure 3 Object Attribute Comments 3.4.2 Ladder Logic Comments Each LD rung must include a comment clearly describing its purpose and function. The first rung of each routine or section of rungs should summarize the overall function of the routine/section as well as the logic found on the rung. All rungs regardless of how simple or complex must include comments sufficient for someone who did not write the program to understand the function(s) of the rung. This includes all sub-rungs and subroutines. See example in Figure 4 Ladder rung comments. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 13 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Figure 4 Ladder Rung Comments 3.4.3 Function Block Diagram Comments FBD programming increases the readability of complex operations such as those with multiple paths based on Boolean functions(AND/OR/NOT) or selection blocks as well as intermediate calculations(sums and limiting functions). It is required that the programmer provide comments describing the overall function for each connected group of function blocks. Additionally, the programmer shall provide comments for each distinct intermediate calculation (Boolean function, selection, sum, limit, etc.). See example in Figure 5 Function block comments. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 14 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Figure 5 Function Block Comments 3.4.4 Subroutines Documentation Each subroutine of any type will be documented. A single “No Output” [NOP] instruction or unique coil designated as “Comment Coil” on the first rung of routine (see example in Figure 6 First rung NOP rung comments) shall be utilized to provide a location for required subroutine documentation. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 4) Network Design - Maintain drawings depicting PCS/SCADA system interconnections. Maintain the drawings in electronic and hardcopy formats. Electronic copies shall be maintained using the latest version of Microsoft Visio and PDF file formats 5) Physical Drawing - Maintain a Layer 2 drawing that depicts the physical interconnection of network hardware and hosts. Include location, cable type, cable standard, interface speed, host name, description, vendor, model number, MAC address, IP address, and subnet mask. The drawing shall include all components and subcomponents with a MAC address. 6) Logical Drawing - Maintain a Layer 3 drawing consistent with the zones & conduits model used in ISA/IEC 62443. The drawing shall depict the various zones (e.g., networks, subnets, VLANs, etc.) and conduits (e.g., Layer 3 switches, routers, firewalls, etc.) used to connect the zones together. The drawing shall include hosts grouped according to the zone in which they reside and the default gateway for each zone. The drawing shall include WAN links and supporting hardware. Include location, host name, description, vendor, model number, MAC address, IP address, subnet mask, and interface speed. The drawing shall include all components and subcomponents with an IP address. 7) IP Addressing Scheme - Maintain a spreadsheet that documents the IP addresses and related TCP/IP settings (e.g., default gateway, DNS servers, etc.) for all nodes with an IP address. Group the listing by subnet/VLAN. Maintain the addressing scheme for consistency with the City’s existing overall scheme to prevent conflicts with IP addresses used elsewhere in the City’s networks. 8) Ports/Applications/Services - Maintain a listing of services and applications required for each computer system in the PCS/SCADA system. The listing shall include all ports, applications, and services required for normal operation as well as any others required for emergency operation. Ensure an explanation exists to justify why each is necessary. 9) Firewall Rule Sets - Maintain documentation on firewalls and firewall rule sets required for normal and emergency operations. 10)Data Flow Diagram - Maintain a diagram depicting communications between software components. Maintain the diagram in MS Visio format. The diagram shall show communications related to all products listed in the device inventory and software inventory. Include ports, protocols, and components that initiate communication. Use typical depictions for similar communication links. 11)Network Boundary Inventory - Maintain an inventory of all network boundaries where communications is possible with other networks external to the HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Glossary Ethernet: A computer networking standard that defines a family of protocols, communication media, and device interfaces. Human Machine Interface(HMI): A computer that runs graphical interface software for monitoring and supervisory control of a control system. Local Area Network(LAN): Made up of the connection media, networking components, and nearby devices that communicate with each other. Remote Telemetry Unit: A controller for local equipment that communicates with the master telemetry unit. Uninterruptible Power Supply(UPS): A battery-backed source fed by the utility service during normal operation. Variable Frequency Drive(VFD): A motor control that allows for continuous variations in running speed. Virtual Private Network(VPN): A secure means of connecting a physically remote host to a local area network, typically via the Internet. VTScada: The software application used at the HFC AWTP to read and write real-time data to and from controllers. Win 411: A component of Win 911 that enables simple audio reporting of analog values. Win 911: A software application used to annunciate and acknowledge alarms to/from remote users. HFC AWTP Master Plan June 10,2022 Improvements: SCADA Design Criteria Technical Memorandum iv (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 15)Physical access to control panels, wiring closets, server rooms, data centers, and control rooms shall be controlled, at a minimum, through a keyed lock. 16)Unused router and switch ports shall be normally turned off and turned on for configuration and testing purposes. 17)Remote access using the remote desktop protocol(RDP) without domain authentication, authorization, accounting, and encryption is prohibited. The use of remote control software (e.g., Dameware, VNC, RealVNC, TeamViewer, etc.) which does not support domain authentication, authorization, accounting, and strong encryption is prohibited. 18)Remote access shall be performed using laptops owned and configured by the City. Remote access shall utilize a VPN to first connect and authenticate the user to the business network. A second VPN shall be used to connect and authenticate the user to the process information network(PIN). Once the VPN connections are established, RDP shall be used to connect to a jump host(workstation) in the PIN. Then, a second RDP connection shall be made to an engineering workstation on the PCS. All RDP connections shall use strong authentication against an authentication, authorization, accounting(AAA) server and strong encryption. 19)Session controls shall monitor and automatically logout after 10 minutes of inactivity. 20)All software components that are not required for the operation and/or maintenance of the PCS/SCADA system shall be removed. If removal is not technically feasible, software not required for operation and/or maintenance shall be disabled. This removal shall not impede the function of the PCS/SCADA system. If software that is not required cannot be removed or disabled, document a specific explanation. 21)Disable all network ports, protocols, and services not required by the PCS/SCADA system. 22)The PCS/SCADA system networks shall be protected through network segmentation as follows: a. The PCS/SCADA system networks shall be isolated from external connections including the internet. This includes remote SCADA site communications. b. The business network and PCS/SCADA system networks shall be separate and direct communications between the two networks shall be denied. All traffic shall terminate in the PIN described below. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 8 SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 Cybersecurity Policy This section contains cybersecurity requirements for all network components recommended by Hazen at a minimum, however the City shall employ the best practices to promote SCADA & PCS security. This is in addition to specific configuration requirements for integration into PCS/SCADA system networks covered in other sections. All persons are required to review and acknowledge the City’s policies before accessing the PCS/SCADA system. 8.1General 1) All servers, workstations laptops and other devices shall be managed by City policy. 2) The use of unencrypted communication(e.g. email, etc.) for PCS/SCADA system related correspondence which includes information related to cybersecurity and networking is prohibited. All electronically transmitted documents shall be password protected; the password shall be shared with the intended participants via alternate out of band communications channels(e.g. voice, text, etc.). 8.2Asset management 1) Work Management - PCS/SCADA system assets shall be entered into and maintained using the City’s asset management system. Additions, changes, and deletions shall be performed in the system within one week. 2) Device Inventory - Maintain an inventory of all physical devices throughout the PCS/SCADA system networks. Include all network nodes and communication- capable devices (e.g., servers, workstations, laptops, OIT, PLCs, PACs, switches, routers, firewalls, etc.). This inventory shall include all devices, whether connected to the PCS/SCADA system networks or not. Maintain the inventory in a spreadsheet. Include the supplier, manufacturer, model number, serial number, subnet, network address, hardware address, machine name, and installation location. 3) Software Inventory - Maintain an inventory of all software platforms and applications (e.g., operating systems, HMI applications, historians, OPC servers, programming software, IDE software, drivers, office productivity software, utilities, etc.). Maintain the inventory in a spreadsheet. Include supplier, product vendor, product name, product version, patching level, firmware version, licensing methods, and licensing keys. Maintain additional information as required to allow reinstallation of software after final acceptance. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - Policies Volume 1 of 3 February 2025 23 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

Report by exception methods are prohibited. Authentication shall not be based on trusts or any other link with the business network. 2) A backup domain controller shall be maintained in a separate building from the primary domain controller. Both domain controllers shall be fed from separate power sources and back by separate UPSs. 3) All network devices shall use IEEE 802.1x for authentication to the network. 4) A role-based access control system shall be used throughout the PCS/SCADA System and shall be configured to operate using the principle of least privilege. This includes operating system permissions, file access, user accounts, service accounts, application-to-application communications, and component system services. Access permissions shall be centrally managed and limited to only the functionality needed to perform job functions associated with the account. 5) Default host accounts (e.g., guest, administrator, etc.) shall be removed. If the accounts are required for normal or emergency operation, they shall be recreated under a different name than the default account. Coordinate with the PCS/SCADA system software vendors. 6) Unique user IDs shall be used for authentication. The use of shared accounts is prohibited. 7) The PCS/SCADA system shall require identification and authentication before allowing any actions by a user. 8) Wherever it is possible to do so, PCS/SCADA system equipment (e.g., servers, workstations, laptops, etc.) shall, as part of the boot process, display to users a system use notification message or banner before granting access to the system that provides privacy and security notices which states that: a. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 Communications are to be configured in VTScada using the appropriate driver and port tag constructs. The driver tag, specifically, must be appropriate to the communications protocol. Separate, stand-alone servers will be dedicated to data acquisition. There will be at least two servers configured for the system in the roles of primary and backup. This will provide redundancy for the purposes of monitoring the information from field devices and controllers. There are expected to be pairs of data acquisition servers for collections/distributions, waste water operations, and water operations. The data acquisition servers(referred to as default servers) will be added to the system configuration default server list as one, uniquely named device. Under this named configuration the server machines that will act as the primary and backup systems will be listed. The primary data acquisition machine being that system listed first and subsequent backups following in order. In this way the system allows for the growth and expansion of data acquisition clusters servicing the data needs for logical areas of operations as a whole. 3.1.4 Configuration Systems Configuration systems are workstations specifically designed for system configuration and contain the currently running configuration with the system property “Automatically Deploy Local Changes” disabled. These systems have been added to the system server lists under the “Configuration” category. All development and testing will utilize these stations at the City’s Operations Center. Upon successful testing and acceptance, the ChangeSet file will be transferred to deployment nodes. HMI clients are the portals through which the process is monitored and controlled by the operator. These stations may be based on workstations, laptops, or mobile devices. As such additional consideration must be given to the functionality of the screens as the platform changes. All workstation based HMI clients will be added to the configuration of the system. This allows for remote administration of the machines as well as the ability to push updates to the systems. Mobile clients, either portable or vehicle mounted, will be based on Windows 10 with cellular communications for data access. As with all systems these stations will be HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 13 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

The City’s SCADA & PCS consist of numerous highly integrated software packages, which undergo continual change for plant maintenance, operational improvements, and projects. This policy and procedure addresses requirements to promote known good system backups; and procedures to identify and utilize the backups and asset management systems during recovery. This policy applies to all City SCADA & PCS software assets. 7.2Backup Storage Requirements The City has deployed a SCADA & PCS Change Management server for version control and file storage for SCADA & PCS Software applications including PLC, and OIT network application files as well as HMI standard object templates. If the programmer does not have access to the City’s SCADA & PCS Change Management Server, then the programmer shall request a data transfer for the following: - HMI backup - HMI standard object templates - OIT standard object templates The following applications shall be stored in the SCADA & PCS change management server at initial installation and after any modification has been deployed in the production environment: - PLC program files including network configuration files - OIT application files - Network switch configuration files - Radio configuration files All contractors completing on-site startup/commissioning modifications to operating equipment are required to complete change documentation and upload files to SCADA & PCS Change Management Server at the end of each shift. These stations may be based on workstations, laptops, or mobile devices. As such additional consideration must be given to the functionality of the screens as the platform changes. All workstation-based HMI clients will be added to the configuration of the system. This allows for remote administration of the machines as well as the ability to push updates to the systems. Mobile clients, either portable or vehicle mounted, will be based on Windows 10 with cellular communications for data access. As with all systems these stations will be added to the system configuration for centralized management and application synchronization. 3.1.6 Deployment Nodes Deployment nodes are workstations on which new applications will be methodically deployed. These stations will have the system property “Automatically Deploy Local Changes” disabled. These stations accept newly developed/tested and approved ChangeSet files and provide a platform to methodically deploy the edits. Once loaded these changes will be “Accepted for Distribution” and the native application synchronization service will distribute the application to the configured nodes. 3.1.7 Historian The process value historian function will be conversely aligned with the primary and backup function of the data acquisition servers. In this manner the primary data acquisition server system will function as the backup historian server. All process value data storage files will be specified to reside in a subdirectory on a non-operating system drive partition under the name “.\Historian”. The alarm databases will also be located on the data acquisition servers in separate, native alarm database files. The alarm database historian server files will be specified to HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 February 2025 14 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 7.3DNP3 Exception Configuration The communication protocol between remote controllers and the supervisory(SCADA) system will be based on the DNP3 protocol as specified by DNP.ORG. This protocol shall be only used on remote sites. The transport layer for communications is to be appropriate for the application, e.g., LAN, serial modems, etc. No proprietary protocols are to be implemented. The master will be configured to perform its polling utilizing events(exceptions). Only periodically will static values be requested to insure validity and quality. The definition of what constitutes an event are dependent on the nature of the data point and acceptable excursion limits. All events will include a timestamp to facilitate sequence of event generation. The data is to be formatted and marshalled to facilitate optimal polling. Data being read from LAN based sources will by nature support a larger number of points being read than those from serial based devices. As such the data being sent must have the event thresholds and organization considered prior to implementation. Due to the potential communications loss events that can occur to some remote stations the concept of store & forward will be fully implemented. Therefore, all controllers must support this method of operation. This functionality may be supplied either natively or by the use of in-chassis interface modules. Each device is to be configured to maintain the data as long as possible, this is heavily dependent on the device design. Standard polling of remote locations by the SCADA system is to be configured such that the entire store & forward buffer may be read. This is much more critical when communicating to devices utilizing serial or cellular modems where outstations are typically read in a round-robin approach. In these cases, acquiring the data and storing the data into the historian in chronological order is critical. Stations that have a higher probability of continuous connection may be configured for unsolicited messaging. This feature is to be used sparingly and only for critical events. This feature will not be supportable in systems employing round-robin polling. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 8.6Information Protection Processes and Procedures Maintain a patch management system to ensure that PCS/SCADA system production operating systems and third-party software are running the most recent security updates provided by the software vendors. Maintain a patch management server in the PIN. Work with the City’s information technology department to maintain a secure method of automatically updating the patch management server in the industrial DMZ using the City’s business network and internet connection. Maintain an anti-virus definition update system to ensure that PCS/SCADA system production operating systems are using the latest anti-virus definitions. Maintain an antivirus update server in the PIN. Work with the City’s information technology department to implement a secure method of automatically updating the anti-virus server in the industrial DMZ using the City’s business network and internet connection. 8.7Maintenance Maintain three laptops dedicated to onsite PCS/SCADA system development. Ensure the laptops are secured in accordance with relevant host requirements elsewhere in policies, standards, guidelines, and procedures. The use of any other laptops on the SCADA and PCS is prohibited. Configure the laptops with fixed IP addresses, subnet masks, default gateways, and DNS servers to eliminate the need for DHCP. On a monthly basis, run vulnerability scans on servers, workstation, thin clients, and laptops. The scans shall be performed without an internet connection. Ensure the approach compares the devices to the newest available patches. Install important and critical patches on the systems in accordance with all PCS/SCADA system software vendor recommendations. In a test environment, verify patches will not have an undesirable effect on the PCS/SCADA system production environment prior to deployment. Use operating system restore points to provide the ability to restore systems to pre-patch levels. Subscribe to vulnerability alert systems including ICS-CERT, US-CERT, and WaterISAC. Monitor correspondence from these sources for alerts related to hardware and software provided under this project. Patch affected hardware/software as soon as possible and in accordance with other parts of policies, standards, guidelines, and procedures. 8.8Protective Technology 1) The use of open source software is prohibited. 2) Uninstall all browser and email client software on hosts. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation Communication protocols listed here have been updated based on the review comments from the City and intentions to use Rockwell PLC. The protocols are in the order of priority and also based on the availability of from the equipment supplier. These communication protocols shall be implemented on all future projects within this facility. a. General - Ethernet/ IP, PROFINET, MODBUS TCP/ IP, PROFIBUS DP/PA (depending on number of devices on the network), Modbus RTU, HART, Traditional I/O(DI, DO, AI & AO). b. Instruments - Ethernet/ IP, PROFINET, Modbus TCP/IP, ProfiBUS DP/PA, Hart, Traditional I/O. c. VFDs - Traditional I/O for Critical signals and control and for status signals use Ethernet/ IP, PROFINET, Modbus TCP/IP d. Power Monitoring - Ethernet/ IP, PROFINET, Modbus TCP/IP e. Digital Communication protocols - Ethernet/ IP, PROFINET, Modbus TCP/IP 4.4 PLC Manufacturer Comparison The following section compares the relative advantages and disadvantages of each manufacturer considered in this evaluation. 4.4.1 Allen-Bradley Advantages Allen-Bradley is one of the top two manufacturers of PLC equipment in the Southeast market. Their products are widely used in water and wastewater facilities and are typically familiar to most maintenance personnel. The PLCs have the capacity to operate in ladder logic, structured text, and functional block diagram. Their software also abides by IEC 61131-3 standards, the standard most commonly used when looking at PLC software. Their products were ranked second in reader satisfaction of the four manufacturers considered(as listed in paragraph 5.1) Disadvantages While A-B equipment is well known for its powerful processing systems, lack of coordination between hardware, software, firmware and proper programming can sometimes result in a “buggy” feel. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 4.1 PLC Comparison Factors: 4.1.1 Functionality w/ Ethernet An important function to consider for PLC evaluation is to review the impact on functionality of the PLC when more process equipment is added to the network to communicate with the PLC via any communication protocol. Evaluation is performed with Ethernet(widely used) considered as communication protocol between the various equipment such as VFDs, MCC, and vendor supplied control panels and if the functionality of the PLC would be stable. 4.1.2 Market Share This shows the size of a company, a useful metric in illustrating a company’s dominance and competitiveness in a given field. This factor is a key indicator of a company’s competitiveness, it can also be an indicator of growth, success, and stability. It is considered for the evaluation as key indicator in view of reliability of the PLC and dependability. 4.1.3 PLC Features This is a critical factor when looking at PLC selection criteria and its suitability for the intended application. Adequate number and type of I/O, High-speed processor, large memory capacity, multiple communication options, redundancy and safety features are key factors used for selection. 4.1.4 Support Technical support and service from the manufacturer or vendor is an important evaluation criterion. Reliable customer support, training resources, and a warranty or maintenance program along with local distribution network is key to ensure product availability and quick response in keeping systems in service. 4.1.5 Programmers Availability Local assistance and access to knowledge and resources are invaluable in ensuring PLC system's smooth operation, maintenance and troubleshooting. In any new construction effort, having multiple programmers employed by local integrators will help keep new construction on schedule and affordable. 4.1.6 Local Availability PLC components that are readily available, including spare parts in the event of equipment failure to minimize downtime and ensure that the control system is rapidly operational again, supply chain and lead times are key factors in selecting a PLC lineup. - [Electrical / Power / Control Panels] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Glossary Ethernet: A computer networking standard that defines a family of protocols, communication media, and device interfaces. Human Machine Interface(HMI): A computer that runs graphical interface software for monitoring and supervisory control of a control system. Local Area Network(LAN): Made up of the connection media, networking components, and nearby devices that communicate with each other. Remote Telemetry Unit: A controller for local equipment that communicates with the master telemetry unit. Uninterruptible Power Supply(UPS): A battery-backed source fed by the utility service during normal operation. Variable Frequency Drive(VFD): A motor control that allows for continuous variations in running speed. Virtual Private Network(VPN): A secure means of connecting a physically remote host to a local area network, typically via the Internet. VTScada: The software application used at the HFC AWTP to read and write real-time data to and from controllers. Win 411: A component of Win 911 that enables simple audio reporting of analog values. Win 911: A software application used to annunciate and acknowledge alarms to/from remote users. Existing computer workstations will not be replaced.  Provide independent vendor PLC hardware for individual equipment systems that interface with overall system PLCs provided by integrator. - [Electrical / Power / Control Panels] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 4.1.13 Maintenance PLC requires routine maintenance and upkeeping, software upgrades and version controls are necessary to mitigate PLC downtime. PLCs with tools to manage software version, notification feature and license upgrades are ranked based on the available options. 4.1.14 Disaster Recovery PLCs hard drives occasionally are inoperable for a variety of reasons, such as power quality event impacting the central processing unit(CPU). A PLC with ability to hold program within a USB or SD card or a monitoring tool to provide PLC health condition is an important factor used for evaluation. 4.1.15 PLC change management PLCs program modifications are necessary when operational changes occur. The ability of the PLC to monitor and store the user actions is helpful in change management. PLC evaluation is based on the best tools available within the PLC to monitor user actions. 4.1.16 SCADA Software version control A PLC monitoring the HMI and ease of auditing for program management is a useful feature. PLCs with a cyber security plan are ranked top in this category. 4.1.17 PLC Size Choosing a PLC supplier who offers a diverse choice of PLCs will benefit as each process within the HFC AWTP requires various number of monitoring and control I/O. Availability of wide range of size selection within each PLC supplier is considered for evaluation. 4.1.18 PLC Security and Recovery HFC AWTP safety is a critical consideration. PLC suppliers that offer integrated safety features or compatibility with safety modules are recommended. These features include safety inputs, relay outputs, and the ability to implement safety functions according to relevant safety standards. Compliance with safety requirements adds a layer of protection to the HFC AWTP and is an important factor for evaluation. 4.1.19 Ease of Integration with equipment PLCs with predeveloped custom function blocks between instruments and PLC CPUs, VFDs and MCCs are crucial for ease of integration. Rankings are based on ease of integration with major equipment used at HFC AWTP. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. - [Deliverables / Submittals / Documentation] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 4) Network Design - Maintain drawings depicting PCS/SCADA system interconnections. Maintain the drawings in electronic and hardcopy formats. Electronic copies shall be maintained using the latest version of Microsoft Visio and PDF file formats 5) Physical Drawing - Maintain a Layer 2 drawing that depicts the physical interconnection of network hardware and hosts. Include location, cable type, cable standard, interface speed, host name, description, vendor, model number, MAC address, IP address, and subnet mask. The drawing shall include all components and subcomponents with a MAC address. 6) Logical Drawing - Maintain a Layer 3 drawing consistent with the zones & conduits model used in ISA/IEC 62443. The drawing shall depict the various zones (e.g., networks, subnets, VLANs, etc.) and conduits (e.g., Layer 3 switches, routers, firewalls, etc.) used to connect the zones together. The drawing shall include hosts grouped according to the zone in which they reside and the default gateway for each zone. The drawing shall include WAN links and supporting hardware. Include location, host name, description, vendor, model number, MAC address, IP address, subnet mask, and interface speed. The drawing shall include all components and subcomponents with an IP address. 7) IP Addressing Scheme - Maintain a spreadsheet that documents the IP addresses and related TCP/IP settings (e.g., default gateway, DNS servers, etc.) for all nodes with an IP address. Group the listing by subnet/VLAN. Maintain the addressing scheme for consistency with the City’s existing overall scheme to prevent conflicts with IP addresses used elsewhere in the City’s networks. 8) Ports/Applications/Services - Maintain a listing of services and applications required for each computer system in the PCS/SCADA system. The listing shall include all ports, applications, and services required for normal operation as well as any others required for emergency operation. Ensure an explanation exists to justify why each is necessary. 9) Firewall Rule Sets - Maintain documentation on firewalls and firewall rule sets required for normal and emergency operations. 10)Data Flow Diagram - Maintain a diagram depicting communications between software components. Maintain the diagram in MS Visio format. The diagram shall show communications related to all products listed in the device inventory and software inventory. Include ports, protocols, and components that initiate communication. Use typical depictions for similar communication links. 11)Network Boundary Inventory - Maintain an inventory of all network boundaries where communications is possible with other networks external to the HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. - [Testing / Startup / Commissioning] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 6 SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 Management of Change 6.1Overview The City’s Management of Change Policy is intended to establish requirements for SCADA & PCS change management policies and procedures. The MOC policy and procedure addresses requirements for change management including review/approval process, documentation and usage of the SCADA & PCS Change Management Server. This policy applies to all modifications of City SCADA & PCS assets including but not limited to HMIs, OITs, historians, network equipment, PLC, and radios. 6.2Responsibilities To ensure the MOC policy is implemented and maintained in accordance with the procedures listed in this policy, the identified City Employees and contractors have the responsibilities identified in Section 2. 6.3Management of Change Approval Requirements All modifications to the control system shall be reviewed and approved prior to deployment on the live production system. A change request is also referred to as an MOC request throughout this document. A change request shall be submitted to the City for approval utilizing the SCADA & PCS Change Request Form provided in Appendix B. Change requests shall include the following: 1) Description of change 2) Location of change (e.g., PLC name, subroutine and rungs, HMI screen name, etc.) 3) Name, company name, and contact phone number for person implementing change 4) Copy of completed test plan from the City’s Test Environment 5) Copy of proposed field test verification plan 6) Back-out plan including details on how to revert to a previous known good installation. No change shall be implemented without written approval from the City. The City’s OT contact is responsible for assigning appropriate approval authority and coordinating with City operations. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3 SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 SCADA & PCS Standards Deviation Request 3.1General The City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Convention Manual is intended to provide consistent, reliable controls within all plants. However, it is recognized there may be specific instances in which a contractor or supplier may not be able to fully comply with the standards for software, equipment, cabling and other control system components supplied or developed under a contract or other procurement method or may not be able to program or configure the system in conformance with these standards. When these cases arise, the contractor or supplier shall submit a request for deviation from the City’s standards using the Standards Deviation Request Form included in Appendix B. The City will review the issues documented on the form and any supplemental documentation that is supplied with the request, and if compelling reasons are presented, the City may grant the deviation. The standards deviation request shall meet the following criteria: 1) It shall clearly indicate those sections of the standards relevant to the proposed deviation(s). 2) It shall verify that compliance with the standards form which the deviation(s) is requested is, under the circumstances, not feasible. 3) It shall include a specific description of the proposed alternative to the requirements in the City’s standards and include supporting documentation. Documentation may include references demonstrating successful use by other public utilities. 3.2Procedure The process to request a deviation from the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions is as follows: 1) Fill out and submit the Standards Deviation Request Form. The form shall be filled out electronically and submitted via e-mail to the City’s designated project manager or other designated contact. The city will supply the blank electronic form on request. A copy of the form is included in Appendix B. 2) Transmit supporting documentation securely through City approved transmittal methods if file contents are sensitive such as VTS changesets, PLC programs, etc. Drawings, mark-ups, photographs, sketches, or vendor-supplied product information with the deviation request e-mail. All setpoints will be supplied in engineering units. 5.1.2.2 Toggling Alarms Alarms cannot be individually enabled/disabled. Equipment must be disabled in order to disable the alarms associated to that piece of equipment. Otherwise, all the equipment’s associated alarms will remain enabled. 5.1.2.3 Debounce Times Debounce times are the period(in mSec) for which the signal must be in excess of the indicated setpoint limit before the alarm is generated. Setting a value to zero eliminates the debounce function completely. Alarms are currently all set to have debounce times at zero 5.1.2.4 Dead-Band Amount The dead-band amount is used to eliminate the chattering of alarms around the setpoint by requiring the process variable to retreat from the setpoint by a set amount before the alarm resets. A dead-band setting is applied to all limit alarms monitoring a process value. The configuration parameter is applicable to analog alarms only. The amount is supplied in engineering units with a value of zero deactivating the feature. Alarms are currently all set to have debounce times at zero. 5.1.2.5 Shelving or Bypassing The ability to bypass, or shelve, an alarm for a specified period will not be provided. Alarms cannot be bypassed or shelved; alarms can only be disabled. Disabling alarms is done through disabling the associated equipment. 5.1.3 Alarm conditions All alarm conditions are expected to apply positive logic. When the alarm value is a Boolean 1/true/on then the alarm is to be considered present/active. When the alarm value is a Boolean 0/false/off then the alarm is considered to be not present/inactive. 5.2Definition and Rationalization 5.2.1 Analog Alarms The following are available alarm conditions for analog values. These will support the basic typical requirements. Condition Description Low-Low(LL) The current scaled value is below the configured Low-Low setpoint(value < Low-Low SP) Low(L) The current scaled value is below the configured HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. In order to provide a consistent color pallet throughout the application colors have been defined for use. Refer to Appendix A, Definitions and General Reference, for a listing of those colors approved for use. The colors have been defined using the standard RGB color system. The color name is as defined by Microsoft along with the color values in red, green, and blue and hex code. Throughout this document, where specific colors are required, these color names will be used. The only times the full color spectrum is allowed is on map based screens and those places where photographs are utilized. 7.2Process Values Displaying live process values will be handled differently than static text. Values depicted from the controller will be in bold, blue. This is in contrast to the units of measure that will be indicated in non- bold, black either near the process value or not at all in the case of bar graphs or other animated elements. Leading zero’s(0) will not be utilized except to indicate a fraction(e.g., 0.12). When dynamic values are being used in a column generally align the values on the decimal point. In mixed cases, floating point values and integers, the values are to be aligned to the implied decimal point. In integer only cases the values are to be right aligned. Where applicable and feasible, the process value will indicate its quality. This will be accomplished by including a small indication adjacent to the value indicating “BAD”. This shall be highlighted with a white background to improve legibility. In the event that the value is currently under simulation, that is a manual value being driven by the operator, the indication “SIM” will be used in place of “BAD”. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Communications to remote controllers will utilize comprehensive fault monitoring of the status. The message execution complete and error results will be monitored and be utilized as data quality status. Additionally, the data being transferred will contain a watchdog register with each message packet. This value will be used to drive a timer which indicates the information being read from the foreign controller is valid. At the time of development, the failure modes for the communicated signals and the local process. Each signal must be evaluated for Fail-Last, Fail-to-Zero, etc. This will be performed in conjunction with the City. All failure mode determination and conditioning is to take place in the communications handler. Data that is being supplied as status inputs to remote controllers will be marshalled in routines following naming conventions outlined above. The data will be passed in arrays of Integers(INT) or double integers(DINT), whichever is the native integer format of the controllers. Structures of data utilizing UDTs are not permitted. Structures do not accommodate last minute changes without requiring both controllers to be redownloaded. Additionally, some devices might not support structured data beyond arrays. The first element in the transmission array will always be the clock time in seconds and milliseconds. This value is the watchdog timing element for the receiving end. A seconds-based number is suitable as communications polling periods should not exceed five seconds(0.2 Hz). Subsequent elements of the array are to contain the data values. It is understood that not all data exists as integers. In the case of Boolean data that may be transferred in a one bit per word or may be packed with multiple bits into one word. In the case of reals(floating point) these are to be multiplied by a suitable factor of 10 to fit within the integer value space. The multiplicative factor needs to consider the significant digits of the value in question. In example, a value of 43.56 would be transmitted as 4356. In all cases with packed array of data documentation is critical. All of the data that is being transmitted must be clearly defined and detailed. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 42 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 4.1 PLC Comparison Factors: 4.1.1 Functionality w/ Ethernet An important function to consider for PLC evaluation is to review the impact on functionality of the PLC when more process equipment is added to the network to communicate with the PLC via any communication protocol. Evaluation is performed with Ethernet(widely used) considered as communication protocol between the various equipment such as VFDs, MCC, and vendor supplied control panels and if the functionality of the PLC would be stable. 4.1.2 Market Share This shows the size of a company, a useful metric in illustrating a company’s dominance and competitiveness in a given field. This factor is a key indicator of a company’s competitiveness, it can also be an indicator of growth, success, and stability. It is considered for the evaluation as key indicator in view of reliability of the PLC and dependability. 4.1.3 PLC Features This is a critical factor when looking at PLC selection criteria and its suitability for the intended application. Adequate number and type of I/O, High-speed processor, large memory capacity, multiple communication options, redundancy and safety features are key factors used for selection. 4.1.4 Support Technical support and service from the manufacturer or vendor is an important evaluation criterion. Reliable customer support, training resources, and a warranty or maintenance program along with local distribution network is key to ensure product availability and quick response in keeping systems in service. 4.1.5 Programmers Availability Local assistance and access to knowledge and resources are invaluable in ensuring PLC system's smooth operation, maintenance and troubleshooting. In any new construction effort, having multiple programmers employed by local integrators will help keep new construction on schedule and affordable. 4.1.6 Local Availability PLC components that are readily available, including spare parts in the event of equipment failure to minimize downtime and ensure that the control system is rapidly operational again, supply chain and lead times are key factors in selecting a PLC lineup. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] 6.3.1 Periodic tasks Periodic tasks are designed to execute on a fixed period of time, typically in milliseconds. These tasks are conducive when working with a process or system that is slow, or requires the processing on a regular basis. Creation of periodic tasks should be kept to a minimum(six or less) as required by the controlled process or area. The periodic task execution rate should be set to allow ample time for program execution without burdening the processor. Programmers shall frequently monitor and adjust the execution rate based on the task time. All programs shall be commissioned with task settings tuned such that no overlaps exist in the program. During commissioning, the main program overlap counter shall be confirmed at zero and FAULTLOG object shall not have any minor faults for overlap. Each periodic task must be set with a priority from 1 to 15, with 1 representing the highest priority. It is recommended that each task be set with a unique priority. (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 8 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 HMI Graphics Alarming 8.1Overview Alarm configuration requirements are contained in the City’s Alarm Management Strategy (Reference the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual, Volume 2). Any alarm modification, addition or deletion must follow alarm definition and rationalization procedures discussed in the alarm management strategy prior to implementation. It is important to note that although OIT and HMI system functions can be used to monitor and generate alarms, in the City’s PCS process alarms shall be generated in the PLCs which will be logged and displayed on the HMIs and OITs. HMI application shall not be configured to perform alarm calculations. HMI generated alarms will be utilized only for alarms and events initiated from within the HMI software such as software status alarms, loss of communications with a PLC and other system level alarming. Alarm limits must be adjustable through the HMI graphics screens; however, those alarm limits will be used by the PLC in determining if there is an alarm condition. The nature of the distributed PCS is such that the PLC-based process control logic can and will continue to operate, even if one or more HMIs become unavailable. As a result, the PLC must be able to determine alarm conditions and act accordingly without the need for continuous communication with the HMI system. The City’s Standard Object Library and add-on instruction(AOI) standard library objects shall be utilized for configuration of PLC and HMI. 8.2Nuisance Alarm Suppression Techniques All nuisance alarm suppression shall be by approved methods documented in the City’s Alarm Management Strategy (Reference the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual, Volume 2 for more details on alarm suppression techniques). Disabling equipment will disable the alarms associated with the equipment. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 o AC = Reference to the type of card, AC indicates voltage type. o DI = Reference to type of input, DI indicates discrete input. o MOD-04 = Indicates the module number. 4.5Tasks This section describes the various objects available for the organization of program logic within the controller and their associated parameters. The section provides guidelines for selection and organization of logic into these objects and configuration of their properties. Three tasks are used, fast, normal, and slow. The tasks are typically reserved for message communications with other devices. The fast scan task has the highest priority of 1, the normal scan task has a priority of 3, being used for all regular monitoring and control operations. The slow scan task has a priority of 5, being reserved for processor monitoring. Each task shall contain a single program within itself and program name shall follow a similar naming as the task. 4.5.1 Task Structure Tasks are the highest organizational unit in the controller and dictate the order of execution. The nomenclature applied to tasks must represent the broadest definition of the contents. 4.5.2 Program Structure Programs represent the middle tier of the organizational structure. At this layer there is more additional granularity added to the overall structure and the naming conventions must reflect that. Naming at this layer must not overlap with the layer above. For example: - P01_Fast Program - P02_Normal Program - P03_Slow Program Individual names at this tier may be duplicated in other tasks but must be unique per task. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 24 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 4.5.3 Routine Structure This is the lowest tier of programming structure. As such the name must provide clarity into the exact programming construct. Each routine is linked to a process area for fast identification and each process area has 3 subroutines associated, except for IO subroutines that are 4. For Example, - _000_ is associated with IO Mapping. o _000_ANALOG_INPUT_MAPPING o _000_ANALOG_OUTPUT_MAPPING o _000_DIGITAL_IO_MAPPING o _000_DISCRETE_IO_MAPPING - _010_Main_PUMP_STATION_ALM - _010_Main_PUMP_STATION_ASSETS - _010 Main_PUMP_STATION_CONTROLS HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. General Programming Practices 3.1Languages The IEC 61131-3 standard includes for five languages: Ladder Logic(LD), Function Block Diagram(FBD), Instruction List, and Sequential Function Flowchart. The only allowable languages for all City projects and implementations are LD, and FBD. 3.1.1 Ladder Logic LD is a graphical, symbol-based language with a foundation in circuit diagrams and relay logic hardware. The logic is organized in a series of rungs composed of Boolean elements whose logical result is then applied to outputs or action elements. This can simplistically be considered to be a series of IF/THEN statements. The ladder rungs are scanned in a top-down, left to right manner enforcing strict order of execution. Figure 1 Ladder Logic HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 10 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

A dead-band setting is applied to all limit alarms monitoring a process value. The configuration parameter is applicable to analog alarms only. The amount is supplied in engineering units with a value of zero deactivating the feature. Alarms are currently all set to have debounce times at zero. (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
