- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] 5.2.3 Alarm Rationalization Alarm rationalization is the process of reviewing, validating, and justifying the alarms that meet the criteria of a critical alarm. In other words, the rationalization specifies only those points in the process system that require alarming. The ultimate goal of alarm rationalization is to determine the most efficient number of alarms to ensure that the process system is safe and remains within normal operating range. Alarm rationalization must be accomplished in conjunction with the City at the preprogramming workshop. The basic process can be outlined as: - Check Alarm Validity - Determine the consequence of inaction - Document Cause, confirmation, and corrective action - Document operator response time - Assign alarm priority - Alarm classification - Determine alarm attributes - Address need for special handling All unused or deprecated alarms are to be removed from the system. Alarms that are no longer needed are not to be abandoned in place (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Slow Scan Task Parameter Setting Default rate 500ms Priority 5 6.4Main or “0” Each program folder must contain a routine to be named “Main” or “0”; the language type specified for this routine should be LD. The purpose of the main routine is to govern the flow of the program. The jump subroutine(JSR) instructions shall be used to reference all other logical routines in the PLC program. No other logic is permitted in the main routine. The first JSR of the main routine shall be to the initialization subroutine conditioned on first scan flag from PLC. This will allow initialization logic to run each time the PLC returns to run after power cycle or other interruption. 6.5Subroutines Routines are the lowest level of program structure; routines are subsets of programs. Subroutines are routines that are not designated as the main routine. Subroutines should contain the code for control modules, equipment modules, or other compartmentalized pieces of code or equipment. Subroutines are beneficial for encapsulating a specific function that is a subset of the larger program scope. For traceability, all routines are called from the program main routine. This allows for a single location where the program flow can be quickly identified. The following are exceptions: - Routines that capture event data such as hourly data or report data Routine names shall be named after the process areas the routine interacts with. The naming shall be _XXX_AREA_NAME, where XXX indicates the loop number and AREA_NAME is the description of the loop number area 6.6Functions and Function Blocks There are a set of predefined blocks supplied by the City to be used for specific functions. These blocks provide a common, known functionality in the PLC whose interface connects to OITs, HMIs and SCADA system. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 o AC = Reference to the type of card, AC indicates voltage type. o DI = Reference to type of input, DI indicates discrete input. o MOD-04 = Indicates the module number. 4.5Tasks This section describes the various objects available for the organization of program logic within the controller and their associated parameters. The section provides guidelines for selection and organization of logic into these objects and configuration of their properties. Three tasks are used, fast, normal, and slow. The tasks are typically reserved for message communications with other devices. The fast scan task has the highest priority of 1, the normal scan task has a priority of 3, being used for all regular monitoring and control operations. The slow scan task has a priority of 5, being reserved for processor monitoring. Each task shall contain a single program within itself and program name shall follow a similar naming as the task. 4.5.1 Task Structure Tasks are the highest organizational unit in the controller and dictate the order of execution. The nomenclature applied to tasks must represent the broadest definition of the contents. 4.5.2 Program Structure Programs represent the middle tier of the organizational structure. At this layer there is more additional granularity added to the overall structure and the naming conventions must reflect that. Naming at this layer must not overlap with the layer above. For example: - P01_Fast Program - P02_Normal Program - P03_Slow Program Individual names at this tier may be duplicated in other tasks but must be unique per task. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 24 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 4.5.3 Routine Structure This is the lowest tier of programming structure. As such the name must provide clarity into the exact programming construct. Each routine is linked to a process area for fast identification and each process area has 3 subroutines associated, except for IO subroutines that are 4. For Example, - _000_ is associated with IO Mapping. o _000_ANALOG_INPUT_MAPPING o _000_ANALOG_OUTPUT_MAPPING o _000_DIGITAL_IO_MAPPING o _000_DISCRETE_IO_MAPPING - _010_Main_PUMP_STATION_ALM - _010_Main_PUMP_STATION_ASSETS - _010 Main_PUMP_STATION_CONTROLS HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. All setpoints will be supplied in engineering units. 5.1.2.2 Toggling Alarms Alarms cannot be individually enabled/disabled. Equipment must be disabled in order to disable the alarms associated to that piece of equipment. Otherwise, all the equipment’s associated alarms will remain enabled. 5.1.2.3 Debounce Times Debounce times are the period(in mSec) for which the signal must be in excess of the indicated setpoint limit before the alarm is generated. Setting a value to zero eliminates the debounce function completely. Alarms are currently all set to have debounce times at zero 5.1.2.4 Dead-Band Amount The dead-band amount is used to eliminate the chattering of alarms around the setpoint by requiring the process variable to retreat from the setpoint by a set amount before the alarm resets. A dead-band setting is applied to all limit alarms monitoring a process value. The configuration parameter is applicable to analog alarms only. The amount is supplied in engineering units with a value of zero deactivating the feature. Alarms are currently all set to have debounce times at zero. 5.1.2.5 Shelving or Bypassing The ability to bypass, or shelve, an alarm for a specified period will not be provided. Alarms cannot be bypassed or shelved; alarms can only be disabled. Disabling alarms is done through disabling the associated equipment. 5.1.3 Alarm conditions All alarm conditions are expected to apply positive logic. When the alarm value is a Boolean 1/true/on then the alarm is to be considered present/active. When the alarm value is a Boolean 0/false/off then the alarm is considered to be not present/inactive. 5.2Definition and Rationalization 5.2.1 Analog Alarms The following are available alarm conditions for analog values. These will support the basic typical requirements. Condition Description Low-Low(LL) The current scaled value is below the configured Low-Low setpoint(value < Low-Low SP) Low(L) The current scaled value is below the configured HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Alarms that are no longer needed are not to be abandoned in place 5.2.4 Alarm Generation Guidelines The generation of alarms is to be consistent with the ISA-18.2 alarm management guidelines. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 4.1.3 Equipment and Instrument Tags The City has been using an alphanumeric designation for process equipment identification. This was to be used for equipment tags. The alphanumeric designations to use for equipment are outlined in Appendix A. Additional designations are to follow the ISA standards as presented on instrument legends followed by “000” to be the same alphanumeric format as equipment tags. 4.1.4 Loop number This refers to the specific loop number as illustrated on the P&IDs. This is required to maintain a direct relationship between the P&IDs and generated information. Loop numbers will be assigned on the P&IDs following the process flow for each line. Loop numbers will increment starting at the top left most process line and follow that process line until it ends or joins with another, sheet to sheet, for all process equipment within a particular building. Loops are defined by each separate function in a process (manual valve, pump with related integral instruments, control valves, etc.) as assigned by the instrument engineer. Process equipment that works as a system, such as centrifuge sludge feed pump 1,2, and 3, shall share the same loop number and increment the equipment number. For example, if pump 1 has [loop]-[equipment] 101-01 pump two would be 101-02, and pump 3 would be 101-03. This also applies to dedicated equipment for that pump; an upstream and downstream pump isolation valve could be 100-01 and 102-01 respectively. Pumps 2 and 3 upstream would be 100-02,102-02, and 100-03,102-03. Instrumentation associated with a pump (pressure switch, temperature transmitter, etc.) installed upstream or downstream of the pup, without a process control equipment separating the pump from the instrumentation shall, shall use the same loop number as the pump. There will be more than 100 loops within a building so three digits will be used. The City will also use loop numbers separately numbered for equipment that normally is not shown on P&IDs (switchgear, network equipment, etc.) as well as all assets that don’t have control signals associated with them (manual valves, check valves, air release valves, etc.). Each P&ID will indicate the first and last tag used on the drawing along with 10 loop numbers for future use. The sequential loop numbers are outlined in Appendix A HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 20 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Module configurations are to be kept as close to manufacturer default(out of the box) as possible. This is to facilitate the replacement of modules on failure at some point in the future. In all cases ‘Hot Swappable’ modules are preferred. Modules that support advanced features, such as the HART protocol, should adhere to the spirit of the specification as much as possible. The ability to employ these additional features, such as multiple data points on a signal channel, offer cost savings and convenience. However, beyond returning the value and any indication of the quality of that data, extended features should not be utilized. Diagnostic points such as broken wire detect, under range, and over range are to be enabled, if selectable, and the signals utilized as faults to the analog input blocks. Module fault information is to be aggregated into a common signal and utilized as fault inputs to the analog blocks. In the case of inputs and outputs, either fault status information should be supplied to the primary controlling element. This allows the blockware to be cognizant that the element(s) that are being controlled will be non-responsive. This is applicable to equipment position control/feedback and speed control/feedback. 6.8.2 Digital IO Each point is assigned a variable. Typically, there are few configuration elements; however, in the case that such configuration elements are available, those elements are to be used sparingly or not at all. All signal diagnostic capabilities are to be employed. Filters on the input signals are not to be utilized and in all cases turned off. In the case of outputs, fault status information should be supplied to the primary controlling element. This allows the blockware to be cognizant that the element(s) that are being controlled will be non-responsive. 6.8.3 Message IO All peer-to-peer tag values passed through message arrays shall be read through the fast scan. Message routines shall be named by applying the prefix “MSG_” to the paired controller’s name, followed by “_IO”. An example would be “MSG_ H-010-PLC000-800­ 01_IO” (Howard F. Curren AWTP, MPS Electrical room, PLC number 1). Messaged data will be handled in a similar manner as module supplied data, through analog blocks. In the case of messaged data, the success or failure of communications will be used as the data quality. As the receiving controller is not the owner of the data HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 37 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 7.3DNP3 Exception Configuration The communication protocol between remote controllers and the supervisory(SCADA) system will be based on the DNP3 protocol as specified by DNP.ORG. This protocol shall be only used on remote sites. The transport layer for communications is to be appropriate for the application, e.g., LAN, serial modems, etc. No proprietary protocols are to be implemented. The master will be configured to perform its polling utilizing events(exceptions). Only periodically will static values be requested to insure validity and quality. The definition of what constitutes an event are dependent on the nature of the data point and acceptable excursion limits. All events will include a timestamp to facilitate sequence of event generation. The data is to be formatted and marshalled to facilitate optimal polling. Data being read from LAN based sources will by nature support a larger number of points being read than those from serial based devices. As such the data being sent must have the event thresholds and organization considered prior to implementation. Due to the potential communications loss events that can occur to some remote stations the concept of store & forward will be fully implemented. Therefore, all controllers must support this method of operation. This functionality may be supplied either natively or by the use of in-chassis interface modules. Each device is to be configured to maintain the data as long as possible, this is heavily dependent on the device design. Standard polling of remote locations by the SCADA system is to be configured such that the entire store & forward buffer may be read. This is much more critical when communicating to devices utilizing serial or cellular modems where outstations are typically read in a round-robin approach. In these cases, acquiring the data and storing the data into the historian in chronological order is critical. Stations that have a higher probability of continuous connection may be configured for unsolicited messaging. This feature is to be used sparingly and only for critical events. This feature will not be supportable in systems employing round-robin polling. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 43 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 6.9.2 User Defined Data Types Data is the foundation of what the controllers are utilizing. All controllers support and supply atomic data types; examples are Booleans, integers, and real numbers. These are the fundamental elements upon which control programs are built. Controllers also natively support arrays of these basic types, providing a primitive complex type. The user definted data type(UDT) is the logical extension of the primitive complex type into a structure that is constructed of multiple data types. In this case a UDT is a container for Boolean elements, integers, reals, arrays, and even other UDTs. UDTs are not to be used for programming HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 40 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 appended to the controller name utilizing the structure _r## where the hash marks reflect the specific version number. The specifics of the version as well as the reasoning for incrementing will be contained in the internal program documentation. An example of this is: H_075_PLC000_800_01_r04.ext The file name above indicates this is version 4 of the controller H_075_PLC000_800_01 The ‘ext’ indicates the manufacturer’s assigned extension for the file. When periodic backups are taken, the files will have a date code added to the file name. This will be in the form of yymmdd. This reflects the year, month, and day, in two(2) digit format upon which the file was saved. If further granularity is required then hhmm, representing hour in 24 hour time and the minute, may be added to the date code. A full example of the backup file naming convention is: H-075_PLC000_800_01_r04_2410041603.ext This indicates that the backup was captured on October 4,2024 at 4:03 pm. In this manner, multiple versions and backups may exist in the same subdirectory without interfering with each other. The data coding also leverages the native lexicographic sort order of Microsoft file systems. 4.4Module Naming Each module must be assigned a unique description in the controller to aid in identifying the hardware. The naming convention for modules is defined as: R#S#_[Module Type] Where: R# = Physical Rack number S# = Physical Slot number [Module Type] = the common acronym taken from the manufacturers part number Examples: - 1756_IA16I_R2S4_ACDI_MOD_04 o 1756_IA16I = This indicates the model ref. o R2S4 = Reference to location, rack 2 slot 4. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 23 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 FBD sheets should be limited in size to that which can be printed on an 11” x 17” sheet of paper. 6.3Task and Program Files Tasks provide the broadest level of organization. They also provide a level of execution control, that being continuous, periodic, event amongst others. Only two types of tasks are to be employed at the City: continuous and periodic. All other types of task types will not be employed. 6.3.1 Periodic tasks Periodic tasks are designed to execute on a fixed period of time, typically in milliseconds. These tasks are conducive when working with a process or system that is slow, or requires the processing on a regular basis. Creation of periodic tasks should be kept to a minimum(six or less) as required by the controlled process or area. The periodic task execution rate should be set to allow ample time for program execution without burdening the processor. Programmers shall frequently monitor and adjust the execution rate based on the task time. All programs shall be commissioned with task settings tuned such that no overlaps exist in the program. During commissioning, the main program overlap counter shall be confirmed at zero and FAULTLOG object shall not have any minor faults for overlap. Each periodic task must be set with a priority from 1 to 15, with 1 representing the highest priority. It is recommended that each task be set with a unique priority. Fast Scan Task Parameter Setting Default rate 10ms Priority 1 Normal Scan Task Parameter Setting Default rate 250ms Priority 3 HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 31 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 3.1.4 VTScada integration The software must be able to pull data from the VTScada database without the user having access to the VTScada system or database. This capability is crucial for a plant operator because it ensures seamless integration and data flow without compromising the security and integrity of the VTScada system. By allowing data to be accessed and utilized without direct interaction with the VTScada environment, plant operators can streamline operations, improve data handling efficiency, and minimize the risk of unauthorized access or potential system disruptions. This feature also facilitates the creation of comprehensive reports and dashboards, enabling integrators to monitor and optimize plant performance while maintaining stringent security protocols. Due to the level of importance of ensuring that the software platform is well integrated with the report creating platform this requirement is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.5 Other Databases The software must be able to pull data from sources other than VTScada such as LIMS for lab works, HSQ for data not yet available in VTScada. This capability is crucial for a wastewater plant operator because it ensures comprehensive data integration and accessibility. By allowing the software to gather information from multiple databases, operators can obtain a holistic view of the plant's operations. This integrated approach facilitates better monitoring and management of plant metrics, enhances decisionmaking processes, and ensures compliance with regulatory standards. Moreover, it minimizes the risk of data silos, improves operational efficiency, and supports the creation of detailed and accurate reports, which are essential for optimizing plant performance and maintaining environmental safety. Due to the importance of being able to link to other databases this requirement is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.6 Automated Report Generation The software must have the ability to automatically generate reports and email these reports to users on a schedule. This feature is particularly vital for operators as it ensures timely access to essential data without the need for manual report generation. Automated reports can include critical metrics such as water quality indicators, equipment performance data, and regulatory compliance status. By receiving these reports on a predefined schedule, operators can swiftly identify and address potential issues, streamline operational workflows, and maintain adherence to environmental regulations. Additionally, automated report generation reduces administrative burden, allowing operators to focus on more strategic and high-priority tasks. Due to the level critical nature of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.7 Security The software must be compliant with NIST standards for cybersecurity to ensure safety for use in the plant. This is crucial for a wastewater operator because it guarantees that all data and operations are protected from cyber threats. Given the sensitive nature of wastewater treatment processes, any breach could lead to significant environmental, and risk to human health and lives. Robust cybersecurity ensures the integrity and reliability of the system, allowing operators to focus on maintaining plant performance HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 12 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 5. Software recommendation Based on the outcome of the decision matrix the best choice for report creation software would be Ocean Data System’s DreamReport platform. Although other platforms had advantages over the DreamReport platform the overall scores for the DreamReport platform were 205 for the weighted score and 52 for the unweighted score. DreamReport offers a range of advantages that make it a valuable software platform for various applications. Its dashboard usability is commendable, allowing users to display specific data metrics over a range of time and create additional dashboards for viewing performance metrics. This flexibility in dashboard configuration enhances the user experience by providing tailored views of critical data. The platform supports custom report creation, although it is limited by the number of available licenses. However, additional licenses can be added to the system, which allows for greater customization and scalability in reporting capabilities. Integration with VTScada is available, with over 100 drivers available to connect different databases, making it compatible with many different systems. This extensive integration capability ensures that DreamReport can work seamlessly with various data sources, enhancing its utility in diverse operational environments. The software also excels in database integration due to the large number of available drivers, which facilitates smooth data flow and management across different platforms. Automatic triggered reports can be set up based on specific conditions or schedules, providing flexibility in automation. For instance, reports can be generated when a specific water parameter or equipment function reaches a certain threshold, or they can be scheduled to run at regular intervals. These reports can be sent out to specific individuals through email in various formats, such as PDF, ensuring timely and efficient communication of critical information. DreamReport is compliant with NIST cybersecurity standards and can be set up to be 21 CFR part 11 compliant, enhancing its security credentials and making it suitable for use in regulated industries. While the browser usability is good for viewing, report editing must be done through the report creator software. This limitation is offset by the ease of use and look of the browser user interface, which scored well in evaluations. Additionally, DreamReport provides regular security and general updates as part of its subscription model, ensuring that the software remains up-to-date and secure. The availability of a redundancy license at a competitive cost further enhances its appeal, providing a cost-effective solution for ensuring system reliability. The company behind DreamReport, Ocean Data Systems, is noted for its responsive and knowledgeable customer service. Their sales representatives are quick to respond and provide exceptional knowledge of the software platform, which adds to the overall positive user experience. In summary, DreamReport stands out for its robust integration capabilities, flexible automation options, and strong security compliance. While there are some limitations in terms of scalability and browserbased report creation, the platform's specialized functions, regular updates, and excellent customer service make it a compelling choice for organizations looking to enhance their data reporting and management capabilities. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 Key Concepts 3.1VTScada Development platform The VTScada development platform contains key concepts that need to be understood in order to plan, design, and implement an application. Key concepts for tag based configuration, tag widgets data acquisition, historian, configuration nodes, HMI clients, and development nodes are described below. 3.1.1 Tag Based Configuration Tags are software elements representing physical objects or concepts. Tags represent communications ports, device drivers, I/O elements for a device, historical logging of data, and alarms. The combination of various functional tags into larger units will create orderly, consistent structures that may then be directly utilized or combined together further with other functional elements. The underlying concept to the system is that everything is a tag. Whether a single atomic element or a collection of complex elements the end result is simply considered to be a tag. This in turn allows the tags to be referenced as a single item with all of the inheritted properties immediately available. 3.1.2 Tag Widgets Tag information is visualized to the operators utilizing widgets. These widgets may be indicators, push buttons, numeric values, numeric entries, gauges, variable fill, and more. The crucial point to this is that the visualization elements are very tightly coupled with the data elements. 3.1.3 Data Acquisition Data acquisition(DA) is to be configured as appropriate to the controller(s) being monitored. The preferred communications pathways are as follows: - For PCS or process: Ethernet/IP or Modbus TCP - For remote telemetry: DNP3 protocol over Ethernet HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Recommend Network Sequencing 3.1 Maintaining Monitoring and Reporting Once the P&IDs are developed and preliminary control descriptions are available, discussion on where new PLC panels will be located and how I/O will be connected to the new PLC modules will be made at milestones for each project. A detailed sequence on how to maintain monitoring and reporting will be developed as part of these discussions. These sequencing plans will be reviewed with City staff prior to completing the 90% Design. 3.2 Replacing Fiber Cables All fiber cables for each process, which includes fibers for the camera feeds, will be replaced as part of any improvement to that process. During development of the Preliminary P&IDs a thorough site investigations shall be performed to verify the existing fiber runs from the associated PLCs back to the main network fiber patch panel. This includes removing electrical manhole covers and draining the manholes. The purpose of these investigations is to determine what existing conduit runs will be used to best show on the design site plans for the fiber installation. At any point thereafter, investigations shall be performed to confirm new cable can be run through the existing conduits without removing the existing fiber cables. Final network diagram will include fiber cable labeling. Labeling for the cables and for the ports at patch panels will be installed in the field to match the network diagram. Multiple cables run through the same conduits and manholes will be depicted on the network site plan. 3.3 Conversion to VTScada VTScada HMI programming will be developed and placed in service parallel to the existing HSQ HMI system on a separate network rack and made available for interface on new workstations throughout the plant, as well as on wireless devices. As a process is made available on the VTScada HMI and is installed, tested, and passed an approved reliability demonstration period, the associated HSQ HMI display screens could be removed or made view only. The goal will be to get each process on the VTScada HMI to use for setpoint adjustment, monitoring, and/or manual override. However, there will be some parameters that may impact separate processes still controlled only through HSQ. A plan will need to be developed for such situations to provide a protocol to operations until all control parameters have been switched over to VTScada. After commissioning activities have been completed, system shall run for 30 days in hands-off mode to document alarm rates for modified alarms. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. The historian aspect used for historization will be a structured query language(SQL) based server located in the PIN(DMZ) network. This server(s) will receive critical and compliance information pulled from the applications on the data acquisition servers. This database will use the table configurations specified by VTScada. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 6 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Program Structure The City’s programs will be written utilizing LD, and FBD languages only. Any exceptions must be submitted for approval following the procedures contained in the City’s Standards Deviation Request Policy (Reference Volume 1, SCADA & PCS Standards Deviation Request). 6.1Ladder Logic LD is the preferred language when performing complex Boolean logic. This would include actions such as top-down Boolean logic, interlock development, sequencing equipment operations, or control of I/O elements. Function blocks(AOIs, FBs, FCs) may be utilized as part of the rung logic; however, this approach should be minimized. Block programming requirements often require support branches whose sole purpose is to port data into and out of the block. This approach results in rungs that are large and less comprehensible. The preferred approach in this case is to only use blocks that all of the parameters are supplied and visible; typical of internal instructions. Rungs shall be organized in a one concept per rung manner. Stringing logic across rungs through the use of intermediary bits is to be avoided as much as possible. Logic that becomes excessively large will require being broken into multiple rungs. In cases where logic is split, it is incumbent on the developer to provide clarity and explain logic flow. LD shall be limited in size to that which can be printed on an 11” x 17” sheet of paper, either portrait or landscape. LD is a suitable language for the construction of custom function blocks. The same programming constraints are to be applied in this case as well. 6.2Function Block Diagram FBD is preferred when working with blocks and process values and control. Operations such as analog/digital input mapping, PID control elements, stand-alone alarm handling, and totalization should be handled in this manner. It is possible to perform Boolean logic in FBD; however, this should be kept to a minimum. The density of information contained on the sheet should be as concise and legible as possible. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 30 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] 5.2.3 Alarm Rationalization Alarm rationalization is the process of reviewing, validating, and justifying the alarms that meet the criteria of a critical alarm. In other words, the rationalization specifies only those points in the process system that require alarming. The ultimate goal of alarm rationalization is to determine the most efficient number of alarms to ensure that the process system is safe and remains within normal operating range. Alarm rationalization must be accomplished in conjunction with the City at the preprogramming workshop. The basic process can be outlined as: - Check Alarm Validity - Determine the consequence of inaction - Document Cause, confirmation, and corrective action - Document operator response time - Assign alarm priority - Alarm classification - Determine alarm attributes - Address need for special handling All unused or deprecated alarms are to be removed from the system. Alarms that are no longer needed are not to be abandoned in place (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 Key Concepts 3.1VTScada Development platform The VTScada development platform contains key concepts that need to be understood in order to plan, design, and implement an application. Key concepts for tag based configuration, tag widgets data acquisition, historian, configuration nodes, HMI clients, and development nodes are described below. 3.1.1 Tag Based Configuration Tags are software elements representing physical objects or concepts. Tags represent communications ports, device drivers, I/O elements for a device, historical logging of data, and alarms. The combination of various functional tags into larger units will create orderly, consistent structures that may then be directly utilized or combined together further with other functional elements. The underlying concept to the system is that everything is a tag. Whether a single atomic element or a collection of complex elements the end result is simply considered to be a tag. This in turn allows the tags to be referenced as a single item with all of the inheritted properties immediately available. 3.1.2 Tag Widgets Tag information is visualized to the operators utilizing widgets. These widgets may be indicators, push buttons, numeric values, numeric entries, gauges, variable fill, and more. The crucial point to this is that the visualization elements are very tightly coupled with the data elements. 3.1.3 Data Acquisition Data acquisition(DA) is to be configured as appropriate to the controller(s) being monitored. The preferred communications pathways are as follows: - For PCS or process: Ethernet/IP or Modbus TCP - For remote telemetry: DNP3 protocol over Ethernet HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 no alarming information will be generated. Scaling, beyond the conversion of integers to reals, is not to be employed. All failure states will be discussed with the City at pre-programming workshop to determine the proper course of action. Messages array bits are each mapped to a variable where variables 0XXX (where XXX is the unique variable number) are reserved for digital outputs, 1XXX (where XXX is the unique variable number) are reserved for digital inputs, and 4XXX (where XXX is the unique variable name) are reserved for analog inputs/outputs which are read/write blocks. 6.8.4 SCADA IO This is data and information that is being specifically marshalled for use by the SCADA system. These SCADA IO points shall be kept minimal, the basic ones that are provided and used are the simulation and disable IO points, some may be added if needed and should be documented accordingly. 6.9Data Structures Internal to most modern controllers is the concept of structured data. This is typically represented employing two methods, add-on instructions/function blocks and user data types. Each of these concepts offers opportunities to add clarity and reusability to the overall control program. User data types shall not be used for the development of new code. Only predefined add on instructions shall be used. 6.9.1 Function Blocks/Add-on Instructions For the purpose of this discussion these will be referred to as add-on instructions(AOIs) AOIs represent a unique collection of data and logic. The logic is customarily developed using the controller’s standard programming languages. The AOI also presents a usable interface to the logic through a series of parameters, both inputs and outputs. The AOI also can contain internal data registers that maintain the unique state of the instance of the AOI. The AOI in short is a complete, unique encapsulation of control data and logic. The primary benefit of the AOI is in its reusability. The advantage to the developer is that logic only has to be developed and tested once. Once proven, this logic can be reused countless times with the assurance that it will always function the same across all instances. This aids in the rapid development of entire systems. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 1. Introduction The Howard F. Curren Advanced Water Treatment Plant(HFC AWTP) owns and operates a Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) system currently composed mainly of General Electric(GE) PACSystems Rx3i and 90-30 series programmable logic controllers(PLCs) at the field level. General Electric was acquired by Emerson in February, 2019. This document refers to GE equipment as GE to maintain familiarity with City’s existing Standards. As manufacturers continue to make advancements in product development, older versions of hardware advance towards “legacy” status. Products in legacy status are taken out of production and typically are no longercommercially available from the manufacturer. Replacement parts must then be sourced from third party sellers, increasing the risk for price inflation and long lead times. In that case, a set of spares must be stringently maintained to prevent unnecessary down time in the event of a failure. Additionally, manufacturers eventually stop developing and releasing software updates for legacy controllers, which may pose a security risk on network connected systems. A majority of HFC AWTP’s PLC hardware has entered the legacy phase. While PLC upgrades may be costly up front, updating the PLCs reduces the risk of unplanned downtime and enhances network security. 2. Objectives A majority of the existing PLCs reached legacy status in 2018 (GE 90-30 and GE 90-70 series). PLC hardware for these products are no longer supported by the manufacturer. With the impending replacement of all PLC hardware as part of the on-going design-build project, the City has chosen to evaluate alternatives to develop a new standard for PLCs at the plant. Existing PLCs at the plant were evaluated by the City. As a result, Hazen and Sawyer(Hazen) was asked to review the existing PLC hardware to determine equivalent alternatives. Four major PLC vendors were evaluated for consideration whenever existing PLC line ups are to be replaced as part of on-going process improvements at HFC AWTP. The different PLC models were compared including features and budgetary costs for full system replacement. The findings are summarized in this PLC Hardware Evaluation along with a recommendation for the standard PLC hardware platform going forward. 3. Assessment of Existing PLCs & Industry Review 3.1 Existing PLCs An audit was performed by McKim & Creed & Hazen in June 2018 with assistance from HFC AWTP staff to quantify the number of existing PLCs installed at the facility. HFC AWTP staff provided information about the existing PLCs at these sites including existing network diagrams, model of PLC at each location, quantity, for review and use for this evaluation. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System PLC Hardware Evaluation 6 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 FBD sheets should be limited in size to that which can be printed on an 11” x 17” sheet of paper. 6.3Task and Program Files Tasks provide the broadest level of organization. They also provide a level of execution control, that being continuous, periodic, event amongst others. Only two types of tasks are to be employed at the City: continuous and periodic. All other types of task types will not be employed. 6.3.1 Periodic tasks Periodic tasks are designed to execute on a fixed period of time, typically in milliseconds. These tasks are conducive when working with a process or system that is slow, or requires the processing on a regular basis. Creation of periodic tasks should be kept to a minimum(six or less) as required by the controlled process or area. The periodic task execution rate should be set to allow ample time for program execution without burdening the processor. Programmers shall frequently monitor and adjust the execution rate based on the task time. All programs shall be commissioned with task settings tuned such that no overlaps exist in the program. During commissioning, the main program overlap counter shall be confirmed at zero and FAULTLOG object shall not have any minor faults for overlap. Each periodic task must be set with a priority from 1 to 15, with 1 representing the highest priority. It is recommended that each task be set with a unique priority. Fast Scan Task Parameter Setting Default rate 10ms Priority 1 Normal Scan Task Parameter Setting Default rate 250ms Priority 3 HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] 6.4Main or "0" Each program folder must contain a routine to be named "Main" or "0"; the language type specified for this routine should be LD. The purpose of the main routine is to govern the flow of the program. The jump subroutine(JSR) instructions shall be used to reference all other logical routines in the PLC program. No other logic is permitted in the main routine. The first JSR of the main routine shall be to the initialization subroutine conditioned on first scan flag from PLC. This will allow initialization logic to run each time the PLC returns to run after power cycle or other interruption. (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 2 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 HMI Software The SCADA software currently utilized by the City is VTScada 12.1.58, development environment from Trihedral Engineering, contact the City for version being used. This software suite forms the basis of the system, as well as providing the majority of the data acquisition drivers. All development of HMI screens and system wide visualization will utilize this software. All new HMI screens and graphical elements are required to be developed using the latest available City provided ChangeSet file. A ChangeSet file contains the instructions required to re-create an application. This is not an exact backup but a compendium of changes and the base information to reproduce a functional application. In general, ChangeSets are used to distribute new applications, distribute updates to an existing application, or add extra features to OEM layers. This ChangeSet file will contain all of the latest container tags, display definitions, and library widgets needed to support development activities. Each project shall request, via email to the City’s project manager (or assigned City Operational Technology(OT) contact), a copy of the current ChangeSet application immediately prior to initiating programming activities. Although updates to the standard may occasionally occur, the files provided on request date shall be utilized throughout the project. Only those updates which are determined mission critical (have severe consequences to operations, life or equipment safety) will be handled on a case by case basis through direct notice to programmers by the City’s project manager or assigned OT contact. The distribution ChangeSet file will be re-created periodically as functional elements are added and/or enhanced. Additionally, other exports will be created as required in order to maintain an appropriate level of application consistency between the City’s templates and any standalone in development. The City’s SCADA system is a managed VTScada application which has been developed using custom City developed library widgets. Refer to the City Standard Object Library Programming Guideline for details on the currently available object template library or submit an email request to the City’s project manager. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 February 2025 10 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] **2.2.2 Field Investigation** [ TABLE ] In order to verify the exact routing of the fiber network, site investigations were performed. The following observations were made during these investigations: - PLC 19 in the Main Pump Station is connected to the facility network rack directly over a CAT6 cable, fiber is not used. - There are eleven main trunk fiber cables landed at a fiber patch panel for process connections located at the Main Pump Station that then are connected to the facility network rack. - There are additional fiber cables landed at a separate fiber patch panel in the network rack room, however these fiber lines were installed by the City for various staff workstations and not part of the facility process fiber network. - The Red Zone was found to consist of the following fiber runs: - o One fiber cable that connects the network to PLC 11, PLC 12, PLC 13, and PLC10 as follows: - Patch panel near PLC 12 has a trunk cable back to the network rack patch panel - PLC 12 connects by fiber to the patch panel near PLC 12 - PLC 11 connects by fiber to the patch panel near PLC 12 - PLC 13 connects by fiber to a patch panel near PLC 13 which then connects to the patch panel near PLC 12 - PLC 10 connects by fiber to the patch panel near PLC 13 - o One fiber cable that connects the network to PLC 5 and PLC 39 as follows: - PLC 39 connects by fiber to a patch panel at PLC 5 - PLC 5 connects by fiber to the patch panel in PLC 5 panel HFC AWTP Master Plan **June 10,2022** Improvements: SCADA Design Criteria ![](_page_22_Picture_0.jpeg) ![](_page_22_Picture_1.jpeg) - New PLCs can connect directly to plant fiber network, they do not have to connect through a remote telemetry unit. - Uninterruptible power supplies shall be an APC UPS without ethernet connections and with a bypass switch for easy change out. Relays shall be used on UPS outputs to provide alarm when panel is on UPS power. - Panels shall be provided with twenty-precent(20%) spare capacity for I/O with ten-precent(10%) installed spare I/O. **Table 2-4: Standard Control Panel Components** (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 15)Physical access to control panels, wiring closets, server rooms, data centers, and control rooms shall be controlled, at a minimum, through a keyed lock. 16)Unused router and switch ports shall be normally turned off and turned on for configuration and testing purposes. 17)Remote access using the remote desktop protocol(RDP) without domain authentication, authorization, accounting, and encryption is prohibited. The use of remote control software (e.g., Dameware, VNC, RealVNC, TeamViewer, etc.) which does not support domain authentication, authorization, accounting, and strong encryption is prohibited. 18)Remote access shall be performed using laptops owned and configured by the City. Remote access shall utilize a VPN to first connect and authenticate the user to the business network. A second VPN shall be used to connect and authenticate the user to the process information network(PIN). Once the VPN connections are established, RDP shall be used to connect to a jump host(workstation) in the PIN. Then, a second RDP connection shall be made to an engineering workstation on the PCS. All RDP connections shall use strong authentication against an authentication, authorization, accounting(AAA) server and strong encryption. 19)Session controls shall monitor and automatically logout after 10 minutes of inactivity. 20)All software components that are not required for the operation and/or maintenance of the PCS/SCADA system shall be removed. If removal is not technically feasible, software not required for operation and/or maintenance shall be disabled. This removal shall not impede the function of the PCS/SCADA system. If software that is not required cannot be removed or disabled, document a specific explanation. 21)Disable all network ports, protocols, and services not required by the PCS/SCADA system. 22)The PCS/SCADA system networks shall be protected through network segmentation as follows: a. The PCS/SCADA system networks shall be isolated from external connections including the internet. This includes remote SCADA site communications. b. The business network and PCS/SCADA system networks shall be separate and direct communications between the two networks shall be denied. All traffic shall terminate in the PIN described below. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 8 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 HMI Graphics Alarming 8.1Overview Alarm configuration requirements are contained in the City’s Alarm Management Strategy (Reference the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual, Volume 2). Any alarm modification, addition or deletion must follow alarm definition and rationalization procedures discussed in the alarm management strategy prior to implementation. It is important to note that although OIT and HMI system functions can be used to monitor and generate alarms, in the City’s PCS process alarms shall be generated in the PLCs which will be logged and displayed on the HMIs and OITs. HMI application shall not be configured to perform alarm calculations. HMI generated alarms will be utilized only for alarms and events initiated from within the HMI software such as software status alarms, loss of communications with a PLC and other system level alarming. Alarm limits must be adjustable through the HMI graphics screens; however, those alarm limits will be used by the PLC in determining if there is an alarm condition. The nature of the distributed PCS is such that the PLC-based process control logic can and will continue to operate, even if one or more HMIs become unavailable. As a result, the PLC must be able to determine alarm conditions and act accordingly without the need for continuous communication with the HMI system. The City’s Standard Object Library and add-on instruction(AOI) standard library objects shall be utilized for configuration of PLC and HMI. 8.2Nuisance Alarm Suppression Techniques All nuisance alarm suppression shall be by approved methods documented in the City’s Alarm Management Strategy (Reference the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual, Volume 2 for more details on alarm suppression techniques). Disabling equipment will disable the alarms associated with the equipment. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 February 2025 44 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 16 4 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Naming Conventions Consistent naming across systems and controllers facilitates the City’s technicians’ efforts during troubleshooting. As such, a series of standards has been developed to address these needs. 4.1Tag Naming Conventions As data is generated by the various elements of the process, the easiest way to track things is through human decipherable tag names. The tags stand in the place of the somewhat arcane addressing and naming conventions that manufacturers use to address their points. This also results in a language construct that is regular and nonrepetitive. It is extremely important that the naming system yield unique names with a consistent format throughout the entire system. As such, the following naming convention is to be utilized throughout the entire City system. These mnemonics are to be applied to any and all elements that might be transferred from a controller to another system, i.e., SCADA, HMI, OIT, gateway, or controller. This system shall be utilized, but in a paired down manner, for internal registers and flags within a controller. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 17 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Figure 8 Tag Naming Convention PLC programming does not accept hyphens, so PLC tags must use underscores to be compatible with the program. The tagging standards shall remain the same in the PLC with the only difference being the use of underscores instead of hyphens. The figure below shows the tagging as it will be used inside the PLC program. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 18 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

These stations may be based on workstations, laptops, or mobile devices. As such additional consideration must be given to the functionality of the screens as the platform changes. All workstation-based HMI clients will be added to the configuration of the system. This allows for remote administration of the machines as well as the ability to push updates to the systems. Mobile clients, either portable or vehicle mounted, will be based on Windows 10 with cellular communications for data access. As with all systems these stations will be added to the system configuration for centralized management and application synchronization. 3.1.6 Deployment Nodes Deployment nodes are workstations on which new applications will be methodically deployed. These stations will have the system property “Automatically Deploy Local Changes” disabled. These stations accept newly developed/tested and approved ChangeSet files and provide a platform to methodically deploy the edits. Once loaded these changes will be “Accepted for Distribution” and the native application synchronization service will distribute the application to the configured nodes. 3.1.7 Historian The process value historian function will be conversely aligned with the primary and backup function of the data acquisition servers. In this manner the primary data acquisition server system will function as the backup historian server. All process value data storage files will be specified to reside in a subdirectory on a non-operating system drive partition under the name “.\Historian”. The alarm databases will also be located on the data acquisition servers in separate, native alarm database files. The alarm database historian server files will be specified to HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 February 2025 14 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 The physical data is required to be aggregated with the quality information into one cohesive unit with a new unique name. This point or tag name is what will be used in lieu of the physical I/O address and any/all of its quality Programmers shall organize all I/O, (local, networked and messaged) into four subroutines, _000_ANALOG_INPUT_MAPPING, _000_ANALOG_OUTPUT_MAPPING, _000_DIGITAL_IO_MAPPING, _000_DISCRETE_IO_MAPPING To clarify the above: - All Inputs must be mapped into variable objects with unique, symbolic tag names - In all cases input points must be used with quality information supplied. - All outputs will be directly controlled by unique, symbolic tag names. I/O mapping programs shall be organized within the normal scan periodic task. Program nomenclature shall follow the guideline provided below. The number and types shall be limited to those required for the project but will likely include at least some of these: - Analog_IO - Digital_IO - Message_IO - Network_IO - Modbus_IO Some controllers support the use of tag aliasing, which allows for the abstraction of complex input or output addressing into a single symbolic name. This feature is not to be employed in place of the mapping requirements outlined above. This methodology does not include the quality of the data in question. 6.8.1 Analog IO The configuration of the individual point type is to be performed in the module. Modules that support multiple types of input, such as 4-20mA and 0-10VDC must be set to the appropriate selection at the module. Any input module specific configurations, such as scaling and alarming are not to be utilized at the module level. All signal diagnostic capabilities are to be employed. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 8.3Commissioning Requirements Commissioning activities create abnormal alarm situations; this section contains requirements for alarm and event system during commissioning activities. 8.3.1 Operational Readiness Review Meetings Alarms and events risk review shall be reviewed at operational readiness meetings to determine alarm owner(contractor or operations) based on risk to process and processing equipment. 8.3.2 Alarms in Active Commissioning State Alarms and events which are not turned over to operations at operational readiness review are required to be suppressed by means of PLC logic suppression outside of testing hours to avoid presenting operators with nuisance and/or unknown alarms. 8.3.3 Alarm and Event Acceptance Matrices The City utilizes the defined average alarm rate of less than six alarms per hour period as defined in alarm management strategy in Volume 2 as maximum allowable alarm rate for acceptance of modifications to alarm system. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 7 SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 Recovery and Restore Policy 7.1General The City’s SCADA & PCS Recovery and Restore Policy is intended to establish requirements to promote efficient and accurate system restoration after a failure has occurred. The City’s SCADA & PCS consist of numerous highly integrated software packages, which undergo continual change for plant maintenance, operational improvements, and projects. This policy and procedure addresses requirements to promote known good system backups; and procedures to identify and utilize the backups and asset management systems during recovery. This policy applies to all City SCADA & PCS software assets. 7.2Backup Storage Requirements The City has deployed a SCADA & PCS Change Management server for version control and file storage for SCADA & PCS Software applications including PLC, and OIT network application files as well as HMI standard object templates. If the programmer does not have access to the City’s SCADA & PCS Change Management Server, then the programmer shall request a data transfer for the following: - HMI backup - HMI standard object templates - OIT standard object templates The following applications shall be stored in the SCADA & PCS change management server at initial installation and after any modification has been deployed in the production environment: - PLC program files including network configuration files - OIT application files - Network switch configuration files - Radio configuration files All contractors completing on-site startup/commissioning modifications to operating equipment are required to complete change documentation and upload files to SCADA & PCS Change Management Server at the end of each shift. The ISA 101 HMI standard serves as a guideline to help organizations design, build and operate effective HMI applications. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 4.1 PLC Comparison Factors: 4.1.1 Functionality w/ Ethernet An important function to consider for PLC evaluation is to review the impact on functionality of the PLC when more process equipment is added to the network to communicate with the PLC via any communication protocol. Evaluation is performed with Ethernet(widely used) considered as communication protocol between the various equipment such as VFDs, MCC, and vendor supplied control panels and if the functionality of the PLC would be stable. 4.1.2 Market Share This shows the size of a company, a useful metric in illustrating a company’s dominance and competitiveness in a given field. This factor is a key indicator of a company’s competitiveness, it can also be an indicator of growth, success, and stability. It is considered for the evaluation as key indicator in view of reliability of the PLC and dependability. 4.1.3 PLC Features This is a critical factor when looking at PLC selection criteria and its suitability for the intended application. Adequate number and type of I/O, High-speed processor, large memory capacity, multiple communication options, redundancy and safety features are key factors used for selection. 4.1.4 Support Technical support and service from the manufacturer or vendor is an important evaluation criterion. Reliable customer support, training resources, and a warranty or maintenance program along with local distribution network is key to ensure product availability and quick response in keeping systems in service. 4.1.5 Programmers Availability Local assistance and access to knowledge and resources are invaluable in ensuring PLC system's smooth operation, maintenance and troubleshooting. In any new construction effort, having multiple programmers employed by local integrators will help keep new construction on schedule and affordable. 4.1.6 Local Availability PLC components that are readily available, including spare parts in the event of equipment failure to minimize downtime and ensure that the control system is rapidly operational again, supply chain and lead times are key factors in selecting a PLC lineup. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System PLC Hardware Evaluation 10 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 3.1.12 HMI Integration The software should be easy to integrate into The City’s HMI - VTScada. This integration is crucial for a wastewater operator because VTScada is a widely used HMI system that provides realtime monitoring and control of plant operations. By ensuring compatibility with VTScada, the software allows operators to seamlessly access and manage all relevant data through a single interface, enhancing operational efficiency and reducing the risk of errors. This not only supports proactive maintenance and troubleshooting but also helps in ensuring regulatory compliance and optimizing plant performance. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. 3.1.13 Software support and maintenance The software should have support security updates and technical support. This is important for a wastewater operator because security is paramount in protecting sensitive data and ensuring the integrity of the system. Regular security updates help safeguard the system against potential cyber threats, vulnerabilities, and unauthorized access, which can compromise the operation of the plant. By having these updates included without extra charges, operators can maintain a high level of security consistently, ensuring continuous protection without worrying about additional expenses. This proactive approach not only secures the plant's operations but also aligns with regulatory standards and best practices for infrastructure management. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. 3.1.14 Redundancy The software should be able to be set up with a redundant system to allow for the use of the report creator even if the main server is taken offline. This is crucial for a wastewater operator because redundancy ensures continuous access to critical reporting tools, minimizing downtime and maintaining smooth operations. In the event of a system failure or maintenance, having a redundant setup means that data analysis can proceed without interruption, thus supporting timely decision-making and regulatory compliance. This resilience is essential in safeguarding the plant's functionality and ensuring that operators can respond promptly to any issues that arise. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. 3.1.15 Company prominence The software company should be a reputable company with good industry presence and expertise. This is important for a wastewater operator because a reputable company ensures that the software is reliable, secure, and compliant with industry standards. A company with a strong industry presence is more likely to be aware of the latest advancements and regulatory changes, ensuring that the software remains up-todate and effective in managing the complex requirements of wastewater treatment. Expertise in the field means that the company can provide high-quality support and tailored solutions, addressing specific operational challenges and improving overall plant performance. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 14 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 PCS/SCADA system networks (e.g., PIN, business network, remote access, dial-up modems, alarm notification systems, vendors, other municipalities, etc.). 12)Authentication System Inventory - Maintain an inventory of all authentication systems in use by the PCS/SCADA system (e.g., Active Directory, LDAP, RADIUS, TACACS+, etc.). Indicate where local authentication is used exclusively. 8.3Access control 1) Identities and credentials shall be centrally managed through domain level services for authorized devices and users. The authentication solution shall be dedicated to the PCS/SCADA system using a different set of identities and credentials than the business network. Authentication shall not be based on trusts or any other link with the business network. 2) A backup domain controller shall be maintained in a separate building from the primary domain controller. Both domain controllers shall be fed from separate power sources and back by separate UPSs. 3) All network devices shall use IEEE 802.1x for authentication to the network. 4) A role-based access control system shall be used throughout the PCS/SCADA System and shall be configured to operate using the principle of least privilege. This includes operating system permissions, file access, user accounts, service accounts, application-to-application communications, and component system services. Access permissions shall be centrally managed and limited to only the functionality needed to perform job functions associated with the account. 5) Default host accounts (e.g., guest, administrator, etc.) shall be removed. If the accounts are required for normal or emergency operation, they shall be recreated under a different name than the default account. Coordinate with the PCS/SCADA system software vendors. 6) Unique user IDs shall be used for authentication. The use of shared accounts is prohibited. 7) The PCS/SCADA system shall require identification and authentication before allowing any actions by a user. 8) Wherever it is possible to do so, PCS/SCADA system equipment (e.g., servers, workstations, laptops, etc.) shall, as part of the boot process, display to users a system use notification message or banner before granting access to the system that provides privacy and security notices which states that: a. Users are accessing a critical infrastructure information system HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - Policies Volume 1 of 3 February 2025 25 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 c. An industrial DMZ shall act as a buffer network(i.e., DMZ) between the business network and the PCS/SCADA system networks. i. The industrial DMZ shall be the only means of communications beyond the PCS/SCADA system domain. Other architectures (including the use of dual or multi-homed hosts) are prohibited. The use of dial-up modems is prohibited. The use of cellular routers is prohibited. ii. The PCS/SCADA system shall operate autonomously (industrial DMZ, other plant PCS, etc.) except for remote SCADA sites associated with the SCADA system. Remote SCADA sites shall operate autonomously with no dependency on SCADA communications; the only exception would be a site that must receive a monitoring signal from another site for control purposes (e.g., an elevated tank sends a level signal to pump station to control the level in the elevated tank). d. The industrial DMZ may, based on project scope, contain physical or virtual workstations and servers needed to facilitate data exchange with business network applications. Firewall rules shall be configured to only allow ports and protocols required for the following functions that could be provided. i. Antivirus definition updates ii. Operating system patches iii. IDS/IPS definition updates iv. Syslog servers v. Web portal vi. Process control historian(Tier 2) vii. FTPS Server viii. Security information and event management(SIEM) ix. Email relay x. Backup repository e. The industrial DMZ shall be configured to prevent host-to-host communications unless specifically required to meet operational requirements. In such cases, ports, protocols, and initiating direction shall be configured to minimize connectivity in accordance with least functionality and least privilege principles. f. The PCS/SCADA system Tier-1 historian shall push data into a Tier-2 historian in the PIN. Business network users and enterprise HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software DreamReport has the advantage of being NIST certified for cybersecurity. Wastewater treatment is a critical part of everyday life that the environment and many people’s lives depend on. Being certified for adequate cybersecurity standards ensures adequate safety and security is being followed. DreamReport also can meet 21 CFR part 11 compliance requirements, this makes the reports and findings trustworthy, reliable, and equivalent to paper records and handwritten signatures. DreamReport is extensively used in the life sciences industry due to this ability. Additionally, DreamReport can be integrated into HMI and PLCs, readily and easily with the large number of adapters that are available on the software platform. This can make it easy to integrate with the Plant’s current system and any potential changes that may happen in the long term. Having this flexibility is a significant advantage Disadvantages DreamReport has some disadvantages related to the size of Ocean Data Systems, the company that created the software platform. Although it is not the smallest company out of the set of companies that were reviewed it is a non-domestic company. Although this may not be a significant issue for the usability of the software it should be considered in the case that there are any restrictions on whether or not the City is allowed to operate software from non-domestic companies. DreamReport’s licensing structure for concurrent users is similar to that of the tag numbers but with a small amount of added flexibility with the licensing sizes. DreamReport has licenses that can be combined for the number of concurrent users. Meaning that if the plant requires for example 15 users a 10 user license and a 5 user license can be combined to meet the number of required licenses. This makes increasing the number of users incur an additional recurring cost that should be taken into account. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 7 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Standard Logic Descriptions 7.1Initialization and Non-Volatile Memory The controller maintains its program, data, and configuration through non-volatile memory(NVM). This is memory that can exist in several forms, battery backed up RAM, EEPROM, or compact flash device(SD card). This technology allows the processor to return to its previous state after being powered up. The controller must provide some type of non-volatile memory. At no time is it acceptable to rely on continuous power. The NVM must be capable of withstanding the same environmental conditions as the controller. The particular form-factor of the NVM is dependent on the manufacturer. The preferred types of NVM are those that do not rely on batteries, or other mechanical devices(such as hard disk drives). This memory shall also not be read-only but support re-writing using the native development tools. The process of loading the program after power loss must be inherent in the controller natively. At a minimum the non-volatile memory must return the control program to active RAM. The ability of returning retained variable data to its previous state is a function that is desirable. However, when the ability to return retained data values is not supported, the control code itself must account for this and suitable initialization and start-up logic. 7.2Peer-to-peer Communications Within the plant all messaging shall be performed over Ethernet. If Ethernet is not available to the device, alternatives may be employed with City approval. For remote locations the preferred platform is Cellular modems however, if cellular modems are not appropriate, with City approval, acceptable alternative platforms are serial radio modems, and satellite for extremely remote locations. In all cases, alternative peer-to-peer communications are to be submitted to the City for review and approval prior to implementation. All inter-processor communications will be performed utilizing native communication control elements. An example of this is to utilize the “MSG” instruction in a ControlLogix controller. All communications will be performed in a read-only manner. At no time is a data write, or push, allowed. This leaves the receiving end in a non-deterministic condition in which the receiving controller is unsure of all of the factors affecting communications. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 New software requirements must be submitted to the City with suitable lead time, a minimum of four weeks, for a security evaluation and loading. It is the developer’s responsibility to ensure all of the required tools are in place prior to beginning a start-up. General Programming Practices 3.1Languages The IEC 61131-3 standard includes for five languages: Ladder Logic(LD), Function Block Diagram(FBD), Instruction List, and Sequential Function Flowchart. The only allowable languages for all City projects and implementations are LD, and FBD. 3.1.1 Ladder Logic LD is a graphical, symbol-based language with a foundation in circuit diagrams and relay logic hardware. The logic is organized in a series of rungs composed of Boolean elements whose logical result is then applied to outputs or action elements. This can simplistically be considered to be a series of IF/THEN statements. The ladder rungs are scanned in a top-down, left to right manner enforcing strict order of execution. Figure 1 Ladder Logic HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 10 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Figure 3 Object Attribute Comments 3.4.2 Ladder Logic Comments Each LD rung must include a comment clearly describing its purpose and function. The first rung of each routine or section of rungs should summarize the overall function of the routine/section as well as the logic found on the rung. All rungs regardless of how simple or complex must include comments sufficient for someone who did not write the program to understand the function(s) of the rung. This includes all sub-rungs and subroutines. See example in Figure 4 Ladder rung comments. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 13 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Figure 4 Ladder Rung Comments 3.4.3 Function Block Diagram Comments FBD programming increases the readability of complex operations such as those with multiple paths based on Boolean functions(AND/OR/NOT) or selection blocks as well as intermediate calculations(sums and limiting functions). It is required that the programmer provide comments describing the overall function for each connected group of function blocks. Additionally, the programmer shall provide comments for each distinct intermediate calculation (Boolean function, selection, sum, limit, etc.). See example in Figure 5 Function block comments. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 14 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Figure 5 Function Block Comments 3.4.4 Subroutines Documentation Each subroutine of any type will be documented. A single “No Output” [NOP] instruction or unique coil designated as “Comment Coil” on the first rung of routine (see example in Figure 6 First rung NOP rung comments) shall be utilized to provide a location for required subroutine documentation. The following information shall be included in the rung description: - Original programming date - Initials and company of author - Descriptive summary of the routine functions HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F.
