Introduction 1.1 Background The HFC AWTP is permitted to treat 96 mgd of raw sewage with a Type I two-stage, high rate (pure oxygen and conventional fine bubble aeration) activated sludge biological domestic wastewater treatment plant. This plant is operated to reduce the Total Nitrogen(TN) in the effluent to advanced standards, using biological treatment for nitrification and filtration for denitrification, and to discharge high-level disinfected and dechlorinated effluent to Tampa Bay. Residuals generated by this facility are anaerobically digested and dewatered for land application as a Class B residual. Currently, annual average daily flows are approximately 60 mgd and peak flows are approximately 200 mgd. The rehabilitation to the HFC AWTP will consist of the implementation of improvements identified in the Facility Master Plan Capital Improvement Project List for Fiscal Years 0-5 to maintain system reliability, increase operating efficiency, and meet current and future regulatory requirements. This Technical Memorandum details the SCADA Design Criteria. ### Project Overview
**Project Overview Summary: Howard F. Curren Advanced Wastewater Treatment Plant (HFC AWTP) SCADA Migration & Infrastructure Upgrade**

The City of Tampa, Florida is soliciting proposals via RFQ No. 26-D-00017 for a comprehensive Design-Build project to migrate monitoring and control capabilities at the Howard F. Curren Advanced Wastewater Treatment Plant (HFC AWTP) from legacy systems—specifically High-Speed Query (HSQ) software—to **VTScada 12.1.58** manufactured by Trihedral, while simultaneously replacing the existing fiber optic cable network and upgrading PLC hardware. This initiative aligns with an ongoing Master Plan to upgrade operational reliability and efficiency at a facility permitted to treat approximately 96 mgd of raw sewage using a Type I two-stage activated sludge process (pure oxygen/conventional fine bubble aeration). Current annual average flows are ~60 mgd, with peaks up to 200 mgd. The estimated fee range for engineering services covering software selection, cost estimation, contract document preparation, and construction plan development is **$2,000,000 – $4,000,000**.

**Key Project Objectives & Scope:**
*   **SCADA Migration & Software Implementation:** The project involves migrating remaining facilities operating on HSQ software to VTScada. This includes transitioning PLC hardware from legacy General Electric PAC System Rx3i/90-30 series controllers to advanced Allen Bradley (Rockwell Automation) units for critical processes such as the O2 Plant, Filter Building No. 1, Main Pump Station, HPO Reactor Improvements, Dewatering Facility Replacement, Junction Chamber #1, Screen and Grit Buildings, Primary Sedimentation Tanks, Digestion, and STAR facilities.
    *   **Data Continuity:** The new system must retain historical data dating back to the year 2000 (with potential retention of earlier records) while generating combined current/historical reports.
    *   **Parallel Operation & Transition:** Migration will occur parallel to the current HSQ system until reliability is demonstrated; once validated, legacy screens may be retired or set to view-only mode before decommissioning. The project ensures continuous operation throughout the migration via online switchover capabilities.
*   **HMI & Reporting Development:** All new Human-Machine Interface (HMI) screens must utilize a specific City-provided ChangeSet file and custom library widgets per *City Standard Object Library Programming Guideline*. Designs will support hierarchical navigation from facility overviews down to individual equipment dashboards, with options for distinct servers by area.
    *   **Report Creator Selection:** The project evaluates report creator software (specifically assessing candidates like **XLReport**). Key criteria include generating accurate real-time reports for regulatory compliance and operational metrics (water flow rates, chemical usage), seamless integration with existing databases, support via Microsoft Excel technology to reduce training costs, NIST security compliance, scalability, and browser usability.
*   **Fiber Optic Network Replacement:** The project replaces fiber cables that have exceeded their useful life after decades of service. The recommended approach involves installing new parallel fiber runs through upgraded PLCs in existing conduit duct banks (e.g., E-6 or E-7) and manholes to a new patch panel near the Main Pump Station rack, establishing redundant loops for critical areas without immediately removing old infrastructure.
    *   **Redundancy & Security:** New paths prevent total shutdowns if a PLC fails. Camera feeds remain on separate fibers within these cables. The existing SONY camera network is being replaced with a GENETEC system featuring new servers and integration capabilities, alongside modernized site security including card readers at entry gates and two-way voice communication capabilities.
*   **Structural & Instrumentation Updates:** Assigning new building numbers (065 and 066) for dewatering structures and sludge feed pump stations, correcting instrument tagging per revised P&IDs, and preparing control panel data sheets against component lists from workshops.

**Technical Standards & Architecture:**
*   **Design Criteria:** Established based on workshops held between October 2021 and June 2022 (and extending into the Fiscal Years 0–5 Master Plan), adhering to ISA-95/ISA-101 standards where applicable. These cover P&ID formats (matching existing layouts), tagging systems (`DDDYYY-XXX-ZZ` format), hardware specifications (e.g., matching PLC speeds of 100Mbps with new network equipment in the operations area), and internet/site security protection.
*   **System Architecture:** The design distinguishes between local Process Control Systems (PCS) within plant boundaries and remote SCADA sites designed to run autonomously. Cybersecurity measures include VPN connections for vendor access, rigorous labeling protocols at patch panels, adherence to NIST cybersecurity standards, 21 CFR Part 11

# Procurement and Commercial Requirements Summary: Howard F. Curren Advanced Water Treatment Plant (HFC AWTP)

## Executive Overview
The City of Tampa, Florida (Project No. 20-C-00001), is soliciting proposals for the Howard F. Curren Advanced Water Treatment Plant (HFC AWTP). The procurement strategy prioritizes hardware reliability, cybersecurity, seamless integration with existing infrastructure, and long-term viability. A primary objective is replacing legacy Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs)—specifically GE PACSystems Rx3i and 90-30/70 series—to mitigate risks associated with discontinued support, price inflation from third-party parts, long lead times, and cybersecurity vulnerabilities. Concurrently, evaluations were conducted on Report Creator Software platforms to select a solution that supports plant expansion without incurring additional licensing fees (Source: *HFC AWTP Master Plan documents*).

The evaluation prioritizes **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** over extended horizons (5, 10, and 15 years). While upfront costs vary significantly—such as the high initial price for Ignition's non-cloud edition ($24,337.50)—the five-year TCO remains competitive against solutions like DreamReport due to lower long-term operational expenses. Commercial decisions must account for restrictive licensing structures; platforms requiring licenses based on concurrent users or specific tag thresholds incur additional costs as the facility scales, whereas options offering unlimited categories provide better value for growing operations. Redundancy is a critical commercial consideration, often available at 50% of main license prices to ensure business continuity without prohibitive costs (Source: *City's SCADA & PCS Recovery and Restore Policy*).

The solicitation deadline is **March 19, 2026**, at 2 P.M. Submissions must be sent in searchable PDF format under 10MB to `ContractAdministration@tampagov.net`. It is the bidder's responsibility to confirm receipt, as acknowledgements may not be provided for same-day submissions (Source: *City of Tampa - RFQ amount of City Work*).

## Compliance and Legal Obligations
*   **Statutory Adherence & Exemptions:** Firms must demonstrate familiarity with Florida Statutes Section 287.055(10) regarding document reuse, as well as Sections 119.071(3), 119.07(1), and Article I, Section 24(a) of the Florida Constitution concerning "Exempt Plans" (building plans/blueprints exempt from public records laws). The City maintains confidentiality for materials claimed under Chapter 119 exemptions.
*   **Non-Discrimination:** The City will not consider a bidder's social, political, or ideological interests when determining responsible vendor status nor give preference based on such factors. Firms are encouraged to align with the City's Climate Action and Equity Plan (Source: *City of Tampa - RFQ...*).
*   **Contact Restrictions:** During the solicitation period, protests, or appeals, no contact with City officers is permitted unless specifically stated in the solicitation. The City reserves the right to cancel, modify, reject responses, and waive irregularities at its discretion (Source: *City of Tampa - RFQ...*).
*   **Vendor Screening:** Firms or affiliates placed on the convicted vendor list under §287.133, Fla. Stat., are prohibited from submitting bids or performing work for public entities. Proposers must certify they have not been listed (Source: *City of Tampa - RFQ...*).

## Good Faith Effort (GFE) and Small Business Enterprise (SLBE) Requirements
The procurement process mandates strict adherence to the Good Faith Effort Compliance Plan (GFECP) guidelines regarding SLBE participation. All prime contractors, including certified SLBEs, must perform outreach regardless of whether they intend to self-perform all work or use subcontractors. If no subcontracting occurs, the contractor must explain why on the required forms.

*   **Solicitation Timeline:** Firms on the SLBE Goal Setting List must be solicited via written or electronic notification at least five city business days prior to the bid date (Source: *City of Tampa - RFQ...*).
*   **Documentation Standards:** Documentation must confirm that specific services were offered, plans reviewed, and assistance provided for bonding, insurance, equipment mobilization, and subcontracting opportunities. Failure to complete Forms 10, 20, or 50 renders a proposal non-responsive (Source: *HFC AWTP Master Plan documents*).

# Scope of Work: SCADA System Modernization, Upgrade, and Compliance Framework for HFC AWTP

The **Scope of Work** defines comprehensive technical standards, procedural requirements, migration objectives, security protocols, and compliance guidelines for the development, management, operation, and maintenance of Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) and Process Control Systems (PCS). This framework applies to contractors, consultants, and in-house personnel working on systems within critical infrastructure projects managed by the City. Specifically addressing the **Howard F. Curren Advanced Wastewater Treatment Plant (HFC AWTP)**—permitted to treat 96 mgd of raw sewage with a Type I two-stage activated sludge plant currently operating at approximately 60 mgd average daily flow—these standards govern instrumentation, control systems, fiber communications network replacement, site security upgrades, and Fiscal Years 0–5 improvements. The project encompasses specific Master Plan Improvement projects (including the Main Pump Station, HPO Reactor, Dewatering Facility, O2 Plant, Filter Building, and Standby Power) with a primary objective of migrating monitoring capabilities from HSQ to **VTScada** software while ensuring continuous plant operation and regulatory reporting capability.

## 1. Project Mandate and Infrastructure Upgrades
The City's mandate includes evaluating report creation software options that integrate with existing database systems to analyze data (e.g., water flow rates, chemical usage) while ensuring regulatory compliance. The scope further encompasses the rehabilitation of plant areas and specific infrastructure upgrades:

*   **Surveillance Systems:** Replacing SONY cameras with GNETEC units for all plant areas and integrating card readers into perimeter gates.
*   **Cybersecurity & Physical Security:** Installing cyber locks on building entryways, motion detectors, and planning an 8-foot high fence replacement. Access controls include keyed locks at physical access points; unauthenticated remote tools like RDP/VNC are prohibited unless protected by AAA encryption. Mandatory VPN usage is required for all remote connections to the Process Information Network (PIN), with automatic session logout enforced after 10 minutes.
*   **Network Infrastructure:** The fiber optic cable network replacement requires preparing cost estimates, construction sequencing, signed/sealed plans with specifications, final cost estimates, and contract documents for bidding. Services during this phase include shop drawing reviews and responding to Requests for Information (RFIs). Planning must account for scenarios where loss of the PLC would shut down permit analyzers and chemical feeds. Field investigations are required to verify existing conduit runs, drain manholes (including removing covers), confirm new cable routing without disturbing existing fibers, install field labeling for cables/ports matching final network diagrams, and re-route specific fibers due to structural changes like building removals.
*   **Legacy Transition:** Evaluating legacy hardware, specifically transitioning from aging General Electric (GE/PACSystems Rx3i and 90-30 series) controllers—now in "legacy" status since 2018 due to discontinued support and security risks—to equivalent alternatives that balance features against budgetary costs.
*   **Facility Coverage:** Ensuring safety, efficiency, and regulatory compliance across wastewater treatment facilities including Main Pump Stations, Intermediate Pump Stations, RAS stations, Oxygen Plants, stormwater stations, and lift station systems.

## 2. Software Architecture & Compliance
The control system utilizes **VTScada version 12.1.58** (developed by Trihedral Engineering) as the core software suite; developers are strictly prohibited from using newer versions or relying on cross-version controls for new deployments (*HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 – SCADA Standards Policies*). All applications are developed using VTScada software based on City-provided ChangeSet files to ensure application consistency. These "ChangeSet" files serve as a compendium of changes and base information rather than exact backups; they must be requested prior to programming initiation unless mission-critical updates are issued directly by City management.

*   **Tagging System:** A consistent, human-decipherable tagging system is required for all elements transferred between controllers, SCADA, HMI, OIT, gateways, and other systems to ensure unique names with a regular format throughout the entire City system (e.g., codes for site location like "H" for HFW AWTP). PLC tags must use underscores instead of hyphens due to program limitations.
*   **Validation Protocols:** All software versions, patches, application programs (PLC, HMI, OIT), and firmware updates must be validated in a limited-capacity City Test Environment before live production deployment. Updates require rigorous testing involving 100 hours of hands-off error-free operation and validation of fault tables/HMI logs prior to implementation via an approved Management of Change (MOC) plan (*Management of Change*).
*   **Access & Scheduling:** Requests for test environment access require an emailed schedule at least two weeks in advance, with confirmation provided one week prior. Updates must be performed using a City

# Controls And Integration Requirements Summary

The **Controls And Integration Requirements** for the Howard F. Curren Advanced Wastewater Treatment Plant (HFC AWTP) establish strict protocols for system configuration, communication standards, security maintenance, hardware selection, and documentation to ensure reliability, redundancy, and centralized management of the SCADA/PCS environment. These requirements are derived from HFC AWTP Master Plan documents dated December 2024 (*SCADA System Report*), June 10, 2022 (*Improvements: SCADA Design Criteria*), February 2025 (*PLC Volume*), and October 2021 (*Design-Build Workshop*). The architecture mandates adherence to unified hardware, software, security, and nomenclature standards across all programmers, vendors, and integrators. All projects must adhere to the *Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards* manuals (Volumes 1–3), with specific references to definitions in **Appendix A** and forms for deviations located in **Appendix B**.

## System Architecture, Redundancy & Resilience
The control system design prioritizes resilience through redundant server setups, robust non-volatile memory (NVM) configurations, and rigorous physical infrastructure planning. Controllers must maintain programs, data, and configurations in NVM—such as battery-backed RAM, EEPROM, or compact flash devices—to ensure the system returns to a previous state after power loss without relying on continuous power. While returning retained variable data is desirable, control code must include native initialization logic if hardware does not support it.

*   **Server Redundancy:** The infrastructure requires at least two dedicated, stand-alone servers for each logical area (collections/distributions, waste water operations, and water operations). These are configured as primary and backup units within the default server list to provide failover capabilities.
*   **Hardware Resilience & Selection Factors:** When evaluating PLCs or hardware improvements for new construction or upgrades, selection factors include functionality with Ethernet protocols when adding process equipment (e.g., VFDs/MCCs), market share as an indicator of dependability and company competitiveness, feature sets (I/O count, processor speed, memory), technical support availability, local programmer access, and supply chain readiness.
*   **Spare Capacity:** Critical components such as CPUs, I/O cards, network switches, fuses, and spare parts require specific make/model numbers rather than "or equal" interpretations to ensure reliability. Specifications mandate twenty percent (20%) spare capacity for Input/Output (I/O), including ten percent (10%) installed as actual spare I/O.
*   **UPS Specifications:** Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS) must be APC models featuring bypass switches for easy maintenance and relay outputs for alarm signaling during power transitions. UPS units shall not have Ethernet connections; relays on UPS outputs must provide alarm signals when the panel is running on backup power.

## Network Architecture, Communication Standards & Security
All plant messaging occurs over an industrial fiber optic backbone installed in concrete-encased ducts within a spoke-hub ("star") configuration originating near PLC 19, comprising 11 main trunk lines to various process regions. All fibers converge at the central network rack in the Main Pump Station; consequently, damage to conduits serving critical hubs (e.g., PLC 1, PLC 9, and PLC 18) would impact all connected devices. To mitigate this risk, redundant fiber loops are recommended at outer edges before implementation on less critical areas.

*   **Network Segmentation:** The architecture requires complete isolation of the PCS/SCADA system networks from external connections (including the internet) and separation from the business network. An industrial DMZ must act as the sole buffer between business networks and PCS/SCADA systems. Dual-homed hosts, dial-up modems, cellular routers, or direct connections to remote sites are prohibited within this zone unless specifically required for autonomous operation under least privilege principles (e.g., elevated tank monitoring).
*   **Communication Protocols:** Remote site communications strictly utilize the DNP3 protocol as specified by DNP.ORG; proprietary protocols are prohibited. The master station performs polling based on events (exceptions) rather than static values to optimize data flow, with timestamps included for sequence generation. To mitigate communication loss at remote stations (particularly those using serial or cellular modems), a "store & forward" capability is fully implemented and required in all controllers. This ensures data is maintained and read chronologically before being pushed from Tier-1 historians to the PIN's Tier-2 historian.
*   **Inter-Controller Communication:** Inter-processor communications must utilize native control elements (e.g., "MSG" instructions in ControlLogix controllers). Crucially, all inter-controller communications are restricted to a read-only manner; data writes or pushes are prohibited to prevent non-deterministic conditions where the receiving controller is unsure of affecting factors.
*   **Firewall & Access Rules:** Firewall rules in the DMZ are limited strictly to

# Network And Electrical Infrastructure Requirements

The **Network And Electrical Infrastructure** requirements mandate comprehensive documentation, design standards, and upgrade strategies to ensure system reliability, security, and consistency with industry norms (ISA/IEC 62443) for the Howard F. Curren Advanced Wastewater Treatment Plant (HFC AWTP). These standards apply across the HFC AWTP Master Plan documents (Volumes 1–3), specifically referencing SCADA Standards Volume 1 regarding design and addressing, Volume 2 discussing fiber topology and reliability improvements, and Appendix B covering supporting forms like Change Request Forms.

## Documentation & Drawings
All plant messaging must be performed over Ethernet, with cellular modems preferred for remote locations and satellite or serial radio used as approved alternatives. Comprehensive documentation is required in both electronic formats (Microsoft Visio, PDF) and hardcopy to ensure consistency with City-wide IP schemes. This includes three specific drawing types:

*   **Physical Drawings:** Must depict Layer 2 physical interconnections detailing hardware location, cable type/standard/interface speed, host details (name, description, vendor, model), MAC addresses, IP addresses, subnet masks, and all subcomponents.
*   **Logical Drawings:** Must illustrate the zones/conduits model (Layer 3) showing networks, VLANs, routers, firewalls, WAN links, default gateways per zone, host groupings by zone, and interface speeds for components with IP addresses.
*   **Supporting Inventories & Diagrams:** Requirements include a spreadsheet documenting TCP/IP settings (gateway, DNS) grouped by subnet/VLAN to prevent conflicts; a listing of all required ports/applications/services for normal and emergency operations with justification; firewall rule set documentation; Data Flow Diagrams in MS Visio showing software component communications, protocols, initiating components, and related inventory items; and an inventory tracking all network boundaries allowing external communication.

## Hardware Status & Evaluation
The facility relies on a mix of legacy and active control hardware supported by specific fiber optic architecture:

*   **PLC Systems:** The infrastructure primarily utilizes GE PACSystems Rx3i series PLCs in an "Active" state across various buildings (e.g., Buildings 10, 59, 78), alongside legacy GE 90-30 units which reached end-of-life status as of 2018. While the Rx3i I/O modules remain in production, they may face obsolescence seven years after an End of Life announcement. The evaluation also considered Rockwell (Allen Bradley) and Schneider Modicon products as viable alternatives for future upgrades, noting that GE and Rockwell consistently rank in the top three for water/wastewater processing according to CONTROL 2023 Readers' Choice Awards.
*   **Instrumentation:** Field instrumentation includes level float switches from Anchor Scientific, limit/proximity switches (Allen Bradley), valve actuators operating at either 480V or 120V, and analyzers for nutrients (ChemScan), dissolved oxygen/pH/ORP (YSI IQ SensorNet), turbidity (Swan AMI Turbiwell 7027), residual chlorine (Hydro Instruments RAH-210), temperature sensors (Dwyer "hockey puck"), and weigh scales from Metler. Power monitoring utilizes manufacturer-standard gear, while surge protection is described as a critical requirement for field devices.
*   **Electrical Components:** Electrical infrastructure includes specific control panel components and standard instruments organized within the fiber network mapped in Appendix A and Table 2-3. The facility utilizes various building types for operations, including sludge drying beds (Building 055), anaerobic digestion tanks (Building 072), standby power facilities (Building 078), and generator buildings (Buildings 080/081).

## Network Architecture & Topology
The existing fiber optic network follows a "spoke-hub" or star configuration with the main rack located near PLC-19 in the Main Pump Station. The system consists of 11 main fiber trunk lines (Lines 0 through 10) running to various process regions via concrete encased ducts.

*   **Aging Infrastructure & Upgrade Strategy:** These cables have reached the end of their useful life after decades of service and lack sufficient slack for direct replacement within the current conduit space. Consequently, upgrades must involve running new parallel fiber cables alongside existing runs (potentially utilizing MaxCell sleeves) or installing entirely new conduits if racks remain in place to prevent interruption during transition.
*   **Redundancy:** New installations should provide redundant fiber loops at outer edges (e.g., between PLC 17 and PLC 7) to ensure data transmission continues even if conduit runs are lost, which is critical for preventing plant shutdowns related to permit analyzers or chemical feed failures. Camera feeds share fibers with SCADA systems using separate strands within the same cable.
*   **Risk Mitigation:** A

# Deliverables And Commissioning Summary

The **Deliverables And Commissioning** category outlines strict protocols for software development, testing, system deployment, and validation within the City of Tampa's critical infrastructure control systems (SCADA/PCS). These requirements encompass SCADA configuration, task management, HMI graphics standards, troubleshooting displays, flow monitoring metrics, and tagging conventions.

### Software Licensing and Security Protocols
Key deliverable requirements mandate providing fully licensed programming software—specifically **Studio 5000 Logix Designer Version 36 or later**—with all licenses transferred to City management prior to site acceptance. Security is paramount: development must occur on vendor equipment, while implementation and debugging are restricted to City-owned computers that have undergone virus scanning. Direct connections of vendor hardware to the Process Control Network (PCN) are prohibited during these phases.

### Task Management & Commissioning
The system utilizes two types of tasks for execution control: **continuous** and **periodic**. To prevent processor burdening, periodic tasks must be limited to six or fewer per process/area with unique priorities ranging from 1 (highest) to 15. During commissioning, programmers must tune task settings so that no program overlaps exist; specifically, the main program overlap counter must confirm at zero, and the `FAULTLOG` object must show no minor faults related to overlaps.

### Commissioning Procedures and Validation
Commissioning procedures mandate rigorous validation before live deployment:
*   **Firmware Updates:** All updates require written approval from the OT contact and verification against compatibility matrices for all I/O cards.
*   **Test Environment Requirements:** A comprehensive test plan detailing rack layouts must be submitted prior to testing in the City's Test Environment. Tests must run hands-off without error for a minimum of 100 hours, validating PLC fault tables, HMI logs, and standard object blocks.
*   **Implementation Plan:** Upon successful testing, an implementation plan is required before modifying the live production environment.

### HMI Graphics & Data Visualization Standards
HMI design must adhere to specific visual standards:
*   **Digital Values:** Displayed as bold blue text within easily understood descriptions or surrounding rectangles if multiple values exist in a table.
*   **Alarm Status:** Must be clearly indicated alongside process conditions using consistent iconography per Section 8.0 standards.
*   **Operator Editability:** Operator editable values (e.g., setpoints) are distinguished by green, bold font. When the operator has edit rights, a bounding box and darker background appear; insufficient rights result in non-selectable text without these visual cues.

### Troubleshooting & Flow Monitoring
Troubleshooting displays must follow a hierarchical drill-down approach to provide detailed health status for field equipment, PLCs, communications drivers, controllers, and fault diagnostics at maximum depth. Specific deliverables include:
*   **Flow Totalization:** Displays spanning the plant (not exceeding it) with clustered zones for wet wells or towers, providing current, previous day, monthly, and annual totals.
*   **Runtime & Starts:** Displays offering overviews of motor/pump usage across the facility, detailing electromechanical device operation history on a daily, monthly, and annual basis.

### Tagging Standards
Equipment tags must follow an alphanumeric format (**DDDYYY-XXX-ZZ**) referencing City CMMS asset lists:
*   **DDD:** Plant area/equipment type.
*   **YYY:** Unit number.
*   **XXX:** Building number (1–91).
*   **ZZ:** Device number.

These standards apply across various facility buildings, ranging from Building 050 (DAR Junction Chamber) through Building 091 (Methanol Station).

***

*   Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 & HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 (February 2025)
*   Job no Meeting Minutes October 5, 2021

### Qualifications And Staffing
The provided evidence regarding **Qualifications And Staffing** primarily addresses regulatory compliance and vendor background rather than specific personnel credentials or project team composition:

*   **Regulatory Compliance:** The submission requirements mandate that firms must be licensed, permitted, and certified to do business in Florida. Furthermore, the firm (and its affiliates) certifies under Tampa Code Section 2-284 ("Ban-The-Box") that it has not been placed on a convicted vendor list for public entity crimes within the preceding 36 months.
*   **Vendor Background:** The evaluation notes that Inductive Automation, the provider of the Ignition SCADA software, was founded in 2003 and is headquartered in Folsom, California. They possess over 20 years of experience in industrial automation with a significant industry presence, including clients such as Fortune 500 companies and the City of Orlando.
*   **Contact Information:** Abran Mathews (amathews@inductiveautomation.com) was identified via their website as the primary point of contact for software information during the evaluation process.

The document does not contain specific details regarding individual staff qualifications, resumes, or dedicated project staffing plans beyond these general corporate and compliance attributes.

### Risks And Clarifications
**Summary: Risks and Clarifications regarding City of Tampa RFQ Compliance, Restrictions, and Technical Standards**

Firms responding to the City of Tampa's Request for Qualification (RFQ) must adhere to specific legal exemptions and operational restrictions outlined in **Section 287.055(10), Florida Statutes**, which exempts certain building plans and blueprints from public disclosure under Section 119.071(3). Respondents confirm familiarity with these statutes, agree to maintain the exempt status of such "Exempt Plans" while in their possession, and must strictly comply with **City Code Section 2-282**, which prohibits any contact between proposers and City officers/employees during the solicitation period (including protests or appeals) unless explicitly authorized.

The City reserves the right to cancel, withdraw, modify the RFQ at any time, reject responses, and waive irregularities in its best interest. While social, political, or ideological interests will not influence vendor responsibility determinations, firms seeking "Ban-The-Box" bonus points must provide specific documentation per the relevant ordinance (**https://library.municode.com/fl/tampa/ordinances/code_of_ordinances?nodeId=1171018**). Additionally, all submissions must be a single searchable PDF under 10MB containing the RFQ Transmittal Memorandum, Letter of Interest addressed to Brad L. Baird, P.E., and Statement of Qualifications evaluated against criteria including comparable experience (35 pts), qualifications (30 pts), workload/availability (5 pts), and past performance (**26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf**).

Technically, implementers are responsible for updating all City SCADA & PCS documentation following changes to the Howard F. Curren Advanced Wastewater Treatment Plant (AWTP) system. This includes maintaining records of Process Control Descriptions (PCD), alarm/historian databases, P&IDs, loop diagrams, panel drawings, standard object libraries, reports, historian change logs, and network diagrams in accordance with **SCADA Standards Volume 1** (**26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf**). These standards were established through three workshops addressing P&ID/fiber network security (Workshop 1), hardware specifications (Workshop 2), and design approach sequencing (Workshop 3) to support the facility's treatment of up to 96 mgd with peak flows near 200 mgd (**HFC AWTP Master Plan June 10, 2022**).

### Executive Summary
**Executive Summary: Howard F. Curren Advanced Wastewater Treatment Plant (HFC AWTP) Modernization Project**

The City of Tampa is soliciting proposals for a comprehensive Design-Build initiative to modernize critical infrastructure at the HFC AWTP, which treats approximately 60 mgd of raw sewage with peak capacities up to 200 mgd. The project mandates migrating legacy High-Speed Query (HSQ) monitoring systems and discontinued GE PLC hardware (discontinued since 2018) to **VTScada version 12.1.58** on Allen Bradley controllers, while strictly prohibiting newer versions or cross-version controls. This transition must occur without disrupting continuous plant operation during the parallel migration phase.

The system design enforces unified hardware and software architectures centered on VTScada 12.1.58 to ensure consistency across all controllers. Key technical requirements include:
*   **Network & Communication:** Replacing aging fiber optic networks with redundant "spoke-hub" architectures within isolated DMZs; utilizing DNP3 over Ethernet for communication protocols to prevent non-deterministic conditions and ensuring data integrity via "store & forward" capabilities at remote sites. Redundant server setups are required for every logical area, alongside the implementation of redundant communication loops.
*   **Security Protocols:** Upgrading site security cameras from SONY to GENETEC; integrating cyber locks and motion detectors; enforcing mandatory VPN usage with automatic session timeouts for remote access while prohibiting unauthenticated tools like RDP/VNC without AAA encryption. Equipment tagging follows a defined alphanumeric convention aligned with City CMMS asset lists.
*   **Operational Standards:** Implementing native initialization logic in non-volatile memory (NVM) and prioritizing hardware selection based on market share, supply chain readiness, and 20% spare I/O capacity. All software updates and patches must undergo a minimum of 100 hours of error-free testing in the City Test Environment before production deployment via an approved Management of Change (MOC) plan.

The scope further requires developing compliant HMIs that adhere to City Standard Object Library guidelines, featuring standardized graphics with clear alarm status indicators. A scalable report creator solution must be selected for regulatory compliance, evaluating options such as XLReport and Studio 5000 Logix Designer v36+. Engineering services are estimated between $2 million and $4 million, with a solicitation deadline set for March 19, 2026.

All proposals must strictly adhere to Florida statutory requirements regarding document confidentiality, non-discrimination, vendor screening (including "Ban-The-Box" certification ordinances), Good Faith Effort protocols for Small Business Enterprise participation, and regulatory exemptions regarding exempt plans. Successful execution requires maintaining comprehensive documentation of Process Control Descriptions (PCDs) and network diagrams throughout system evolution to support diverse field instrumentation including nutrient analyzers, dissolved oxygen sensors, and valve actuators across multiple facility buildings.

# System Architecture, Infrastructure, Security, and Operational Standards Strategy

## 1. Network Topology & Physical Infrastructure
The facility-wide fiber optic network utilizes a "spoke-hub" star topology originating at the Main Pump Station with 11 main trunk lines. While this existing infrastructure is over 30 years old, new runs must be installed in parallel within existing conduits or via new paths without interrupting current operations until verification confirms feasibility for sequential replacement starting with line "0."

**Redundancy & Critical Path Management:**
*   **Mandatory Loops:** Redundant fiber loops are mandatory at outer edges (e.g., between PLC 17 and PLC 7) to ensure data continuity if critical conduits or paths are compromised. The existing star topology lacks resilience against conduit damage; therefore, design requirements mandate the incorporation of these redundant loops to mitigate single points of failure.
*   **Critical Single Points of Failure:** Specific units act as central hubs where loss of communication cascades to all downstream units. Critical PLCs include 1, 9, and 18 (specifically PLC 18 in Filter Building No. 1). Loss of these key units could shut down the STAR plant; therefore, immediate reconfiguration for backup communication paths is required upon failure detection.
*   **Installation Protocols:** Field investigations involving manhole cover removal and draining may be necessary to verify conduit runs before installation. Old cables must remain in place as pull strings if conduits allow, though contingency planning for potential re-routing (e.g., PLC17 to PLC16) is required if access is blocked.
*   **Camera Feeds:** Camera feeds require full replacement and must remain on separate fibers within SCADA cables; this requirement applies strictly to all future fiber installations.

**Cabling & Panel Standards:**
*   **Wiring Specifications:** All wiring within panels must use shielded industrial CAT6 cable rated for 600V if exposed to 480V power, with strict separation of wires by voltage level and purpose. PLCs (e.g., PLC 19) may connect directly over CAT6 rather than fiber where appropriate.
*   **Hardware Selection:** New installations utilize shielded CAT6 cable within NEMA 4x rated panels (specifically Square D 9001SK series components). Preferred vendor B&B Electronics should be considered for specific components, though industrial-grade NEMA 4x components are required generally.

## 2. Hardware Lifecycle Management & Standardization
All PLC hardware must be replaced as legacy GE units entered end-of-life status in 2018; replacements are critical to mitigate unplanned downtime risks associated with third-party spare parts sourcing, long lead times, and network security vulnerabilities from discontinued updates. The evaluation is strictly limited to centralized architecture systems (Rockwell Allen-Bradley Logix, Schneider Modicon M540/580). Distributed Control Systems like Ovation or Foxboro are explicitly excluded.

**Critical Infrastructure Specifications:**
*   **PLC Standards & Memory:** Critical processes (e.g., O₂ plants, filter buildings) mandate the use of advanced Allen Bradley units with hot-swappable CPU capabilities and Non-Volatile Memory (NVM). Controllers must utilize NVM capable of withstanding environmental conditions without relying on batteries or mechanical devices. Systems cannot rely on continuous power; they must natively restore programs and data after a loss event.
*   **Legacy Hardware:** While legacy GE Rx3i/90-30 series controllers were previously in service, current replacements must utilize new standard platforms to mitigate security risks and supply chain downtime. Hardwired control I/O is mandatory for all programmers and vendors adhering to identical standards utilizing specific NEMA4X industrial grade controls (e.g., Square D).
*   **Redundancy Requirements:** PLC panels for critical dewatering structures (Buildings 065/066) must utilize dual Output Input Terminals (OITs)—one dedicated to setpoints and one to monitoring—with switchover testing required before touching critical filter processes. Control panels require 20% spare I/O capacity plus a critical inventory of spare parts for CPUs, switches, fuses, and cards.
*   **Independent Vendors:** Vendor-supplied PLC hardware for individual equipment systems is permitted, but all control signals between vendors and the system integrator's PLCs must be hard-wired to ensure reliability. Supplemental diagnostics may use Ethernet links to integrator system PLCs.

**Manufacturer Prioritization & Compatibility:**
*   **Top-Tier Alternatives:** Allen-Bradley is prioritized as a top manufacturer choice due to its high market share in water/wastewater facilities; however, evaluation must account for potential coordination gaps between hardware and software/firmware that may cause instability. Rockwell (Allen Bradley) and Schneider Modicon products were identified as viable top-tier alternatives for future upgrades based on industry rankings.
*

The City has recently completed new SCADA and Process Control System(PCS) programming, configuration and design standards. The project will ensure that the plant’s monitoring and control systems meet these standards. The objectives of the project will include, but not limited to: - Migrate the SCADA system from the existing HSQ software to VTScada software while ensuring continuous operation of the treatment plant. - Ensure that the new monitoring and control system aligns with the City's standards, including the use of approved widgets and graphic symbols for operator screens. - Implement a new software system that can generate plant operating reports needed for regulatory requirements and for monitoring plant performance. The new software system shall retain historical data from HSQ and shall be capable of generating reports using both historical and current data. - Ensure the system is scalable for future expansions and upgrades. - Provide the design and implementation plan for replacing the treatment plant’s fiber optic cable network. Services will include, but will not be limited to the following: - Complete an assessment of the existing SCADA system and related infrastructure to determine the requirements, scopes of work, priorities, and cost estimates to complete the migration to the VTScada software. - [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 1. Objectives With the changes being implemented at the HFC AWTP, the City aims to select a new report creation software to ensure efficient identification of plant performance and regulatory compliance. Given the critical nature of these reports, it is vital that the chosen software integrates seamlessly with the existing database systems and offers comprehensive functionality for data analysis and reporting. To achieve this goal, the City has tasked Hazen and Sawyer(Hazen) with reviewing and evaluating various software platforms capable of meeting the plant's rigorous requirements. As part of this mandate, Hazen conducted a thorough assessment of multiple report creation software options to determine the most suitable solution for the HFC AWTP's needs. The evaluation process involved comparing the features, capabilities, and budgetary costs of each software platform. Hazen's objective was to identify a software solution that not only provides robust reporting capabilities but also enhances the overall operational efficiency of the plant. This includes the ability to generate accurate, real-time reports that offer insight into key metrics such as water flow rates, chemical usage, and equipment status. Moreover, the ideal software should facilitate user-friendly report creation and customization, enabling plant operators and management to easily interpret data and make informed decisions. Ensuring consistency and reliability in report generation is paramount, as these reports play a crucial role in maintaining the plant’s operational excellence and regulatory compliance. The findings of Hazen's comprehensive evaluation are summarized in the Report Generator Software Evaluation document, which outlines the strengths and weaknesses of each software platform considered. Based on this evaluation, Hazen has provided a recommendation for the standard software platform to be adopted for report creation moving forward. This recommendation is aimed at optimizing the plant's reporting processes and supporting the City's ongoing commitment to operational improvement and regulatory adherence. Recommend Network Sequencing 3.1 Maintaining Monitoring and Reporting Once the P&IDs are developed and preliminary control descriptions are available, discussion on where new PLC panels will be located and how I/O will be connected to the new PLC modules will be made at milestones for each project. A detailed sequence on how to maintain monitoring and reporting will be developed as part of these discussions. These sequencing plans will be reviewed with City staff prior to completing the 90% Design. 3.2 Replacing Fiber Cables All fiber cables for each process, which includes fibers for the camera feeds, will be replaced as part of any improvement to that process. During development of the Preliminary P&IDs a thorough site investigations shall be performed to verify the existing fiber runs from the associated PLCs back to the main network fiber patch panel. This includes removing electrical manhole covers and draining the manholes. The purpose of these investigations is to determine what existing conduit runs will be used to best show on the design site plans for the fiber installation. At any point thereafter, investigations shall be performed to confirm new cable can be run through the existing conduits without removing the existing fiber cables. Final network diagram will include fiber cable labeling. Labeling for the cables and for the ports at patch panels will be installed in the field to match the network diagram. Multiple cables run through the same conduits and manholes will be depicted on the network site plan. 3.3 Conversion to VTScada VTScada HMI programming will be developed and placed in service parallel to the existing HSQ HMI system on a separate network rack and made available for interface on new workstations throughout the plant, as well as on wireless devices. As a process is made available on the VTScada HMI and is installed, tested, and passed an approved reliability demonstration period, the associated HSQ HMI display screens could be removed or made view only. The goal will be to get each process on the VTScada HMI to use for setpoint adjustment, monitoring, and/or manual override. However, there will be some parameters that may impact separate processes still controlled only through HSQ. A plan will need to be developed for such situations to provide a protocol to operations until all control parameters have been switched over to VTScada. - [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Appendix B Introduction This is Appendix B of the City of Tampa’s standards for Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) and Process Control System(PCS) programming, configuration and design standards for The City of Tampa referred to as “The City” or abbreviated as “COT” throughout this document Appendix B contains the referenced forms and documents required to perform deviations of standards and changes of the City of Tampa’s process control system. The City SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual is intended to be applied by The City’s contractors, consultants, and in-house personnel when developing or modifying any portion of The City’s control system. The control system is a utility-wide system built upon a common human machine interface(HMI) system and is comprised of the following: Process Control System(PCS): The industrial control system for processes within the boundaries of the plant(Howard F. Curren AWTP). The primary function of the system is control and monitoring of plant operations. The PCS is comprised of the local HMI subsystem and various programmable logic controllers(PLCs) and operator interface terminals(OITs). Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) System: The industrial control system for processes outside the boundaries of the plants. The primary function of this system is data acquisition; remote sites are designed to run autonomously without supervisory control function. The SCADA system serves both Water SCADA and Sewer SCADA in an integrated environment and is comprised of telemetry equipment with a supervisory HMI. This manual is divided into three main volumes plus two appendices: Volume 1 contains the control system policies and procedures that apply to the management, maintenance and modification of the control system. Volume 1 includes policies for: - Programming laptop use - Management of change(MOC) - Software and application revision control - Cybersecurity - Other related topics. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards Appendix B ­ Forms and Documents January 2025 3 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software Introduction The Howard F. Curren Advanced Water Treatment Plant(HFC AWTP) owns and operates a comprehensive Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) system. This system is primarily composed of General Electric(GE) PAC System Rx3i and 90-30 series programmable logic controllers(PLCs) at the field level, and VTScada by Trihedral at the SCADA/HMI level. SCADA systems are critical for monitoring and controlling industrial processes, providing real-time data, and enabling remote control and automation of plant operations. As part of ongoing improvement efforts, the City is currently in the process of upgrading the controllers throughout all facilities to more advanced Allen Bradley controllers, which are manufactured by Rockwell Automation. These new controllers are expected to enhance the reliability and efficiency of the plant’s operations, offering improved performance and extended capabilities. The data generated by the SCADA system is stored in a robust database, which serves as the backbone for gathering insights into the plant's performance. This data includes various metrics such as water flow rates, chemical usage, and equipment status, among others. It is essential for ensuring that the plant meets stringent regulatory requirements for water quality, which are set by environmental authorities to protect public health and the environment. To maintain these high standards, the data needs to be analyzed periodically. This analysis involves examining trends, identifying any anomalies or deviations, and generating reports that summarize the plant's performance. These reports are crucial for the plant's operators and management, as they provide a clear overview of the plant’s operations and highlight any areas that may require attention or improvement. Therefore, selecting the right report creation software is of paramount importance. The software must integrate seamlessly with the existing database, ensuring that the data is accurately captured and processed. It should also offer user-friendly interfaces for creating and customizing reports, allowing users to easily interpret the data and make informed decisions. Consistency and reliability in report generation are critical, as these reports play a vital role in maintaining the plant’s operational excellence and regulatory compliance. - [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 2 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 HMI Software The SCADA software currently utilized by the City is VTScada 12.1.58, development environment from Trihedral Engineering, contact the City for version being used. This software suite forms the basis of the system, as well as providing the majority of the data acquisition drivers. All development of HMI screens and system wide visualization will utilize this software. All new HMI screens and graphical elements are required to be developed using the latest available City provided ChangeSet file. A ChangeSet file contains the instructions required to re-create an application. This is not an exact backup but a compendium of changes and the base information to reproduce a functional application. In general, ChangeSets are used to distribute new applications, distribute updates to an existing application, or add extra features to OEM layers. This ChangeSet file will contain all of the latest container tags, display definitions, and library widgets needed to support development activities. Each project shall request, via email to the City’s project manager (or assigned City Operational Technology(OT) contact), a copy of the current ChangeSet application immediately prior to initiating programming activities. Although updates to the standard may occasionally occur, the files provided on request date shall be utilized throughout the project. Only those updates which are determined mission critical (have severe consequences to operations, life or equipment safety) will be handled on a case by case basis through direct notice to programmers by the City’s project manager or assigned OT contact. The distribution ChangeSet file will be re-created periodically as functional elements are added and/or enhanced. Additionally, other exports will be created as required in order to maintain an appropriate level of application consistency between the City’s templates and any standalone in development. The City’s SCADA system is a managed VTScada application which has been developed using custom City developed library widgets. Refer to the City Standard Object Library Programming Guideline for details on the currently available object template library or submit an email request to the City’s project manager. - [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Replacement of the Existing Fiber Cables: The existing fiber cables included in the network are past their useful life having been installed several decades ago. If the cables were to be replaced, it would have to be done with parallel new fiber cables that allow the existing network to function without interruption. However, the 11 fiber cables entering through a conduit beneath the main patch panel in the Main Pump Station Building network room leave the conduit with very little room to pass through additional fiber. Although it’s possible to utilize MaxCell sleeves to snake new fiber cables through the one existing conduit, it would be easier to install a new conduit run if the network rack were to remain where it is currently located. 2.2.3 Recommended Fiber Network Upgrades Given the available documentation on the fiber network and the site investigation, the following is recommended for upgrading the network:  Run new fiber cable in existing fiber cable conduit from new control systems at each building installed under each individual amendment package (to the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvement project) to connect to a new patch panel near the existing network rack. New fiber runs from this new patch panel are to the be connected to the existing network equipment. The existing network rack is located within the existing Main Pump Station network equipment room.  Install new fiber cables from the upgraded PLCs associated with process improvement, utilizing existing conduit duct bank and manholes, through either electrical manhole E-6 or E-7, then through the new conduits to the new network rack.  Gradually remove existing fiber runs as associated processes are upgraded and successfully running off the new network.  Keep both networks operational until the last replacement fiber run is complete. The existing plant fiber patch panel located near the existing network rack is to be kept in service until all processes have been updated and connected with new fiber runs upon which the existing patch panel can be removed.  Provide redundant fiber loops at the outer edges. As an example, a new fiber run between PLC 17 and PLC 7 would allow data transmission for PLCs in either quadrant back to the main network rack regardless of where conduit runs might be lost. PLC 18 (located in Filter Building No. 1) is the most critical to have fiber redundancy as loss of this PLC would result in loss of the plant’s permit analyzers and flow-proportional chemical feeds and samplers, which could shut down STAR. Fiber loops would require some reconfiguration of the programming to designate the “normal” communication path and a backup path if normal route is lost. During the design phase of each improvement to HFC AWTP, the existing network diagram and the new network diagram will be updated to maintain accurate records of the both systems until the existing is fully removed from service. Camera feeds are on the same fiber cable as SCADA, utilizing separate fibers. This approach shall be continued for new fiber cables. This gives the XLReport software platform a ranking of 2 for the specialized functions category. XLReport can integrate with several different kinds of HMIs as it has a multitude of adapters after considering the ranking of other categories, the software platform ranks 2 for the HMI integration category. Software security and bug updates are provided for the software platform whenever there are any vulnerabilities identified. maintenance is provided through the subscription model, this is comparable to other offerings, because of this the XLReport platform ranks 2 for the software support and maintenance A redundant server can be installed at a cost of 50% of the main license which is standard with the other offerings. The software platform ranks 3 on the redundancy category for this reason. SmartSights is a small company with 50-200 employees that is based out of Austin Texas, it is a medium sized company that brings up about 79,000 matches on google with more employees than the other domestic option. Because of this it ranks 1 on the company prominence category Customer service was relatively quick with the sales representatives being able to provide technical information on the 2nd call and being able to answer questions relatively quickly. Although the response was quick the quality of the reports that were provided were substandard as they were split over several pages and incomplete. Because of this the company ranks 2 on the customer service category. Advantages XLReport has the advantage of having an unlimited number of tags that are available to be tracked, this gives the software great flexibility and a great ability to scale without worry of having to handle an additional expense if more tags are required to be added. This gives the software great flexibility and can allow the plant to grow and expand without worry about the number of tags. Another advantage for XLReport is that the technology they are based around is Microsoft Excel which makes the software intuitive to use and make it easier for plant operators to be proficient in than other software platforms. Most individuals have worked with or seen Excel before which can decrease training costs. Like the other software platforms analyzed XLReport is also able to be accessed through a web portal on a browser to get added flexibility for the operators, this will allow the operators to access report making abilities on any device that is able to open a web browser. XLReport is able to communicate with VTScada and pull from the database to generate reports. Disadvantages One of the biggest disadvantages of XLReport is the fact that the software platform is not NIST compliant. This is a significant disadvantage as this means that the software could be vulnerable and provide a backdoor for any potential malicious actors to gain access to The City’s systems. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] The historical data from HSQ is to be saved and made available in the VTScada system. This shall be addressed in the Main Pump Station and HPO Reactor Improvements project currently under design. At a minimum, data starting from the beginning of 2000 shall be transferred. 2.3.2 PLC / HMI Software Integration The design team will coordinate with the process integrator and the City to develop the software. City staff will participate in actual development of graphics, tag creation, and programming to become more familiar with VTScada for future development and system troubleshooting. At the beginning of each construction phase, a pre-construction instrumentation coordination meeting will be held to review software development scope of work and to determine the role and responsibilities. The City staff will provide oversight of the HMI screen and PLC controls development. For the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvements Dewatering Facility Replacement project, RTP will be developing the HMI screens with oversight from the City. These displays will be used to set standards for displays to provide for all improvements. 2.3.3 HMI Security The following extent of exposure to internet connectivity will be incorporated into the SCADA design for each process improvement:  VPN connection for remote setup for vendor access to HMI when allowed by the City.  Similar to existing, access to HMI by City staff through handheld devices and laptops to connect through internet VPN over wireless cellular means. Previous efforts to create wireless hotspots proved to be inadequate at this site.  New controls at the PLC level on the fiber network shall be accessible through similar wireless internet VPN connectivity. 2.4 Site Security & Monitoring The existing plant camera system only includes ten(10) cameras. These are listed in Appendix G. With each improvement, design will include new cameras to eventually be able to monitor all areas within the plant. Location, number, and type of cameras will be discussed with City staff as part of the detail design phase of each HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvement. The existing SONY system is to be replaced with a GENETEC system, including a new server. The initial upgrade will occur during the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvements Dewatering Facility Replacement, which is concurrently under construction. All existing cameras will be transitioned to the new GENETEC system once it is operational. Entry gates will utilize GENETEC card reader system with interface to the new base system to allow monitoring of entry points, two-way voice communication for visitors, and remote operation of gates. Additionally, the City may replace the site perimeter fence with an eight-foot(8’) tall fence at some point in the future. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software Introduction The Howard F. Curren Advanced Water Treatment Plant(HFC AWTP) owns and operates a comprehensive Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) system. This system is primarily composed of General Electric(GE) PAC System Rx3i and 90-30 series programmable logic controllers(PLCs) at the field level, and VTScada by Trihedral at the SCADA/HMI level. SCADA systems are critical for monitoring and controlling industrial processes, providing real-time data, and enabling remote control and automation of plant operations. As part of ongoing improvement efforts, the City is currently in the process of upgrading the controllers throughout all facilities to more advanced Allen Bradley controllers, which are manufactured by Rockwell Automation. These new controllers are expected to enhance the reliability and efficiency of the plant’s operations, offering improved performance and extended capabilities. The data generated by the SCADA system is stored in a robust database, which serves as the backbone for gathering insights into the plant's performance. This data includes various metrics such as water flow rates, chemical usage, and equipment status, among others. It is essential for ensuring that the plant meets stringent regulatory requirements for water quality, which are set by environmental authorities to protect public health and the environment. To maintain these high standards, the data needs to be analyzed periodically. This analysis involves examining trends, identifying any anomalies or deviations, and generating reports that summarize the plant's performance. These reports are crucial for the plant's operators and management, as they provide a clear overview of the plant’s operations and highlight any areas that may require attention or improvement. Therefore, selecting the right report creation software is of paramount importance. The software must integrate seamlessly with the existing database, ensuring that the data is accurately captured and processed. It should also offer user-friendly interfaces for creating and customizing reports, allowing users to easily interpret the data and make informed decisions. Consistency and reliability in report generation are critical, as these reports play a vital role in maintaining the plant’s operational excellence and regulatory compliance. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 5 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 4.2 Ignition Cost Ignition non cloud edition has an upfront cost of $24,337.50 with a yearly support cost of $4867.5. The city is expected to spend a total of $29,205.00 on the first year, $53,542.50 for five years, $77,880 for ten years and $102,217.50 at fifteen years. Because of this cost the software platform lands a ranking of 3 on the pricing as although it has a higher initial cost than DreamReport over the course of a few years the cost is lower than DreamReport. Overview Ignition in the non-cloud version of the software lacks the functionality of being able to be set up so that multiple users can access different kinds of data, view and create reports. This would mean that to gain this feature the software would need to be run in a hybrid method that requires a cloud version of the software to be run in parallel. Because of this the software scored a ranking of 2 for the user dashboard configuration, and 1 for custom report creation. The platform is able to integrate with VTScada depending on how the database is set up. This is comparable to other offerings; Ignition scored a rank of 1 for the VTScada integration category after taking into consideration the need to have a link between the cloud edition of the software to VTScada as well. Ignition can also integrate with LIMS as it has many generic communication systems, and it should be able to pull the data from any system if it uses a generic system. Due to the required cloud link to the plant network, it also scored a 1 in the database integration category. Ignition can schedule reports that can be emailed out to users, but because it lacks other significant features in this category to make it stand out it scored a ranking of 1 in the category of automated reports. NIST cybersecurity standards are self-attested to be followed by Ignition however it is not certified to be following these standards which could leave room for uncertainty. Due to this level of uncertainty the software scores a rank of 2 for the Security category. The software platform can be set up to meet 21 CFR part 11 compliance requirements like the other platforms. The software platform ranks 1 for 21CFR part 11 after taking into consideration other categories’ rankings. Browser usability is not available without having the hybrid model due to this lack of capability from the platform in the non cloud version of the software it scores a rank of 1 for the browser usability category. Ignition has an exceptional capability to expand without limit with an unlimited number of tags and users being able to be used this unlimited ability to expand allows the software platform to score a rank of 4 for the scalability category due to the lack of any restrictions on the size of the platform. Ignition is also able to support custom functions and SQL scripts like the other platforms after considering the rankings on other categories it scored a rank of 1 for the specialized functions category HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 8 SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 Cybersecurity Policy This section contains cybersecurity requirements for all network components recommended by Hazen at a minimum, however the City shall employ the best practices to promote SCADA & PCS security. This is in addition to specific configuration requirements for integration into PCS/SCADA system networks covered in other sections. All persons are required to review and acknowledge the City’s policies before accessing the PCS/SCADA system. 8.1General 1) All servers, workstations laptops and other devices shall be managed by City policy. 2) The use of unencrypted communication(e.g. email, etc.) for PCS/SCADA system related correspondence which includes information related to cybersecurity and networking is prohibited. All electronically transmitted documents shall be password protected; the password shall be shared with the intended participants via alternate out of band communications channels(e.g. voice, text, etc.). 8.2Asset management 1) Work Management - PCS/SCADA system assets shall be entered into and maintained using the City’s asset management system. Additions, changes, and deletions shall be performed in the system within one week. 2) Device Inventory - Maintain an inventory of all physical devices throughout the PCS/SCADA system networks. Include all network nodes and communication- capable devices (e.g., servers, workstations, laptops, OIT, PLCs, PACs, switches, routers, firewalls, etc.). This inventory shall include all devices, whether connected to the PCS/SCADA system networks or not. Maintain the inventory in a spreadsheet. Include the supplier, manufacturer, model number, serial number, subnet, network address, hardware address, machine name, and installation location. 3) Software Inventory - Maintain an inventory of all software platforms and applications (e.g., operating systems, HMI applications, historians, OPC servers, programming software, IDE software, drivers, office productivity software, utilities, etc.). Maintain the inventory in a spreadsheet. Include supplier, product vendor, product name, product version, patching level, firmware version, licensing methods, and licensing keys. Maintain additional information as required to allow reinstallation of software after final acceptance. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 1. Introduction 1.1 Background The HFC AWTP is permitted to treat 96 mgd of raw sewage with a Type I two-stage, high rate (pure oxygen and conventional fine bubble aeration) activated sludge biological domestic wastewater treatment plant. This plant is operated to reduce the Total Nitrogen(TN) in the effluent to advanced standards, using biological treatment for nitrification and filtration for denitrification, and to discharge high-level disinfected and dechlorinated effluent to Tampa Bay. Residuals generated by this facility are anaerobically digested and dewatered for land application as a Class B residual. Currently, annual average daily flows are approximately 60 mgd and peak flows are approximately 200 mgd. The rehabilitation to the HFC AWTP will consist of the implementation of improvements identified in the Facility Master Plan Capital Improvement Project List for Fiscal Years 0-5 to maintain system reliability, increase operating efficiency, and meet current and future regulatory requirements. This Technical Memorandum details the SCADA Design Criteria. These criteria will be utilized for all instrumentation and control design performed as part of these improvements. 1.2 Workshops Three(3) workshops were held with the City in developing the SCADA Design Criteria. The findings of these workshops are documented herein and establish the minimum standards to use for developing P&IDs, specifying software and hardware, and guide the overall approach and sequencing for the replacement of the facility wide fiber communications network. The workshops addressed the following topics:  Workshop 1 - P&ID standards, facility fiber network improvements, approach to PLC/HMI software development, and internet/site security protection  Workshop 2 - Hardware standards  Workshop 3 - Design approach Meeting notes are provided in Appendix E for reference. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] CITY OF TAMPA, FLORIDA - RFQ objective of the project is to migrate the remaining facilities operating on HSQ software to the VTScada software so that the entire plant is monitored and controlled through the VTScada software system. The City has recently completed new SCADA and Process Control System(PCS) programming, configuration and design standards. The project will ensure that the plant’s monitoring and control systems meet these standards. The objectives of the project will include, but not limited to: - Migrate the SCADA system from the existing HSQ software to VTScada software while ensuring continuous operation of the treatment plant. - Ensure that the new monitoring and control system aligns with the City's standards, including the use of approved widgets and graphic symbols for operator screens. - Implement a new software system that can generate plant operating reports needed for regulatory requirements and for monitoring plant performance. The new software system shall retain historical data from HSQ and shall be capable of generating reports using both historical and current data. - Ensure the system is scalable for future expansions and upgrades. - Provide the design and implementation plan for replacing the treatment plant’s fiber optic cable network. Services will include, but will not be limited to the following: - Complete an assessment of the existing SCADA system and related infrastructure to determine the requirements, scopes of work, priorities, and cost estimates to complete the migration to the VTScada software. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3. Recommend Network Sequencing 3.1 Maintaining Monitoring and Reporting Once the P&IDs are developed and preliminary control descriptions are available, discussion on where new PLC panels will be located and how I/O will be connected to the new PLC modules will be made at milestones for each project. A detailed sequence on how to maintain monitoring and reporting will be developed as part of these discussions. These sequencing plans will be reviewed with City staff prior to completing the 90% Design. 3.2 Replacing Fiber Cables All fiber cables for each process, which includes fibers for the camera feeds, will be replaced as part of any improvement to that process. During development of the Preliminary P&IDs a thorough site investigations shall be performed to verify the existing fiber runs from the associated PLCs back to the main network fiber patch panel. This includes removing electrical manhole covers and draining the manholes. The purpose of these investigations is to determine what existing conduit runs will be used to best show on the design site plans for the fiber installation. At any point thereafter, investigations shall be performed to confirm new cable can be run through the existing conduits without removing the existing fiber cables. Final network diagram will include fiber cable labeling. Labeling for the cables and for the ports at patch panels will be installed in the field to match the network diagram. Multiple cables run through the same conduits and manholes will be depicted on the network site plan. 3.3 Conversion to VTScada VTScada HMI programming will be developed and placed in service parallel to the existing HSQ HMI system on a separate network rack and made available for interface on new workstations throughout the plant, as well as on wireless devices. As a process is made available on the VTScada HMI and is installed, tested, and passed an approved reliability demonstration period, the associated HSQ HMI display screens could be removed or made view only. The goal will be to get each process on the VTScada HMI to use for setpoint adjustment, monitoring, and/or manual override. However, there will be some parameters that may impact separate processes still controlled only through HSQ. A plan will need to be developed for such situations to provide a protocol to operations until all control parameters have been switched over to VTScada.
