Ignition’s offering of a permanent license for the on-site server has regular security updates. The cloud version of the server will be updated with the latest features and security updates. Support can be provided by Ignition for the on-site server which would help ensure that the platform runs smoothly. This is comparable to other platforms however the necessity of having to manage a cloud server in addition to the on-site server gives the platform a rank of 1 for the Software support and maintenance category. Ignition offers a redundancy license that is provided at 50% the cost of the main license which is in line with other offerings and scores it a rank of 2 on the redundancy category. Inductive Automation is a small company based out of Folsom California. With between 11 to 50 employees and 9 million matches on Google. After considering the size of the other companies reviewed it scores a ranking of 2 in the company prominence category. Customer service was quick and knowledgeable, with pricing clearly visible on the website. Sales representatives were quick to answer messages, schedule meetings, answer questions and return quotes in a timely manner. Ignition scores a ranking of 3 on the customer service category. Advantages Ignition is an all-in-one solution where licensing is based upon the server license and the amount of time that the cloud server is running. The Ignition platform has an unlimited number of tags can be tracked, and an unlimited number of people can edit and create reports at the same time. This allows for great flexibility as once the software is purchased no additional costs will concur for additional licensing for tags or users, so The City is able to expand their processes as much as they desire. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 1 SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 Introduction This is Volume 1 of the City of Tampa’s standards for Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) and Process Control System(PCS) programming, configuration and design standards for The City of Tampa referred to as “The City” or abbreviated as “COT” throughout this document Volume 1 is a compilation of policies and procedures and provides both guidance and specific requirements for control system work. The City SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual is intended to be applied by the City’s contractors, consultants, and in-house personnel when developing or modifying any portion of the City’s control system. The control system is a utility-wide system built upon a common human machine interface(HMI) system and is comprised of the following: Process Control System(PCS): The industrial control system for processes within the boundaries of the plant(Howard F. Curren AWTP). The primary function of the system is control and monitoring of plant operations. The PCS is comprised of the local HMI subsystem and various programmable logic controllers(PLCs) and operator interface terminals(OITs). Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) System: The industrial control system for processes outside the boundaries of the process areas, ability to monitor and/or control the process from the control room from outside the plant. The primary function of this system is data acquisition; processes or remote sites are designed to run autonomously without supervisory control function. The SCADA system serves both remote sites such as lift stations and wastewater treatment plant facility in an integrated environment and is comprised of telemetry equipment with a supervisory HMI. This manual is divided into three main volumes plus two appendices: Volume 1 contains the control system policies and procedures that apply to the management, maintenance and modification of the control system. Volume 1 includes policies for: - Programming laptop use - Management of change(MOC) - Software and application revision control - Cybersecurity - Other related topics. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - Policies Volume 1 of 3 February 2025 6 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] CITY OF TAMPA, FLORIDA - RFQ objective of the project is to migrate the remaining facilities operating on HSQ software to the VTScada software so that the entire plant is monitored and controlled through the VTScada software system. The City has recently completed new SCADA and Process Control System(PCS) programming, configuration and design standards. The project will ensure that the plant’s monitoring and control systems meet these standards. The objectives of the project will include, but not limited to: - Migrate the SCADA system from the existing HSQ software to VTScada software while ensuring continuous operation of the treatment plant. - Ensure that the new monitoring and control system aligns with the City's standards, including the use of approved widgets and graphic symbols for operator screens. - Implement a new software system that can generate plant operating reports needed for regulatory requirements and for monitoring plant performance. The new software system shall retain historical data from HSQ and shall be capable of generating reports using both historical and current data. - Ensure the system is scalable for future expansions and upgrades. - Provide the design and implementation plan for replacing the treatment plant’s fiber optic cable network. Services will include, but will not be limited to the following: - Complete an assessment of the existing SCADA system and related infrastructure to determine the requirements, scopes of work, priorities, and cost estimates to complete the migration to the VTScada software. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 1. Objectives With the changes being implemented at the HFC AWTP, the City aims to select a new report creation software to ensure efficient identification of plant performance and regulatory compliance. Given the critical nature of these reports, it is vital that the chosen software integrates seamlessly with the existing database systems and offers comprehensive functionality for data analysis and reporting. To achieve this goal, the City has tasked Hazen and Sawyer(Hazen) with reviewing and evaluating various software platforms capable of meeting the plant's rigorous requirements. As part of this mandate, Hazen conducted a thorough assessment of multiple report creation software options to determine the most suitable solution for the HFC AWTP's needs. The evaluation process involved comparing the features, capabilities, and budgetary costs of each software platform. Hazen's objective was to identify a software solution that not only provides robust reporting capabilities but also enhances the overall operational efficiency of the plant. This includes the ability to generate accurate, real-time reports that offer insight into key metrics such as water flow rates, chemical usage, and equipment status. Moreover, the ideal software should facilitate user-friendly report creation and customization, enabling plant operators and management to easily interpret data and make informed decisions. Ensuring consistency and reliability in report generation is paramount, as these reports play a crucial role in maintaining the plant’s operational excellence and regulatory compliance. The findings of Hazen's comprehensive evaluation are summarized in the Report Generator Software Evaluation document, which outlines the strengths and weaknesses of each software platform considered. Based on this evaluation, Hazen has provided a recommendation for the standard software platform to be adopted for report creation moving forward. This recommendation is aimed at optimizing the plant's reporting processes and supporting the City's ongoing commitment to operational improvement and regulatory adherence. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 6 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] **3.3 Industry Reviews** The GE product line used at the HFC AWTP is one of many PLC manufacturers in the United States and this product line has a significant share of the municipal wastewater industry. Two other prominent PLC manufacturers that have significant market share in the municipal wastewater include: - **Rockwell(Allen Bradley Logix series)** - **Schneider(Modicon M540/580)** The products of these manufacturers, along with those of **GE(RX3i series)**, were selected as viable options to be considered for further evaluation in the PLC upgrades at the HFC AWTP as all three are widely known, utilized, and recognized in the municipal wastewater treatment market. The PLC evaluation performed here focused on PLC using a centralized architecture with a single processing unit and not Distributed Control Systems(DCS) such as Ovation, Foxboro and PlantPAX and thereby no reference to DCS is covered in this evaluation. - [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 1. Introduction The Howard F. Curren Advanced Water Treatment Plant(HFC AWTP) owns and operates a Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) system currently composed mainly of General Electric(GE) PACSystems Rx3i and 90-30 series programmable logic controllers(PLCs) at the field level. General Electric was acquired by Emerson in February, 2019. This document refers to GE equipment as GE to maintain familiarity with City’s existing Standards. As manufacturers continue to make advancements in product development, older versions of hardware advance towards “legacy” status. Products in legacy status are taken out of production and typically are no longercommercially available from the manufacturer. Replacement parts must then be sourced from third party sellers, increasing the risk for price inflation and long lead times. In that case, a set of spares must be stringently maintained to prevent unnecessary down time in the event of a failure. Additionally, manufacturers eventually stop developing and releasing software updates for legacy controllers, which may pose a security risk on network connected systems. A majority of HFC AWTP’s PLC hardware has entered the legacy phase. While PLC upgrades may be costly up front, updating the PLCs reduces the risk of unplanned downtime and enhances network security. 2. Objectives A majority of the existing PLCs reached legacy status in 2018 (GE 90-30 and GE 90-70 series). PLC hardware for these products are no longer supported by the manufacturer. With the impending replacement of all PLC hardware as part of the on-going design-build project, the City has chosen to evaluate alternatives to develop a new standard for PLCs at the plant. Existing PLCs at the plant were evaluated by the City. As a result, Hazen and Sawyer(Hazen) was asked to review the existing PLC hardware to determine equivalent alternatives. Four major PLC vendors were evaluated for consideration whenever existing PLC line ups are to be replaced as part of on-going process improvements at HFC AWTP. The different PLC models were compared including features and budgetary costs for full system replacement. The findings are summarized in this PLC Hardware Evaluation along with a recommendation for the standard PLC hardware platform going forward. 3. Assessment of Existing PLCs & Industry Review 3.1 Existing PLCs An audit was performed by McKim & Creed & Hazen in June 2018 with assistance from HFC AWTP staff to quantify the number of existing PLCs installed at the facility. HFC AWTP staff provided information about the existing PLCs at these sites including existing network diagrams, model of PLC at each location, quantity, for review and use for this evaluation. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System PLC Hardware Evaluation 6 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 3.1.12 HMI Integration The software should be easy to integrate into The City’s HMI - VTScada. This integration is crucial for a wastewater operator because VTScada is a widely used HMI system that provides realtime monitoring and control of plant operations. By ensuring compatibility with VTScada, the software allows operators to seamlessly access and manage all relevant data through a single interface, enhancing operational efficiency and reducing the risk of errors. This not only supports proactive maintenance and troubleshooting but also helps in ensuring regulatory compliance and optimizing plant performance. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. 3.1.13 Software support and maintenance The software should have support security updates and technical support. This is important for a wastewater operator because security is paramount in protecting sensitive data and ensuring the integrity of the system. Regular security updates help safeguard the system against potential cyber threats, vulnerabilities, and unauthorized access, which can compromise the operation of the plant. By having these updates included without extra charges, operators can maintain a high level of security consistently, ensuring continuous protection without worrying about additional expenses. This proactive approach not only secures the plant's operations but also aligns with regulatory standards and best practices for infrastructure management. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. 3.1.14 Redundancy The software should be able to be set up with a redundant system to allow for the use of the report creator even if the main server is taken offline. This is crucial for a wastewater operator because redundancy ensures continuous access to critical reporting tools, minimizing downtime and maintaining smooth operations. In the event of a system failure or maintenance, having a redundant setup means that data analysis can proceed without interruption, thus supporting timely decision-making and regulatory compliance. This resilience is essential in safeguarding the plant's functionality and ensuring that operators can respond promptly to any issues that arise. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. 3.1.15 Company prominence The software company should be a reputable company with good industry presence and expertise. This is important for a wastewater operator because a reputable company ensures that the software is reliable, secure, and compliant with industry standards. A company with a strong industry presence is more likely to be aware of the latest advancements and regulatory changes, ensuring that the software remains up-todate and effective in managing the complex requirements of wastewater treatment. Expertise in the field means that the company can provide high-quality support and tailored solutions, addressing specific operational challenges and improving overall plant performance. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Software Comparison 3.1 Software Requirements 3.1.1 Cost The software should be priced competitively and not require unnecessary expenses. This is important for a wastewater operator because managing a wastewater treatment plant involves many ongoing costs, and budget constraints are often a significant concern. By ensuring that the software is price competitive, operators can allocate resources more effectively across various critical areas, including maintenance, staffing, and system upgrades. Competitive pricing also allows for the possibility of adopting other necessary technologies or services without stretching the budget too thin. Moreover, avoiding unnecessary expenses helps in achieving long-term financial sustainability, ensuring that the plant can continue to operate efficiently and respond to any emergent needs without financial strain. Due to the financial importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.2 User Dashboard customization On the user’s own dashboard, the software should have the ability to create data trends and graphs saved to the user’s account for private viewing or public access by other users. The dashboard will include customization for the user to create their own pages with tag linking for simple key performance indicators and allow the display to be saved for future viewing or sharing. This functionality is particularly important for plant operators as it enables efficient monitoring of critical plant metrics, such as water quality indicators and equipment performance. By having the ability to customize and save a dashboard, operators can quickly access vital information, make informed decisions, and ensure compliance with regulatory standards. Due to the operational impact of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.3 Custom Report Creation On the user’s own dashboard, the software should have the ability to create data trends and graphs saved to the user’s account for private viewing or public access by other users. - [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 4.1.7 Longevity Established and reputable PLC manufacturers have a proven track record of delivering quality products and reliable support. Based on customer reviews, industry feedback, and the company's history to gauge its product line implementation and commitment to customer satisfaction were used for ranking. 4.1.8 Compatibility with HMI(VT SCADA) software HFC AWTP uses VT SCADA as HMI software. Evaluated PLCs were ranked based on the compatibility with VT SCADA. This is to ensure that the PLC can be easily incorporated into the existing control system and that the operator interface is simple. 4.1.9 Library Components Common industrial protocol(CIP) represents a well-defined data representation, connection management, and messaging protocol that operates over an independent transport and physical layer. A PLC using a CIP is considered flexible for integrating to any HMI software like VT SCADA, it provides simplicity in Automatic Tag Creation, a huge benefit. PLCs are compared and ranking is based on availability and extent of library components in VT SCADA. 4.1.10 Cost Another important issue for evaluation is cost of PLC, which includes the cost of hardware, software, overall value and also considering factors beyond the initial purchase cost. Additionally, factored in with the evaluation are the cost of communication modules, and accessories required for HFC AWTP applications. 4.1.11 Asset and Program management PLC brands with a centralized tool capable of securing, managing, versioning, tracking and reporting automation-related asset information are evaluated within this category. 4.1.12 Functionality and Diagnostics feasibility A wide varity of software and hardware are necessary to enhance the functionality and diagnostics of PLCs. Best choice is given to the PLC that has diagnostics and troubleshooting tools for swiftly identifying defects and difficulties. This can help with troubleshooting the various faults so as to reduce downtime. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. - [Proposal / Procurement Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 3.1.16 Customer service The company must have representatives that are easy to reach out to and get in touch with, the representatives must also be well versed with the software and its capabilities. The company must have representatives that are easy to reach out to and get in touch with, and the representatives must also be well-versed with the software and its capabilities. This is crucial for a wastewater operator because having accessible and knowledgeable support ensures that any issues or questions can be promptly addressed, minimizing downtime and maintaining smooth operations. Quick and efficient customer service can make a significant difference in resolving technical problems, optimizing the use of the software, and ensuring that the wastewater treatment plant operates at peak efficiency. This level of support is essential for handling the complex and critical nature of wastewater management, where delays or errors can have significant consequences. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. 3.2 Software comparison matrix The software platforms DreamReport, Ignition, PI System, and XLReport are all reliable reporting software platforms and within each of the evaluation factors no one supplier was always top ranked as each software has differences in making a recommendation difficult. A matrix decision analysis will be used with weighted values to identify the most optimal software solution. Each Software was ranked relative to each other on a scale of 1-4 with 4 being considered the most favorable and 1 being the least favorable for that category. Then a requirement importance factor will be applied to each parameter, a 5 being considered the highest importance and 1 being considered the of least importance. Rankings were assigned based on the review of the software representative responses as well as further research when needed. Weight factors were assigned based on Hazen’s perception of what would be most important to HFC AWTP staff. A summation of each of the ranking score times the respective weight factor was made. The Software supplier with the highest overall number was determined to be the best choice based on Hazen’s evaluation. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. This volume provides both guidance and specific examples to set up these interfaces The City SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual is intended to be applied by the City’s contractors, consultants, and in-house personnel when developing or modifying any portion of the City’s control system. The control system is a utility-wide system built upon a common human machine interface(HMI) system and is comprised of the following: Process Control System(PCS): The industrial control system for processes within the boundaries of the plant(Howard F. Curren AWTP). The primary function of the system is control and monitoring of plant operations. The PCS is comprised of the local HMI subsystem and various programmable logic controllers(PLCs) and operator interface terminals(OITs). Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) System: The industrial control system for processes outside the boundaries of the process areas, ability to monitor and/or control the process from the control room from outside the plant. The primary function of this system is data acquisition; processes or remote sites are designed to run autonomously without supervisory control function. The SCADA system serves both remote sites such as lift stations and wastewater treatment plant facility in an integrated environment and is comprised of telemetry equipment with a supervisory HMI. This manual is divided into three main volumes plus two appendices: Volume 1 contains the control system policies and procedures that apply to the management, maintenance and modification of the control system. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation Glossary Ethernet: A computer networking standard that defines a family of protocols, communication media, and device interfaces. Human Machine Interface(HMI): A computer that runs graphical interface software for monitoring and supervisory control of a control system. Local Area Network(LAN): Made up of the connection media, networking components, and nearby devices that communicate with each other. Remote Telemetry Unit: A controller for local equipment that communicates with the master telemetry unit. Uninterruptible Power Supply(UPS): A battery-backed source fed by the utility service during normal operation. Variable Frequency Drive(VFD): A motor control that allows for continuous variations in running speed. Virtual Private Network(VPN): A secure means of connecting a physically remote host to a local area network, typically via the Internet. VTScada: The software application used at the HFC AWTP to read and write real-time data to and from controllers. Win 411: A component of Win 911 that enables simple audio reporting of analog values. Win 911: A software application used to annunciate and acknowledge alarms to/from remote users. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] **3.3 Industry Reviews** The GE product line used at the HFC AWTP is one of many PLC manufacturers in the United States and this product line has a significant share of the municipal wastewater industry. Two other prominent PLC manufacturers that have significant market share in the municipal wastewater include: - **Rockwell(Allen Bradley Logix series)** - **Schneider(Modicon M540/580)** The products of these manufacturers, along with those of **GE(RX3i series)**, were selected as viable options to be considered for further evaluation in the PLC upgrades at the HFC AWTP as all three are widely known, utilized, and recognized in the municipal wastewater treatment market. The PLC evaluation performed here focused on PLC using a centralized architecture with a single processing unit and not Distributed Control Systems(DCS) such as Ovation, Foxboro and PlantPAX and thereby no reference to DCS is covered in this evaluation. (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 4.1.7 Longevity Established and reputable PLC manufacturers have a proven track record of delivering quality products and reliable support. Based on customer reviews, industry feedback, and the company's history to gauge its product line implementation and commitment to customer satisfaction were used for ranking. 4.1.8 Compatibility with HMI(VT SCADA) software HFC AWTP uses VT SCADA as HMI software. Evaluated PLCs were ranked based on the compatibility with VT SCADA. This is to ensure that the PLC can be easily incorporated into the existing control system and that the operator interface is simple. 4.1.9 Library Components Common industrial protocol(CIP) represents a well-defined data representation, connection management, and messaging protocol that operates over an independent transport and physical layer. A PLC using a CIP is considered flexible for integrating to any HMI software like VT SCADA, it provides simplicity in Automatic Tag Creation, a huge benefit. PLCs are compared and ranking is based on availability and extent of library components in VT SCADA. 4.1.10 Cost Another important issue for evaluation is cost of PLC, which includes the cost of hardware, software, overall value and also considering factors beyond the initial purchase cost. Additionally, factored in with the evaluation are the cost of communication modules, and accessories required for HFC AWTP applications. 4.1.11 Asset and Program management PLC brands with a centralized tool capable of securing, managing, versioning, tracking and reporting automation-related asset information are evaluated within this category. 4.1.12 Functionality and Diagnostics feasibility A wide varity of software and hardware are necessary to enhance the functionality and diagnostics of PLCs. Best choice is given to the PLC that has diagnostics and troubleshooting tools for swiftly identifying defects and difficulties. This can help with troubleshooting the various faults so as to reduce downtime. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation Communication protocols listed here have been updated based on the review comments from the City and intentions to use Rockwell PLC. The protocols are in the order of priority and also based on the availability of from the equipment supplier. These communication protocols shall be implemented on all future projects within this facility. a. General - Ethernet/ IP, PROFINET, MODBUS TCP/ IP, PROFIBUS DP/PA (depending on number of devices on the network), Modbus RTU, HART, Traditional I/O(DI, DO, AI & AO). b. Instruments - Ethernet/ IP, PROFINET, Modbus TCP/IP, ProfiBUS DP/PA, Hart, Traditional I/O. c. VFDs - Traditional I/O for Critical signals and control and for status signals use Ethernet/ IP, PROFINET, Modbus TCP/IP d. Power Monitoring - Ethernet/ IP, PROFINET, Modbus TCP/IP e. Digital Communication protocols - Ethernet/ IP, PROFINET, Modbus TCP/IP 4.4 PLC Manufacturer Comparison The following section compares the relative advantages and disadvantages of each manufacturer considered in this evaluation. 4.4.1 Allen-Bradley Advantages Allen-Bradley is one of the top two manufacturers of PLC equipment in the Southeast market. Their products are widely used in water and wastewater facilities and are typically familiar to most maintenance personnel. The PLCs have the capacity to operate in ladder logic, structured text, and functional block diagram. Their software also abides by IEC 61131-3 standards, the standard most commonly used when looking at PLC software. Their products were ranked second in reader satisfaction of the four manufacturers considered(as listed in paragraph 5.1) Disadvantages While A-B equipment is well known for its powerful processing systems, lack of coordination between hardware, software, firmware and proper programming can sometimes result in a “buggy” feel. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 HMI tags for controller referenced data will maintain the same encapsulated methodology as is employed in the controllers. These tags will be constructed from context type tags designed to mimic the structure of the controller data. A one to one relationship shall be maintained. In places where data is not contained in encapsulating structures, the HMI tag will still be constructed in a structured manner. The individual addresses will be handled internally to the object itself. The structure will be developed in such a way as to allow passing of data en masse to display elements and widgets. The individual tag thus representing the complete definition of data and information for the device or concept. 6.2.2 HMI Exclusive Tag References For those instances when data is to be managed exclusively in the HMI, those tags will follow the same naming conventions as outlined in the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual Volume 2 PLC, This applies to the following types of tags: - Alarm Tags - Logging and Reporting Tags - Calculation and Inquiry Tags - Station Tags - Configuration Tags The data that is exclusive to the HMI will be managed in a structured manner employing context tags as the container. Tag construction will be approached such that as needs change elements inside of a context tag can be added or deprecated without having to edit all graphical elements utilizing the tag. 6.3Controller Tag Naming Standard Reference the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual, Volume 2, PLC, for tag naming requirements. In the event that the naming standards does not have the desired abbreviations the developer is to submit a list of abbreviations to the City for review and approval. All tags created within the HMI and SCADA system must be distinctive. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 February 2025 28 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Figure 10 Recommended Alarm Rater per Operator Guidelines It is critical that alarms be generated at a rate at which the operator can recognize the alarm and address the cause. Control system acceptance requires the average alarm rate per operator to fall within the predictive classification, number of operators for a particular system will be confirmed at pre-programming workshop. 5.2.5 Cascaded Alarms Cascaded alarms are those in which one precipitating alarm also results in multiple others. The subsequent alarms are considered to be false alarms and are to be eliminated from the system. The generation of alarms should be considered, as much as possible, in a first-in-first-out manner. Additionally, an event should only generate one unique alarm. An example of this is a motor stopping while running. The precipitating event is the Uncommanded Stop. Subsequent, related alarms such as Fail to Start are not to be generated. Alarms, such as a process generating a Low and subsequently a Low-Low alarm are not considered to be cascaded events since each condition has a separate setpoint and the duration between events provides additional troubleshooting information to the operator. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 29 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3. Recommend Network Sequencing 3.1 Maintaining Monitoring and Reporting Once the P&IDs are developed and preliminary control descriptions are available, discussion on where new PLC panels will be located and how I/O will be connected to the new PLC modules will be made at milestones for each project. A detailed sequence on how to maintain monitoring and reporting will be developed as part of these discussions. These sequencing plans will be reviewed with City staff prior to completing the 90% Design. 3.2 Replacing Fiber Cables All fiber cables for each process, which includes fibers for the camera feeds, will be replaced as part of any improvement to that process. During development of the Preliminary P&IDs a thorough site investigations shall be performed to verify the existing fiber runs from the associated PLCs back to the main network fiber patch panel. This includes removing electrical manhole covers and draining the manholes. The purpose of these investigations is to determine what existing conduit runs will be used to best show on the design site plans for the fiber installation. At any point thereafter, investigations shall be performed to confirm new cable can be run through the existing conduits without removing the existing fiber cables. Final network diagram will include fiber cable labeling. Labeling for the cables and for the ports at patch panels will be installed in the field to match the network diagram. Multiple cables run through the same conduits and manholes will be depicted on the network site plan. 3.3 Conversion to VTScada VTScada HMI programming will be developed and placed in service parallel to the existing HSQ HMI system on a separate network rack and made available for interface on new workstations throughout the plant, as well as on wireless devices. As a process is made available on the VTScada HMI and is installed, tested, and passed an approved reliability demonstration period, the associated HSQ HMI display screens could be removed or made view only. The goal will be to get each process on the VTScada HMI to use for setpoint adjustment, monitoring, and/or manual override. However, there will be some parameters that may impact separate processes still controlled only through HSQ. A plan will need to be developed for such situations to provide a protocol to operations until all control parameters have been switched over to VTScada. PLCs with tools to manage software version, notification feature and license upgrades are ranked based on the available options. 4.1.14 Disaster Recovery PLCs hard drives occasionally are inoperable for a variety of reasons, such as power quality event impacting the central processing unit(CPU). - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 6.4Tag Naming Best Practices The following are a series of best practices and limitations for tags. The differences in tagging are in part based on the differences between PLC programming and the limitations of VTScada. For example, PLC programming is restricted to using underscores as dashes are not able to be used. In comparison dashes are able to be used in HMI programming. These practices do not supersede the tag naming convention specified in Volume 2. Reference the tag naming conventions in Volume 2 for details. This is provided for consideration of locally created tags. These rules particularly apply to context tags used in the construction of complex object tags. - Tag names must be no longer than 64 characters in length (This rule refers only to the tag's immediate name, not its full name, which may include a lengthy list of parent tags.) - Full tag names must be no longer than 253 characters in length (This rule refers to the tag's full name, which may include many parent tags.) - Tag names must not duplicate other named Items. (tags cannot be named "TagBrowser" or "Test", since those names are used elsewhere in VTScada.) - A tag may not be named "Value" or "Area" since those are the names of tag properties. Tags, pages, and widgets may not share a name. Tag names may not duplicate type names - a tag cannot be named "Calculation" or "Analog Status". - Avoid using common English words unless appended with a prefix or suffix. - Valid tag name characters include: o Any combination of alphanumeric characters(A through Z and 0 through 9) o Spaces, except at the beginning or end of the name. o Period(.) o Symbols other than those listed below (e.g., use of the number hatch(#), and underscore(_) characters in tag names is acceptable) - Invalid tag name characters consist of: o Backslash (\ used to separate parent names) o Forward slash(/) o Colon(:) o Asterisk(*) HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 29 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 1. Introduction The Howard F. Curren Advanced Water Treatment Plant(HFC AWTP) owns and operates a Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) system currently composed mainly of General Electric(GE) PACSystems Rx3i and 90-30 series programmable logic controllers(PLCs) at the field level. General Electric was acquired by Emerson in February, 2019. This document refers to GE equipment as GE to maintain familiarity with City’s existing Standards. As manufacturers continue to make advancements in product development, older versions of hardware advance towards “legacy” status. Products in legacy status are taken out of production and typically are no longercommercially available from the manufacturer. Replacement parts must then be sourced from third party sellers, increasing the risk for price inflation and long lead times. In that case, a set of spares must be stringently maintained to prevent unnecessary down time in the event of a failure. Additionally, manufacturers eventually stop developing and releasing software updates for legacy controllers, which may pose a security risk on network connected systems. A majority of HFC AWTP’s PLC hardware has entered the legacy phase. While PLC upgrades may be costly up front, updating the PLCs reduces the risk of unplanned downtime and enhances network security. 2. Objectives A majority of the existing PLCs reached legacy status in 2018 (GE 90-30 and GE 90-70 series). PLC hardware for these products are no longer supported by the manufacturer. With the impending replacement of all PLC hardware as part of the on-going design-build project, the City has chosen to evaluate alternatives to develop a new standard for PLCs at the plant. Existing PLCs at the plant were evaluated by the City. As a result, Hazen and Sawyer(Hazen) was asked to review the existing PLC hardware to determine equivalent alternatives. Four major PLC vendors were evaluated for consideration whenever existing PLC line ups are to be replaced as part of on-going process improvements at HFC AWTP. The different PLC models were compared including features and budgetary costs for full system replacement. The findings are summarized in this PLC Hardware Evaluation along with a recommendation for the standard PLC hardware platform going forward. 3. Assessment of Existing PLCs & Industry Review 3.1 Existing PLCs An audit was performed by McKim & Creed & Hazen in June 2018 with assistance from HFC AWTP staff to quantify the number of existing PLCs installed at the facility. HFC AWTP staff provided information about the existing PLCs at these sites including existing network diagrams, model of PLC at each location, quantity, for review and use for this evaluation. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System PLC Hardware Evaluation 6 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

Software Comparison 3.1 Software Requirements 3.1.1 Cost The software should be priced competitively and not require unnecessary expenses. This is important for a wastewater operator because managing a wastewater treatment plant involves many ongoing costs, and budget constraints are often a significant concern. By ensuring that the software is price competitive, operators can allocate resources more effectively across various critical areas, including maintenance, staffing, and system upgrades. Competitive pricing also allows for the possibility of adopting other necessary technologies or services without stretching the budget too thin. Moreover, avoiding unnecessary expenses helps in achieving long-term financial sustainability, ensuring that the plant can continue to operate efficiently and respond to any emergent needs without financial strain. Due to the financial importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.2 User Dashboard customization On the user’s own dashboard, the software should have the ability to create data trends and graphs saved to the user’s account for private viewing or public access by other users. The dashboard will include customization for the user to create their own pages with tag linking for simple key performance indicators and allow the display to be saved for future viewing or sharing. This functionality is particularly important for plant operators as it enables efficient monitoring of critical plant metrics, such as water quality indicators and equipment performance. By having the ability to customize and save a dashboard, operators can quickly access vital information, make informed decisions, and ensure compliance with regulatory standards. Due to the operational impact of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.3 Custom Report Creation On the user’s own dashboard, the software should have the ability to create data trends and graphs saved to the user’s account for private viewing or public access by other users. The dashboard should include customization for the user to create their own pages with tag linking for simple key performance indicators and allow the display to be saved for future viewing or sharing. This functionality is particularly important for plant operators as it enables the efficient monitoring of critical plant metrics, such as water quality indicators and equipment performance. By having the ability to customize and save specific data points, operators can quickly access vital information, make informed decisions, and ensure compliance with regulatory standards. Due to the operational impact and importance of this requirement it is given an importance factor of 5. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. However, it is recognized there may be specific instances in which a contractor or supplier may not be able to fully comply with the standards for software, equipment, cabling and other control system components supplied or developed under a contract or other procurement method or may not be able to program or configure the system in conformance with these standards. When these cases arise, the contractor or supplier shall submit a request for deviation from the City’s standards using the Standards Deviation Request Form included in Appendix B. The City will review the issues documented on the form and any supplemental documentation that is supplied with the request, and if compelling reasons are presented, the City may grant the deviation. The standards deviation request shall meet the following criteria: 1) It shall clearly indicate those sections of the standards relevant to the proposed deviation(s). 2) It shall verify that compliance with the standards form which the deviation(s) is requested is, under the circumstances, not feasible. 3) It shall include a specific description of the proposed alternative to the requirements in the City’s standards and include supporting documentation. Documentation may include references demonstrating successful use by other public utilities. 3.2Procedure The process to request a deviation from the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions is as follows: 1) Fill out and submit the Standards Deviation Request Form. The form shall be filled out electronically and submitted via e-mail to the City’s designated project manager or other designated contact. The city will supply the blank electronic form on request. A copy of the form is included in Appendix B. 2) Transmit supporting documentation securely through City approved transmittal methods if file contents are sensitive such as VTS changesets, PLC programs, etc. Drawings, mark-ups, photographs, sketches, or vendor-supplied product information with the deviation request e-mail. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 3.1.4 VTScada integration The software must be able to pull data from the VTScada database without the user having access to the VTScada system or database. This capability is crucial for a plant operator because it ensures seamless integration and data flow without compromising the security and integrity of the VTScada system. By allowing data to be accessed and utilized without direct interaction with the VTScada environment, plant operators can streamline operations, improve data handling efficiency, and minimize the risk of unauthorized access or potential system disruptions. This feature also facilitates the creation of comprehensive reports and dashboards, enabling integrators to monitor and optimize plant performance while maintaining stringent security protocols. Due to the level of importance of ensuring that the software platform is well integrated with the report creating platform this requirement is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.5 Other Databases The software must be able to pull data from sources other than VTScada such as LIMS for lab works, HSQ for data not yet available in VTScada. This capability is crucial for a wastewater plant operator because it ensures comprehensive data integration and accessibility. By allowing the software to gather information from multiple databases, operators can obtain a holistic view of the plant's operations. This integrated approach facilitates better monitoring and management of plant metrics, enhances decisionmaking processes, and ensures compliance with regulatory standards. Moreover, it minimizes the risk of data silos, improves operational efficiency, and supports the creation of detailed and accurate reports, which are essential for optimizing plant performance and maintaining environmental safety. Due to the importance of being able to link to other databases this requirement is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.6 Automated Report Generation The software must have the ability to automatically generate reports and email these reports to users on a schedule. This feature is particularly vital for operators as it ensures timely access to essential data without the need for manual report generation. Automated reports can include critical metrics such as water quality indicators, equipment performance data, and regulatory compliance status. By receiving these reports on a predefined schedule, operators can swiftly identify and address potential issues, streamline operational workflows, and maintain adherence to environmental regulations. Additionally, automated report generation reduces administrative burden, allowing operators to focus on more strategic and high-priority tasks. Due to the level critical nature of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.7 Security The software must be compliant with NIST standards for cybersecurity to ensure safety for use in the plant. This is crucial for a wastewater operator because it guarantees that all data and operations are protected from cyber threats. Given the sensitive nature of wastewater treatment processes, any breach could lead to significant environmental, and risk to human health and lives. Robust cybersecurity ensures the integrity and reliability of the system, allowing operators to focus on maintaining plant performance HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. When these cases arise, the contractor or supplier shall submit a request for deviation from the City’s standards using the Standards Deviation Request Form included in Appendix B. The City will review the issues documented on the form and any supplemental documentation that is supplied with the request, and if compelling reasons are presented, the City may grant the deviation. The standards deviation request shall meet the following criteria: 1) It shall clearly indicate those sections of the standards relevant to the proposed deviation(s). 2) It shall verify that compliance with the standards form which the deviation(s) is requested is, under the circumstances, not feasible. 3) It shall include a specific description of the proposed alternative to the requirements in the City’s standards and include supporting documentation. Documentation may include references demonstrating successful use by other public utilities. 3.2Procedure The process to request a deviation from the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions is as follows: 1) Fill out and submit the Standards Deviation Request Form. The form shall be filled out electronically and submitted via e-mail to the City’s designated project manager or other designated contact. The city will supply the blank electronic form on request. A copy of the form is included in Appendix B. 2) Transmit supporting documentation securely through City approved transmittal methods if file contents are sensitive such as VTS changesets, PLC programs, etc. Drawings, mark-ups, photographs, sketches, or vendor-supplied product information with the deviation request e-mail. If attachments exceed mailbox HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - Policies Volume 1 of 3 February 2025 9 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Testing / Startup / Commissioning] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 2.5 Hardware 2.5.1 General Requirements All improvements shall adhere the following requirements:  All programmers, whether vendor or integrator, are to follow the same standards for hardware and software.  Provide computer workstations in key buildings as directed by the City to allow full control and monitoring of entire plant from each location. Existing computer workstations will not be replaced.  Provide independent vendor PLC hardware for individual equipment systems that interface with overall system PLCs provided by integrator. Signals to vendor supplied controls shall be hardwired for all control I/O. Supplemental information such as power usage or other diagnostics can be over Ethernet link from vendor supplied PLCs.  Provide a local control station at each piece of equipment(HOR, L/R, FOR, Start/Stop, etc) for local control with a means to lockout the equipment(lockout stop pushbutton) when in the off position. All such controls to be NEMA4X industrial grade and Square D 9001SK series.  Provide spare parts for CPUs, I/O Cards, Network Switches, fuses, and other components that are critical for operation or prone to wearing out.  Instrument specifications will be in data sheet format for specific make and model numbers rather than lengthy descriptions intended for “or equal” interpretation.  Electrical gear shall be provide with the manufacturer’s standard power monitoring devices.  Witnessed factory testing will not be required. Specifications will require onsite checkout with City staff followed by a 48-hour demonstration test.  Project specifications will require training for both operations and maintenance staff. Operation and maintenance manuals shall be required to be on hand for associated training. 2.5.2 Control Panels Control Panels shall be specified to be provided with the components outlined in Table 2-4, for panel components not listed in this table the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvements Dewatering Facility Replacement project will be used as a standard. Improvements shall include the following requirements:  Dual OITs will be provided on all panels with PLCs, this includes main process panels and individual equipment panels. Both OITs shall have full redundancy, generally one will be used for setpoints and the other for monitoring.  Within panels, CAT6 cable shall be shielded industrial cable and rated for 600V if exposed to 480V power.  Wiring within panels shall separate for different voltages and purposes. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software DreamReport has the advantage of being NIST certified for cybersecurity. Wastewater treatment is a critical part of everyday life that the environment and many people’s lives depend on. Being certified for adequate cybersecurity standards ensures adequate safety and security is being followed. DreamReport also can meet 21 CFR part 11 compliance requirements, this makes the reports and findings trustworthy, reliable, and equivalent to paper records and handwritten signatures. DreamReport is extensively used in the life sciences industry due to this ability. Additionally, DreamReport can be integrated into HMI and PLCs, readily and easily with the large number of adapters that are available on the software platform. This can make it easy to integrate with the Plant’s current system and any potential changes that may happen in the long term. Having this flexibility is a significant advantage Disadvantages DreamReport has some disadvantages related to the size of Ocean Data Systems, the company that created the software platform. Although it is not the smallest company out of the set of companies that were reviewed it is a non-domestic company. Although this may not be a significant issue for the usability of the software it should be considered in the case that there are any restrictions on whether or not the City is allowed to operate software from non-domestic companies. DreamReport’s licensing structure for concurrent users is similar to that of the tag numbers but with a small amount of added flexibility with the licensing sizes. DreamReport has licenses that can be combined for the number of concurrent users. Meaning that if the plant requires for example 15 users a 10 user license and a 5 user license can be combined to meet the number of required licenses. This makes increasing the number of users incur an additional recurring cost that should be taken into account. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 20 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

Software Comparison 3.1 Software Requirements 3.1.1 Cost The software should be priced competitively and not require unnecessary expenses. This is important for a wastewater operator because managing a wastewater treatment plant involves many ongoing costs, and budget constraints are often a significant concern. By ensuring that the software is price competitive, operators can allocate resources more effectively across various critical areas, including maintenance, staffing, and system upgrades. Competitive pricing also allows for the possibility of adopting other necessary technologies or services without stretching the budget too thin. Moreover, avoiding unnecessary expenses helps in achieving long-term financial sustainability, ensuring that the plant can continue to operate efficiently and respond to any emergent needs without financial strain. Due to the financial importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.2 User Dashboard customization On the user’s own dashboard, the software should have the ability to create data trends and graphs saved to the user’s account for private viewing or public access by other users. The dashboard will include customization for the user to create their own pages with tag linking for simple key performance indicators and allow the display to be saved for future viewing or sharing. This functionality is particularly important for plant operators as it enables efficient monitoring of critical plant metrics, such as water quality indicators and equipment performance. By having the ability to customize and save a dashboard, operators can quickly access vital information, make informed decisions, and ensure compliance with regulatory standards. Due to the operational impact of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.3 Custom Report Creation On the user’s own dashboard, the software should have the ability to create data trends and graphs saved to the user’s account for private viewing or public access by other users. The dashboard should include customization for the user to create their own pages with tag linking for simple key performance indicators and allow the display to be saved for future viewing or sharing. This functionality is particularly important for plant operators as it enables the efficient monitoring of critical plant metrics, such as water quality indicators and equipment performance. By having the ability to customize and save specific data points, operators can quickly access vital information, make informed decisions, and ensure compliance with regulatory standards. Due to the operational impact and importance of this requirement it is given an importance factor of 5. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 3.1.4 VTScada integration The software must be able to pull data from the VTScada database without the user having access to the VTScada system or database. This capability is crucial for a plant operator because it ensures seamless integration and data flow without compromising the security and integrity of the VTScada system. By allowing data to be accessed and utilized without direct interaction with the VTScada environment, plant operators can streamline operations, improve data handling efficiency, and minimize the risk of unauthorized access or potential system disruptions. This feature also facilitates the creation of comprehensive reports and dashboards, enabling integrators to monitor and optimize plant performance while maintaining stringent security protocols. Due to the level of importance of ensuring that the software platform is well integrated with the report creating platform this requirement is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.5 Other Databases The software must be able to pull data from sources other than VTScada such as LIMS for lab works, HSQ for data not yet available in VTScada. This capability is crucial for a wastewater plant operator because it ensures comprehensive data integration and accessibility. By allowing the software to gather information from multiple databases, operators can obtain a holistic view of the plant's operations. This integrated approach facilitates better monitoring and management of plant metrics, enhances decisionmaking processes, and ensures compliance with regulatory standards. Moreover, it minimizes the risk of data silos, improves operational efficiency, and supports the creation of detailed and accurate reports, which are essential for optimizing plant performance and maintaining environmental safety. Due to the importance of being able to link to other databases this requirement is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.6 Automated Report Generation The software must have the ability to automatically generate reports and email these reports to users on a schedule. This feature is particularly vital for operators as it ensures timely access to essential data without the need for manual report generation. Automated reports can include critical metrics such as water quality indicators, equipment performance data, and regulatory compliance status. By receiving these reports on a predefined schedule, operators can swiftly identify and address potential issues, streamline operational workflows, and maintain adherence to environmental regulations. Additionally, automated report generation reduces administrative burden, allowing operators to focus on more strategic and high-priority tasks. Due to the level critical nature of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.7 Security The software must be compliant with NIST standards for cybersecurity to ensure safety for use in the plant. This is crucial for a wastewater operator because it guarantees that all data and operations are protected from cyber threats. Given the sensitive nature of wastewater treatment processes, any breach could lead to significant environmental, and risk to human health and lives. Robust cybersecurity ensures the integrity and reliability of the system, allowing operators to focus on maintaining plant performance HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Replacement of the Existing Fiber Cables: The existing fiber cables included in the network are past their useful life having been installed several decades ago. If the cables were to be replaced, it would have to be done with parallel new fiber cables that allow the existing network to function without interruption. However, the 11 fiber cables entering through a conduit beneath the main patch panel in the Main Pump Station Building network room leave the conduit with very little room to pass through additional fiber. Although it’s possible to utilize MaxCell sleeves to snake new fiber cables through the one existing conduit, it would be easier to install a new conduit run if the network rack were to remain where it is currently located. 2.2.3 Recommended Fiber Network Upgrades Given the available documentation on the fiber network and the site investigation, the following is recommended for upgrading the network:  Run new fiber cable in existing fiber cable conduit from new control systems at each building installed under each individual amendment package (to the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvement project) to connect to a new patch panel near the existing network rack. New fiber runs from this new patch panel are to the be connected to the existing network equipment. The existing network rack is located within the existing Main Pump Station network equipment room.  Install new fiber cables from the upgraded PLCs associated with process improvement, utilizing existing conduit duct bank and manholes, through either electrical manhole E-6 or E-7, then through the new conduits to the new network rack.  Gradually remove existing fiber runs as associated processes are upgraded and successfully running off the new network.  Keep both networks operational until the last replacement fiber run is complete. The existing plant fiber patch panel located near the existing network rack is to be kept in service until all processes have been updated and connected with new fiber runs upon which the existing patch panel can be removed.  Provide redundant fiber loops at the outer edges. As an example, a new fiber run between PLC 17 and PLC 7 would allow data transmission for PLCs in either quadrant back to the main network rack regardless of where conduit runs might be lost. PLC 18 (located in Filter Building No. 1) is the most critical to have fiber redundancy as loss of this PLC would result in loss of the plant’s permit analyzers and flow-proportional chemical feeds and samplers, which could shut down STAR. Fiber loops would require some reconfiguration of the programming to designate the “normal” communication path and a backup path if normal route is lost. During the design phase of each improvement to HFC AWTP, the existing network diagram and the new network diagram will be updated to maintain accurate records of the both systems until the existing is fully removed from service. Camera feeds are on the same fiber cable as SCADA, utilizing separate fibers. This approach shall be continued for new fiber cables. - [Scope of Work] 5.2.3 Alarm Rationalization Alarm rationalization is the process of reviewing, validating, and justifying the alarms that meet the criteria of a critical alarm. In other words, the rationalization specifies only those points in the process system that require alarming. The ultimate goal of alarm rationalization is to determine the most efficient number of alarms to ensure that the process system is safe and remains within normal operating range. Alarm rationalization must be accomplished in conjunction with the City at the preprogramming workshop. The basic process can be outlined as: - Check Alarm Validity - Determine the consequence of inaction - Document Cause, confirmation, and corrective action - Document operator response time - Assign alarm priority - Alarm classification - Determine alarm attributes - Address need for special handling All unused or deprecated alarms are to be removed from the system. Alarms that are no longer needed are not to be abandoned in place (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 FBD sheets should be limited in size to that which can be printed on an 11” x 17” sheet of paper. 6.3Task and Program Files Tasks provide the broadest level of organization. They also provide a level of execution control, that being continuous, periodic, event amongst others. Only two types of tasks are to be employed at the City: continuous and periodic. All other types of task types will not be employed. 6.3.1 Periodic tasks Periodic tasks are designed to execute on a fixed period of time, typically in milliseconds. These tasks are conducive when working with a process or system that is slow, or requires the processing on a regular basis. Creation of periodic tasks should be kept to a minimum(six or less) as required by the controlled process or area. The periodic task execution rate should be set to allow ample time for program execution without burdening the processor. Programmers shall frequently monitor and adjust the execution rate based on the task time. All programs shall be commissioned with task settings tuned such that no overlaps exist in the program. During commissioning, the main program overlap counter shall be confirmed at zero and FAULTLOG object shall not have any minor faults for overlap. Each periodic task must be set with a priority from 1 to 15, with 1 representing the highest priority. It is recommended that each task be set with a unique priority. Fast Scan Task Parameter Setting Default rate 10ms Priority 1 Normal Scan Task Parameter Setting Default rate 250ms Priority 3 HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. - [Scope of Work] 6.4Main or "0" Each program folder must contain a routine to be named "Main" or "0"; the language type specified for this routine should be LD. The purpose of the main routine is to govern the flow of the program. The jump subroutine(JSR) instructions shall be used to reference all other logical routines in the PLC program. No other logic is permitted in the main routine. The first JSR of the main routine shall be to the initialization subroutine conditioned on first scan flag from PLC. This will allow initialization logic to run each time the PLC returns to run after power cycle or other interruption. (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] CITY OF TAMPA, FLORIDA - RFQ objective of the project is to migrate the remaining facilities operating on HSQ software to the VTScada software so that the entire plant is monitored and controlled through the VTScada software system. The City has recently completed new SCADA and Process Control System(PCS) programming, configuration and design standards. The project will ensure that the plant’s monitoring and control systems meet these standards. The objectives of the project will include, but not limited to: - Migrate the SCADA system from the existing HSQ software to VTScada software while ensuring continuous operation of the treatment plant. - Ensure that the new monitoring and control system aligns with the City's standards, including the use of approved widgets and graphic symbols for operator screens. - Implement a new software system that can generate plant operating reports needed for regulatory requirements and for monitoring plant performance. The new software system shall retain historical data from HSQ and shall be capable of generating reports using both historical and current data. - Ensure the system is scalable for future expansions and upgrades. - Provide the design and implementation plan for replacing the treatment plant’s fiber optic cable network. Services will include, but will not be limited to the following: - Complete an assessment of the existing SCADA system and related infrastructure to determine the requirements, scopes of work, priorities, and cost estimates to complete the migration to the VTScada software. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] The historical data from HSQ is to be saved and made available in the VTScada system. This shall be addressed in the Main Pump Station and HPO Reactor Improvements project currently under design. At a minimum, data starting from the beginning of 2000 shall be transferred. 2.3.2 PLC / HMI Software Integration The design team will coordinate with the process integrator and the City to develop the software. City staff will participate in actual development of graphics, tag creation, and programming to become more familiar with VTScada for future development and system troubleshooting. At the beginning of each construction phase, a pre-construction instrumentation coordination meeting will be held to review software development scope of work and to determine the role and responsibilities. The City staff will provide oversight of the HMI screen and PLC controls development. For the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvements Dewatering Facility Replacement project, RTP will be developing the HMI screens with oversight from the City. These displays will be used to set standards for displays to provide for all improvements. 2.3.3 HMI Security The following extent of exposure to internet connectivity will be incorporated into the SCADA design for each process improvement:  VPN connection for remote setup for vendor access to HMI when allowed by the City.  Similar to existing, access to HMI by City staff through handheld devices and laptops to connect through internet VPN over wireless cellular means. Previous efforts to create wireless hotspots proved to be inadequate at this site.  New controls at the PLC level on the fiber network shall be accessible through similar wireless internet VPN connectivity. 2.4 Site Security & Monitoring The existing plant camera system only includes ten(10) cameras. These are listed in Appendix G. With each improvement, design will include new cameras to eventually be able to monitor all areas within the plant. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3 SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 SCADA & PCS Standards Deviation Request 3.1General The City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Convention Manual is intended to provide consistent, reliable controls within all plants. However, it is recognized there may be specific instances in which a contractor or supplier may not be able to fully comply with the standards for software, equipment, cabling and other control system components supplied or developed under a contract or other procurement method or may not be able to program or configure the system in conformance with these standards. When these cases arise, the contractor or supplier shall submit a request for deviation from the City’s standards using the Standards Deviation Request Form included in Appendix B. The City will review the issues documented on the form and any supplemental documentation that is supplied with the request, and if compelling reasons are presented, the City may grant the deviation. The standards deviation request shall meet the following criteria: 1) It shall clearly indicate those sections of the standards relevant to the proposed deviation(s). 2) It shall verify that compliance with the standards form which the deviation(s) is requested is, under the circumstances, not feasible. 3) It shall include a specific description of the proposed alternative to the requirements in the City’s standards and include supporting documentation. Documentation may include references demonstrating successful use by other public utilities. 3.2Procedure The process to request a deviation from the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions is as follows: 1) Fill out and submit the Standards Deviation Request Form. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 The AOI interface contains a duality in its nature. From the programmer’s perspective, this allows them to interact with the code-block in a regular manner. From the graphical developer’s perspective, this presents a reliable interface to contained data that simplifies their data management requirements. This interface is a strictly enforced contract with all parties into what the contained data is and to which the inner workings of the blocks code can respond. The parameters are constructed of the controller’s basic data types. Examples are Booleans, integers, and real numbers. The parameters are also capable of handling complex data elements like arrays and user defined types(UDTs). This allows the developer the opportunity to source whatever data is required for the contained logic process. There are more subtle advantages to the use of AOIs from the controller’s perspective. Typically, the controller only maintains one copy of the executable code without regard to the total number of actual used instances. The only requirement on the controller is to have enough memory to contain the interface and internal variable data. The end result is a much smaller controller footprint. AOIs shall be used for any function that will be repetitive. An example of this is a valve. There is a high probability that a system will contain multiple valves. As such the generation of one block of code that addresses the needs for all similar acting valves can be a significant time saver. AOIs should not be used for one time code or code that has the potential to be modified(such as during start-up). AOIs are typically compiled and subsequently downloaded to the controller for optimization purposes. Modifying them typically requires the entire controller to be taken out of active state while a completely new download takes place. This can have a significant impact on the process. A secondary function of AOIs is the development and encapsulation of calculations or custom control requirements. Calculations are generally fixed in nature; an example being the solution to the quadratic equation. There is little value or clarity to be gained by exposing the details of the equation to the general codebase. The only thing that realistically matters are the results. There is also the case of supplementing or extending the functionality of native programming elements. There will often be cases where special requirements need to be applied to a native function that is not supplied or supported. In these cases, an AOI provides the opportunity for a simplified solution. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 39 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 8.3Commissioning Requirements Commissioning activities create abnormal alarm situations; this section contains requirements for alarm and event system during commissioning activities. 8.3.1 Operational Readiness Review Meetings Alarms and events risk review shall be reviewed at operational readiness meetings to determine alarm owner(contractor or operations) based on risk to process and processing equipment. 8.3.2 Alarms in Active Commissioning State Alarms and events which are not turned over to operations at operational readiness review are required to be suppressed by means of PLC logic suppression outside of testing hours to avoid presenting operators with nuisance and/or unknown alarms. 8.3.3 Alarm and Event Acceptance Matrices The City utilizes the defined average alarm rate of less than six alarms per hour period as defined in alarm management strategy in Volume 2 as maximum allowable alarm rate for acceptance of modifications to alarm system. After commissioning activities have been completed, system shall run for 30 days in hands-off mode to document alarm rates for modified alarms. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 o AC = Reference to the type of card, AC indicates voltage type. o DI = Reference to type of input, DI indicates discrete input. o MOD-04 = Indicates the module number. 4.5Tasks This section describes the various objects available for the organization of program logic within the controller and their associated parameters. The section provides guidelines for selection and organization of logic into these objects and configuration of their properties. Three tasks are used, fast, normal, and slow. The tasks are typically reserved for message communications with other devices. The fast scan task has the highest priority of 1, the normal scan task has a priority of 3, being used for all regular monitoring and control operations. The slow scan task has a priority of 5, being reserved for processor monitoring. Each task shall contain a single program within itself and program name shall follow a similar naming as the task. 4.5.1 Task Structure Tasks are the highest organizational unit in the controller and dictate the order of execution. The nomenclature applied to tasks must represent the broadest definition of the contents. 4.5.2 Program Structure Programs represent the middle tier of the organizational structure. At this layer there is more additional granularity added to the overall structure and the naming conventions must reflect that. Naming at this layer must not overlap with the layer above. For example: - P01_Fast Program - P02_Normal Program - P03_Slow Program Individual names at this tier may be duplicated in other tasks but must be unique per task. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 24 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 4.5.3 Routine Structure This is the lowest tier of programming structure. As such the name must provide clarity into the exact programming construct. Each routine is linked to a process area for fast identification and each process area has 3 subroutines associated, except for IO subroutines that are 4. For Example, - _000_ is associated with IO Mapping. o _000_ANALOG_INPUT_MAPPING o _000_ANALOG_OUTPUT_MAPPING o _000_DIGITAL_IO_MAPPING o _000_DISCRETE_IO_MAPPING - _010_Main_PUMP_STATION_ALM - _010_Main_PUMP_STATION_ASSETS - _010 Main_PUMP_STATION_CONTROLS HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F.
