- [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 1. Objectives With the changes being implemented at the HFC AWTP, the City aims to select a new report creation software to ensure efficient identification of plant performance and regulatory compliance. Given the critical nature of these reports, it is vital that the chosen software integrates seamlessly with the existing database systems and offers comprehensive functionality for data analysis and reporting. To achieve this goal, the City has tasked Hazen and Sawyer(Hazen) with reviewing and evaluating various software platforms capable of meeting the plant's rigorous requirements. As part of this mandate, Hazen conducted a thorough assessment of multiple report creation software options to determine the most suitable solution for the HFC AWTP's needs. The evaluation process involved comparing the features, capabilities, and budgetary costs of each software platform. Hazen's objective was to identify a software solution that not only provides robust reporting capabilities but also enhances the overall operational efficiency of the plant. This includes the ability to generate accurate, real-time reports that offer insight into key metrics such as water flow rates, chemical usage, and equipment status. Moreover, the ideal software should facilitate user-friendly report creation and customization, enabling plant operators and management to easily interpret data and make informed decisions. Ensuring consistency and reliability in report generation is paramount, as these reports play a crucial role in maintaining the plant’s operational excellence and regulatory compliance. The findings of Hazen's comprehensive evaluation are summarized in the Report Generator Software Evaluation document, which outlines the strengths and weaknesses of each software platform considered. Based on this evaluation, Hazen has provided a recommendation for the standard software platform to be adopted for report creation moving forward. This recommendation is aimed at optimizing the plant's reporting processes and supporting the City's ongoing commitment to operational improvement and regulatory adherence. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 6 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 4) Network Design - Maintain drawings depicting PCS/SCADA system interconnections. Maintain the drawings in electronic and hardcopy formats. Electronic copies shall be maintained using the latest version of Microsoft Visio and PDF file formats 5) Physical Drawing - Maintain a Layer 2 drawing that depicts the physical interconnection of network hardware and hosts. Include location, cable type, cable standard, interface speed, host name, description, vendor, model number, MAC address, IP address, and subnet mask. The drawing shall include all components and subcomponents with a MAC address. 6) Logical Drawing - Maintain a Layer 3 drawing consistent with the zones & conduits model used in ISA/IEC 62443. The drawing shall depict the various zones (e.g., networks, subnets, VLANs, etc.) and conduits (e.g., Layer 3 switches, routers, firewalls, etc.) used to connect the zones together. The drawing shall include hosts grouped according to the zone in which they reside and the default gateway for each zone. The drawing shall include WAN links and supporting hardware. Include location, host name, description, vendor, model number, MAC address, IP address, subnet mask, and interface speed. The drawing shall include all components and subcomponents with an IP address. 7) IP Addressing Scheme - Maintain a spreadsheet that documents the IP addresses and related TCP/IP settings (e.g., default gateway, DNS servers, etc.) for all nodes with an IP address. Group the listing by subnet/VLAN. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 2 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 HMI Software The SCADA software currently utilized by the City is VTScada 12.1.58, development environment from Trihedral Engineering, contact the City for version being used. This software suite forms the basis of the system, as well as providing the majority of the data acquisition drivers. All development of HMI screens and system wide visualization will utilize this software. All new HMI screens and graphical elements are required to be developed using the latest available City provided ChangeSet file. A ChangeSet file contains the instructions required to re-create an application. This is not an exact backup but a compendium of changes and the base information to reproduce a functional application. In general, ChangeSets are used to distribute new applications, distribute updates to an existing application, or add extra features to OEM layers. This ChangeSet file will contain all of the latest container tags, display definitions, and library widgets needed to support development activities. Each project shall request, via email to the City’s project manager (or assigned City Operational Technology(OT) contact), a copy of the current ChangeSet application immediately prior to initiating programming activities. Although updates to the standard may occasionally occur, the files provided on request date shall be utilized throughout the project. Only those updates which are determined mission critical (have severe consequences to operations, life or equipment safety) will be handled on a case by case basis through direct notice to programmers by the City’s project manager or assigned OT contact. The distribution ChangeSet file will be re-created periodically as functional elements are added and/or enhanced. Additionally, other exports will be created as required in order to maintain an appropriate level of application consistency between the City’s templates and any standalone in development. The City’s SCADA system is a managed VTScada application which has been developed using custom City developed library widgets. Refer to the City Standard Object Library Programming Guideline for details on the currently available object template library or submit an email request to the City’s project manager. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 New software requirements must be submitted to the City with suitable lead time, a minimum of four weeks, for a security evaluation and loading. It is the developer’s responsibility to ensure all of the required tools are in place prior to beginning a start-up. General Programming Practices 3.1Languages The IEC 61131-3 standard includes for five languages: Ladder Logic(LD), Function Block Diagram(FBD), Instruction List, and Sequential Function Flowchart. The only allowable languages for all City projects and implementations are LD, and FBD. 3.1.1 Ladder Logic LD is a graphical, symbol-based language with a foundation in circuit diagrams and relay logic hardware. The logic is organized in a series of rungs composed of Boolean elements whose logical result is then applied to outputs or action elements. This can simplistically be considered to be a series of IF/THEN statements. The ladder rungs are scanned in a top-down, left to right manner enforcing strict order of execution. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 7 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 HMI Graphics General Requirements Graphic screens will be composed of many individual types of data elements, a significant portion of which are alpha-numeric. To maintain consistency across the application, the following requirements are to be adhered to. 7.1Color The use of color throughout the application is limited. The nature of high performance graphics restricts colors to those elements that require attention. In order to provide a consistent color pallet throughout the application colors have been defined for use. Refer to Appendix A, Definitions and General Reference, for a listing of those colors approved for use. The colors have been defined using the standard RGB color system. The color name is as defined by Microsoft along with the color values in red, green, and blue and hex code. Throughout this document, where specific colors are required, these color names will be used. The only times the full color spectrum is allowed is on map based screens and those places where photographs are utilized. 7.2Process Values Displaying live process values will be handled differently than static text. Values depicted from the controller will be in bold, blue. This is in contrast to the units of measure that will be indicated in non- bold, black either near the process value or not at all in the case of bar graphs or other animated elements. Leading zero’s(0) will not be utilized except to indicate a fraction(e.g., 0.12). When dynamic values are being used in a column generally align the values on the decimal point. In mixed cases, floating point values and integers, the values are to be aligned to the implied decimal point. In integer only cases the values are to be right aligned. Where applicable and feasible, the process value will indicate its quality. This will be accomplished by including a small indication adjacent to the value indicating “BAD”. This shall be highlighted with a white background to improve legibility. In the event that the value is currently under simulation, that is a manual value being driven by the operator, the indication “SIM” will be used in place of “BAD”. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 1. Introduction 1.1 Background The HFC AWTP is permitted to treat 96 mgd of raw sewage with a Type I two-stage, high rate (pure oxygen and conventional fine bubble aeration) activated sludge biological domestic wastewater treatment plant. This plant is operated to reduce the Total Nitrogen(TN) in the effluent to advanced standards, using biological treatment for nitrification and filtration for denitrification, and to discharge high-level disinfected and dechlorinated effluent to Tampa Bay. Residuals generated by this facility are anaerobically digested and dewatered for land application as a Class B residual. Currently, annual average daily flows are approximately 60 mgd and peak flows are approximately 200 mgd. The rehabilitation to the HFC AWTP will consist of the implementation of improvements identified in the Facility Master Plan Capital Improvement Project List for Fiscal Years 0-5 to maintain system reliability, increase operating efficiency, and meet current and future regulatory requirements. This Technical Memorandum details the SCADA Design Criteria. These criteria will be utilized for all instrumentation and control design performed as part of these improvements. 1.2 Workshops Three(3) workshops were held with the City in developing the SCADA Design Criteria. The findings of these workshops are documented herein and establish the minimum standards to use for developing P&IDs, specifying software and hardware, and guide the overall approach and sequencing for the replacement of the facility wide fiber communications network. The workshops addressed the following topics:  Workshop 1 - P&ID standards, facility fiber network improvements, approach to PLC/HMI software development, and internet/site security protection  Workshop 2 - Hardware standards  Workshop 3 - Design approach Meeting notes are provided in Appendix E for reference. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 6 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Program Structure The City’s programs will be written utilizing LD, and FBD languages only. Any exceptions must be submitted for approval following the procedures contained in the City’s Standards Deviation Request Policy (Reference Volume 1, SCADA & PCS Standards Deviation Request). 6.1Ladder Logic LD is the preferred language when performing complex Boolean logic. This would include actions such as top-down Boolean logic, interlock development, sequencing equipment operations, or control of I/O elements. Function blocks(AOIs, FBs, FCs) may be utilized as part of the rung logic; however, this approach should be minimized. Block programming requirements often require support branches whose sole purpose is to port data into and out of the block. This approach results in rungs that are large and less comprehensible. The preferred approach in this case is to only use blocks that all of the parameters are supplied and visible; typical of internal instructions. Rungs shall be organized in a one concept per rung manner. Stringing logic across rungs through the use of intermediary bits is to be avoided as much as possible. Logic that becomes excessively large will require being broken into multiple rungs. In cases where logic is split, it is incumbent on the developer to provide clarity and explain logic flow. LD shall be limited in size to that which can be printed on an 11” x 17” sheet of paper, either portrait or landscape. LD is a suitable language for the construction of custom function blocks. The same programming constraints are to be applied in this case as well. 6.2Function Block Diagram FBD is preferred when working with blocks and process values and control. Operations such as analog/digital input mapping, PID control elements, stand-alone alarm handling, and totalization should be handled in this manner. It is possible to perform Boolean logic in FBD; however, this should be kept to a minimum. The density of information contained on the sheet should be as concise and legible as possible. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Slow Scan Task Parameter Setting Default rate 500ms Priority 5 6.4Main or “0” Each program folder must contain a routine to be named “Main” or “0”; the language type specified for this routine should be LD. The purpose of the main routine is to govern the flow of the program. The jump subroutine(JSR) instructions shall be used to reference all other logical routines in the PLC program. No other logic is permitted in the main routine. The first JSR of the main routine shall be to the initialization subroutine conditioned on first scan flag from PLC. This will allow initialization logic to run each time the PLC returns to run after power cycle or other interruption. 6.5Subroutines Routines are the lowest level of program structure; routines are subsets of programs. Subroutines are routines that are not designated as the main routine. Subroutines should contain the code for control modules, equipment modules, or other compartmentalized pieces of code or equipment. Subroutines are beneficial for encapsulating a specific function that is a subset of the larger program scope. For traceability, all routines are called from the program main routine. This allows for a single location where the program flow can be quickly identified. The following are exceptions: - Routines that capture event data such as hourly data or report data Routine names shall be named after the process areas the routine interacts with. The naming shall be _XXX_AREA_NAME, where XXX indicates the loop number and AREA_NAME is the description of the loop number area 6.6Functions and Function Blocks There are a set of predefined blocks supplied by the City to be used for specific functions. These blocks provide a common, known functionality in the PLC whose interface connects to OITs, HMIs and SCADA system. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software The scalability of this software is more restrictive than other software platforms as the number of tags and users are licensed, this means that for smaller installations there will be an increase in cost for more tags being added but once a certain threshold is met then an unlimited tag licensing option is available, this is the same case with the number of concurrent users that can create reports. Even though the City’s tag licensing would fall under the unlimited category the more restrictive nature of the licensing of the software caused the platform to score a ranking of 1 when comparing against the other platforms on the scalability evaluation criteria. Specialized functionality allows plant operators to create custom SQL queries and create customized scripts to generate reports or extract data in ways that would not otherwise be available DreamReport is able to run specialized functions and calculations because of this it scores a ranking of 4 on the specialized functions category. DreamReport is able to be integrated with the HMI through their large number of drivers and connectors, which enables the triggered report functionality. Due to this DreamReport scores a ranking of 4 on the HMI integration category. DreamReport security updates and general updates are provided as part of the subscription-based model, the software scores a ranking of 3 for the software support and maintenance category. A redundancy license is available for 50% of the cost of the main license, this is comparable to other offerings, the software scores a ranking of 4 on the redundancy category. Ocean Data Systems, the company that makes DreamReport is a small company of 11-50 employees based out of Europe, with 75 million matches on Google, it scores a ranking of 3 on the company prominence category. Ocean Data Systems had by far the quickest responding and most knowledgeable sales representatives who were able to be reached quickly and provided exceptional knowledge of the uses of the software platform and had the required documentation quickly available the platform scored a ranking of 4 on the customer service category. Advantages DreamReport has several different advantages that make it a unique and useful tool for The City. The software has some useful advantages such as the ability to automatically generate reports upon specific triggers. For example, a water sample parameter going above a certain threshold could trigger an automatic report generation that could display the historical trendline of the parameter alongside other important data to figure out the reason for parameter deviation. This gives the ability to streamline meeting regulatory compliance. Another advantage for DreamReport is the multitude of drivers that are available to The City. DreamReport’s large number of drivers make it easy to integrate with other software and other databases that make integration much easier than other reporting platforms including manual data entry. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 1. Introduction The Howard F. Curren Advanced Water Treatment Plant(HFC AWTP) owns and operates a Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) system currently composed mainly of General Electric(GE) PACSystems Rx3i and 90-30 series programmable logic controllers(PLCs) at the field level. General Electric was acquired by Emerson in February, 2019. This document refers to GE equipment as GE to maintain familiarity with City’s existing Standards. As manufacturers continue to make advancements in product development, older versions of hardware advance towards “legacy” status. Products in legacy status are taken out of production and typically are no longercommercially available from the manufacturer. Replacement parts must then be sourced from third party sellers, increasing the risk for price inflation and long lead times. In that case, a set of spares must be stringently maintained to prevent unnecessary down time in the event of a failure. Additionally, manufacturers eventually stop developing and releasing software updates for legacy controllers, which may pose a security risk on network connected systems. A majority of HFC AWTP’s PLC hardware has entered the legacy phase. While PLC upgrades may be costly up front, updating the PLCs reduces the risk of unplanned downtime and enhances network security. 2. Objectives A majority of the existing PLCs reached legacy status in 2018 (GE 90-30 and GE 90-70 series). PLC hardware for these products are no longer supported by the manufacturer. With the impending replacement of all PLC hardware as part of the on-going design-build project, the City has chosen to evaluate alternatives to develop a new standard for PLCs at the plant. Existing PLCs at the plant were evaluated by the City. As a result, Hazen and Sawyer(Hazen) was asked to review the existing PLC hardware to determine equivalent alternatives. Four major PLC vendors were evaluated for consideration whenever existing PLC line ups are to be replaced as part of on-going process improvements at HFC AWTP. The different PLC models were compared including features and budgetary costs for full system replacement. The findings are summarized in this PLC Hardware Evaluation along with a recommendation for the standard PLC hardware platform going forward. 3. Assessment of Existing PLCs & Industry Review 3.1 Existing PLCs An audit was performed by McKim & Creed & Hazen in June 2018 with assistance from HFC AWTP staff to quantify the number of existing PLCs installed at the facility. HFC AWTP staff provided information about the existing PLCs at these sites including existing network diagrams, model of PLC at each location, quantity, for review and use for this evaluation. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System PLC Hardware Evaluation 6 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 6.9.2 User Defined Data Types Data is the foundation of what the controllers are utilizing. All controllers support and supply atomic data types; examples are Booleans, integers, and real numbers. These are the fundamental elements upon which control programs are built. Controllers also natively support arrays of these basic types, providing a primitive complex type. The user definted data type(UDT) is the logical extension of the primitive complex type into a structure that is constructed of multiple data types. In this case a UDT is a container for Boolean elements, integers, reals, arrays, and even other UDTs. UDTs are not to be used for programming HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. - [Scope of Work] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Replacement of the Existing Fiber Cables: The existing fiber cables included in the network are past their useful life having been installed several decades ago. If the cables were to be replaced, it would have to be done with parallel new fiber cables that allow the existing network to function without interruption. However, the 11 fiber cables entering through a conduit beneath the main patch panel in the Main Pump Station Building network room leave the conduit with very little room to pass through additional fiber. Although it’s possible to utilize MaxCell sleeves to snake new fiber cables through the one existing conduit, it would be easier to install a new conduit run if the network rack were to remain where it is currently located. 2.2.3 Recommended Fiber Network Upgrades Given the available documentation on the fiber network and the site investigation, the following is recommended for upgrading the network:  Run new fiber cable in existing fiber cable conduit from new control systems at each building installed under each individual amendment package (to the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvement project) to connect to a new patch panel near the existing network rack. New fiber runs from this new patch panel are to the be connected to the existing network equipment. The existing network rack is located within the existing Main Pump Station network equipment room.  Install new fiber cables from the upgraded PLCs associated with process improvement, utilizing existing conduit duct bank and manholes, through either electrical manhole E-6 or E-7, then through the new conduits to the new network rack.  Gradually remove existing fiber runs as associated processes are upgraded and successfully running off the new network.  Keep both networks operational until the last replacement fiber run is complete. The existing plant fiber patch panel located near the existing network rack is to be kept in service until all processes have been updated and connected with new fiber runs upon which the existing patch panel can be removed.  Provide redundant fiber loops at the outer edges. As an example, a new fiber run between PLC 17 and PLC 7 would allow data transmission for PLCs in either quadrant back to the main network rack regardless of where conduit runs might be lost. PLC 18 (located in Filter Building No. 1) is the most critical to have fiber redundancy as loss of this PLC would result in loss of the plant’s permit analyzers and flow-proportional chemical feeds and samplers, which could shut down STAR. Fiber loops would require some reconfiguration of the programming to designate the “normal” communication path and a backup path if normal route is lost. During the design phase of each improvement to HFC AWTP, the existing network diagram and the new network diagram will be updated to maintain accurate records of the both systems until the existing is fully removed from service. Camera feeds are on the same fiber cable as SCADA, utilizing separate fibers. This approach shall be continued for new fiber cables. - [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 1 SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 Introduction This is Volume 1 of the City of Tampa’s standards for Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) and Process Control System(PCS) programming, configuration and design standards for The City of Tampa referred to as “The City” or abbreviated as “COT” throughout this document Volume 1 is a compilation of policies and procedures and provides both guidance and specific requirements for control system work. The City SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual is intended to be applied by the City’s contractors, consultants, and in-house personnel when developing or modifying any portion of the City’s control system. The control system is a utility-wide system built upon a common human machine interface(HMI) system and is comprised of the following: Process Control System(PCS): The industrial control system for processes within the boundaries of the plant(Howard F. Curren AWTP). The primary function of the system is control and monitoring of plant operations. The PCS is comprised of the local HMI subsystem and various programmable logic controllers(PLCs) and operator interface terminals(OITs). Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) System: The industrial control system for processes outside the boundaries of the process areas, ability to monitor and/or control the process from the control room from outside the plant. The primary function of this system is data acquisition; processes or remote sites are designed to run autonomously without supervisory control function. The SCADA system serves both remote sites such as lift stations and wastewater treatment plant facility in an integrated environment and is comprised of telemetry equipment with a supervisory HMI. This manual is divided into three main volumes plus two appendices: Volume 1 contains the control system policies and procedures that apply to the management, maintenance and modification of the control system. Volume 1 includes policies for: - Programming laptop use - Management of change(MOC) - Software and application revision control - Cybersecurity - Other related topics. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - Policies Volume 1 of 3 February 2025 6 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

Due to the impact of plant safety this requirement it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.8 21 CFR Part 11 Compliance The software should be able to meet 21 CFR Part 11 compliance. This ensures that electronic records and signatures are trustworthy, reliable, and equivalent to paper records. Compliance with 21 CFR Part 11 means that the software adheres to strict standards for data integrity, security, and traceability, which are essential for regulatory audits and inspections. This level of compliance helps operators maintain accurate records of plant operations, ensure the validity of data used for decision-making, and demonstrate adherence to legal and environmental regulations. Ultimately, 21 CFR Part 11 compliance provides a framework that supports the integrity and reliability of the plant’s data management system. Due to the non-critical but important nature of this requirement it is given an importance factor of 3. 3.1.9 Browser Usability The software should have the ability to operate out of a browser setting to generate reports and to allow for the quick ability to view trends, and other plant information. This is crucial for a wastewater operator as it provides flexibility to access real-time data and reports from any device with internet connectivity, without being confined to a specific workstation. This ensures that operators can monitor plant performance, track water quality indicators, and respond promptly to any issues, no matter where they are. As this requirement directly impacts plant performance it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.10 Scalability The software should be flexible enough to expand if The City requires an expansion without incurring a significant increase in cost or complexity. The software should allow the system to grow and adapt in response to the changing needs and demands of the plant. As the plant expands, either through increased capacity or the addition of new processes and technologies, the software must be able to accommodate these changes seamlessly. Scalable software ensures that operators can continue to manage and monitor the plant effectively without facing major disruptions. Due to the non-critical aspect of this requirement and lower level of importance it is given an importance factor of 2. 3.1.11 Specialized functions Running custom SQL queries enables operators to extract specific datasets from the database, facilitating in-depth analysis and troubleshooting. This flexibility ensures that the software can be adapted to evolving operational requirements and provides the operator with powerful tools to maintain optimum plant performance, identify trends, and make data-driven decisions. Overall, these features contribute to a more responsive, efficient, and innovative wastewater management system. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 no alarming information will be generated. Scaling, beyond the conversion of integers to reals, is not to be employed. All failure states will be discussed with the City at pre-programming workshop to determine the proper course of action. Messages array bits are each mapped to a variable where variables 0XXX (where XXX is the unique variable number) are reserved for digital outputs, 1XXX (where XXX is the unique variable number) are reserved for digital inputs, and 4XXX (where XXX is the unique variable name) are reserved for analog inputs/outputs which are read/write blocks. 6.8.4 SCADA IO This is data and information that is being specifically marshalled for use by the SCADA system. These SCADA IO points shall be kept minimal, the basic ones that are provided and used are the simulation and disable IO points, some may be added if needed and should be documented accordingly. 6.9Data Structures Internal to most modern controllers is the concept of structured data. This is typically represented employing two methods, add-on instructions/function blocks and user data types. Each of these concepts offers opportunities to add clarity and reusability to the overall control program. User data types shall not be used for the development of new code. Only predefined add on instructions shall be used. 6.9.1 Function Blocks/Add-on Instructions For the purpose of this discussion these will be referred to as add-on instructions(AOIs) AOIs represent a unique collection of data and logic. The logic is customarily developed using the controller’s standard programming languages. The AOI also presents a usable interface to the logic through a series of parameters, both inputs and outputs. The AOI also can contain internal data registers that maintain the unique state of the instance of the AOI. The AOI in short is a complete, unique encapsulation of control data and logic. The primary benefit of the AOI is in its reusability. The advantage to the developer is that logic only has to be developed and tested once. Once proven, this logic can be reused countless times with the assurance that it will always function the same across all instances. This aids in the rapid development of entire systems. All workstation based HMI clients will be added to the configuration of the system. This allows for remote administration of the machines as well as the ability to push updates to the systems. Mobile clients, either portable or vehicle mounted, will be based on Windows 10 with cellular communications for data access. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 5 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Alarms The basic definition of an alarm(per ISA-18.2, Alarm Management) is an audible and/or visual indication to the operator that an equipment malfunction, process deviation or other abnormal condition requires a response. This standard contains definitions that help to define the various elements that must be addressed for IO derived and Setpoint derived alarms and what functionality must be applied. 5.1General Requirements The following section contains requirements and best practices that are to be applied to City alarms. 5.1.1 Generation Location All alarms will be generated and managed as close to the source as possible. This implies that all alarms will be generated in the controller, if at all possible. Utilizing alarming at the HMI/SCADA layer is the least desirable and is only to be utilized if controller based is not possible. Alarm generation will be consistent throughout the system. The alarms must mean the same thing in all cases and must be generated utilizing consistent logic. All capabilities must be implemented consistently. An example of this would be: Current value > Alarm setpoint vs Current Value ≥ Alarm setpoint ( the former case of ‘>’ is preferred) for setpoint derived alarms. Alarm generation capabilities that might be contained in the analog I/O modules themselves are to be used. These embedded alarming capabilities are not available in all modules and thus represent a mixed system. Alarming will be generated by the first or primary utilizing element. This would mean that analog values would be alarmed at the block that was performing the conversion from raw input value to scaled engineering units. In the case of discrete inputs this would be in the block that is performing the primary use or monitoring. 5.1.2 Basic configuration The following outlines the various basic elements for alarms. 5.1.2.1 Setpoints Setpoints for each alarm condition will be provided. This will be appropriate to HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 26 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 LD is the preferred language for Boolean equations. This is also an acceptable language for the development of functions and functional blocks. 3.1.2 Function Block Diagram FBD is a graphical, symbolic language based on the assembly of interconnected functional elements. The logic is similar to ladder logic, with left to right, top to bottom order. The individual functional elements(blocks) can be, and often are, complex with numerous input and output points, as well as configuration points. Program constructs are organized into “sheets”, akin to a page, on which the blocks are rendered. FBD offers the opportunity to implement consistent, repetitive programming in a compact, regular manner. As such, this approach allows for the simplified representation of complex operations. Figure 2 Function Block Diagram FBD is the preferred language for process values as well as utilization of complex functional elements. 3.2Object Library Usage The library of function blocks, and functions remove large blocks of repetitive code as well as aggregate data into one cohesive unit. Any communication between the controller and the HMI or OIT(s) must utilize City approved objects. Refer to Section 6.6 for a listing of supplied function blocks. During development the programmer shall review and, where possible, populate all relevant parameter fields for each object instance. For example, this may include setting engineering unit scaling for analog instructions, programming limit switch and position HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Source protection of code is prohibited. This includes all code created or used by systems integrators, programmers, vendors and OEM suppliers. Unless contractually stipulated to the contrary, all source code, images, databases, and supporting documentation are the property of the City. As such, all of this information is to be supplied to the City no later than initiation of site acceptance testing. 3.4Documentation It is the programmer’s responsibility to provide an annotated program compliant with the minimum documentation requirements defined in this section for all program files. As a general guideline, the level of documentation provided should allow for a technician with no familiarity of the functions of the specific unit process to read the comments and fully understand the inputs, outputs, and logic implemented in the program. 3.4.1 Object Parameters, UDT and Tag Comments It is required that all tags including object parameters and UDT elements be provided with completed description fields. Object parameters, UDT elements, and tags are subject to tag name and description requirements defined in Section 4.1 of this volume. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 2 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 HMI Software The SCADA software currently utilized by the City is VTScada 12.1.58, development environment from Trihedral Engineering, contact the City for version being used. This software suite forms the basis of the system, as well as providing the majority of the data acquisition drivers. All development of HMI screens and system wide visualization will utilize this software. All new HMI screens and graphical elements are required to be developed using the latest available City provided ChangeSet file. A ChangeSet file contains the instructions required to re-create an application. This is not an exact backup but a compendium of changes and the base information to reproduce a functional application. In general, ChangeSets are used to distribute new applications, distribute updates to an existing application, or add extra features to OEM layers. This ChangeSet file will contain all of the latest container tags, display definitions, and library widgets needed to support development activities. Each project shall request, via email to the City’s project manager (or assigned City Operational Technology(OT) contact), a copy of the current ChangeSet application immediately prior to initiating programming activities. Although updates to the standard may occasionally occur, the files provided on request date shall be utilized throughout the project. Only those updates which are determined mission critical (have severe consequences to operations, life or equipment safety) will be handled on a case by case basis through direct notice to programmers by the City’s project manager or assigned OT contact. The distribution ChangeSet file will be re-created periodically as functional elements are added and/or enhanced. Additionally, other exports will be created as required in order to maintain an appropriate level of application consistency between the City’s templates and any standalone in development. The City’s SCADA system is a managed VTScada application which has been developed using custom City developed library widgets. Refer to the City Standard Object Library Programming Guideline for details on the currently available object template library or submit an email request to the City’s project manager. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 4.10Troubleshooting Troubleshooting displays provide overview and detailed status into the health of the system. These displays are organized in a hierarchical manner utilizing the drill-down approach. At their maximum depth the displays will provide detailed information into the selected component. This methodology applies to field equipment as well as system hardware and components such as PLCs, and communications drivers. All of the displays, by nature, are to be customized to address the specifics of the equipment. At a minimum the controller and fault status along with its diagnostic information is to be provided. As much information as possible is to be provided. 4.11Flow Totals Flow totalization displays provide a quick overview of the complete facility flow values. These displays are considered to be in the process unit overview family; however, often reach into more areas than a process unit. Flow total displays should span no more than the plant to which they are attached. Totals associated with wet wells or towers will be clustered in zones/regions similar to the Remote Site summaries. These displays will represent a basic overview of the facility with the flows and totals through the critical sections. The individual totalizer faceplates will provide additional depth of information into current day totals, previous day totals, monthly totals, and annual totals. 4.12Runtime and Starts Totals Runtime and starts totals displays provide a quick overview of the complete facility motor and pump usage. These displays are considered to be in the process unit overview family. These displays will provide detailed information into the operation of electromechanical devices. The individual motor and pump faceplates will provide additional depth of information into the current day usage, previous day, monthly, and annual totals. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 February 2025 60 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 Figure 37 Standard Objects Once an object is created, it can be reused by replacing its default linked variable with a new variable associated with a different device. For example, the Knife Valve object was initially created with a default link to the variable H_010_KV047_101_01, as shown in Figure 38 This default configuration serves as a template for the object. When reusing the object, the default variable can be replaced with a new variable, such as H_010_KV047_102_02, to associate it with a different Open-Close Actuator as shown in Figure 39. It is important to emphasize that this object can only be reused for data types that are compatible, such as Open-Close Actuator. Ensuring data type compatibility is critical to maintaining the integrity and functionality of the system. This procedure is not limited to the Knife Valve object; it is extensible to all other objects in the system. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 February 2025 61 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 6 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Program Structure The City’s programs will be written utilizing LD, and FBD languages only. Any exceptions must be submitted for approval following the procedures contained in the City’s Standards Deviation Request Policy (Reference Volume 1, SCADA & PCS Standards Deviation Request). 6.1Ladder Logic LD is the preferred language when performing complex Boolean logic. This would include actions such as top-down Boolean logic, interlock development, sequencing equipment operations, or control of I/O elements. Function blocks(AOIs, FBs, FCs) may be utilized as part of the rung logic; however, this approach should be minimized. Block programming requirements often require support branches whose sole purpose is to port data into and out of the block. This approach results in rungs that are large and less comprehensible. The preferred approach in this case is to only use blocks that all of the parameters are supplied and visible; typical of internal instructions. Rungs shall be organized in a one concept per rung manner. Stringing logic across rungs through the use of intermediary bits is to be avoided as much as possible. Logic that becomes excessively large will require being broken into multiple rungs. In cases where logic is split, it is incumbent on the developer to provide clarity and explain logic flow. LD shall be limited in size to that which can be printed on an 11” x 17” sheet of paper, either portrait or landscape. LD is a suitable language for the construction of custom function blocks. The same programming constraints are to be applied in this case as well. 6.2Function Block Diagram FBD is preferred when working with blocks and process values and control. Operations such as analog/digital input mapping, PID control elements, stand-alone alarm handling, and totalization should be handled in this manner. It is possible to perform Boolean logic in FBD; however, this should be kept to a minimum. The density of information contained on the sheet should be as concise and legible as possible. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 30 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 The AOI interface contains a duality in its nature. From the programmer’s perspective, this allows them to interact with the code-block in a regular manner. From the graphical developer’s perspective, this presents a reliable interface to contained data that simplifies their data management requirements. This interface is a strictly enforced contract with all parties into what the contained data is and to which the inner workings of the blocks code can respond. The parameters are constructed of the controller’s basic data types. Examples are Booleans, integers, and real numbers. The parameters are also capable of handling complex data elements like arrays and user defined types(UDTs). This allows the developer the opportunity to source whatever data is required for the contained logic process. There are more subtle advantages to the use of AOIs from the controller’s perspective. Typically, the controller only maintains one copy of the executable code without regard to the total number of actual used instances. The only requirement on the controller is to have enough memory to contain the interface and internal variable data. The end result is a much smaller controller footprint. AOIs shall be used for any function that will be repetitive. An example of this is a valve. There is a high probability that a system will contain multiple valves. As such the generation of one block of code that addresses the needs for all similar acting valves can be a significant time saver. AOIs should not be used for one time code or code that has the potential to be modified(such as during start-up). AOIs are typically compiled and subsequently downloaded to the controller for optimization purposes. Modifying them typically requires the entire controller to be taken out of active state while a completely new download takes place. This can have a significant impact on the process. A secondary function of AOIs is the development and encapsulation of calculations or custom control requirements. Calculations are generally fixed in nature; an example being the solution to the quadratic equation. There is little value or clarity to be gained by exposing the details of the equation to the general codebase. The only thing that realistically matters are the results. There is also the case of supplementing or extending the functionality of native programming elements. There will often be cases where special requirements need to be applied to a native function that is not supplied or supported. In these cases, an AOI provides the opportunity for a simplified solution. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 39 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 4.1.5 Equipment Number Distinct equipment numbers shall be incremented sequentially based on multiple equipment working as part of a system in parallel or series operation. Wherever possible, the equipment number shall match the equipment descriptor or start with 01 and increment based on the number of matching loop number and type items in that system. For example: - Sludge feed pump 1 shall have an equipment number of 01, pump 2 shall have an equipment number of 02, and so on. - Three discharge pumps from one tank, pumping to a common header, would share the same loop number and iterate the equipment number to make the tag unique. - Three tanks, each with one discharge pump will have the same equipment number, but the loop numbers will be different to make the tag unique. 4.1.6 Redundancy letter If two or more instruments serve the same purpose at an identical location in a loop, they shall be labeled with an identical loop and equipment number with a letter appended, starting at A, to identify the instrument as redundant. For example if there are two PIT instruments that share a header one should be labeled H-065-PIT-110-01-A and the other labeled H-065-PIT-110-01-B 4.1.7 ISA Naming Convention The ISA naming are used to standardize the identification of instruments and equipment in process control systems. Each instrument or equipment is assigned a unique tag consisting of the loop number followed by up to three uppercase letters that describe the function. For example - “FIT” stands for Flow Indicating Transmitter - “YA” stands for State Alarm These conventions ensure consistency and clarity in documentation and communication across different disciplines. Please refer to Appendix A for the ISA naming conventions. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 21 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] 5.2.3 Alarm Rationalization Alarm rationalization is the process of reviewing, validating, and justifying the alarms that meet the criteria of a critical alarm. In other words, the rationalization specifies only those points in the process system that require alarming. The ultimate goal of alarm rationalization is to determine the most efficient number of alarms to ensure that the process system is safe and remains within normal operating range. Alarm rationalization must be accomplished in conjunction with the City at the preprogramming workshop. The basic process can be outlined as: - Check Alarm Validity - Determine the consequence of inaction - Document Cause, confirmation, and corrective action - Document operator response time - Assign alarm priority - Alarm classification - Determine alarm attributes - Address need for special handling All unused or deprecated alarms are to be removed from the system. Alarms that are no longer needed are not to be abandoned in place (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Slow Scan Task Parameter Setting Default rate 500ms Priority 5 6.4Main or “0” Each program folder must contain a routine to be named “Main” or “0”; the language type specified for this routine should be LD. The purpose of the main routine is to govern the flow of the program. The jump subroutine(JSR) instructions shall be used to reference all other logical routines in the PLC program. No other logic is permitted in the main routine. The first JSR of the main routine shall be to the initialization subroutine conditioned on first scan flag from PLC. This will allow initialization logic to run each time the PLC returns to run after power cycle or other interruption. 6.5Subroutines Routines are the lowest level of program structure; routines are subsets of programs. Subroutines are routines that are not designated as the main routine. Subroutines should contain the code for control modules, equipment modules, or other compartmentalized pieces of code or equipment. Subroutines are beneficial for encapsulating a specific function that is a subset of the larger program scope. For traceability, all routines are called from the program main routine. This allows for a single location where the program flow can be quickly identified. The following are exceptions: - Routines that capture event data such as hourly data or report data Routine names shall be named after the process areas the routine interacts with. The naming shall be _XXX_AREA_NAME, where XXX indicates the loop number and AREA_NAME is the description of the loop number area 6.6Functions and Function Blocks There are a set of predefined blocks supplied by the City to be used for specific functions. These blocks provide a common, known functionality in the PLC whose interface connects to OITs, HMIs and SCADA system. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 o AC = Reference to the type of card, AC indicates voltage type. o DI = Reference to type of input, DI indicates discrete input. o MOD-04 = Indicates the module number. 4.5Tasks This section describes the various objects available for the organization of program logic within the controller and their associated parameters. The section provides guidelines for selection and organization of logic into these objects and configuration of their properties. Three tasks are used, fast, normal, and slow. The tasks are typically reserved for message communications with other devices. The fast scan task has the highest priority of 1, the normal scan task has a priority of 3, being used for all regular monitoring and control operations. The slow scan task has a priority of 5, being reserved for processor monitoring. Each task shall contain a single program within itself and program name shall follow a similar naming as the task. 4.5.1 Task Structure Tasks are the highest organizational unit in the controller and dictate the order of execution. The nomenclature applied to tasks must represent the broadest definition of the contents. 4.5.2 Program Structure Programs represent the middle tier of the organizational structure. At this layer there is more additional granularity added to the overall structure and the naming conventions must reflect that. Naming at this layer must not overlap with the layer above. For example: - P01_Fast Program - P02_Normal Program - P03_Slow Program Individual names at this tier may be duplicated in other tasks but must be unique per task. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 24 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 4.5.3 Routine Structure This is the lowest tier of programming structure. As such the name must provide clarity into the exact programming construct. Each routine is linked to a process area for fast identification and each process area has 3 subroutines associated, except for IO subroutines that are 4. For Example, - _000_ is associated with IO Mapping. o _000_ANALOG_INPUT_MAPPING o _000_ANALOG_OUTPUT_MAPPING o _000_DIGITAL_IO_MAPPING o _000_DISCRETE_IO_MAPPING - _010_Main_PUMP_STATION_ALM - _010_Main_PUMP_STATION_ASSETS - _010 Main_PUMP_STATION_CONTROLS HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 25 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 the signal being monitored, analog or digital. All setpoints will be supplied in engineering units. 5.1.2.2 Toggling Alarms Alarms cannot be individually enabled/disabled. Equipment must be disabled in order to disable the alarms associated to that piece of equipment. Otherwise, all the equipment’s associated alarms will remain enabled. 5.1.2.3 Debounce Times Debounce times are the period(in mSec) for which the signal must be in excess of the indicated setpoint limit before the alarm is generated. Setting a value to zero eliminates the debounce function completely. Alarms are currently all set to have debounce times at zero 5.1.2.4 Dead-Band Amount The dead-band amount is used to eliminate the chattering of alarms around the setpoint by requiring the process variable to retreat from the setpoint by a set amount before the alarm resets. A dead-band setting is applied to all limit alarms monitoring a process value. The configuration parameter is applicable to analog alarms only. The amount is supplied in engineering units with a value of zero deactivating the feature. Alarms are currently all set to have debounce times at zero. 5.1.2.5 Shelving or Bypassing The ability to bypass, or shelve, an alarm for a specified period will not be provided. Alarms cannot be bypassed or shelved; alarms can only be disabled. Disabling alarms is done through disabling the associated equipment. 5.1.3 Alarm conditions All alarm conditions are expected to apply positive logic. When the alarm value is a Boolean 1/true/on then the alarm is to be considered present/active. When the alarm value is a Boolean 0/false/off then the alarm is considered to be not present/inactive. 5.2Definition and Rationalization 5.2.1 Analog Alarms The following are available alarm conditions for analog values. These will support the basic typical requirements. Condition Description Low-Low(LL) The current scaled value is below the configured Low-Low setpoint(value < Low-Low SP) Low(L) The current scaled value is below the configured HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. The ultimate goal of alarm rationalization is to determine the most efficient number of alarms to ensure that the process system is safe and remains within normal operating range. Alarms that are no longer needed are not to be abandoned in place 5.2.4 Alarm Generation Guidelines The generation of alarms is to be consistent with the ISA-18.2 alarm management guidelines. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 4.1.3 Equipment and Instrument Tags The City has been using an alphanumeric designation for process equipment identification. This was to be used for equipment tags. The alphanumeric designations to use for equipment are outlined in Appendix A. Additional designations are to follow the ISA standards as presented on instrument legends followed by “000” to be the same alphanumeric format as equipment tags. 4.1.4 Loop number This refers to the specific loop number as illustrated on the P&IDs. This is required to maintain a direct relationship between the P&IDs and generated information. Loop numbers will be assigned on the P&IDs following the process flow for each line. Loop numbers will increment starting at the top left most process line and follow that process line until it ends or joins with another, sheet to sheet, for all process equipment within a particular building. Loops are defined by each separate function in a process (manual valve, pump with related integral instruments, control valves, etc.) as assigned by the instrument engineer. Process equipment that works as a system, such as centrifuge sludge feed pump 1,2, and 3, shall share the same loop number and increment the equipment number. For example, if pump 1 has [loop]-[equipment] 101-01 pump two would be 101-02, and pump 3 would be 101-03. This also applies to dedicated equipment for that pump; an upstream and downstream pump isolation valve could be 100-01 and 102-01 respectively. Pumps 2 and 3 upstream would be 100-02,102-02, and 100-03,102-03. Instrumentation associated with a pump (pressure switch, temperature transmitter, etc.) installed upstream or downstream of the pup, without a process control equipment separating the pump from the instrumentation shall, shall use the same loop number as the pump. There will be more than 100 loops within a building so three digits will be used. The City will also use loop numbers separately numbered for equipment that normally is not shown on P&IDs (switchgear, network equipment, etc.) as well as all assets that don’t have control signals associated with them (manual valves, check valves, air release valves, etc.). Each P&ID will indicate the first and last tag used on the drawing along with 10 loop numbers for future use. The sequential loop numbers are outlined in Appendix A HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 20 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Module configurations are to be kept as close to manufacturer default(out of the box) as possible. This is to facilitate the replacement of modules on failure at some point in the future. In all cases ‘Hot Swappable’ modules are preferred. Modules that support advanced features, such as the HART protocol, should adhere to the spirit of the specification as much as possible. The ability to employ these additional features, such as multiple data points on a signal channel, offer cost savings and convenience. However, beyond returning the value and any indication of the quality of that data, extended features should not be utilized. Diagnostic points such as broken wire detect, under range, and over range are to be enabled, if selectable, and the signals utilized as faults to the analog input blocks. Module fault information is to be aggregated into a common signal and utilized as fault inputs to the analog blocks. In the case of inputs and outputs, either fault status information should be supplied to the primary controlling element. This allows the blockware to be cognizant that the element(s) that are being controlled will be non-responsive. This is applicable to equipment position control/feedback and speed control/feedback. 6.8.2 Digital IO Each point is assigned a variable. Typically, there are few configuration elements; however, in the case that such configuration elements are available, those elements are to be used sparingly or not at all. All signal diagnostic capabilities are to be employed. Filters on the input signals are not to be utilized and in all cases turned off. In the case of outputs, fault status information should be supplied to the primary controlling element. This allows the blockware to be cognizant that the element(s) that are being controlled will be non-responsive. 6.8.3 Message IO All peer-to-peer tag values passed through message arrays shall be read through the fast scan. Message routines shall be named by applying the prefix “MSG_” to the paired controller’s name, followed by “_IO”. An example would be “MSG_ H-010-PLC000-800­ 01_IO” (Howard F. Curren AWTP, MPS Electrical room, PLC number 1). Messaged data will be handled in a similar manner as module supplied data, through analog blocks. In the case of messaged data, the success or failure of communications will be used as the data quality. As the receiving controller is not the owner of the data HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 37 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 7.3DNP3 Exception Configuration The communication protocol between remote controllers and the supervisory(SCADA) system will be based on the DNP3 protocol as specified by DNP.ORG. This protocol shall be only used on remote sites. The transport layer for communications is to be appropriate for the application, e.g., LAN, serial modems, etc. No proprietary protocols are to be implemented. The master will be configured to perform its polling utilizing events(exceptions). Only periodically will static values be requested to insure validity and quality. The definition of what constitutes an event are dependent on the nature of the data point and acceptable excursion limits. All events will include a timestamp to facilitate sequence of event generation. The data is to be formatted and marshalled to facilitate optimal polling. Data being read from LAN based sources will by nature support a larger number of points being read than those from serial based devices. As such the data being sent must have the event thresholds and organization considered prior to implementation. Due to the potential communications loss events that can occur to some remote stations the concept of store & forward will be fully implemented. Therefore, all controllers must support this method of operation. This functionality may be supplied either natively or by the use of in-chassis interface modules. Each device is to be configured to maintain the data as long as possible, this is heavily dependent on the device design. Standard polling of remote locations by the SCADA system is to be configured such that the entire store & forward buffer may be read. This is much more critical when communicating to devices utilizing serial or cellular modems where outstations are typically read in a round-robin approach. In these cases, acquiring the data and storing the data into the historian in chronological order is critical. Stations that have a higher probability of continuous connection may be configured for unsolicited messaging. This feature is to be used sparingly and only for critical events. This feature will not be supportable in systems employing round-robin polling. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 43 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 6.9.2 User Defined Data Types Data is the foundation of what the controllers are utilizing. All controllers support and supply atomic data types; examples are Booleans, integers, and real numbers. These are the fundamental elements upon which control programs are built. Controllers also natively support arrays of these basic types, providing a primitive complex type. The user definted data type(UDT) is the logical extension of the primitive complex type into a structure that is constructed of multiple data types. In this case a UDT is a container for Boolean elements, integers, reals, arrays, and even other UDTs. UDTs are not to be used for programming HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 40 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 appended to the controller name utilizing the structure _r## where the hash marks reflect the specific version number. The specifics of the version as well as the reasoning for incrementing will be contained in the internal program documentation. An example of this is: H_075_PLC000_800_01_r04.ext The file name above indicates this is version 4 of the controller H_075_PLC000_800_01 The ‘ext’ indicates the manufacturer’s assigned extension for the file. When periodic backups are taken, the files will have a date code added to the file name. This will be in the form of yymmdd. This reflects the year, month, and day, in two(2) digit format upon which the file was saved. If further granularity is required then hhmm, representing hour in 24 hour time and the minute, may be added to the date code. A full example of the backup file naming convention is: H-075_PLC000_800_01_r04_2410041603.ext This indicates that the backup was captured on October 4,2024 at 4:03 pm. In this manner, multiple versions and backups may exist in the same subdirectory without interfering with each other. The data coding also leverages the native lexicographic sort order of Microsoft file systems. 4.4Module Naming Each module must be assigned a unique description in the controller to aid in identifying the hardware. The naming convention for modules is defined as: R#S#_[Module Type] Where: R# = Physical Rack number S# = Physical Slot number [Module Type] = the common acronym taken from the manufacturers part number Examples: - 1756_IA16I_R2S4_ACDI_MOD_04 o 1756_IA16I = This indicates the model ref. o R2S4 = Reference to location, rack 2 slot 4. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 23 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 FBD sheets should be limited in size to that which can be printed on an 11” x 17” sheet of paper. 6.3Task and Program Files Tasks provide the broadest level of organization. They also provide a level of execution control, that being continuous, periodic, event amongst others. Only two types of tasks are to be employed at the City: continuous and periodic. All other types of task types will not be employed. 6.3.1 Periodic tasks Periodic tasks are designed to execute on a fixed period of time, typically in milliseconds. These tasks are conducive when working with a process or system that is slow, or requires the processing on a regular basis. Creation of periodic tasks should be kept to a minimum(six or less) as required by the controlled process or area. The periodic task execution rate should be set to allow ample time for program execution without burdening the processor. Programmers shall frequently monitor and adjust the execution rate based on the task time. All programs shall be commissioned with task settings tuned such that no overlaps exist in the program. During commissioning, the main program overlap counter shall be confirmed at zero and FAULTLOG object shall not have any minor faults for overlap. Each periodic task must be set with a priority from 1 to 15, with 1 representing the highest priority. It is recommended that each task be set with a unique priority. Fast Scan Task Parameter Setting Default rate 10ms Priority 1 Normal Scan Task Parameter Setting Default rate 250ms Priority 3 HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 3.1.4 VTScada integration The software must be able to pull data from the VTScada database without the user having access to the VTScada system or database. This capability is crucial for a plant operator because it ensures seamless integration and data flow without compromising the security and integrity of the VTScada system. By allowing data to be accessed and utilized without direct interaction with the VTScada environment, plant operators can streamline operations, improve data handling efficiency, and minimize the risk of unauthorized access or potential system disruptions. This feature also facilitates the creation of comprehensive reports and dashboards, enabling integrators to monitor and optimize plant performance while maintaining stringent security protocols. Due to the level of importance of ensuring that the software platform is well integrated with the report creating platform this requirement is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.5 Other Databases The software must be able to pull data from sources other than VTScada such as LIMS for lab works, HSQ for data not yet available in VTScada. This capability is crucial for a wastewater plant operator because it ensures comprehensive data integration and accessibility. By allowing the software to gather information from multiple databases, operators can obtain a holistic view of the plant's operations. This integrated approach facilitates better monitoring and management of plant metrics, enhances decisionmaking processes, and ensures compliance with regulatory standards. Moreover, it minimizes the risk of data silos, improves operational efficiency, and supports the creation of detailed and accurate reports, which are essential for optimizing plant performance and maintaining environmental safety. Due to the importance of being able to link to other databases this requirement is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.6 Automated Report Generation The software must have the ability to automatically generate reports and email these reports to users on a schedule. This feature is particularly vital for operators as it ensures timely access to essential data without the need for manual report generation. Automated reports can include critical metrics such as water quality indicators, equipment performance data, and regulatory compliance status. By receiving these reports on a predefined schedule, operators can swiftly identify and address potential issues, streamline operational workflows, and maintain adherence to environmental regulations. Additionally, automated report generation reduces administrative burden, allowing operators to focus on more strategic and high-priority tasks. Due to the level critical nature of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 5. 3.1.7 Security The software must be compliant with NIST standards for cybersecurity to ensure safety for use in the plant. This is crucial for a wastewater operator because it guarantees that all data and operations are protected from cyber threats. Given the sensitive nature of wastewater treatment processes, any breach could lead to significant environmental, and risk to human health and lives. Robust cybersecurity ensures the integrity and reliability of the system, allowing operators to focus on maintaining plant performance HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 12 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 5. Software recommendation Based on the outcome of the decision matrix the best choice for report creation software would be Ocean Data System’s DreamReport platform. Although other platforms had advantages over the DreamReport platform the overall scores for the DreamReport platform were 205 for the weighted score and 52 for the unweighted score. DreamReport offers a range of advantages that make it a valuable software platform for various applications. Its dashboard usability is commendable, allowing users to display specific data metrics over a range of time and create additional dashboards for viewing performance metrics. This flexibility in dashboard configuration enhances the user experience by providing tailored views of critical data. The platform supports custom report creation, although it is limited by the number of available licenses. However, additional licenses can be added to the system, which allows for greater customization and scalability in reporting capabilities. Integration with VTScada is available, with over 100 drivers available to connect different databases, making it compatible with many different systems. This extensive integration capability ensures that DreamReport can work seamlessly with various data sources, enhancing its utility in diverse operational environments. The software also excels in database integration due to the large number of available drivers, which facilitates smooth data flow and management across different platforms. Automatic triggered reports can be set up based on specific conditions or schedules, providing flexibility in automation. For instance, reports can be generated when a specific water parameter or equipment function reaches a certain threshold, or they can be scheduled to run at regular intervals. These reports can be sent out to specific individuals through email in various formats, such as PDF, ensuring timely and efficient communication of critical information. DreamReport is compliant with NIST cybersecurity standards and can be set up to be 21 CFR part 11 compliant, enhancing its security credentials and making it suitable for use in regulated industries. While the browser usability is good for viewing, report editing must be done through the report creator software. This limitation is offset by the ease of use and look of the browser user interface, which scored well in evaluations. Additionally, DreamReport provides regular security and general updates as part of its subscription model, ensuring that the software remains up-to-date and secure. The availability of a redundancy license at a competitive cost further enhances its appeal, providing a cost-effective solution for ensuring system reliability. The company behind DreamReport, Ocean Data Systems, is noted for its responsive and knowledgeable customer service. Their sales representatives are quick to respond and provide exceptional knowledge of the software platform, which adds to the overall positive user experience. In summary, DreamReport stands out for its robust integration capabilities, flexible automation options, and strong security compliance. While there are some limitations in terms of scalability and browserbased report creation, the platform's specialized functions, regular updates, and excellent customer service make it a compelling choice for organizations looking to enhance their data reporting and management capabilities. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 30 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 Key Concepts 3.1VTScada Development platform The VTScada development platform contains key concepts that need to be understood in order to plan, design, and implement an application. Key concepts for tag based configuration, tag widgets data acquisition, historian, configuration nodes, HMI clients, and development nodes are described below. 3.1.1 Tag Based Configuration Tags are software elements representing physical objects or concepts. Tags represent communications ports, device drivers, I/O elements for a device, historical logging of data, and alarms. The combination of various functional tags into larger units will create orderly, consistent structures that may then be directly utilized or combined together further with other functional elements. The underlying concept to the system is that everything is a tag. Whether a single atomic element or a collection of complex elements the end result is simply considered to be a tag. This in turn allows the tags to be referenced as a single item with all of the inheritted properties immediately available. 3.1.2 Tag Widgets Tag information is visualized to the operators utilizing widgets. These widgets may be indicators, push buttons, numeric values, numeric entries, gauges, variable fill, and more. The crucial point to this is that the visualization elements are very tightly coupled with the data elements. 3.1.3 Data Acquisition Data acquisition(DA) is to be configured as appropriate to the controller(s) being monitored. The preferred communications pathways are as follows: - For PCS or process: Ethernet/IP or Modbus TCP - For remote telemetry: DNP3 protocol over Ethernet HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 February 2025 12 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
