The following observations were made during these investigations: - PLC 19 in the Main Pump Station is connected to the facility network rack directly over a CAT6 cable, fiber is not used. - There are eleven main trunk fiber cables landed at a fiber patch panel for process connections located at the Main Pump Station that then are connected to the facility network rack. - There are additional fiber cables landed at a separate fiber patch panel in the network rack room, however these fiber lines were installed by the City for various staff workstations and not part of the facility process fiber network. - The Red Zone was found to consist of the following fiber runs: - o One fiber cable that connects the network to PLC 11, PLC 12, PLC 13, and PLC10 as follows: - Patch panel near PLC 12 has a trunk cable back to the network rack patch panel - PLC 12 connects by fiber to the patch panel near PLC 12 - PLC 11 connects by fiber to the patch panel near PLC 12 - PLC 13 connects by fiber to a patch panel near PLC 13 which then connects to the patch panel near PLC 12 - PLC 10 connects by fiber to the patch panel near PLC 13 - o One fiber cable that connects the network to PLC 5 and PLC 39 as follows: - PLC 39 connects by fiber to a patch panel at PLC 5 - PLC 5 connects by fiber to the patch panel in PLC 5 panel HFC AWTP Master Plan **June 10,2022** Improvements: SCADA Design Criteria ![](_page_22_Picture_0.jpeg) ![](_page_22_Picture_1.jpeg) - New PLCs can connect directly to plant fiber network, they do not have to connect through a remote telemetry unit. - Uninterruptible power supplies shall be an APC UPS without ethernet connections and with a bypass switch for easy change out. Recommend Network Sequencing 3.1 Maintaining Monitoring and Reporting Once the P&IDs are developed and preliminary control descriptions are available, discussion on where new PLC panels will be located and how I/O will be connected to the new PLC modules will be made at milestones for each project. A detailed sequence on how to maintain monitoring and reporting will be developed as part of these discussions. These sequencing plans will be reviewed with City staff prior to completing the 90% Design. 3.2 Replacing Fiber Cables All fiber cables for each process, which includes fibers for the camera feeds, will be replaced as part of any improvement to that process. During development of the Preliminary P&IDs a thorough site investigations shall be performed to verify the existing fiber runs from the associated PLCs back to the main network fiber patch panel. This includes removing electrical manhole covers and draining the manholes. The purpose of these investigations is to determine what existing conduit runs will be used to best show on the design site plans for the fiber installation. At any point thereafter, investigations shall be performed to confirm new cable can be run through the existing conduits without removing the existing fiber cables. Final network diagram will include fiber cable labeling. Labeling for the cables and for the ports at patch panels will be installed in the field to match the network diagram. Multiple cables run through the same conduits and manholes will be depicted on the network site plan. 3.3 Conversion to VTScada VTScada HMI programming will be developed and placed in service parallel to the existing HSQ HMI system on a separate network rack and made available for interface on new workstations throughout the plant, as well as on wireless devices. As a process is made available on the VTScada HMI and is installed, tested, and passed an approved reliability demonstration period, the associated HSQ HMI display screens could be removed or made view only. The goal will be to get each process on the VTScada HMI to use for setpoint adjustment, monitoring, and/or manual override. However, there will be some parameters that may impact separate processes still controlled only through HSQ. A plan will need to be developed for such situations to provide a protocol to operations until all control parameters have been switched over to VTScada. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 6 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Program Structure The City’s programs will be written utilizing LD, and FBD languages only. Any exceptions must be submitted for approval following the procedures contained in the City’s Standards Deviation Request Policy (Reference Volume 1, SCADA & PCS Standards Deviation Request). 6.1Ladder Logic LD is the preferred language when performing complex Boolean logic. This would include actions such as top-down Boolean logic, interlock development, sequencing equipment operations, or control of I/O elements. Function blocks(AOIs, FBs, FCs) may be utilized as part of the rung logic; however, this approach should be minimized. Block programming requirements often require support branches whose sole purpose is to port data into and out of the block. This approach results in rungs that are large and less comprehensible. The preferred approach in this case is to only use blocks that all of the parameters are supplied and visible; typical of internal instructions. Rungs shall be organized in a one concept per rung manner. Stringing logic across rungs through the use of intermediary bits is to be avoided as much as possible. Logic that becomes excessively large will require being broken into multiple rungs. In cases where logic is split, it is incumbent on the developer to provide clarity and explain logic flow. LD shall be limited in size to that which can be printed on an 11” x 17” sheet of paper, either portrait or landscape. LD is a suitable language for the construction of custom function blocks. The same programming constraints are to be applied in this case as well. 6.2Function Block Diagram FBD is preferred when working with blocks and process values and control. Operations such as analog/digital input mapping, PID control elements, stand-alone alarm handling, and totalization should be handled in this manner. It is possible to perform Boolean logic in FBD; however, this should be kept to a minimum. The density of information contained on the sheet should be as concise and legible as possible. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 30 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

This integration is crucial for a wastewater operator because VTScada is a widely used HMI system that provides realtime monitoring and control of plant operations. By ensuring compatibility with VTScada, the software allows operators to seamlessly access and manage all relevant data through a single interface, enhancing operational efficiency and reducing the risk of errors. This not only supports proactive maintenance and troubleshooting but also helps in ensuring regulatory compliance and optimizing plant performance. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. 3.1.13 Software support and maintenance The software should have support security updates and technical support. This is important for a wastewater operator because security is paramount in protecting sensitive data and ensuring the integrity of the system. Regular security updates help safeguard the system against potential cyber threats, vulnerabilities, and unauthorized access, which can compromise the operation of the plant. By having these updates included without extra charges, operators can maintain a high level of security consistently, ensuring continuous protection without worrying about additional expenses. This proactive approach not only secures the plant's operations but also aligns with regulatory standards and best practices for infrastructure management. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. 3.1.14 Redundancy The software should be able to be set up with a redundant system to allow for the use of the report creator even if the main server is taken offline. This is crucial for a wastewater operator because redundancy ensures continuous access to critical reporting tools, minimizing downtime and maintaining smooth operations. In the event of a system failure or maintenance, having a redundant setup means that data analysis can proceed without interruption, thus supporting timely decision-making and regulatory compliance. This resilience is essential in safeguarding the plant's functionality and ensuring that operators can respond promptly to any issues that arise. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. 3.1.15 Company prominence The software company should be a reputable company with good industry presence and expertise. This is important for a wastewater operator because a reputable company ensures that the software is reliable, secure, and compliant with industry standards. A company with a strong industry presence is more likely to be aware of the latest advancements and regulatory changes, ensuring that the software remains up-todate and effective in managing the complex requirements of wastewater treatment. Expertise in the field means that the company can provide high-quality support and tailored solutions, addressing specific operational challenges and improving overall plant performance. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] 5.2.3 Alarm Rationalization Alarm rationalization is the process of reviewing, validating, and justifying the alarms that meet the criteria of a critical alarm. In other words, the rationalization specifies only those points in the process system that require alarming. The ultimate goal of alarm rationalization is to determine the most efficient number of alarms to ensure that the process system is safe and remains within normal operating range. Alarm rationalization must be accomplished in conjunction with the City at the preprogramming workshop. The basic process can be outlined as: - Check Alarm Validity - Determine the consequence of inaction - Document Cause, confirmation, and corrective action - Document operator response time - Assign alarm priority - Alarm classification - Determine alarm attributes - Address need for special handling All unused or deprecated alarms are to be removed from the system. Alarms that are no longer needed are not to be abandoned in place (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 Key Concepts 3.1VTScada Development platform The VTScada development platform contains key concepts that need to be understood in order to plan, design, and implement an application. Key concepts for tag based configuration, tag widgets data acquisition, historian, configuration nodes, HMI clients, and development nodes are described below. 3.1.1 Tag Based Configuration Tags are software elements representing physical objects or concepts. Tags represent communications ports, device drivers, I/O elements for a device, historical logging of data, and alarms. The combination of various functional tags into larger units will create orderly, consistent structures that may then be directly utilized or combined together further with other functional elements. The underlying concept to the system is that everything is a tag. Whether a single atomic element or a collection of complex elements the end result is simply considered to be a tag. This in turn allows the tags to be referenced as a single item with all of the inheritted properties immediately available. 3.1.2 Tag Widgets Tag information is visualized to the operators utilizing widgets. These widgets may be indicators, push buttons, numeric values, numeric entries, gauges, variable fill, and more. The crucial point to this is that the visualization elements are very tightly coupled with the data elements. 3.1.3 Data Acquisition Data acquisition(DA) is to be configured as appropriate to the controller(s) being monitored. The preferred communications pathways are as follows: - For PCS or process: Ethernet/IP or Modbus TCP - For remote telemetry: DNP3 protocol over Ethernet HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 February 2025 12 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 no alarming information will be generated. Scaling, beyond the conversion of integers to reals, is not to be employed. All failure states will be discussed with the City at pre-programming workshop to determine the proper course of action. Messages array bits are each mapped to a variable where variables 0XXX (where XXX is the unique variable number) are reserved for digital outputs, 1XXX (where XXX is the unique variable number) are reserved for digital inputs, and 4XXX (where XXX is the unique variable name) are reserved for analog inputs/outputs which are read/write blocks. 6.8.4 SCADA IO This is data and information that is being specifically marshalled for use by the SCADA system. These SCADA IO points shall be kept minimal, the basic ones that are provided and used are the simulation and disable IO points, some may be added if needed and should be documented accordingly. 6.9Data Structures Internal to most modern controllers is the concept of structured data. This is typically represented employing two methods, add-on instructions/function blocks and user data types. Each of these concepts offers opportunities to add clarity and reusability to the overall control program. User data types shall not be used for the development of new code. Only predefined add on instructions shall be used. 6.9.1 Function Blocks/Add-on Instructions For the purpose of this discussion these will be referred to as add-on instructions(AOIs) AOIs represent a unique collection of data and logic. The logic is customarily developed using the controller’s standard programming languages. The AOI also presents a usable interface to the logic through a series of parameters, both inputs and outputs. The AOI also can contain internal data registers that maintain the unique state of the instance of the AOI. The AOI in short is a complete, unique encapsulation of control data and logic. The primary benefit of the AOI is in its reusability. The advantage to the developer is that logic only has to be developed and tested once. Once proven, this logic can be reused countless times with the assurance that it will always function the same across all instances. This aids in the rapid development of entire systems. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 1. Introduction The Howard F. Curren Advanced Water Treatment Plant(HFC AWTP) owns and operates a Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition(SCADA) system currently composed mainly of General Electric(GE) PACSystems Rx3i and 90-30 series programmable logic controllers(PLCs) at the field level. General Electric was acquired by Emerson in February, 2019. This document refers to GE equipment as GE to maintain familiarity with City’s existing Standards. As manufacturers continue to make advancements in product development, older versions of hardware advance towards “legacy” status. Products in legacy status are taken out of production and typically are no longercommercially available from the manufacturer. Replacement parts must then be sourced from third party sellers, increasing the risk for price inflation and long lead times. In that case, a set of spares must be stringently maintained to prevent unnecessary down time in the event of a failure. Additionally, manufacturers eventually stop developing and releasing software updates for legacy controllers, which may pose a security risk on network connected systems. A majority of HFC AWTP’s PLC hardware has entered the legacy phase. While PLC upgrades may be costly up front, updating the PLCs reduces the risk of unplanned downtime and enhances network security. 2. Objectives A majority of the existing PLCs reached legacy status in 2018 (GE 90-30 and GE 90-70 series). PLC hardware for these products are no longer supported by the manufacturer. With the impending replacement of all PLC hardware as part of the on-going design-build project, the City has chosen to evaluate alternatives to develop a new standard for PLCs at the plant. Existing PLCs at the plant were evaluated by the City. As a result, Hazen and Sawyer(Hazen) was asked to review the existing PLC hardware to determine equivalent alternatives. Four major PLC vendors were evaluated for consideration whenever existing PLC line ups are to be replaced as part of on-going process improvements at HFC AWTP. The different PLC models were compared including features and budgetary costs for full system replacement. The findings are summarized in this PLC Hardware Evaluation along with a recommendation for the standard PLC hardware platform going forward. 3. Assessment of Existing PLCs & Industry Review 3.1 Existing PLCs An audit was performed by McKim & Creed & Hazen in June 2018 with assistance from HFC AWTP staff to quantify the number of existing PLCs installed at the facility. HFC AWTP staff provided information about the existing PLCs at these sites including existing network diagrams, model of PLC at each location, quantity, for review and use for this evaluation. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System PLC Hardware Evaluation 6 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 FBD sheets should be limited in size to that which can be printed on an 11” x 17” sheet of paper. 6.3Task and Program Files Tasks provide the broadest level of organization. They also provide a level of execution control, that being continuous, periodic, event amongst others. Only two types of tasks are to be employed at the City: continuous and periodic. All other types of task types will not be employed. 6.3.1 Periodic tasks Periodic tasks are designed to execute on a fixed period of time, typically in milliseconds. These tasks are conducive when working with a process or system that is slow, or requires the processing on a regular basis. Creation of periodic tasks should be kept to a minimum(six or less) as required by the controlled process or area. The periodic task execution rate should be set to allow ample time for program execution without burdening the processor. Programmers shall frequently monitor and adjust the execution rate based on the task time. All programs shall be commissioned with task settings tuned such that no overlaps exist in the program. During commissioning, the main program overlap counter shall be confirmed at zero and FAULTLOG object shall not have any minor faults for overlap. Each periodic task must be set with a priority from 1 to 15, with 1 representing the highest priority. It is recommended that each task be set with a unique priority. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] 6.4Main or "0" Each program folder must contain a routine to be named "Main" or "0"; the language type specified for this routine should be LD. The purpose of the main routine is to govern the flow of the program. The jump subroutine(JSR) instructions shall be used to reference all other logical routines in the PLC program. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 2 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 HMI Software The SCADA software currently utilized by the City is VTScada 12.1.58, development environment from Trihedral Engineering, contact the City for version being used. This software suite forms the basis of the system, as well as providing the majority of the data acquisition drivers. All development of HMI screens and system wide visualization will utilize this software. All new HMI screens and graphical elements are required to be developed using the latest available City provided ChangeSet file. A ChangeSet file contains the instructions required to re-create an application. This is not an exact backup but a compendium of changes and the base information to reproduce a functional application. In general, ChangeSets are used to distribute new applications, distribute updates to an existing application, or add extra features to OEM layers. This ChangeSet file will contain all of the latest container tags, display definitions, and library widgets needed to support development activities. Each project shall request, via email to the City’s project manager (or assigned City Operational Technology(OT) contact), a copy of the current ChangeSet application immediately prior to initiating programming activities. Although updates to the standard may occasionally occur, the files provided on request date shall be utilized throughout the project. Only those updates which are determined mission critical (have severe consequences to operations, life or equipment safety) will be handled on a case by case basis through direct notice to programmers by the City’s project manager or assigned OT contact. The distribution ChangeSet file will be re-created periodically as functional elements are added and/or enhanced. Additionally, other exports will be created as required in order to maintain an appropriate level of application consistency between the City’s templates and any standalone in development. The City’s SCADA system is a managed VTScada application which has been developed using custom City developed library widgets. Refer to the City Standard Object Library Programming Guideline for details on the currently available object template library or submit an email request to the City’s project manager. For the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvements Dewatering Facility Replacement project, RTP will be developing the HMI screens with oversight from the City. These displays will be used to set standards for displays to provide for all improvements. 2.3.3 HMI Security The following extent of exposure to internet connectivity will be incorporated into the SCADA design for each process improvement:  VPN connection for remote setup for vendor access to HMI when allowed by the City.  Similar to existing, access to HMI by City staff through handheld devices and laptops to connect through internet VPN over wireless cellular means. Previous efforts to create wireless hotspots proved to be inadequate at this site.  New controls at the PLC level on the fiber network shall be accessible through similar wireless internet VPN connectivity. 2.4 Site Security & Monitoring The existing plant camera system only includes ten(10) cameras. These are listed in Appendix G. With each improvement, design will include new cameras to eventually be able to monitor all areas within the plant. Location, number, and type of cameras will be discussed with City staff as part of the detail design phase of each HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvement. The existing SONY system is to be replaced with a GENETEC system, including a new server. The initial upgrade will occur during the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvements Dewatering Facility Replacement, which is concurrently under construction. All existing cameras will be transitioned to the new GENETEC system once it is operational. Entry gates will utilize GENETEC card reader system with interface to the new base system to allow monitoring of entry points, two-way voice communication for visitors, and remote operation of gates. Additionally, the City may replace the site perimeter fence with an eight-foot(8’) tall fence at some point in the future. HFC AWTP Master Plan June 10,2022 Improvements: SCADA Design Criteria Technical Memorandum Page 11 of 15 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 2.5 Hardware 2.5.1 General Requirements All improvements shall adhere the following requirements:  All programmers, whether vendor or integrator, are to follow the same standards for hardware and software.  Provide computer workstations in key buildings as directed by the City to allow full control and monitoring of entire plant from each location. Existing computer workstations will not be replaced.  Provide independent vendor PLC hardware for individual equipment systems that interface with overall system PLCs provided by integrator. Signals to vendor supplied controls shall be hardwired for all control I/O. Supplemental information such as power usage or other diagnostics can be over Ethernet link from vendor supplied PLCs.  Provide a local control station at each piece of equipment(HOR, L/R, FOR, Start/Stop, etc) for local control with a means to lockout the equipment(lockout stop pushbutton) when in the off position. All such controls to be NEMA4X industrial grade and Square D 9001SK series.  Provide spare parts for CPUs, I/O Cards, Network Switches, fuses, and other components that are critical for operation or prone to wearing out.  Instrument specifications will be in data sheet format for specific make and model numbers rather than lengthy descriptions intended for “or equal” interpretation.  Electrical gear shall be provide with the manufacturer’s standard power monitoring devices.  Witnessed factory testing will not be required. Specifications will require onsite checkout with City staff followed by a 48-hour demonstration test.  Project specifications will require training for both operations and maintenance staff. Operation and maintenance manuals shall be required to be on hand for associated training. 2.5.2 Control Panels Control Panels shall be specified to be provided with the components outlined in Table 2-4, for panel components not listed in this table the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvements Dewatering Facility Replacement project will be used as a standard. Improvements shall include the following requirements:  Dual OITs will be provided on all panels with PLCs, this includes main process panels and individual equipment panels. Both OITs shall have full redundancy, generally one will be used for setpoints and the other for monitoring.  Within panels, CAT6 cable shall be shielded industrial cable and rated for 600V if exposed to 480V power.  Wiring within panels shall separate for different voltages and purposes. Role Name Description Logged off This is the specific role that defines the functions and access to the system when no user is logged on. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 8 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 HMI Graphics Alarming 8.1Overview Alarm configuration requirements are contained in the City’s Alarm Management Strategy (Reference the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual, Volume 2). Any alarm modification, addition or deletion must follow alarm definition and rationalization procedures discussed in the alarm management strategy prior to implementation. It is important to note that although OIT and HMI system functions can be used to monitor and generate alarms, in the City’s PCS process alarms shall be generated in the PLCs which will be logged and displayed on the HMIs and OITs. HMI application shall not be configured to perform alarm calculations. HMI generated alarms will be utilized only for alarms and events initiated from within the HMI software such as software status alarms, loss of communications with a PLC and other system level alarming. Alarm limits must be adjustable through the HMI graphics screens; however, those alarm limits will be used by the PLC in determining if there is an alarm condition. The nature of the distributed PCS is such that the PLC-based process control logic can and will continue to operate, even if one or more HMIs become unavailable. As a result, the PLC must be able to determine alarm conditions and act accordingly without the need for continuous communication with the HMI system. The City’s Standard Object Library and add-on instruction(AOI) standard library objects shall be utilized for configuration of PLC and HMI. 8.2Nuisance Alarm Suppression Techniques All nuisance alarm suppression shall be by approved methods documented in the City’s Alarm Management Strategy (Reference the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual, Volume 2 for more details on alarm suppression techniques). Disabling equipment will disable the alarms associated with the equipment. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. As such, a series of standards has been developed to address these needs. 4.1Tag Naming Conventions As data is generated by the various elements of the process, the easiest way to track things is through human decipherable tag names. The tags stand in the place of the somewhat arcane addressing and naming conventions that manufacturers use to address their points. This also results in a language construct that is regular and nonrepetitive. It is extremely important that the naming system yield unique names with a consistent format throughout the entire system. As such, the following naming convention is to be utilized throughout the entire City system. These mnemonics are to be applied to any and all elements that might be transferred from a controller to another system, i.e., SCADA, HMI, OIT, gateway, or controller. This system shall be utilized, but in a paired down manner, for internal registers and flags within a controller. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. These stations may be based on workstations, laptops, or mobile devices. As such additional consideration must be given to the functionality of the screens as the platform changes. All workstation-based HMI clients will be added to the configuration of the system. This allows for remote administration of the machines as well as the ability to push updates to the systems. Mobile clients, either portable or vehicle mounted, will be based on Windows 10 with cellular communications for data access. As with all systems these stations will be added to the system configuration for centralized management and application synchronization. 3.1.6 Deployment Nodes Deployment nodes are workstations on which new applications will be methodically deployed. These stations will have the system property “Automatically Deploy Local Changes” disabled. These stations accept newly developed/tested and approved ChangeSet files and provide a platform to methodically deploy the edits. Once loaded these changes will be “Accepted for Distribution” and the native application synchronization service will distribute the application to the configured nodes. 3.1.7 Historian The process value historian function will be conversely aligned with the primary and backup function of the data acquisition servers. In this manner the primary data acquisition server system will function as the backup historian server. All process value data storage files will be specified to reside in a subdirectory on a non-operating system drive partition under the name “.\Historian”. The alarm databases will also be located on the data acquisition servers in separate, native alarm database files. The alarm database historian server files will be specified to HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 February 2025 14 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 The physical data is required to be aggregated with the quality information into one cohesive unit with a new unique name. This point or tag name is what will be used in lieu of the physical I/O address and any/all of its quality Programmers shall organize all I/O, (local, networked and messaged) into four subroutines, _000_ANALOG_INPUT_MAPPING, _000_ANALOG_OUTPUT_MAPPING, _000_DIGITAL_IO_MAPPING, _000_DISCRETE_IO_MAPPING To clarify the above: - All Inputs must be mapped into variable objects with unique, symbolic tag names - In all cases input points must be used with quality information supplied. - All outputs will be directly controlled by unique, symbolic tag names. I/O mapping programs shall be organized within the normal scan periodic task. Program nomenclature shall follow the guideline provided below. The number and types shall be limited to those required for the project but will likely include at least some of these: - Analog_IO - Digital_IO - Message_IO - Network_IO - Modbus_IO Some controllers support the use of tag aliasing, which allows for the abstraction of complex input or output addressing into a single symbolic name. This feature is not to be employed in place of the mapping requirements outlined above. This methodology does not include the quality of the data in question. 6.8.1 Analog IO The configuration of the individual point type is to be performed in the module. Modules that support multiple types of input, such as 4-20mA and 0-10VDC must be set to the appropriate selection at the module. Any input module specific configurations, such as scaling and alarming are not to be utilized at the module level. All signal diagnostic capabilities are to be employed. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 8.3Commissioning Requirements Commissioning activities create abnormal alarm situations; this section contains requirements for alarm and event system during commissioning activities. 8.3.1 Operational Readiness Review Meetings Alarms and events risk review shall be reviewed at operational readiness meetings to determine alarm owner(contractor or operations) based on risk to process and processing equipment. 8.3.2 Alarms in Active Commissioning State Alarms and events which are not turned over to operations at operational readiness review are required to be suppressed by means of PLC logic suppression outside of testing hours to avoid presenting operators with nuisance and/or unknown alarms. 8.3.3 Alarm and Event Acceptance Matrices The City utilizes the defined average alarm rate of less than six alarms per hour period as defined in alarm management strategy in Volume 2 as maximum allowable alarm rate for acceptance of modifications to alarm system. After commissioning activities have been completed, system shall run for 30 days in hands-off mode to document alarm rates for modified alarms. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 7 SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 Recovery and Restore Policy 7.1General The City’s SCADA & PCS Recovery and Restore Policy is intended to establish requirements to promote efficient and accurate system restoration after a failure has occurred. The City’s SCADA & PCS consist of numerous highly integrated software packages, which undergo continual change for plant maintenance, operational improvements, and projects. This policy and procedure addresses requirements to promote known good system backups; and procedures to identify and utilize the backups and asset management systems during recovery. This policy applies to all City SCADA & PCS software assets. 7.2Backup Storage Requirements The City has deployed a SCADA & PCS Change Management server for version control and file storage for SCADA & PCS Software applications including PLC, and OIT network application files as well as HMI standard object templates. If the programmer does not have access to the City’s SCADA & PCS Change Management Server, then the programmer shall request a data transfer for the following: - HMI backup - HMI standard object templates - OIT standard object templates The following applications shall be stored in the SCADA & PCS change management server at initial installation and after any modification has been deployed in the production environment: - PLC program files including network configuration files - OIT application files - Network switch configuration files - Radio configuration files All contractors completing on-site startup/commissioning modifications to operating equipment are required to complete change documentation and upload files to SCADA & PCS Change Management Server at the end of each shift. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] During the installation of new fiber cables, the existing cables shall be left in place. It is recommended to perform field investigations during the design phases to verify new fiber cable can to installed utilizing the existing infrastruce. 2.3 HMI Software Conversion 2.3.1 VTScada The City is in the process of creating VTScada screens to replace the existing HSQ system. Currently, the City has implement monitoring only screens for some of the facilities through VTScada, this application is running in parallel the existing HSQ application. The following facilities have VTScada screens for monitoring only:  Junction Chamber #1  Screen and Grit Buildings  Primary Sedimentation Tanks  Digestion  STAR The following City preferences have been identified for implementing the HMI software conversion:  Migrate all monitoring and control capabilities to VTScada. All new HMI software applications and screens will be based on using VTScada.  Screens developed in VTScada are to be similar to existing screens, matching the layout of the P&IDs.  An Overview of each facility is to be provided that is similar to existing overview displays.  Control and monitoring of particular process areas within the facility shall be accessible by clicking on that area on the overview display to bring up the “P&ID” type display for the area.  Individual equipment control shall be accessible by clicking on that equipment to bring up dashboard style pop up control screens similar to existing.  During development of displays, City staff will be provided examples of high-performance graphic displays for their review to decide if such displays offer a better grasp of overview for each process area. At this time, the plant processes, collection system pump stations, and stormwater stations have separate VTScada realms within one application on the same server. The City is considering implementing separate VTScada applications for each of these areas. HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvement projects will include some high-performance screens following ISA101 Design Standard. Workshops for operators will be held as part of each project. Control descriptions will be developed prior to those workshops. High performance displays will be discussed at these workshops to decide design of such displays and if they are to be provided. The ISA 101 HMI standard serves as a guideline to help organizations design, build and operate effective HMI applications. The City’s Standard Object Library incorporates recommendations from ISA 101. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 4.1 PLC Comparison Factors: 4.1.1 Functionality w/ Ethernet An important function to consider for PLC evaluation is to review the impact on functionality of the PLC when more process equipment is added to the network to communicate with the PLC via any communication protocol. Evaluation is performed with Ethernet(widely used) considered as communication protocol between the various equipment such as VFDs, MCC, and vendor supplied control panels and if the functionality of the PLC would be stable. 4.1.2 Market Share This shows the size of a company, a useful metric in illustrating a company’s dominance and competitiveness in a given field. This factor is a key indicator of a company’s competitiveness, it can also be an indicator of growth, success, and stability. It is considered for the evaluation as key indicator in view of reliability of the PLC and dependability. 4.1.3 PLC Features This is a critical factor when looking at PLC selection criteria and its suitability for the intended application. Adequate number and type of I/O, High-speed processor, large memory capacity, multiple communication options, redundancy and safety features are key factors used for selection. 4.1.4 Support Technical support and service from the manufacturer or vendor is an important evaluation criterion. Reliable customer support, training resources, and a warranty or maintenance program along with local distribution network is key to ensure product availability and quick response in keeping systems in service. 4.1.5 Programmers Availability Local assistance and access to knowledge and resources are invaluable in ensuring PLC system's smooth operation, maintenance and troubleshooting. In any new construction effort, having multiple programmers employed by local integrators will help keep new construction on schedule and affordable. 4.1.6 Local Availability PLC components that are readily available, including spare parts in the event of equipment failure to minimize downtime and ensure that the control system is rapidly operational again, supply chain and lead times are key factors in selecting a PLC lineup. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System PLC Hardware Evaluation 10 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 3.1.12 HMI Integration The software should be easy to integrate into The City’s HMI - VTScada. This integration is crucial for a wastewater operator because VTScada is a widely used HMI system that provides realtime monitoring and control of plant operations. By ensuring compatibility with VTScada, the software allows operators to seamlessly access and manage all relevant data through a single interface, enhancing operational efficiency and reducing the risk of errors. This not only supports proactive maintenance and troubleshooting but also helps in ensuring regulatory compliance and optimizing plant performance. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. 3.1.13 Software support and maintenance The software should have support security updates and technical support. This is important for a wastewater operator because security is paramount in protecting sensitive data and ensuring the integrity of the system. Regular security updates help safeguard the system against potential cyber threats, vulnerabilities, and unauthorized access, which can compromise the operation of the plant. By having these updates included without extra charges, operators can maintain a high level of security consistently, ensuring continuous protection without worrying about additional expenses. This proactive approach not only secures the plant's operations but also aligns with regulatory standards and best practices for infrastructure management. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. 3.1.14 Redundancy The software should be able to be set up with a redundant system to allow for the use of the report creator even if the main server is taken offline. This is crucial for a wastewater operator because redundancy ensures continuous access to critical reporting tools, minimizing downtime and maintaining smooth operations. In the event of a system failure or maintenance, having a redundant setup means that data analysis can proceed without interruption, thus supporting timely decision-making and regulatory compliance. This resilience is essential in safeguarding the plant's functionality and ensuring that operators can respond promptly to any issues that arise. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. 3.1.15 Company prominence The software company should be a reputable company with good industry presence and expertise. This is important for a wastewater operator because a reputable company ensures that the software is reliable, secure, and compliant with industry standards. A company with a strong industry presence is more likely to be aware of the latest advancements and regulatory changes, ensuring that the software remains up-todate and effective in managing the complex requirements of wastewater treatment. Expertise in the field means that the company can provide high-quality support and tailored solutions, addressing specific operational challenges and improving overall plant performance. Due to the non-critical importance of this requirement, it is given an importance factor of 3. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 14 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] **2.2.2 Field Investigation** [ TABLE ] In order to verify the exact routing of the fiber network, site investigations were performed. The following observations were made during these investigations: - PLC 19 in the Main Pump Station is connected to the facility network rack directly over a CAT6 cable, fiber is not used. - There are eleven main trunk fiber cables landed at a fiber patch panel for process connections located at the Main Pump Station that then are connected to the facility network rack. - There are additional fiber cables landed at a separate fiber patch panel in the network rack room, however these fiber lines were installed by the City for various staff workstations and not part of the facility process fiber network. - The Red Zone was found to consist of the following fiber runs: - o One fiber cable that connects the network to PLC 11, PLC 12, PLC 13, and PLC10 as follows: - Patch panel near PLC 12 has a trunk cable back to the network rack patch panel - PLC 12 connects by fiber to the patch panel near PLC 12 - PLC 11 connects by fiber to the patch panel near PLC 12 - PLC 13 connects by fiber to a patch panel near PLC 13 which then connects to the patch panel near PLC 12 - PLC 10 connects by fiber to the patch panel near PLC 13 - o One fiber cable that connects the network to PLC 5 and PLC 39 as follows: - PLC 39 connects by fiber to a patch panel at PLC 5 - PLC 5 connects by fiber to the patch panel in PLC 5 panel HFC AWTP Master Plan **June 10,2022** Improvements: SCADA Design Criteria ![](_page_22_Picture_0.jpeg) ![](_page_22_Picture_1.jpeg) - New PLCs can connect directly to plant fiber network, they do not have to connect through a remote telemetry unit. - Uninterruptible power supplies shall be an APC UPS without ethernet connections and with a bypass switch for easy change out. Relays shall be used on UPS outputs to provide alarm when panel is on UPS power. - Panels shall be provided with twenty-precent(20%) spare capacity for I/O with ten-precent(10%) installed spare I/O. **Table 2-4: Standard Control Panel Components** (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

P&ID Instrument tags - Each building as a building number but some are also designated with a letter code. Building letter designations are listed along with the building numbers on the last two pages of these notes (some additional letter designations will be needed for buildings/areas that aren’t already designated). Authentication shall not be based on trusts or any other link with the business network. 2) A backup domain controller shall be maintained in a separate building from the primary domain controller. This includes operating system permissions, file access, user accounts, service accounts, application-to-application communications, and component system services. Access permissions shall be centrally managed and limited to only the functionality needed to perform job functions associated with the account. 5) Default host accounts (e.g., guest, administrator, etc.) shall be removed. Coordinate with the PCS/SCADA system software vendors. 6) Unique user IDs shall be used for authentication. The use of shared accounts is prohibited. 7) The PCS/SCADA system shall require identification and authentication before allowing any actions by a user. 8) Wherever it is possible to do so, PCS/SCADA system equipment (e.g., servers, workstations, laptops, etc.) shall, as part of the boot process, display to users a system use notification message or banner before granting access to the system that provides privacy and security notices which states that: a. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software DreamReport has the advantage of being NIST certified for cybersecurity. Wastewater treatment is a critical part of everyday life that the environment and many people’s lives depend on. Being certified for adequate cybersecurity standards ensures adequate safety and security is being followed. DreamReport also can meet 21 CFR part 11 compliance requirements, this makes the reports and findings trustworthy, reliable, and equivalent to paper records and handwritten signatures. DreamReport is extensively used in the life sciences industry due to this ability. Additionally, DreamReport can be integrated into HMI and PLCs, readily and easily with the large number of adapters that are available on the software platform. This can make it easy to integrate with the Plant’s current system and any potential changes that may happen in the long term. Having this flexibility is a significant advantage Disadvantages DreamReport has some disadvantages related to the size of Ocean Data Systems, the company that created the software platform. Although it is not the smallest company out of the set of companies that were reviewed it is a non-domestic company. Although this may not be a significant issue for the usability of the software it should be considered in the case that there are any restrictions on whether or not the City is allowed to operate software from non-domestic companies. DreamReport’s licensing structure for concurrent users is similar to that of the tag numbers but with a small amount of added flexibility with the licensing sizes. DreamReport has licenses that can be combined for the number of concurrent users. Meaning that if the plant requires for example 15 users a 10 user license and a 5 user license can be combined to meet the number of required licenses. This makes increasing the number of users incur an additional recurring cost that should be taken into account. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 20 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 7 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Standard Logic Descriptions 7.1Initialization and Non-Volatile Memory The controller maintains its program, data, and configuration through non-volatile memory(NVM). This is memory that can exist in several forms, battery backed up RAM, EEPROM, or compact flash device(SD card). This technology allows the processor to return to its previous state after being powered up. The controller must provide some type of non-volatile memory. At no time is it acceptable to rely on continuous power. The NVM must be capable of withstanding the same environmental conditions as the controller. The particular form-factor of the NVM is dependent on the manufacturer. The preferred types of NVM are those that do not rely on batteries, or other mechanical devices(such as hard disk drives). This memory shall also not be read-only but support re-writing using the native development tools. The process of loading the program after power loss must be inherent in the controller natively. At a minimum the non-volatile memory must return the control program to active RAM. The ability of returning retained variable data to its previous state is a function that is desirable. However, when the ability to return retained data values is not supported, the control code itself must account for this and suitable initialization and start-up logic. 7.2Peer-to-peer Communications Within the plant all messaging shall be performed over Ethernet. If Ethernet is not available to the device, alternatives may be employed with City approval. For remote locations the preferred platform is Cellular modems however, if cellular modems are not appropriate, with City approval, acceptable alternative platforms are serial radio modems, and satellite for extremely remote locations. In all cases, alternative peer-to-peer communications are to be submitted to the City for review and approval prior to implementation. All inter-processor communications will be performed utilizing native communication control elements. An example of this is to utilize the “MSG” instruction in a ControlLogix controller. All communications will be performed in a read-only manner. At no time is a data write, or push, allowed. This leaves the receiving end in a non-deterministic condition in which the receiving controller is unsure of all of the factors affecting communications. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 41 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 3 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 New software requirements must be submitted to the City with suitable lead time, a minimum of four weeks, for a security evaluation and loading. It is the developer’s responsibility to ensure all of the required tools are in place prior to beginning a start-up. General Programming Practices 3.1Languages The IEC 61131-3 standard includes for five languages: Ladder Logic(LD), Function Block Diagram(FBD), Instruction List, and Sequential Function Flowchart. The only allowable languages for all City projects and implementations are LD, and FBD. 3.1.1 Ladder Logic LD is a graphical, symbol-based language with a foundation in circuit diagrams and relay logic hardware. The logic is organized in a series of rungs composed of Boolean elements whose logical result is then applied to outputs or action elements. This can simplistically be considered to be a series of IF/THEN statements. The ladder rungs are scanned in a top-down, left to right manner enforcing strict order of execution. Figure 1 Ladder Logic HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 10 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Figure 3 Object Attribute Comments 3.4.2 Ladder Logic Comments Each LD rung must include a comment clearly describing its purpose and function. The first rung of each routine or section of rungs should summarize the overall function of the routine/section as well as the logic found on the rung. All rungs regardless of how simple or complex must include comments sufficient for someone who did not write the program to understand the function(s) of the rung. This includes all sub-rungs and subroutines. See example in Figure 4 Ladder rung comments. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 13 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Figure 4 Ladder Rung Comments 3.4.3 Function Block Diagram Comments FBD programming increases the readability of complex operations such as those with multiple paths based on Boolean functions(AND/OR/NOT) or selection blocks as well as intermediate calculations(sums and limiting functions). It is required that the programmer provide comments describing the overall function for each connected group of function blocks. Additionally, the programmer shall provide comments for each distinct intermediate calculation (Boolean function, selection, sum, limit, etc.). See example in Figure 5 Function block comments. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 14 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Figure 5 Function Block Comments 3.4.4 Subroutines Documentation Each subroutine of any type will be documented. A single “No Output” [NOP] instruction or unique coil designated as “Comment Coil” on the first rung of routine (see example in Figure 6 First rung NOP rung comments) shall be utilized to provide a location for required subroutine documentation. The following information shall be included in the rung description: - Original programming date - Initials and company of author - Descriptive summary of the routine functions HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 15 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

The drawing shall include hosts grouped according to the zone in which they reside and the default gateway for each zone. The drawing shall include WAN links and supporting hardware. Include location, host name, description, vendor, model number, MAC address, IP address, subnet mask, and interface speed. The drawing shall include all components and subcomponents with an IP address. 7) IP Addressing Scheme - Maintain a spreadsheet that documents the IP addresses and related TCP/IP settings (e.g., default gateway, DNS servers, etc.) for all nodes with an IP address. Group the listing by subnet/VLAN. Maintain the addressing scheme for consistency with the City’s existing overall scheme to prevent conflicts with IP addresses used elsewhere in the City’s networks. 8) Ports/Applications/Services - Maintain a listing of services and applications required for each computer system in the PCS/SCADA system. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Glossary Ethernet: A computer networking standard that defines a family of protocols, communication media, and device interfaces. Human Machine Interface(HMI): A computer that runs graphical interface software for monitoring and supervisory control of a control system. Local Area Network(LAN): Made up of the connection media, networking components, and nearby devices that communicate with each other. Remote Telemetry Unit: A controller for local equipment that communicates with the master telemetry unit. Uninterruptible Power Supply(UPS): A battery-backed source fed by the utility service during normal operation. Variable Frequency Drive(VFD): A motor control that allows for continuous variations in running speed. Virtual Private Network(VPN): A secure means of connecting a physically remote host to a local area network, typically via the Internet. VTScada: The software application used at the HFC AWTP to read and write real-time data to and from controllers. Win 411: A component of Win 911 that enables simple audio reporting of analog values. Win 911: A software application used to annunciate and acknowledge alarms to/from remote users. All RDP connections shall use strong authentication against an authentication, authorization, accounting(AAA) server and strong encryption. 19)Session controls shall monitor and automatically logout after 10 minutes of inactivity. 20)All software components that are not required for the operation and/or maintenance of the PCS/SCADA system shall be removed. If software that is not required cannot be removed or disabled, document a specific explanation. 21)Disable all network ports, protocols, and services not required by the PCS/SCADA system. 22)The PCS/SCADA system networks shall be protected through network segmentation as follows: a. The PCS/SCADA system networks shall be isolated from external connections including the internet. This includes remote SCADA site communications. b. The business network and PCS/SCADA system networks shall be separate and direct communications between the two networks shall be denied. All traffic shall terminate in the PIN described below. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 8 SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 Cybersecurity Policy This section contains cybersecurity requirements for all network components recommended by Hazen at a minimum, however the City shall employ the best practices to promote SCADA & PCS security. This is in addition to specific configuration requirements for integration into PCS/SCADA system networks covered in other sections. All persons are required to review and acknowledge the City’s policies before accessing the PCS/SCADA system. 8.1General 1) All servers, workstations laptops and other devices shall be managed by City policy. 2) The use of unencrypted communication(e.g. email, etc.) for PCS/SCADA system related correspondence which includes information related to cybersecurity and networking is prohibited. All electronically transmitted documents shall be password protected; the password shall be shared with the intended participants via alternate out of band communications channels(e.g. voice, text, etc.). 8.2Asset management 1) Work Management - PCS/SCADA system assets shall be entered into and maintained using the City’s asset management system. Additions, changes, and deletions shall be performed in the system within one week. 2) Device Inventory - Maintain an inventory of all physical devices throughout the PCS/SCADA system networks. Include all network nodes and communication- capable devices (e.g., servers, workstations, laptops, OIT, PLCs, PACs, switches, routers, firewalls, etc.). This inventory shall include all devices, whether connected to the PCS/SCADA system networks or not. Maintain the inventory in a spreadsheet. Include the supplier, manufacturer, model number, serial number, subnet, network address, hardware address, machine name, and installation location. 3) Software Inventory - Maintain an inventory of all software platforms and applications (e.g., operating systems, HMI applications, historians, OPC servers, programming software, IDE software, drivers, office productivity software, utilities, etc.). Maintain the inventory in a spreadsheet. Include supplier, product vendor, product name, product version, patching level, firmware version, licensing methods, and licensing keys. Maintain additional information as required to allow reinstallation of software after final acceptance. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 7.3DNP3 Exception Configuration The communication protocol between remote controllers and the supervisory(SCADA) system will be based on the DNP3 protocol as specified by DNP.ORG. This protocol shall be only used on remote sites. The transport layer for communications is to be appropriate for the application, e.g., LAN, serial modems, etc. No proprietary protocols are to be implemented. The master will be configured to perform its polling utilizing events(exceptions). Only periodically will static values be requested to insure validity and quality. The definition of what constitutes an event are dependent on the nature of the data point and acceptable excursion limits. All events will include a timestamp to facilitate sequence of event generation. The data is to be formatted and marshalled to facilitate optimal polling. Data being read from LAN based sources will by nature support a larger number of points being read than those from serial based devices. As such the data being sent must have the event thresholds and organization considered prior to implementation. Due to the potential communications loss events that can occur to some remote stations the concept of store & forward will be fully implemented. Therefore, all controllers must support this method of operation. This functionality may be supplied either natively or by the use of in-chassis interface modules. Each device is to be configured to maintain the data as long as possible, this is heavily dependent on the device design. Standard polling of remote locations by the SCADA system is to be configured such that the entire store & forward buffer may be read. This is much more critical when communicating to devices utilizing serial or cellular modems where outstations are typically read in a round-robin approach. In these cases, acquiring the data and storing the data into the historian in chronological order is critical. Stations that have a higher probability of continuous connection may be configured for unsolicited messaging. This feature is to be used sparingly and only for critical events. This feature will not be supportable in systems employing round-robin polling. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 43 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 8.6Information Protection Processes and Procedures Maintain a patch management system to ensure that PCS/SCADA system production operating systems and third-party software are running the most recent security updates provided by the software vendors. Maintain a patch management server in the PIN. Work with the City’s information technology department to maintain a secure method of automatically updating the patch management server in the industrial DMZ using the City’s business network and internet connection. Maintain an anti-virus definition update system to ensure that PCS/SCADA system production operating systems are using the latest anti-virus definitions. Maintain an antivirus update server in the PIN. Work with the City’s information technology department to implement a secure method of automatically updating the anti-virus server in the industrial DMZ using the City’s business network and internet connection. 8.7Maintenance Maintain three laptops dedicated to onsite PCS/SCADA system development. Ensure the laptops are secured in accordance with relevant host requirements elsewhere in policies, standards, guidelines, and procedures. The use of any other laptops on the SCADA and PCS is prohibited. Configure the laptops with fixed IP addresses, subnet masks, default gateways, and DNS servers to eliminate the need for DHCP. On a monthly basis, run vulnerability scans on servers, workstation, thin clients, and laptops. The scans shall be performed without an internet connection. Ensure the approach compares the devices to the newest available patches. Install important and critical patches on the systems in accordance with all PCS/SCADA system software vendor recommendations. In a test environment, verify patches will not have an undesirable effect on the PCS/SCADA system production environment prior to deployment. Use operating system restore points to provide the ability to restore systems to pre-patch levels. Subscribe to vulnerability alert systems including ICS-CERT, US-CERT, and WaterISAC. Monitor correspondence from these sources for alerts related to hardware and software provided under this project. Patch affected hardware/software as soon as possible and in accordance with other parts of policies, standards, guidelines, and procedures. 8.8Protective Technology 1) The use of open source software is prohibited. 2) Uninstall all browser and email client software on hosts. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation Communication protocols listed here have been updated based on the review comments from the City and intentions to use Rockwell PLC. The protocols are in the order of priority and also based on the availability of from the equipment supplier. These communication protocols shall be implemented on all future projects within this facility. a. General - Ethernet/ IP, PROFINET, MODBUS TCP/ IP, PROFIBUS DP/PA (depending on number of devices on the network), Modbus RTU, HART, Traditional I/O(DI, DO, AI & AO). b. Instruments - Ethernet/ IP, PROFINET, Modbus TCP/IP, ProfiBUS DP/PA, Hart, Traditional I/O. c. VFDs - Traditional I/O for Critical signals and control and for status signals use Ethernet/ IP, PROFINET, Modbus TCP/IP d. Power Monitoring - Ethernet/ IP, PROFINET, Modbus TCP/IP e. Digital Communication protocols - Ethernet/ IP, PROFINET, Modbus TCP/IP 4.4 PLC Manufacturer Comparison The following section compares the relative advantages and disadvantages of each manufacturer considered in this evaluation. 4.4.1 Allen-Bradley Advantages Allen-Bradley is one of the top two manufacturers of PLC equipment in the Southeast market. Their products are widely used in water and wastewater facilities and are typically familiar to most maintenance personnel. The PLCs have the capacity to operate in ladder logic, structured text, and functional block diagram. Their software also abides by IEC 61131-3 standards, the standard most commonly used when looking at PLC software. Their products were ranked second in reader satisfaction of the four manufacturers considered(as listed in paragraph 5.1) Disadvantages While A-B equipment is well known for its powerful processing systems, lack of coordination between hardware, software, firmware and proper programming can sometimes result in a “buggy” feel. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 4.1 PLC Comparison Factors: 4.1.1 Functionality w/ Ethernet An important function to consider for PLC evaluation is to review the impact on functionality of the PLC when more process equipment is added to the network to communicate with the PLC via any communication protocol. Evaluation is performed with Ethernet(widely used) considered as communication protocol between the various equipment such as VFDs, MCC, and vendor supplied control panels and if the functionality of the PLC would be stable. 4.1.2 Market Share This shows the size of a company, a useful metric in illustrating a company’s dominance and competitiveness in a given field. This factor is a key indicator of a company’s competitiveness, it can also be an indicator of growth, success, and stability. It is considered for the evaluation as key indicator in view of reliability of the PLC and dependability. 4.1.3 PLC Features This is a critical factor when looking at PLC selection criteria and its suitability for the intended application. Adequate number and type of I/O, High-speed processor, large memory capacity, multiple communication options, redundancy and safety features are key factors used for selection. 4.1.4 Support Technical support and service from the manufacturer or vendor is an important evaluation criterion. Reliable customer support, training resources, and a warranty or maintenance program along with local distribution network is key to ensure product availability and quick response in keeping systems in service. 4.1.5 Programmers Availability Local assistance and access to knowledge and resources are invaluable in ensuring PLC system's smooth operation, maintenance and troubleshooting. In any new construction effort, having multiple programmers employed by local integrators will help keep new construction on schedule and affordable. 4.1.6 Local Availability PLC components that are readily available, including spare parts in the event of equipment failure to minimize downtime and ensure that the control system is rapidly operational again, supply chain and lead times are key factors in selecting a PLC lineup. - [Electrical / Power / Control Panels] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Glossary Ethernet: A computer networking standard that defines a family of protocols, communication media, and device interfaces. Human Machine Interface(HMI): A computer that runs graphical interface software for monitoring and supervisory control of a control system. Local Area Network(LAN): Made up of the connection media, networking components, and nearby devices that communicate with each other. Remote Telemetry Unit: A controller for local equipment that communicates with the master telemetry unit. Uninterruptible Power Supply(UPS): A battery-backed source fed by the utility service during normal operation. Variable Frequency Drive(VFD): A motor control that allows for continuous variations in running speed. Virtual Private Network(VPN): A secure means of connecting a physically remote host to a local area network, typically via the Internet. VTScada: The software application used at the HFC AWTP to read and write real-time data to and from controllers. Win 411: A component of Win 911 that enables simple audio reporting of analog values. Win 911: A software application used to annunciate and acknowledge alarms to/from remote users. All such controls to be NEMA4X industrial grade and Square D 9001SK series.  Provide spare parts for CPUs, I/O Cards, Network Switches, fuses, and other components that are critical for operation or prone to wearing out.  Instrument specifications will be in data sheet format for specific make and model numbers rather than lengthy descriptions intended for “or equal” interpretation.  Electrical gear shall be provide with the manufacturer’s standard power monitoring devices.  Witnessed factory testing will not be required. Specifications will require onsite checkout with City staff followed by a 48-hour demonstration test.  Project specifications will require training for both operations and maintenance staff. Operation and maintenance manuals shall be required to be on hand for associated training. 2.5.2 Control Panels Control Panels shall be specified to be provided with the components outlined in Table 2-4, for panel components not listed in this table the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvements Dewatering Facility Replacement project will be used as a standard. Improvements shall include the following requirements:  Dual OITs will be provided on all panels with PLCs, this includes main process panels and individual equipment panels. Both OITs shall have full redundancy, generally one will be used for setpoints and the other for monitoring.  Within panels, CAT6 cable shall be shielded industrial cable and rated for 600V if exposed to 480V power.  Wiring within panels shall separate for different voltages and purposes. - [Electrical / Power / Control Panels] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 4.1 PLC Comparison Factors: 4.1.1 Functionality w/ Ethernet An important function to consider for PLC evaluation is to review the impact on functionality of the PLC when more process equipment is added to the network to communicate with the PLC via any communication protocol. Evaluation is performed with Ethernet(widely used) considered as communication protocol between the various equipment such as VFDs, MCC, and vendor supplied control panels and if the functionality of the PLC would be stable. 4.1.2 Market Share This shows the size of a company, a useful metric in illustrating a company’s dominance and competitiveness in a given field. This factor is a key indicator of a company’s competitiveness, it can also be an indicator of growth, success, and stability. It is considered for the evaluation as key indicator in view of reliability of the PLC and dependability. 4.1.3 PLC Features This is a critical factor when looking at PLC selection criteria and its suitability for the intended application. Adequate number and type of I/O, High-speed processor, large memory capacity, multiple communication options, redundancy and safety features are key factors used for selection. 4.1.4 Support Technical support and service from the manufacturer or vendor is an important evaluation criterion. Reliable customer support, training resources, and a warranty or maintenance program along with local distribution network is key to ensure product availability and quick response in keeping systems in service. 4.1.5 Programmers Availability Local assistance and access to knowledge and resources are invaluable in ensuring PLC system's smooth operation, maintenance and troubleshooting. In any new construction effort, having multiple programmers employed by local integrators will help keep new construction on schedule and affordable. 4.1.6 Local Availability PLC components that are readily available, including spare parts in the event of equipment failure to minimize downtime and ensure that the control system is rapidly operational again, supply chain and lead times are key factors in selecting a PLC lineup. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System PLC Hardware Evaluation 10 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Electrical / Power / Control Panels] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 4.1.13 Maintenance PLC requires routine maintenance and upkeeping, software upgrades and version controls are necessary to mitigate PLC downtime. PLCs with tools to manage software version, notification feature and license upgrades are ranked based on the available options. 4.1.14 Disaster Recovery PLCs hard drives occasionally are inoperable for a variety of reasons, such as power quality event impacting the central processing unit(CPU). A PLC with ability to hold program within a USB or SD card or a monitoring tool to provide PLC health condition is an important factor used for evaluation. 4.1.15 PLC change management PLCs program modifications are necessary when operational changes occur. The ability of the PLC to monitor and store the user actions is helpful in change management. PLC evaluation is based on the best tools available within the PLC to monitor user actions. 4.1.16 SCADA Software version control A PLC monitoring the HMI and ease of auditing for program management is a useful feature. PLCs with a cyber security plan are ranked top in this category. 4.1.17 PLC Size Choosing a PLC supplier who offers a diverse choice of PLCs will benefit as each process within the HFC AWTP requires various number of monitoring and control I/O. Availability of wide range of size selection within each PLC supplier is considered for evaluation. 4.1.18 PLC Security and Recovery HFC AWTP safety is a critical consideration. PLC suppliers that offer integrated safety features or compatibility with safety modules are recommended. These features include safety inputs, relay outputs, and the ability to implement safety functions according to relevant safety standards. Compliance with safety requirements adds a layer of protection to the HFC AWTP and is an important factor for evaluation. 4.1.19 Ease of Integration with equipment PLCs with predeveloped custom function blocks between instruments and PLC CPUs, VFDs and MCCs are crucial for ease of integration. Rankings are based on ease of integration with major equipment used at HFC AWTP. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. - [Testing / Startup / Commissioning] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 6 SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 Management of Change 6.1Overview The City’s Management of Change Policy is intended to establish requirements for SCADA & PCS change management policies and procedures. The MOC policy and procedure addresses requirements for change management including review/approval process, documentation and usage of the SCADA & PCS Change Management Server. This policy applies to all modifications of City SCADA & PCS assets including but not limited to HMIs, OITs, historians, network equipment, PLC, and radios. 6.2Responsibilities To ensure the MOC policy is implemented and maintained in accordance with the procedures listed in this policy, the identified City Employees and contractors have the responsibilities identified in Section 2. 6.3Management of Change Approval Requirements All modifications to the control system shall be reviewed and approved prior to deployment on the live production system. A change request is also referred to as an MOC request throughout this document. A change request shall be submitted to the City for approval utilizing the SCADA & PCS Change Request Form provided in Appendix B. Change requests shall include the following: 1) Description of change 2) Location of change (e.g., PLC name, subroutine and rungs, HMI screen name, etc.) 3) Name, company name, and contact phone number for person implementing change 4) Copy of completed test plan from the City’s Test Environment 5) Copy of proposed field test verification plan 6) Back-out plan including details on how to revert to a previous known good installation. No change shall be implemented without written approval from the City. The City’s OT contact is responsible for assigning appropriate approval authority and coordinating with City operations.
