However, it is recognized there may be specific instances in which a contractor or supplier may not be able to fully comply with the standards for software, equipment, cabling and other control system components supplied or developed under a contract or other procurement method or may not be able to program or configure the system in conformance with these standards. When these cases arise, the contractor or supplier shall submit a request for deviation from the City’s standards using the Standards Deviation Request Form included in Appendix B. The City will review the issues documented on the form and any supplemental documentation that is supplied with the request, and if compelling reasons are presented, the City may grant the deviation. The standards deviation request shall meet the following criteria: 1) It shall clearly indicate those sections of the standards relevant to the proposed deviation(s). 2) It shall verify that compliance with the standards form which the deviation(s) is requested is, under the circumstances, not feasible. 3) It shall include a specific description of the proposed alternative to the requirements in the City’s standards and include supporting documentation. Documentation may include references demonstrating successful use by other public utilities. 3.2Procedure The process to request a deviation from the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions is as follows: 1) Fill out and submit the Standards Deviation Request Form. The form shall be filled out electronically and submitted via e-mail to the City’s designated project manager or other designated contact. The city will supply the blank electronic form on request. A copy of the form is included in Appendix B. 2) Transmit supporting documentation securely through City approved transmittal methods if file contents are sensitive such as VTS changesets, PLC programs, etc. Drawings, mark-ups, photographs, sketches, or vendor-supplied product information with the deviation request e-mail. In addition to this the large presence of the company and large number of clients across the world it demonstrates that their software platforms and tools are reliable and quality tools. It also gives the company a greater level of resources to improve and refine their products and provide the best quality for their clients. A responsive and intuitive interface that is quickly able to be learned can aid in the reduction of errors and man hours spent in training and acquiring staff proficiency on the platform can reduce the long term operating costs of a plant. Disadvantages One of the most prominent disadvantages of the PIVision software is the cost, the high cost of the software makes this the most expensive option of all the software platforms that are available by a significant number. Another disadvantage was the lackluster customer service, the difficulty in getting connected to a sales representative and the lack of knowledge about the software platform might a reflection of how the company might operate. If their sales representatives are difficult to contact and are not very knowledgeable about their product it could be an indication of a company culture of substandard care of their customers and products which could make operating the software difficult in the long term which could have unforeseen expenses in the number of man hours, it might take to remedy an issue with the software or integration of features. All setpoints will be supplied in engineering units. 5.1.2.2 Toggling Alarms Alarms cannot be individually enabled/disabled. Equipment must be disabled in order to disable the alarms associated to that piece of equipment. Otherwise, all the equipment’s associated alarms will remain enabled. 5.1.2.3 Debounce Times Debounce times are the period(in mSec) for which the signal must be in excess of the indicated setpoint limit before the alarm is generated. Setting a value to zero eliminates the debounce function completely. Alarms are currently all set to have debounce times at zero 5.1.2.4 Dead-Band Amount The dead-band amount is used to eliminate the chattering of alarms around the setpoint by requiring the process variable to retreat from the setpoint by a set amount before the alarm resets. A dead-band setting is applied to all limit alarms monitoring a process value. The configuration parameter is applicable to analog alarms only. The amount is supplied in engineering units with a value of zero deactivating the feature. Alarms are currently all set to have debounce times at zero. 5.1.2.5 Shelving or Bypassing The ability to bypass, or shelve, an alarm for a specified period will not be provided. Alarms cannot be bypassed or shelved; alarms can only be disabled. Disabling alarms is done through disabling the associated equipment. 5.1.3 Alarm conditions All alarm conditions are expected to apply positive logic. When the alarm value is a Boolean 1/true/on then the alarm is to be considered present/active. When the alarm value is a Boolean 0/false/off then the alarm is considered to be not present/inactive. 5.2Definition and Rationalization 5.2.1 Analog Alarms The following are available alarm conditions for analog values. These will support the basic typical requirements. Condition Description Low-Low(LL) The current scaled value is below the configured Low-Low setpoint(value < Low-Low SP) Low(L) The current scaled value is below the configured HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 7 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 HMI Graphics General Requirements Graphic screens will be composed of many individual types of data elements, a significant portion of which are alpha-numeric. To maintain consistency across the application, the following requirements are to be adhered to. 7.1Color The use of color throughout the application is limited. The nature of high performance graphics restricts colors to those elements that require attention. In order to provide a consistent color pallet throughout the application colors have been defined for use. Refer to Appendix A, Definitions and General Reference, for a listing of those colors approved for use. The colors have been defined using the standard RGB color system. The color name is as defined by Microsoft along with the color values in red, green, and blue and hex code. Throughout this document, where specific colors are required, these color names will be used. The only times the full color spectrum is allowed is on map based screens and those places where photographs are utilized. 7.2Process Values Displaying live process values will be handled differently than static text. Values depicted from the controller will be in bold, blue. This is in contrast to the units of measure that will be indicated in non- bold, black either near the process value or not at all in the case of bar graphs or other animated elements. Leading zero’s(0) will not be utilized except to indicate a fraction(e.g., 0.12). When dynamic values are being used in a column generally align the values on the decimal point. In mixed cases, floating point values and integers, the values are to be aligned to the implied decimal point. In integer only cases the values are to be right aligned. Where applicable and feasible, the process value will indicate its quality. This will be accomplished by including a small indication adjacent to the value indicating “BAD”. This shall be highlighted with a white background to improve legibility. In the event that the value is currently under simulation, that is a manual value being driven by the operator, the indication “SIM” will be used in place of “BAD”. Source protection of code is prohibited. This includes all code created or used by systems integrators, programmers, vendors and OEM suppliers. As such, all of this information is to be supplied to the City no later than initiation of site acceptance testing. 3.4Documentation It is the programmer’s responsibility to provide an annotated program compliant with the minimum documentation requirements defined in this section for all program files. As a general guideline, the level of documentation provided should allow for a technician with no familiarity of the functions of the specific unit process to read the comments and fully understand the inputs, outputs, and logic implemented in the program. 3.4.1 Object Parameters, UDT and Tag Comments It is required that all tags including object parameters and UDT elements be provided with completed description fields. Object parameters, UDT elements, and tags are subject to tag name and description requirements defined in Section 4.1 of this volume. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Communications to remote controllers will utilize comprehensive fault monitoring of the status. The message execution complete and error results will be monitored and be utilized as data quality status. Additionally, the data being transferred will contain a watchdog register with each message packet. This value will be used to drive a timer which indicates the information being read from the foreign controller is valid. At the time of development, the failure modes for the communicated signals and the local process. Each signal must be evaluated for Fail-Last, Fail-to-Zero, etc. This will be performed in conjunction with the City. All failure mode determination and conditioning is to take place in the communications handler. Data that is being supplied as status inputs to remote controllers will be marshalled in routines following naming conventions outlined above. The data will be passed in arrays of Integers(INT) or double integers(DINT), whichever is the native integer format of the controllers. Structures of data utilizing UDTs are not permitted. Structures do not accommodate last minute changes without requiring both controllers to be redownloaded. Additionally, some devices might not support structured data beyond arrays. The first element in the transmission array will always be the clock time in seconds and milliseconds. This value is the watchdog timing element for the receiving end. A seconds-based number is suitable as communications polling periods should not exceed five seconds(0.2 Hz). Subsequent elements of the array are to contain the data values. It is understood that not all data exists as integers. In the case of Boolean data that may be transferred in a one bit per word or may be packed with multiple bits into one word. In the case of reals(floating point) these are to be multiplied by a suitable factor of 10 to fit within the integer value space. The multiplicative factor needs to consider the significant digits of the value in question. In example, a value of 43.56 would be transmitted as 4356. In all cases with packed array of data documentation is critical. All of the data that is being transmitted must be clearly defined and detailed. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 42 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Hardware Evaluation 4.1 PLC Comparison Factors: 4.1.1 Functionality w/ Ethernet An important function to consider for PLC evaluation is to review the impact on functionality of the PLC when more process equipment is added to the network to communicate with the PLC via any communication protocol. Evaluation is performed with Ethernet(widely used) considered as communication protocol between the various equipment such as VFDs, MCC, and vendor supplied control panels and if the functionality of the PLC would be stable. 4.1.2 Market Share This shows the size of a company, a useful metric in illustrating a company’s dominance and competitiveness in a given field. This factor is a key indicator of a company’s competitiveness, it can also be an indicator of growth, success, and stability. It is considered for the evaluation as key indicator in view of reliability of the PLC and dependability. 4.1.3 PLC Features This is a critical factor when looking at PLC selection criteria and its suitability for the intended application. Adequate number and type of I/O, High-speed processor, large memory capacity, multiple communication options, redundancy and safety features are key factors used for selection. 4.1.4 Support Technical support and service from the manufacturer or vendor is an important evaluation criterion. Reliable customer support, training resources, and a warranty or maintenance program along with local distribution network is key to ensure product availability and quick response in keeping systems in service. 4.1.5 Programmers Availability Local assistance and access to knowledge and resources are invaluable in ensuring PLC system's smooth operation, maintenance and troubleshooting. In any new construction effort, having multiple programmers employed by local integrators will help keep new construction on schedule and affordable. 4.1.6 Local Availability PLC components that are readily available, including spare parts in the event of equipment failure to minimize downtime and ensure that the control system is rapidly operational again, supply chain and lead times are key factors in selecting a PLC lineup. HFC AWTP Master Plan January 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System PLC Hardware Evaluation 10 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] *5.1.2.5 Shelving or Bypassing* The ability to bypass, or shelve, an alarm for a specified period will not be provided. Alarms cannot be bypassed or shelved; alarms can only be disabled. Disabling alarms is done through disabling the associated equipment. (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] 6.3.1 Periodic tasks Periodic tasks are designed to execute on a fixed period of time, typically in milliseconds. These tasks are conducive when working with a process or system that is slow, or requires the processing on a regular basis. Creation of periodic tasks should be kept to a minimum(six or less) as required by the controlled process or area. The periodic task execution rate should be set to allow ample time for program execution without burdening the processor. Programmers shall frequently monitor and adjust the execution rate based on the task time. All programs shall be commissioned with task settings tuned such that no overlaps exist in the program. During commissioning, the main program overlap counter shall be confirmed at zero and FAULTLOG object shall not have any minor faults for overlap. Each periodic task must be set with a priority from 1 to 15, with 1 representing the highest priority. It is recommended that each task be set with a unique priority. (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 8 SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 HMI Graphics Alarming 8.1Overview Alarm configuration requirements are contained in the City’s Alarm Management Strategy (Reference the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual, Volume 2). Any alarm modification, addition or deletion must follow alarm definition and rationalization procedures discussed in the alarm management strategy prior to implementation. It is important to note that although OIT and HMI system functions can be used to monitor and generate alarms, in the City’s PCS process alarms shall be generated in the PLCs which will be logged and displayed on the HMIs and OITs. HMI application shall not be configured to perform alarm calculations. HMI generated alarms will be utilized only for alarms and events initiated from within the HMI software such as software status alarms, loss of communications with a PLC and other system level alarming. Alarm limits must be adjustable through the HMI graphics screens; however, those alarm limits will be used by the PLC in determining if there is an alarm condition. The nature of the distributed PCS is such that the PLC-based process control logic can and will continue to operate, even if one or more HMIs become unavailable. As a result, the PLC must be able to determine alarm conditions and act accordingly without the need for continuous communication with the HMI system. The City’s Standard Object Library and add-on instruction(AOI) standard library objects shall be utilized for configuration of PLC and HMI. 8.2Nuisance Alarm Suppression Techniques All nuisance alarm suppression shall be by approved methods documented in the City’s Alarm Management Strategy (Reference the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual, Volume 2 for more details on alarm suppression techniques). Disabling equipment will disable the alarms associated with the equipment. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 o AC = Reference to the type of card, AC indicates voltage type. o DI = Reference to type of input, DI indicates discrete input. o MOD-04 = Indicates the module number. 4.5Tasks This section describes the various objects available for the organization of program logic within the controller and their associated parameters. The section provides guidelines for selection and organization of logic into these objects and configuration of their properties. Three tasks are used, fast, normal, and slow. The tasks are typically reserved for message communications with other devices. The fast scan task has the highest priority of 1, the normal scan task has a priority of 3, being used for all regular monitoring and control operations. The slow scan task has a priority of 5, being reserved for processor monitoring. Each task shall contain a single program within itself and program name shall follow a similar naming as the task. 4.5.1 Task Structure Tasks are the highest organizational unit in the controller and dictate the order of execution. The nomenclature applied to tasks must represent the broadest definition of the contents. 4.5.2 Program Structure Programs represent the middle tier of the organizational structure. At this layer there is more additional granularity added to the overall structure and the naming conventions must reflect that. Naming at this layer must not overlap with the layer above. For example: - P01_Fast Program - P02_Normal Program - P03_Slow Program Individual names at this tier may be duplicated in other tasks but must be unique per task. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 24 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 4.5.3 Routine Structure This is the lowest tier of programming structure. As such the name must provide clarity into the exact programming construct. Each routine is linked to a process area for fast identification and each process area has 3 subroutines associated, except for IO subroutines that are 4. For Example, - _000_ is associated with IO Mapping. o _000_ANALOG_INPUT_MAPPING o _000_ANALOG_OUTPUT_MAPPING o _000_DIGITAL_IO_MAPPING o _000_DISCRETE_IO_MAPPING - _010_Main_PUMP_STATION_ALM - _010_Main_PUMP_STATION_ASSETS - _010 Main_PUMP_STATION_CONTROLS HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. It is the developer’s responsibility to ensure all of the required tools are in place prior to beginning a start-up. General Programming Practices 3.1Languages The IEC 61131-3 standard includes for five languages: Ladder Logic(LD), Function Block Diagram(FBD), Instruction List, and Sequential Function Flowchart. The only allowable languages for all City projects and implementations are LD, and FBD. 3.1.1 Ladder Logic LD is a graphical, symbol-based language with a foundation in circuit diagrams and relay logic hardware. The logic is organized in a series of rungs composed of Boolean elements whose logical result is then applied to outputs or action elements. This can simplistically be considered to be a series of IF/THEN statements. The ladder rungs are scanned in a top-down, left to right manner enforcing strict order of execution. Figure 1 Ladder Logic HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 10 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

A dead-band setting is applied to all limit alarms monitoring a process value. The configuration parameter is applicable to analog alarms only. The amount is supplied in engineering units with a value of zero deactivating the feature. Alarms are currently all set to have debounce times at zero. (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

Authentication shall not be based on trusts or any other link with the business network. 2) A backup domain controller shall be maintained in a separate building from the primary domain controller. Both domain controllers shall be fed from separate power sources and back by separate UPSs. 3) All network devices shall use IEEE 802.1x for authentication to the network. 4) A role-based access control system shall be used throughout the PCS/SCADA System and shall be configured to operate using the principle of least privilege. This includes operating system permissions, file access, user accounts, service accounts, application-to-application communications, and component system services. Access permissions shall be centrally managed and limited to only the functionality needed to perform job functions associated with the account. 5) Default host accounts (e.g., guest, administrator, etc.) shall be removed. If the accounts are required for normal or emergency operation, they shall be recreated under a different name than the default account. Coordinate with the PCS/SCADA system software vendors. 6) Unique user IDs shall be used for authentication. The use of shared accounts is prohibited. 7) The PCS/SCADA system shall require identification and authentication before allowing any actions by a user. 8) Wherever it is possible to do so, PCS/SCADA system equipment (e.g., servers, workstations, laptops, etc.) shall, as part of the boot process, display to users a system use notification message or banner before granting access to the system that provides privacy and security notices which states that: a. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 7.3DNP3 Exception Configuration The communication protocol between remote controllers and the supervisory(SCADA) system will be based on the DNP3 protocol as specified by DNP.ORG. This protocol shall be only used on remote sites. The transport layer for communications is to be appropriate for the application, e.g., LAN, serial modems, etc. No proprietary protocols are to be implemented. The master will be configured to perform its polling utilizing events(exceptions). Only periodically will static values be requested to insure validity and quality. The definition of what constitutes an event are dependent on the nature of the data point and acceptable excursion limits. All events will include a timestamp to facilitate sequence of event generation. The data is to be formatted and marshalled to facilitate optimal polling. Data being read from LAN based sources will by nature support a larger number of points being read than those from serial based devices. As such the data being sent must have the event thresholds and organization considered prior to implementation. Due to the potential communications loss events that can occur to some remote stations the concept of store & forward will be fully implemented. Therefore, all controllers must support this method of operation. This functionality may be supplied either natively or by the use of in-chassis interface modules. Each device is to be configured to maintain the data as long as possible, this is heavily dependent on the device design. Standard polling of remote locations by the SCADA system is to be configured such that the entire store & forward buffer may be read. This is much more critical when communicating to devices utilizing serial or cellular modems where outstations are typically read in a round-robin approach. In these cases, acquiring the data and storing the data into the historian in chronological order is critical. Stations that have a higher probability of continuous connection may be configured for unsolicited messaging. This feature is to be used sparingly and only for critical events. This feature will not be supportable in systems employing round-robin polling. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 43 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 2 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Programming Software 2.1Development Platform The specific software to be used is Studio 5000 Logix Designer, Version 36 In the case of a new product being introduced to the facility the version will be the most recent available. The software must support all of the capabilities that the hardware offers. The Integrated Development Environment(IDE) shall provide a complete set of tools for platform configuration, application program development, online troubleshooting / debugging, tag management, security, and safety. The PLC software is not to be password protected as requested by the City. Secondary tools, such as firmware update management, are acceptable as stand-alone applications due to their limited use. In all cases, lite, demo, or self-developed versions of development/configuration software are not allowed. All software furnished to the City on a project is to be properly licensed with all licensing transferred to City management no later than initiation of site acceptance testing. It is the responsibility of the developer/systems integrator to provide their own development software and licensing for off-site development. City licensing or systems will not be loaned for offsite use. 2.2Security The City’s facility is critical infrastructure and as such, per the US Department of Homeland Security, requires heightened security. It is expected that software development and preliminary testing of software will take place on vendor computers and equipment. However, implementation and debugging while on site will only be accomplished utilizing City computers and laptops. No vendor equipment will be allowed to be directly connected to the process control network(PCN). The City’s computers and laptops will have the development software suite installed from manufacturers’ media or website. All configuration files and applications will be loaded to the City’s computer equipment after proper virus and malware scanning. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 9 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 Communications are to be configured in VTScada using the appropriate driver and port tag constructs. The driver tag, specifically, must be appropriate to the communications protocol. Separate, stand-alone servers will be dedicated to data acquisition. There will be at least two servers configured for the system in the roles of primary and backup. This will provide redundancy for the purposes of monitoring the information from field devices and controllers. There are expected to be pairs of data acquisition servers for collections/distributions, waste water operations, and water operations. The data acquisition servers(referred to as default servers) will be added to the system configuration default server list as one, uniquely named device. Under this named configuration the server machines that will act as the primary and backup systems will be listed. The primary data acquisition machine being that system listed first and subsequent backups following in order. In this way the system allows for the growth and expansion of data acquisition clusters servicing the data needs for logical areas of operations as a whole. 3.1.4 Configuration Systems Configuration systems are workstations specifically designed for system configuration and contain the currently running configuration with the system property “Automatically Deploy Local Changes” disabled. These systems have been added to the system server lists under the “Configuration” category. All development and testing will utilize these stations at the City’s Operations Center. Upon successful testing and acceptance, the ChangeSet file will be transferred to deployment nodes. HMI clients are the portals through which the process is monitored and controlled by the operator. These stations may be based on workstations, laptops, or mobile devices. As such additional consideration must be given to the functionality of the screens as the platform changes. All workstation based HMI clients will be added to the configuration of the system. This allows for remote administration of the machines as well as the ability to push updates to the systems. Mobile clients, either portable or vehicle mounted, will be based on Windows 10 with cellular communications for data access. As with all systems these stations will be HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 13 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 8.6Information Protection Processes and Procedures Maintain a patch management system to ensure that PCS/SCADA system production operating systems and third-party software are running the most recent security updates provided by the software vendors. Maintain a patch management server in the PIN. Work with the City’s information technology department to maintain a secure method of automatically updating the patch management server in the industrial DMZ using the City’s business network and internet connection. Maintain an anti-virus definition update system to ensure that PCS/SCADA system production operating systems are using the latest anti-virus definitions. Maintain an antivirus update server in the PIN. Work with the City’s information technology department to implement a secure method of automatically updating the anti-virus server in the industrial DMZ using the City’s business network and internet connection. 8.7Maintenance Maintain three laptops dedicated to onsite PCS/SCADA system development. Ensure the laptops are secured in accordance with relevant host requirements elsewhere in policies, standards, guidelines, and procedures. The use of any other laptops on the SCADA and PCS is prohibited. Configure the laptops with fixed IP addresses, subnet masks, default gateways, and DNS servers to eliminate the need for DHCP. On a monthly basis, run vulnerability scans on servers, workstation, thin clients, and laptops. The scans shall be performed without an internet connection. Ensure the approach compares the devices to the newest available patches. Install important and critical patches on the systems in accordance with all PCS/SCADA system software vendor recommendations. In a test environment, verify patches will not have an undesirable effect on the PCS/SCADA system production environment prior to deployment. Use operating system restore points to provide the ability to restore systems to pre-patch levels. Subscribe to vulnerability alert systems including ICS-CERT, US-CERT, and WaterISAC. Monitor correspondence from these sources for alerts related to hardware and software provided under this project. Patch affected hardware/software as soon as possible and in accordance with other parts of policies, standards, guidelines, and procedures. 8.8Protective Technology 1) The use of open source software is prohibited. 2) Uninstall all browser and email client software on hosts. - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Module configurations are to be kept as close to manufacturer default(out of the box) as possible. This is to facilitate the replacement of modules on failure at some point in the future. In all cases ‘Hot Swappable’ modules are preferred. Modules that support advanced features, such as the HART protocol, should adhere to the spirit of the specification as much as possible. The ability to employ these additional features, such as multiple data points on a signal channel, offer cost savings and convenience. However, beyond returning the value and any indication of the quality of that data, extended features should not be utilized. Diagnostic points such as broken wire detect, under range, and over range are to be enabled, if selectable, and the signals utilized as faults to the analog input blocks. Module fault information is to be aggregated into a common signal and utilized as fault inputs to the analog blocks. In the case of inputs and outputs, either fault status information should be supplied to the primary controlling element. This allows the blockware to be cognizant that the element(s) that are being controlled will be non-responsive. This is applicable to equipment position control/feedback and speed control/feedback. 6.8.2 Digital IO Each point is assigned a variable. Typically, there are few configuration elements; however, in the case that such configuration elements are available, those elements are to be used sparingly or not at all. All signal diagnostic capabilities are to be employed. Filters on the input signals are not to be utilized and in all cases turned off. In the case of outputs, fault status information should be supplied to the primary controlling element. This allows the blockware to be cognizant that the element(s) that are being controlled will be non-responsive. 6.8.3 Message IO All peer-to-peer tag values passed through message arrays shall be read through the fast scan. Message routines shall be named by applying the prefix “MSG_” to the paired controller’s name, followed by “_IO”. An example would be “MSG_ H-010-PLC000-800­ 01_IO” (Howard F. Curren AWTP, MPS Electrical room, PLC number 1). Messaged data will be handled in a similar manner as module supplied data, through analog blocks. In the case of messaged data, the success or failure of communications will be used as the data quality. As the receiving controller is not the owner of the data HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - PLC Volume 2 of 3 37 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

The first rung of each routine or section of rungs should summarize the overall function of the routine/section as well as the logic found on the rung. All rungs regardless of how simple or complex must include comments sufficient for someone who did not write the program to understand the function(s) of the rung. It is required that the programmer provide comments describing the overall function for each connected group of function blocks. Additionally, the programmer shall provide comments for each distinct intermediate calculation (Boolean function, selection, sum, limit, etc.). See example in Figure 5 Function block comments. Use of the City’s laptop is encouraged to provide consistent software versioning, ensure use of the SCADA & PCS Change Management Server, and promote SCADA & PCS security. 4.2Procedure The process to request a laptop from the City is as follows: 1) Fill out and submit the Programming Laptop Request Form. The form shall be filled out electronically and submitted via e-mail to the City’s designated project manager or other designated contact. The City will supply the blank electronic form upon request. A copy of the form is included in Appendix B 2) Transmit supporting documentation, such as assigned programmer name, project number/description, expected duration, and required software. 3) The City staff will review the programming laptop request the City will assign one of the following statuses: a. Accepted: If the request is acceptable, the City will notify the requester in writing, note any limitations placed, and provide an anticipated laptop transfer date. b. Rejected: If the request is rejected, the City will notify the requester in writing The City will return a written decision within ten(10) working days under normal circumstances. Laptops will generally be available for transfer ten(10) working days after approval. The City will work with the requester if further explanation or discussion is needed to reach a timely decision. - [Project Identification / Executive Summary] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 4) Network Design - Maintain drawings depicting PCS/SCADA system interconnections. Maintain the drawings in electronic and hardcopy formats. Electronic copies shall be maintained using the latest version of Microsoft Visio and PDF file formats 5) Physical Drawing - Maintain a Layer 2 drawing that depicts the physical interconnection of network hardware and hosts. Include location, cable type, cable standard, interface speed, host name, description, vendor, model number, MAC address, IP address, and subnet mask. The drawing shall include all components and subcomponents with a MAC address. 6) Logical Drawing - Maintain a Layer 3 drawing consistent with the zones & conduits model used in ISA/IEC 62443. The drawing shall depict the various zones (e.g., networks, subnets, VLANs, etc.) and conduits (e.g., Layer 3 switches, routers, firewalls, etc.) used to connect the zones together. The drawing shall include hosts grouped according to the zone in which they reside and the default gateway for each zone. The drawing shall include WAN links and supporting hardware. Include location, host name, description, vendor, model number, MAC address, IP address, subnet mask, and interface speed. The drawing shall include all components and subcomponents with an IP address. 7) IP Addressing Scheme - Maintain a spreadsheet that documents the IP addresses and related TCP/IP settings (e.g., default gateway, DNS servers, etc.) for all nodes with an IP address. Group the listing by subnet/VLAN. Maintain the addressing scheme for consistency with the City’s existing overall scheme to prevent conflicts with IP addresses used elsewhere in the City’s networks. 8) Ports/Applications/Services - Maintain a listing of services and applications required for each computer system in the PCS/SCADA system. The listing shall include all ports, applications, and services required for normal operation as well as any others required for emergency operation. Ensure an explanation exists to justify why each is necessary. 9) Firewall Rule Sets - Maintain documentation on firewalls and firewall rule sets required for normal and emergency operations. 10)Data Flow Diagram - Maintain a diagram depicting communications between software components. Maintain the diagram in MS Visio format. The diagram shall show communications related to all products listed in the device inventory and software inventory. Include ports, protocols, and components that initiate communication. Use typical depictions for similar communication links. 11)Network Boundary Inventory - Maintain an inventory of all network boundaries where communications is possible with other networks external to the HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. The ISA 101 HMI standard serves as a guideline to help organizations design, build and operate effective HMI applications. The City’s Standard Object Library incorporates recommendations from ISA 101. All new objects and displays shall follow the current ISA 101 recommendations at time of development to maintain system with latest industry standards. The City’s Standard Object Library provides a set of ready to use HMI objects for use in the development of HMI graphics. The object library makes it easy to create an interface that is more consistent in both appearance and function. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Replacement of the Existing Fiber Cables: The existing fiber cables included in the network are past their useful life having been installed several decades ago. If the cables were to be replaced, it would have to be done with parallel new fiber cables that allow the existing network to function without interruption. However, the 11 fiber cables entering through a conduit beneath the main patch panel in the Main Pump Station Building network room leave the conduit with very little room to pass through additional fiber. Although it’s possible to utilize MaxCell sleeves to snake new fiber cables through the one existing conduit, it would be easier to install a new conduit run if the network rack were to remain where it is currently located. 2.2.3 Recommended Fiber Network Upgrades Given the available documentation on the fiber network and the site investigation, the following is recommended for upgrading the network:  Run new fiber cable in existing fiber cable conduit from new control systems at each building installed under each individual amendment package (to the HFC AWTP Master Plan Improvement project) to connect to a new patch panel near the existing network rack. New fiber runs from this new patch panel are to the be connected to the existing network equipment. The existing network rack is located within the existing Main Pump Station network equipment room.  Install new fiber cables from the upgraded PLCs associated with process improvement, utilizing existing conduit duct bank and manholes, through either electrical manhole E-6 or E-7, then through the new conduits to the new network rack.  Gradually remove existing fiber runs as associated processes are upgraded and successfully running off the new network.  Keep both networks operational until the last replacement fiber run is complete. The existing plant fiber patch panel located near the existing network rack is to be kept in service until all processes have been updated and connected with new fiber runs upon which the existing patch panel can be removed.  Provide redundant fiber loops at the outer edges. As an example, a new fiber run between PLC 17 and PLC 7 would allow data transmission for PLCs in either quadrant back to the main network rack regardless of where conduit runs might be lost. PLC 18 (located in Filter Building No. 1) is the most critical to have fiber redundancy as loss of this PLC would result in loss of the plant’s permit analyzers and flow-proportional chemical feeds and samplers, which could shut down STAR. Fiber loops would require some reconfiguration of the programming to designate the “normal” communication path and a backup path if normal route is lost. During the design phase of each improvement to HFC AWTP, the existing network diagram and the new network diagram will be updated to maintain accurate records of the both systems until the existing is fully removed from service. Camera feeds are on the same fiber cable as SCADA, utilizing separate fibers. This approach shall be continued for new fiber cables. HFC AWTP Master Plan June 10,2022 Improvements: SCADA Design Criteria Technical Memorandum Page 9 of 15 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 c. An industrial DMZ shall act as a buffer network(i.e., DMZ) between the business network and the PCS/SCADA system networks. i. The industrial DMZ shall be the only means of communications beyond the PCS/SCADA system domain. Other architectures (including the use of dual or multi-homed hosts) are prohibited. The use of dial-up modems is prohibited. The use of cellular routers is prohibited. ii. The PCS/SCADA system shall operate autonomously (industrial DMZ, other plant PCS, etc.) except for remote SCADA sites associated with the SCADA system. Remote SCADA sites shall operate autonomously with no dependency on SCADA communications; the only exception would be a site that must receive a monitoring signal from another site for control purposes (e.g., an elevated tank sends a level signal to pump station to control the level in the elevated tank). d. The industrial DMZ may, based on project scope, contain physical or virtual workstations and servers needed to facilitate data exchange with business network applications. Firewall rules shall be configured to only allow ports and protocols required for the following functions that could be provided. i. Antivirus definition updates ii. Operating system patches iii. IDS/IPS definition updates iv. Syslog servers v. Web portal vi. Process control historian(Tier 2) vii. FTPS Server viii. Security information and event management(SIEM) ix. Email relay x. Backup repository e. The industrial DMZ shall be configured to prevent host-to-host communications unless specifically required to meet operational requirements. In such cases, ports, protocols, and initiating direction shall be configured to minimize connectivity in accordance with least functionality and least privilege principles. f. The PCS/SCADA system Tier-1 historian shall push data into a Tier-2 historian in the PIN. Business network users and enterprise HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. - [Network / Fiber / Communications Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 7 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Standard Logic Descriptions 7.1Initialization and Non-Volatile Memory The controller maintains its program, data, and configuration through non-volatile memory(NVM). This is memory that can exist in several forms, battery backed up RAM, EEPROM, or compact flash device(SD card). This technology allows the processor to return to its previous state after being powered up. The controller must provide some type of non-volatile memory. At no time is it acceptable to rely on continuous power. The NVM must be capable of withstanding the same environmental conditions as the controller. The particular form-factor of the NVM is dependent on the manufacturer. The preferred types of NVM are those that do not rely on batteries, or other mechanical devices(such as hard disk drives). This memory shall also not be read-only but support re-writing using the native development tools. The process of loading the program after power loss must be inherent in the controller natively. At a minimum the non-volatile memory must return the control program to active RAM. The ability of returning retained variable data to its previous state is a function that is desirable. However, when the ability to return retained data values is not supported, the control code itself must account for this and suitable initialization and start-up logic. 7.2Peer-to-peer Communications Within the plant all messaging shall be performed over Ethernet. If Ethernet is not available to the device, alternatives may be employed with City approval. For remote locations the preferred platform is Cellular modems however, if cellular modems are not appropriate, with City approval, acceptable alternative platforms are serial radio modems, and satellite for extremely remote locations. In all cases, alternative peer-to-peer communications are to be submitted to the City for review and approval prior to implementation. All inter-processor communications will be performed utilizing native communication control elements. An example of this is to utilize the “MSG” instruction in a ControlLogix controller. All communications will be performed in a read-only manner. At no time is a data write, or push, allowed. This leaves the receiving end in a non-deterministic condition in which the receiving controller is unsure of all of the factors affecting communications. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. This will be application-dependent, based on the availability of information and field I/O in the controller. 3.3Source Protection Source protection is a feature implemented in many development environments which provides the ability to lock proprietary code so that it cannot be viewed or modified by anyone who does not have the key. Source protection of code is prohibited. This includes all code created or used by systems integrators, programmers, vendors and OEM suppliers. Unless contractually stipulated to the contrary, all source code, images, databases, and supporting documentation are the property of the City. As such, all of this information is to be supplied to the City no later than initiation of site acceptance testing. 3.4Documentation It is the programmer’s responsibility to provide an annotated program compliant with the minimum documentation requirements defined in this section for all program files. As a general guideline, the level of documentation provided should allow for a technician with no familiarity of the functions of the specific unit process to read the comments and fully understand the inputs, outputs, and logic implemented in the program. 3.4.1 Object Parameters, UDT and Tag Comments It is required that all tags including object parameters and UDT elements be provided with completed description fields. Object parameters, UDT elements, and tags are subject to tag name and description requirements defined in Section 4.1 of this volume. HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. - [Testing / Startup / Commissioning] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 Communications are to be configured in VTScada using the appropriate driver and port tag constructs. The driver tag, specifically, must be appropriate to the communications protocol. Separate, stand-alone servers will be dedicated to data acquisition. There will be at least two servers configured for the system in the roles of primary and backup. This will provide redundancy for the purposes of monitoring the information from field devices and controllers. There are expected to be pairs of data acquisition servers for collections/distributions, waste water operations, and water operations. The data acquisition servers(referred to as default servers) will be added to the system configuration default server list as one, uniquely named device. Under this named configuration the server machines that will act as the primary and backup systems will be listed. The primary data acquisition machine being that system listed first and subsequent backups following in order. In this way the system allows for the growth and expansion of data acquisition clusters servicing the data needs for logical areas of operations as a whole. 3.1.4 Configuration Systems Configuration systems are workstations specifically designed for system configuration and contain the currently running configuration with the system property “Automatically Deploy Local Changes” disabled. These systems have been added to the system server lists under the “Configuration” category. All development and testing will utilize these stations at the City’s Operations Center. Upon successful testing and acceptance, the ChangeSet file will be transferred to deployment nodes. HMI clients are the portals through which the process is monitored and controlled by the operator. These stations may be based on workstations, laptops, or mobile devices. As such additional consideration must be given to the functionality of the screens as the platform changes. All workstation based HMI clients will be added to the configuration of the system. This allows for remote administration of the machines as well as the ability to push updates to the systems. Mobile clients, either portable or vehicle mounted, will be based on Windows 10 with cellular communications for data access. As with all systems these stations will be HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 13 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] 7.6Analog IndicationBars Process values that are analog in nature may be indicated in two ways. The first being a dynamic value as addressed in Section(7.2)(#page-251-0) Process Values. The second is through the use of analog indication bars. This allows for a very compact, dynamic illustration of the value within the context of its ranges and alarm points. This is to be applied to values that are non-static in nature and not setpoints or other sub-elements. The following properties will be utilized, if available, for analog values. - Process value - Nominal range - Desired range - Hi and Hi-Hi alarm points (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 3 of 3 HMI tags for controller referenced data will maintain the same encapsulated methodology as is employed in the controllers. These tags will be constructed from context type tags designed to mimic the structure of the controller data. A one to one relationship shall be maintained. In places where data is not contained in encapsulating structures, the HMI tag will still be constructed in a structured manner. The individual addresses will be handled internally to the object itself. The structure will be developed in such a way as to allow passing of data en masse to display elements and widgets. The individual tag thus representing the complete definition of data and information for the device or concept. 6.2.2 HMI Exclusive Tag References For those instances when data is to be managed exclusively in the HMI, those tags will follow the same naming conventions as outlined in the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual Volume 2 PLC, This applies to the following types of tags: - Alarm Tags - Logging and Reporting Tags - Calculation and Inquiry Tags - Station Tags - Configuration Tags The data that is exclusive to the HMI will be managed in a structured manner employing context tags as the container. Tag construction will be approached such that as needs change elements inside of a context tag can be added or deprecated without having to edit all graphical elements utilizing the tag. 6.3Controller Tag Naming Standard Reference the City’s SCADA & PCS Standards and Conventions Manual, Volume 2, PLC, for tag naming requirements. In the event that the naming standards does not have the desired abbreviations the developer is to submit a list of abbreviations to the City for review and approval. All tags created within the HMI and SCADA system must be distinctive. HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. Curren AWTP SCADA Standards - HMI and OIT Volume 3 of 3 February 2025 28 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] PLC Instrument Tags The PLC instrument tagging shall follow the same format as P&IDs. Additionally, these tags shall include any modifiers. The below signal tags correlate to the examples provided for the P&ID tags above. Example 1: FIT-SCB-04-02 Example 2: LIT-DWB-25-03-02 Example 3: HS-DWB-07-02-01-Hand(For the hand position) HMI Tag Names HMI tags shall be the same as the PLC tag with the addition of the “HFC” as a prefix . This will differentiate devices at the HFC AWTP from those at different locations, such as lift stations. Example 1: HFC-FIT-SCB-04-02 Example 2: HFC-LIT-DWB-25-03-02 Example 3: HFC-HS-DWB-07-02-01-Hand(For the hand position) Unique Instrument AAA Designations The City uses the follow unique ISA instrument designations: MN Run Status(ON or OFF) MF Failure LH/LL Level High/Low PH/PL Pressure High/Low SH/SL Speed High/Low TH/TL Temperature High/Low JF Breaker Status ZH Valve/Gate Status 2.2 Fiber Optic Network 2.2.1 Existing Network The existing fiber network is installed in a “spoke-hub” distribution, or “star” configuration, with separate fiber lines branching out from the network rack located in the Main Pump Station near PLC 19. The current HFC AWTP fiber network layout is shown in Appendix A. As shown in the appendix, the network is colorcoded and labeled with line numbers, refer to Table 2-3 for a list of zone colors and lines. Fibers are generally installed in a single conduit along the paths shown, with conduit installed in a concrete encased duct. If a critical conduit is damaged any PLC downstream of that damage will no longer be accessible on the plant SCADA. There are 11 main fiber trunk lines that run out to the various processes within these regions(Lines 0 through 10). - [Risks / Red Flags / Ambiguities] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] SCADA Standards Volume 1 of 3 4) Network Design - Maintain drawings depicting PCS/SCADA system interconnections. Maintain the drawings in electronic and hardcopy formats. Electronic copies shall be maintained using the latest version of Microsoft Visio and PDF file formats 5) Physical Drawing - Maintain a Layer 2 drawing that depicts the physical interconnection of network hardware and hosts. Include location, cable type, cable standard, interface speed, host name, description, vendor, model number, MAC address, IP address, and subnet mask. The drawing shall include all components and subcomponents with a MAC address. 6) Logical Drawing - Maintain a Layer 3 drawing consistent with the zones & conduits model used in ISA/IEC 62443. The drawing shall depict the various zones (e.g., networks, subnets, VLANs, etc.) and conduits (e.g., Layer 3 switches, routers, firewalls, etc.) used to connect the zones together. The drawing shall include hosts grouped according to the zone in which they reside and the default gateway for each zone. The drawing shall include WAN links and supporting hardware. Include location, host name, description, vendor, model number, MAC address, IP address, subnet mask, and interface speed. The drawing shall include all components and subcomponents with an IP address. 7) IP Addressing Scheme - Maintain a spreadsheet that documents the IP addresses and related TCP/IP settings (e.g., default gateway, DNS servers, etc.) for all nodes with an IP address. Group the listing by subnet/VLAN. Maintain the addressing scheme for consistency with the City’s existing overall scheme to prevent conflicts with IP addresses used elsewhere in the City’s networks. 8) Ports/Applications/Services - Maintain a listing of services and applications required for each computer system in the PCS/SCADA system. The listing shall include all ports, applications, and services required for normal operation as well as any others required for emergency operation. Ensure an explanation exists to justify why each is necessary. 9) Firewall Rule Sets - Maintain documentation on firewalls and firewall rule sets required for normal and emergency operations. 10)Data Flow Diagram - Maintain a diagram depicting communications between software components. Maintain the diagram in MS Visio format. The diagram shall show communications related to all products listed in the device inventory and software inventory. Include ports, protocols, and components that initiate communication. Use typical depictions for similar communication links. 11)Network Boundary Inventory - Maintain an inventory of all network boundaries where communications is possible with other networks external to the HFC AWTP Master Plan Howard F. - [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] Report Creator Software 2.3 PI System with PIVision AVEVA, a British multinational software company, originated as the Computer-Aided Design Centre in Cambridge in 1967, created by the UK Ministry of Technology and Cambridge University. Over its 40+ years as an engineering software leader in the plant, power, and marine industries, AVEVA became publicly traded in 1996 and later merged with Schneider Electric’s industrial software business in March 2018. In January 2023, Schneider completed its acquisition, privatizing AVEVA. The company has a prominent industry presence, with approximately 170 million search results on Google and installations worldwide, supporting clients from San Jose, California, to Rotterdam, Netherlands. AVEVA’s PI System, available through a subscription model, provides a comprehensive platform featuring the PI Server, PI System Connections, and PI System Tools. The PI Server is hosted onsite, managing and storing plant data, while PI System Connections enable data collection from various sources, offering over 200 interfaces, including SCADA, DCS, equipment databases, text files, and HTML pages. PI System Tools include a suite of add-ons for data visualization and analysis; for instance, PIVision allows data access from any device through a browser based inteface, enabling report creation and viewing. PIData Link supports data export to Excel, where users can save reports in any Excelcompatible format. Additionally, the software can be configured to automate alerts, sending notifications via email when specified conditions are met. The PI System’s subscription model ensures continuous support and access to the latest software updates from AVEVA. The system accommodates extensive storage, with some customers managing over a million tags on a single system. PI’s integration capabilities with VTScada allow seamless configuration with virtually any data source by selecting the appropriate interface, connector, or adapter. Contact with AVEVA was established through their website, with Andrew Primeaux(andrew.primeaux@aveva.com) serving as the main point of contact. HFC AWTP Master Plan December 2024 Howard F. Curren AWTP - SCADA System Report Creator Software Evaluation 9 (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)
- [SCADA / Control System Requirements] Outreach)** [ TABLE ] 2 SCADA Standards Volume 2 of 3 Programming Software 2.1Development Platform The specific software to be used is Studio 5000 Logix Designer, Version 36 In the case of a new product being introduced to the facility the version will be the most recent available. The software must support all of the capabilities that the hardware offers. The Integrated Development Environment(IDE) shall provide a complete set of tools for platform configuration, application program development, online troubleshooting / debugging, tag management, security, and safety. The PLC software is not to be password protected as requested by the City. Secondary tools, such as firmware update management, are acceptable as stand-alone applications due to their limited use. In all cases, lite, demo, or self-developed versions of development/configuration software are not allowed. All software furnished to the City on a project is to be properly licensed with all licensing transferred to City management no later than initiation of site acceptance testing. It is the responsibility of the developer/systems integrator to provide their own development software and licensing for off-site development. - [Testing / Startup / Commissioning] 6.3.1 Periodic tasks Periodic tasks are designed to execute on a fixed period of time, typically in milliseconds. These tasks are conducive when working with a process or system that is slow, or requires the processing on a regular basis. Creation of periodic tasks should be kept to a minimum(six or less) as required by the controlled process or area. The periodic task execution rate should be set to allow ample time for program execution without burdening the processor. Programmers shall frequently monitor and adjust the execution rate based on the task time. All programs shall be commissioned with task settings tuned such that no overlaps exist in the program. During commissioning, the main program overlap counter shall be confirmed at zero and FAULTLOG object shall not have any minor faults for overlap. Each periodic task must be set with a priority from 1 to 15, with 1 representing the highest priority. It is recommended that each task be set with a unique priority. (26-d-00017-rfqupost.pdf)

Introduction 1.1 Background The HFC AWTP is permitted to treat 96 mgd of raw sewage with a Type I two-stage, high rate (pure oxygen and conventional fine bubble aeration) activated sludge biological domestic wastewater treatment plant. This plant is operated to reduce the Total Nitrogen(TN) in the effluent to advanced standards, using biological treatment for nitrification and filtration for denitrification, and to discharge high-level disinfected and dechlorinated effluent to Tampa Bay. Residuals generated by this facility are anaerobically digested and dewatered for land application as a Class B residual. Currently, annual average daily flows are approximately 60 mgd and peak flows are approximately 200 mgd. The rehabilitation to the HFC AWTP will consist of the implementation of improvements identified in the Facility Master Plan Capital Improvement Project List for Fiscal Years 0-5 to maintain system reliability, increase operating efficiency, and meet current and future regulatory requirements. This Technical Memorandum details the SCADA Design Criteria. These criteria will be utilized for all instrumentation and control design performed as part of these improvements. 1.2 Workshops Three(3) workshops were held with the City in developing the SCADA Design Criteria. ### Project Overview
**Project Overview Summary: Howard F. Curren Advanced Wastewater Treatment Plant (HFC AWTP) SCADA Migration & Infrastructure Upgrade**

The City of Tampa, Florida is soliciting proposals via RFQ No. 26-D-00017 for a comprehensive Design-Build project to migrate monitoring and control capabilities at the Howard F. Curren Advanced Wastewater Treatment Plant (HFC AWTP) from legacy systems—specifically High-Speed Query (HSQ) software—to **VTScada 12.1.58** manufactured by Trihedral, while simultaneously replacing the existing fiber optic cable network and upgrading PLC hardware. This initiative aligns with an ongoing Master Plan to upgrade operational reliability and efficiency at a facility permitted to treat approximately 96 mgd of raw sewage using a Type I two-stage activated sludge process (pure oxygen/conventional fine bubble aeration). Current annual average flows are ~60 mgd, with peaks up to 200 mgd. The estimated fee range for engineering services covering software selection, cost estimation, contract document preparation, and construction plan development is **$2,000,000 – $4,000,000**.

**Key Project Objectives & Scope:**
*   **SCADA Migration & Software Implementation:** The project involves migrating remaining facilities operating on HSQ software to VTScada. This includes transitioning PLC hardware from legacy General Electric PAC System Rx3i/90-30 series controllers to advanced Allen Bradley (Rockwell Automation) units for critical processes such as the O2 Plant, Filter Building No. 1, Main Pump Station, HPO Reactor Improvements, Dewatering Facility Replacement, Junction Chamber #1, Screen and Grit Buildings, Primary Sedimentation Tanks, Digestion, and STAR facilities.
    *   **Data Continuity:** The new system must retain historical data dating back to the year 2000 (with potential retention of earlier records) while generating combined current/historical reports.
    *   **Parallel Operation & Transition:** Migration will occur parallel to the current HSQ system until reliability is demonstrated; once validated, legacy screens may be retired or set to view-only mode before decommissioning. The project ensures continuous operation throughout the migration via online switchover capabilities.
*   **HMI & Reporting Development:** All new Human-Machine Interface (HMI) screens must utilize a specific City-provided ChangeSet file and custom library widgets per *City Standard Object Library Programming Guideline*. Designs will support hierarchical navigation from facility overviews down to individual equipment dashboards, with options for distinct servers by area.
    *   **Report Creator Selection:** The project evaluates report creator software (specifically assessing candidates like **XLReport**). Key criteria include generating accurate real-time reports for regulatory compliance and operational metrics (water flow rates, chemical usage), seamless integration with existing databases, support via Microsoft Excel technology to reduce training costs, NIST security compliance, scalability, and browser usability.
*   **Fiber Optic Network Replacement:** The project replaces fiber cables that have exceeded their useful life after decades of service. The recommended approach involves installing new parallel fiber runs through upgraded PLCs in existing conduit duct banks (e.g., E-6 or E-7) and manholes to a new patch panel near the Main Pump Station rack, establishing redundant loops for critical areas without immediately removing old infrastructure.
    *   **Redundancy & Security:** New paths prevent total shutdowns if a PLC fails. Camera feeds remain on separate fibers within these cables. The existing SONY camera network is being replaced with a GENETEC system featuring new servers and integration capabilities, alongside modernized site security including card readers at entry gates and two-way voice communication capabilities.
*   **Structural & Instrumentation Updates:** Assigning new building numbers (065 and 066) for dewatering structures and sludge feed pump stations, correcting instrument tagging per revised P&IDs, and preparing control panel data sheets against component lists from workshops.

**Technical Standards & Architecture:**
*   **Design Criteria:** Established based on workshops held between October 2021 and June 2022 (and extending into the Fiscal Years 0–5 Master Plan), adhering to ISA-95/ISA-101 standards where applicable. These cover P&ID formats (matching existing layouts), tagging systems (`DDDYYY-XXX-ZZ` format), hardware specifications (e.g., matching PLC speeds of 100Mbps with new network equipment in the operations area), and internet/site security protection.
*   **System Architecture:** The design distinguishes between local Process Control Systems (PCS) within plant boundaries and remote SCADA sites designed to run autonomously. Cybersecurity measures include VPN connections for vendor access, rigorous labeling protocols at patch panels, adherence to NIST cybersecurity standards, 21 CFR Part 11

# Procurement and Commercial Requirements Summary: Howard F. Curren Advanced Water Treatment Plant (HFC AWTP)

## Executive Overview
The City of Tampa, Florida (Project No. 20-C-00001), is soliciting proposals for the Howard F. Curren Advanced Water Treatment Plant (HFC AWTP). The procurement strategy prioritizes hardware reliability, cybersecurity, seamless integration with existing infrastructure, and long-term viability. A primary objective is replacing legacy Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs)—specifically GE PACSystems Rx3i and 90-30/70 series—to mitigate risks associated with discontinued support, price inflation from third-party parts, long lead times, and cybersecurity vulnerabilities. Concurrently, evaluations were conducted on Report Creator Software platforms to select a solution that supports plant expansion without incurring additional licensing fees (Source: *HFC AWTP Master Plan documents*).

The evaluation prioritizes **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** over extended horizons (5, 10, and 15 years). While upfront costs vary significantly—such as the high initial price for Ignition's non-cloud edition ($24,337.50)—the five-year TCO remains competitive against solutions like DreamReport due to lower long-term operational expenses. Commercial decisions must account for restrictive licensing structures; platforms requiring licenses based on concurrent users or specific tag thresholds incur additional costs as the facility scales, whereas options offering unlimited categories provide better value for growing operations. Redundancy is a critical commercial consideration, often available at 50% of main license prices to ensure business continuity without prohibitive costs (Source: *City's SCADA & PCS Recovery and Restore Policy*).

The solicitation deadline is **March 19, 2026**, at 2 P.M. Submissions must be sent in searchable PDF format under 10MB to `ContractAdministration@tampagov.net`. It is the bidder's responsibility to confirm receipt, as acknowledgements may not be provided for same-day submissions (Source: *City of Tampa - RFQ amount of City Work*).

## Compliance and Legal Obligations
*   **Statutory Adherence & Exemptions:** Firms must demonstrate familiarity with Florida Statutes Section 287.055(10) regarding document reuse, as well as Sections 119.071(3), 119.07(1), and Article I, Section 24(a) of the Florida Constitution concerning "Exempt Plans" (building plans/blueprints exempt from public records laws). The City maintains confidentiality for materials claimed under Chapter 119 exemptions.
*   **Non-Discrimination:** The City will not consider a bidder's social, political, or ideological interests when determining responsible vendor status nor give preference based on such factors. Firms are encouraged to align with the City's Climate Action and Equity Plan (Source: *City of Tampa - RFQ...*).
*   **Contact Restrictions:** During the solicitation period, protests, or appeals, no contact with City officers is permitted unless specifically stated in the solicitation. The City reserves the right to cancel, modify, reject responses, and waive irregularities at its discretion (Source: *City of Tampa - RFQ...*).
*   **Vendor Screening:** Firms or affiliates placed on the convicted vendor list under §287.133, Fla. Stat., are prohibited from submitting bids or performing work for public entities. Proposers must certify they have not been listed (Source: *City of Tampa - RFQ...*).

## Good Faith Effort (GFE) and Small Business Enterprise (SLBE) Requirements
The procurement process mandates strict adherence to the Good Faith Effort Compliance Plan (GFECP) guidelines regarding SLBE participation. All prime contractors, including certified SLBEs, must perform outreach regardless of whether they intend to self-perform all work or use subcontractors. If no subcontracting occurs, the contractor must explain why on the required forms.

*   **Solicitation Timeline:** Firms on the SLBE Goal Setting List must be solicited via written or electronic notification at least five city business days prior to the bid date (Source: *City of Tampa - RFQ...*).
*   **Documentation Standards:** Documentation must confirm that specific services were offered, plans reviewed, and assistance provided for bonding, insurance, equipment mobilization, and subcontracting opportunities. Failure to complete Forms 10, 20, or 50 renders a proposal non-responsive (Source: *HFC AWTP Master Plan documents*).
*   **Form Specifications:**
    *   **DMI 10 Form:** Documents firms solicited but who did not provide a quote/response. Requires contractor business details, Federal ID, trade/services (with NIGP codes), contact method used, and response status ("Y/N").
    *   **DMI 20 Form:**

# Scope of Work: SCADA System Modernization, Upgrade, and Compliance Framework for HFC AWTP

The **Scope of Work** defines comprehensive technical standards, procedural requirements, migration objectives, security protocols, and compliance guidelines for the development, management, operation, and maintenance of Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) and Process Control Systems (PCS). This framework applies to contractors, consultants, and in-house personnel working on systems within critical infrastructure projects managed by the City. Specifically addressing the **Howard F. Curren Advanced Wastewater Treatment Plant (HFC AWTP)**—permitted to treat 96 mgd of raw sewage with a Type I two-stage activated sludge plant currently operating at approximately 60 mgd average daily flow—these standards govern instrumentation, control systems, fiber communications network replacement, site security upgrades, and Fiscal Years 0–5 improvements. The project encompasses specific Master Plan Improvement projects (including the Main Pump Station, HPO Reactor, Dewatering Facility, O2 Plant, Filter Building, and Standby Power) with a primary objective of migrating monitoring capabilities from HSQ to **VTScada** software while ensuring continuous plant operation and regulatory reporting capability.

## 1. Project Mandate and Infrastructure Upgrades
The City's mandate includes evaluating report creation software options that integrate with existing database systems to analyze data (e.g., water flow rates, chemical usage) while ensuring regulatory compliance. The scope further encompasses the rehabilitation of plant areas and specific infrastructure upgrades:

*   **Surveillance Systems:** Replacing SONY cameras with GNETEC units for all plant areas and integrating card readers into perimeter gates.
*   **Cybersecurity & Physical Security:** Installing cyber locks on building entryways, motion detectors, and planning an 8-foot high fence replacement. Access controls include keyed locks at physical access points; unauthenticated remote tools like RDP/VNC are prohibited unless protected by AAA encryption. Mandatory VPN usage is required for all remote connections to the Process Information Network (PIN), with automatic session logout enforced after 10 minutes.
*   **Network Infrastructure:** The fiber optic cable network replacement requires preparing cost estimates, construction sequencing, signed/sealed plans with specifications, final cost estimates, and contract documents for bidding. Services during this phase include shop drawing reviews and responding to Requests for Information (RFIs). Planning must account for scenarios where loss of the PLC would shut down permit analyzers and chemical feeds. Field investigations are required to verify existing conduit runs, drain manholes (including removing covers), confirm new cable routing without disturbing existing fibers, install field labeling for cables/ports matching final network diagrams, and re-route specific fibers due to structural changes like building removals.
*   **Legacy Transition:** Evaluating legacy hardware, specifically transitioning from aging General Electric (GE/PACSystems Rx3i and 90-30 series) controllers—now in "legacy" status since 2018 due to discontinued support and security risks—to equivalent alternatives that balance features against budgetary costs.
*   **Facility Coverage:** Ensuring safety, efficiency, and regulatory compliance across wastewater treatment facilities including Main Pump Stations, Intermediate Pump Stations, RAS stations, Oxygen Plants, stormwater stations, and lift station systems.

## 2. Software Architecture & Compliance
The control system utilizes **VTScada version 12.1.58** (developed by Trihedral Engineering) as the core software suite; developers are strictly prohibited from using newer versions or relying on cross-version controls for new deployments (*HFC AWTP Master Plan February 2025 – SCADA Standards Policies*). All applications are developed using VTScada software based on City-provided ChangeSet files to ensure application consistency. These "ChangeSet" files serve as a compendium of changes and base information rather than exact backups; they must be requested prior to programming initiation unless mission-critical updates are issued directly by City management.

*   **Tagging System:** A consistent, human-decipherable tagging system is required for all elements transferred between controllers, SCADA, HMI, OIT, gateways, and other systems to ensure unique names with a regular format throughout the entire City system (e.g., codes for site location like "H" for HFW AWTP). PLC tags must use underscores instead of hyphens due to program limitations.
*   **Validation Protocols:** All software versions, patches, application programs (PLC, HMI, OIT), and firmware updates must be validated in a limited-capacity City Test Environment before live production deployment. Updates require rigorous testing involving 100 hours of hands-off error-free operation and validation of fault tables/HMI logs prior to implementation via an approved Management of Change (MOC) plan (*Management of Change*).
